## 2.5 Freehold vs. Leasehold — Head-to-Head Comparison

| Parameter | **Freehold Interest** | **Leasehold Interest** |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Perpetual (infinite) | Fixed term (finite) |
| **Inheritance** | Yes, fully inheritable | Limited to lease term — heirs only get the *residue* |
| **Market Value Trend** | Stable or appreciating | Depreciating (wasting asset) |
| **Income Stream** | Permanent ground rent (if any) | Limited to unexpired term |
| **Risk Profile** | Lower (no expiry shock) | Higher (expiry cliff) |
| **Valuation Method** | YP on perpetuity basis | YP on annuity basis for term + reversion |
| **Example Instrument** | Sale Deed, Gift Deed | Lease Deed, Rent Agreement |
| **Legal Owner of Land** | Freeholder | Freeholder (lessor) |
| **Right to Sublet** | Absolute (subject to law) | Restricted (as per lease covenants) |
| **Reversionary Value** | Nil (already owns everything) | Yes — reverts to freeholder at expiry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversionary Value** is a key concept: at the end of the lease, the property *reverts* to the freeholder. The freeholder's interest (apart from ground rent) is this **reversionary value**, which is the present value of the property *as a freehold* at the *future* expiry of the lease — discounted back to today.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Years' Purchase for Perpetuity

### 🔬 Starting Concept
Assume a piece of land generates a **net annual income of ₹A** in **perpetuity**, and the market rate of interest is **i** (per unit, per year). The present value (PV) of the land = ?

### 📜 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** If you invest ₹P in a bank at interest rate *i*, the annual interest you receive is:

$$I = P \times i$$

**Step 2:** For the land to be "equivalent" to the bank deposit, the annual income (₹A) must equal the interest earned (₹I):

$$A = P \times i$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the principal P (the present value / capitalised value):

$$P = \frac{A}{i} = A \times \frac{1}{i}$$

**Step 4:** By definition, Years' Purchase is the multiplier that converts annual income into capitalised value:

$$\boxed{\,YP_{perpetuity} = \frac{1}{i}\,}$$

### 📊 Numerical Illustration

Let A = ₹10,000 per year, i = 10% = 0.10

$$P = \frac{10{,}000}{0.10} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000$$

$$YP = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10 \text{ years}$$

> ✅ **Verification:** ₹1,00,000 × 10% = ₹10,000 = annual income. **Match confirmed.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Years' Purchase for a Limited Term (Annuity)

### 🔬 Starting Concept
Suppose a leasehold yields ₹A for **n years only** (then expires). What is the present value of this finite income stream?

### 📜 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** The income received in Year 1 has PV = $A / (1+i)^1$.

**Step 2:** The income received in Year 2 has PV = $A / (1+i)^2$.

**Step 3:** Continuing this pattern, the income received in Year *k* has PV = $A / (1+i)^k$.

**Step 4:** The total present value of all n incomes is the geometric sum:

$$P = A \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} \right]$$

**Step 5:** This is a **Geometric Progression (G.P.)** with first term $a = 1/(1+i)$ and common ratio $r = 1/(1+i)$. The sum of the first *n* terms of a G.P. is:

$$S_n = \frac{a(1 - r^n)}{1 - r}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $a$ and $r$:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}\right]}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)}}$$

**Step 7:** Simplifying the denominator: $1 - 1/(1+i) = i/(1+i)$

$$P = A \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i)^n}}{\frac{i}{(1+i)}}$$

**Step 8:** The $(1+i)$ terms cancel in numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{\,P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} \implies YP_{annuity} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}\,}$$

### 📊 Numerical Illustration
Let A = ₹10,000, i = 10%, n = 20 years.

$$YP = \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{20}}$$

$$(1.10)^{20} \approx 6.7275$$

$$YP = \frac{6.7275 - 1}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.67275} \approx 8.514$$

$$P = 10{,}000 \times 8.514 = \text{₹}85{,}140$$

> ✅ **Comparison with Perpetuity:** For a 20-year term at 10%, YP ≈ 8.51, whereas perpetuity YP = 10.00. The leasehold is **always worth less** than the freehold for the same annual income.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sinking Fund Formula

### 🔬 Starting Concept
A valuer wants to set aside ₹S *each year* into a fund earning interest rate *i* such that at the end of *n* years, the accumulated amount = ₹1. Find S.

### 📜 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** First deposit of ₹S grows for *n* years → $S(1+i)^n$.

**Step 2:** Second deposit of ₹S grows for $(n-1)$ years → $S(1+i)^{n-1}$.

**Step 3:** Continuing this pattern, the *k*-th deposit grows for $(n-k+1)$ years → $S(1+i)^{n-k+1}$.

**Step 4:** The last (n-th) deposit grows for 1 year → $S(1+i)^1$.

**Step 5:** Total accumulated amount = Sum of the G.P.:

$$1 = S \left[ (1+i) + (1+i)^2 + \cdots + (1+i)^n \right]$$

**Step 6:** This G.P. has first term $a = (1+i)$, ratio $r = (1+i)$, and $n$ terms. Sum:

$$1 = S \cdot \frac{(1+i)\left[(1+i)^n - 1\right]}{(1+i) - 1}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify: $(1+i) - 1 = i$:

$$1 = S \cdot \frac{(1+i)\left[(1+i)^n - 1\right]}{i}$$

**Step 8:** Solving for S:

$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} \implies SF = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}\,}$$

### 📊 Numerical Illustration
Let i = 8% = 0.08, n = 10 years.

$$SF = \frac{0.08}{(1.08)^{10} - 1} = \frac{0.08}{1.1589 - 1} = \frac{0.08}{0.1589} \approx 0.0690$$

> ✅ **Interpretation:** To accumulate ₹1 in 10 years at 8% interest, you must save approximately **₹0.069 per year**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Applying YP to Different Valuation Scenarios

| Scenario | Type of Interest | YP Formula Used | Resulting Capitalised Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Land yielding rent in perpetuity | **Freehold** | $1/i$ | $A / i$ |
| Leasehold with 30 years remaining | **Leasehold** | Annuity YP for 30 years | $A \times YP_{30}$ |
| Freehold after lease expires (reversion) | **Reversionary Freehold** | $1/(i \times (1+i)^n)$ | $A / [i(1+i)^n]$ |
| Building replaced via Sinking Fund | **Depreciation analysis** | $SF \times C$ | $C \times i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ |
| Joint: leasehold income + reversion | **Composite** | Annuity YP + Reversion YP | $A \times YP_{annuity} + A \times YP_{reversion}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Whenever a question gives you *both* a lease term *and* a reversion to freehold, the **total freehold value = leasehold value (annuity for term) + reversionary value (deferred perpetuity)**, *not* just the perpetuity. This is the most common trap in KTU Part B questions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Freehold vs. Leasehold Valuation Comparison

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A property yields a **net annual rental income of ₹50,000**. The market rate of interest is **10%**. Compute:
> (a) Value as a **Freehold** (perpetuity)
> (b) Value as a **Leasehold** with **20 years** remaining

### ✅ Solution

**Part (a) — Freehold Valuation (Perpetuity):**

$$YP_{perpetuity} = \frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10$$

$$MV_{Freehold} = 50{,}000 \times 10 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$

**Part (b) — Leasehold Valuation (20-year term):**

$$YP_{annuity} = \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{20}}$$

$$(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$$

$$YP_{annuity} = \frac{6.7275 - 1}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.67275} = 8.5136$$

$$MV_{Leasehold} = 50{,}000 \times 8.5136 = \text{₹}4{,}25{,}680$$

### 📊 Interpretation

$$\text{Loss due to leasehold nature} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}25{,}680 = \text{₹}74{,}320$$

$$\text{Loss percentage} = \frac{74{,}320}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 14.86\%$$

> ✅ **Conclusion:** The leasehold is worth **₹74,320 less** than the freehold — this gap is the *economic cost* of the time-bound restriction. As the lease nears expiry, this gap widens.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The "Layered Cake" Model of Interests in Land

This diagram visually establishes the **stacking of multiple interests** on a single piece of land — a critical conceptual foundation for understanding freehold vs. leasehold valuation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Freeholder: Owns the Land Perpetually] --> B[Leases to Lessee for 30 Years]
    B --> C[Lessee holds Leasehold Interest]
    C --> D[Lessee subleases to Sublessee for 5 Years]
    D --> E[Sublessee holds Sub-Leasehold Interest]
    A --> F[Freehold Value: Annuity 1 over i in perpetuity]
    C --> G[Leasehold Value: Annuity YP for 30 Years]
    C --> H[At Year 30: Reversion to Freeholder]
    E --> I[Sub-Leasehold Value: Annuity YP for 5 Years]
    H -.->|Discounted back to Year 0| J[Reversionary Value added to Freehold]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000
    style E fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000
    style F fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    style G fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style H fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#f4b183,stroke:#7f3a00,color:#000000
    style J fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- **Top Layer (Freeholder):** Owns forever, benefits from ground rent + reversion.
- **Middle Layer (Lessee):** Holds for 30 years, sub-leases the top 5 years, keeps the residual 25 years.
- **Top Layer (Sublessee):** Holds the right to use for the *shortest* term — has the most *time-restricted* interest.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Time-Decay of Leasehold Value

This block diagram illustrates how a **leasehold interest is a "wasting asset"** — its value declines every year as the lease term shortens.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Y0[Year 0: Lease Begins] --> Y5[Year 5: 25 Years Left]
    Y5 --> Y10[Year 10: 20 Years Left]
    Y10 --> Y15[Year 15: 15 Years Left]
    Y15 --> Y20[Year 20: 10 Years Left]
    Y20 --> Y25[Year 25: 5 Years Left]
    Y25 --> Y30[Year 30: LEASE EXPIRES Value = 0]

    Y0 -.->|Max Value| V0[High Capitalised Value]
    Y30 -.->|Zero Value| V30[Zero Worth to Lessee]
    Y30 -.->|Reversion| VREV[Full Value Returns to Freeholder]

    style Y0 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style Y5 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style Y10 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style Y15 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000
    style Y20 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000
    style Y25 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#7f3a00,color:#000000
    style Y30 fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style V0 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    style V30 fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style VREV fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- At **Year 0**, the leasehold has its **maximum value** (full unexpired term).
- As years pass, the unexpired term shrinks — the YP multiplier falls — and the **capitalised value decays**.
- At **Year 30 (expiry)**, the leasehold value = **₹0** for the lessee, but the **freeholder's reversionary interest is fully realised**.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Functional Architecture: How a Valuer Decides Freehold vs. Leasehold

This flowchart shows the **decision logic** a valuer follows when classifying the type of interest before computing value.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Start: Property Valuation Assignment]) --> Q1{Is there a Lease Deed?}
    Q1 -->|No| FH[Interest is FREEHOLD]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{What is the Unexpired Term?}
    Q2 -->|Less than 99 Years| LH[Interest is LEASEHOLD]
    Q2 -->|99 Years or more| QH[Quasi-Freehold: Often Treated as Freehold for Valuation]

    FH --> F1[Apply Perpetuity YP: 1 over i]
    LH --> L1[Apply Annuity YP for Unexpired Term]
    QH --> QH1[Apply Annuity YP for Term + Reversion Discounting]

    L1 --> L2[Compute Leasehold Value]
    L1 --> L3[Compute Reversionary Value to Freeholder]
    L3 --> L4[Total Freehold Value: Leasehold Value plus Reversion]

    F1 --> OUT1[Capitalised Value Output]
    L2 --> OUT2[Leasehold Value Output]
    L4 --> OUT3[Freehold Value Output via Composite Method]
    QH1 --> OUT4[Quasi-Freehold Capitalised Value Output]

    style START fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style FH fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    style LH fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style QH fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000
    style OUT1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style OUT2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#7f3a00,color:#ffffff
    style OUT3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style OUT4 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- A valuer must first ask: **"Is there a lease deed?"**
- If **No** → Freehold (perpetuity formula).
- If **Yes** → Identify the **unexpired term** and apply the **annuity YP** for that term.
- The **reversion to the freeholder** at lease expiry must be valued separately and added to compute the **total freehold value** (composite method).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This decision logic is analogous to choosing a structural system in Civil Engineering — the *type of interest* determines the *valuation methodology*, just as the *type of soil* determines the *foundation design*. A valuer's first decision frames every subsequent calculation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### 🔹 Question A1: Define Freehold Interest in a property. State any two of its salient features. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **Freehold Interest** is the absolute and perpetual ownership of a property, including the land, buildings, sub-soil, and air space, without any time-based restriction. The freeholder holds the title in *fee simple* and can transfer, bequeath, mortgage, or inherit the property indefinitely.

**Two Salient Features:**
1. **Perpetual Duration** — The interest does not expire by efflux of time. It continues in perpetuity unless sold, gifted, or escheated to the State.
2. **Inheritance & Transferability** — The interest passes to legal heirs on the death of the freeholder (under personal law of succession) and can be transferred by sale, gift, mortgage, or exchange, subject only to statutory restrictions (e.g., urban land ceiling, coastal regulation zone rules).

> *(Any two of: perpetual, inheritable, transferable, includes land + structures + air + sub-soil rights)*

---

### 🔹 Question A2: Differentiate between Freehold and Leasehold interests in land. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Freehold | Leasehold |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Duration** | Perpetual (infinite) | Fixed term (finite) |
| 2 | **Owner of Land** | Holder himself | Lessor (landlord) |
| 3 | **Income Stream** | Permanent | Limited to lease term |
| 4 | **Market Value Trend** | Stable / Appreciating | Depreciating (wasting asset) |
| 5 | **Valuation Formula** | $A \times 1/i$ (perpetuity) | $A \times YP_n$ (annuity) |
| 6 | **Example Instrument** | Sale Deed | Lease Deed / Rent Agreement |

*(Any 3 distinctions for full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question B (Option A) — **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **(a)** Define Freehold and Leasehold interests in land. Explain the components of freehold value with a neat sketch. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A property yields a net annual income of **₹75,000** perpetually. Compute its **freehold value** at an interest rate of **9%** p.a. If the same property is on a **lease with 25 years remaining**, compute its **leasehold value**. Also compute the **reversionary value** to the freeholder. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definitions:**

- **Freehold Interest:** The absolute, perpetual, and inheritable ownership of land and the structures upon it, with no pre-determined time limit.
- **Leasehold Interest:** A right to possess and use the property for a defined period as per the lease deed, after which it reverts to the freeholder.

**Components of Freehold Value:**

The market value of a freehold = **Land Value (LV) + Structural Value (SV)**

$$\boxed{\,MV_{Freehold} = LV + SV\,}$$

- **Land Value (LV):** Site value — permanent, appreciating, independent of the structure.
- **Structural Value (SV):** Value of the building and improvements, subject to depreciation.

**Neat Sketch (Conceptual Block Diagram):**

```
+----------------------------------+
|     FREEHOLD VALUE (MV)          |
|     = Land Value + Structural    |
|     Value                        |
+----------------------------------+
   |                          |
   v                          v
+------------+        +----------------+
| LAND VALUE |        | STRUCTURAL     |
| (Permanent)|        | VALUE          |
| (Apprec.)  |        | (Depreciating) |
+------------+        +----------------+
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Net Annual Income (A) = ₹75,000
- Rate of Interest (i) = 9% = 0.09
- Lease Term Remaining (n) = 25 years

**Step 1: Freehold Value (Perpetuity):**

$$YP_{perpetuity} = \frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{0.09} = 11.1111$$

$$MV_{Freehold} = 75{,}000 \times 11.1111 = \text{₹}8{,}33{,}333$$

**Step 2: Leasehold Value (25-Year Annuity):**

$$YP_{annuity} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

$$(1.09)^{25} = 8.6231$$

$$YP_{annuity} = \frac{8.6231 - 1}{0.09 \times 8.6231} = \frac{7.6231}{0.77608} = 9.8226$$

$$MV_{Leasehold} = 75{,}000 \times 9.8226 = \text{₹}7{,}36{,}695$$

**Step 3: Reversionary Value (Deferred Perpetuity, after 25 years):**

$$YP_{reversion} = \frac{1}{i(1+i)^n} = \frac{1}{0.09 \times 8.6231} = \frac{1}{0.77608} = 1.2885$$

$$MV_{Reversion} = 75{,}000 \times 1.2885 = \text{₹}96{,}638$$

**Step 4: Verification (Composite Method):**

$$MV_{Freehold} = MV_{Leasehold} + MV_{Reversion}$$

$$\text{₹}7{,}36{,}695 + \text{₹}96{,}638 = \text{₹}8{,}33{,}333 \quad ✅$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Component | Value (₹) |
|---|---|
| Freehold Value (perpetuity) | **8,33,333** |
| Leasehold Value (25-year term) | **7,36,695** |
| Reversionary Value (deferred) | **96,638** |
| Loss due to leasehold nature | **96,638** (14.86% of freehold) |

---

### 📗 Question B (Option B) — **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Years' Purchase (YP)**. Derive the YP formula for a perpetuity and for a limited term of *n* years. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A leasehold property has **15 years** remaining. The net annual income is **₹60,000** and the rate of interest is **10%** p.a. Compute:
>     (i) Leasehold value
>     (ii) Reversionary value to the freeholder
>     (iii) Total freehold value
> **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Concept of Years' Purchase (YP):**
Years' Purchase is the multiplier that converts a future stream of net income (annual rent, interest, etc.) into its present value (capitalised value). It is the reciprocal of the rate of interest for perpetuities, and a more complex function for limited terms.

**Derivation 1 — YP for Perpetuity:**

If a property yields net annual income *A* perpetually, and the market interest rate is *i*, then the capitalised value P must satisfy:

$$A = P \times i \implies P = \frac{A}{i} = A \times \frac{1}{i}$$

$$\boxed{\,YP_{perpetuity} = \frac{1}{i}\,}$$

**Derivation 2 — YP for Limited Term (n years):**

Sum the present values of each annual income over *n* years:

$$P = A \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} \right]$$

Using the geometric series sum formula and simplifying:

$$\boxed{\,YP_{annuity} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}\,}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Unexpired lease term (n) = 15 years
- Net Annual Income (A) = ₹60,000
- Rate of Interest (i) = 10% = 0.10

**Step 1: Compute $(1.10)^{15}$:**

$$(1.10)^{15} = 4.1772$$

**Step 2: Leasehold Value (Annuity for 15 years):**

$$YP_{annuity} = \frac{4.1772 - 1}{0.10 \times 4.1772} = \frac{3.1772}{0.41772} = 7.6061$$

$$MV_{Leasehold} = 60{,}000 \times 7.6061 = \text{₹}4{,}56{,}366$$

**Step 3: Reversionary Value (Deferred Perpetuity):**

$$YP_{reversion} = \frac{1}{0.10 \times 4.1772} = \frac{1}{0.41772} = 2.3938$$

$$MV_{Reversion} = 60{,}000 \times 2.3938 = \text{₹}1{,}43{,}630$$

**Step 4: Total Freehold Value (Composite):**

$$MV_{Freehold} = 4{,}56{,}366 + 1{,}43{,}630 = \text{₹}5{,}99{,}996 \approx \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000$$

**Verification via Perpetuity Formula:**

$$MV_{Freehold} = 60{,}000 \times \frac{1}{0.10} = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000 \quad ✅$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Component | Value (₹) |
|---|---|
| (i) Leasehold Value (15-year term) | **4,56,366** |
| (ii) Reversionary Value to Freeholder | **1,43,630** |
| (iii) Total Freehold Value | **6,00,000** |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in Freehold vs. Leasehold Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up YP formulas:** Using the **perpetuity formula** ($1/i$) for a *leasehold* (finite term) inflates the value — examiners will mark you down **2–3 marks** instantly. Always check: *"Is the income for ever or for a fixed term?"*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the reversion:** When asked for the *total freehold value* of a leasehold property, students often quote only the annuity value and forget to **add the reversionary value**. This loses **3–4 marks** in the long-answer section.
>
> 3. **Wrong interest rate conversion:** Writing *i = 10%* directly into the formula instead of *i = 0.10* leads to a **calculation cascade error**. Always convert percentages to decimals first.
>
> 4. **Not stating the assumptions:** KTU examiners reward students who explicitly state: *"Assume income is constant,"* *"Assume interest is compounded annually,"* *"Assume lease is renewable at market rent."* Skipping this can cost **1 mark** per assumption.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 🎯 **High-Yield Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Freehold** = Perpetual, absolute, inheritable ownership. Value = $A \times (1/i)$ = **Annuity 1 over i** (perpetuity).
- **Leasehold** = Time-bound right carved out of a freehold. Value = $A \times YP_n$ where $YP_n = [(1+i)^n - 1] / [i(1+i)^n]$.
- **Reversionary Value** = Present value of the property as a freehold *after* the lease expires. Formula: $A / [i(1+i)^n]$ — i.e., a **deferred perpetuity**.
- **Total Freehold Value** = Leasehold Value + Reversionary Value (**Composite Method**).
- **Land is permanent** (does not depreciate); **structures depreciate** with age — separate them in valuation.
- **Sinking Fund (SF)** factor: $i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ — used in depreciation of buildings.
- **Capital Recovery (CR)** factor: $i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ — total annual charge for recovery + interest.
- **A 99-year lease** is often treated as **quasi-freehold** in practice (due to very high YP ≈ perpetuity YP).
- **Leasehold = wasting asset** — its value **decreases** every year, while the freeholder's reversionary interest **increases** yearly.
- **Key Indian legal reference:** Sections 105–117 of the **Transfer of Property Act, 1882** govern leases.
- **Key exam mnemonic:** *"Free is Forever, Lease is Limited."* Use this to instantly recall the duration difference.
- **For KTU answers:** Always show three steps — (1) State assumption, (2) Apply formula with substituted values, (3) State final answer with ₹ symbol and units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forms of Lease, Mortgage and Income
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Forms of Lease, Mortgage and Income # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 LEASE — Definition & Real-World Intuition ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (KTU Standard Terminology)** > A **Lease** is a contractual agreement wherein the **Lessor** (owner of the property) grants the **Lessee** (tenant) the right to possess and use the property for a stipulated period in consideration of a periodic payment called **Rent (Ground Rent / Premium)**. The transfer of possession is temporary, while the ownership (title) remains vested with the lessor. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy** > Think of a lease like **renting a movie on a streaming platform**. You pay a monthly subscription (rent) to access and use the movie (property) for a defined period. The platform company (lessor) still owns the master copy; you only enjoy the *right to use*. The moment your subscription lapses, your access vanishes — but the original content remains theirs. ### Essential Parties in a Lease | Party | Role | Interest | |---|---|---| | **Lessor** | Owner of the property | Retains ownership, receives rent | | **Lessee** | Tenant / Occupant | Holds possession for the lease term, pays rent | | **Sub-Lessee** | Third party to whom lessee re-leases | Holds derivative interest | ### Key Legal / Valuation Phrases - **Premium (Salami / Capital Premium):** Lump-sum non-returnable amount paid upfront to obtain the lease. - **Ground Rent:** Periodic (monthly/annual) payment made to the lessor. - **Reversion:** Right of the property reverting back to the lessor (or his heir) at lease expiry. - **Renewal Clause:** Provision allowing extension of lease on agreed terms. --- ## 1.2 MORTGAGE — Definition & Real-World Intuition ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (KTU Standard Terminology)** > A **Mortgage** is the transfer of an interest in specific immovable property as security for the repayment of a loan (principal + interest). The **Mortgagor** (borrower) retains possession (in most forms) while the **Mortgagee** (lender) holds a conditional interest, which becomes absolute upon default. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy** > Imagine you keep your **gold jewellery** with a banker as a guarantee for a loan. You still wear/use it daily; the banker only *holds a claim* on it. If you repay the loan, the claim is released. If you default, the banker can sell the jewellery to recover dues. A mortgage works the same way on a house — the *house is the gold, the bank is the banker, and the loan is the financial obligation*. ### Key Mortgage Vocabulary - **Principal (Capital Sum):** Original loan amount. - **Interest Rate:** Annual rate at which interest accrues on the outstanding principal. - **Foreclosure:** Legal right of the mortgagee to terminate the mortgagor's right to redeem. - **Redemption:** Right of the mortgagor to reclaim the property by repaying the debt. --- ## 1.3 INCOME — Definition & Real-World Intuition ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (KTU Standard Terminology)** > **Income from a property** is the monetary return generated by a real estate asset over a defined period, classified into **Gross Income (GI)** — total receipts before any deduction — and **Net Income (NI)** — the residual amount available to the owner after deducting all outgoings (taxes, repairs, insurance, management, sinking fund, depreciation). ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy** > Think of a **salary slip**. Your **CTC (Cost to Company)** is like *Gross Income*. But the amount actually credited to your bank account — after TDS, PF, professional tax, insurance — is your *Net Income*. For property, **GI is the rent collected**; **NI is what the owner truly keeps** after paying all the property's "monthly bills" (maintenance, taxes, depreciation provision). ### Core Income Components for Valuation - **Gross Rent / Annual Rental Value (ARV)** - **Allowable Deductions:** Municipal taxes, insurance, repairs, management charges - **Sinking Fund (SF):** Provision for future replacement of short-life assets - **Depreciation (D):** Annual loss in value of building components - **Net Annual Income (NAI):** GI − (all outgoings including SF & D) - **Capitalized Value (CV):** NAI × Years Purchase (YP) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 FORMS OF LEASE — Detailed Analysis ### Classification Tree A lease is classified on the basis of **duration**, **purpose**, and **reversion rights**. The following are the standard KTU-prescribed forms: #### (a) Perpetual Lease - **Concept:** Lease running in **perpetuity** (forever in theory), inheritable by successive heirs. - **Why & How:** Used for long-term commercial / agricultural land grants; the lessee pays a fixed ground rent perpetually. - **Valuation Insight:** Because income is perpetual, **YP = 1/r** (Years Purchase for perpetuity). - **Reversion:** None in the standard sense — lease continues indefinitely. #### (b) Fixed-Period Lease (Term Lease) - **Concept:** Lease granted for a **definite period** (e.g., 30, 50, 99 years). On expiry, property reverts to lessor. - **Why & How:** Common in **urban commercial leases**; widely used in valuation of life-interest properties. - **Valuation Insight:** Apply **YP for limited period** plus the **reversionary value** at the end of the term. #### (c) Life Lease - **Concept:** Lease granted for the **lifetime of the lessee** (or another specified life). - **Why & How:** Common in inheritance settlements, where income is tied to a human lifespan. - **Valuation Insight:** Based on **actuarial life tables** — the lease has no defined end-date, but has a probabilistic end. #### (d) Building Lease - **Concept:** Lessee is given land for the **purpose of constructing a building** within a specified period, after which the building reverts to the lessor. - **Why & How:** Used by **municipalities, railways, port trusts** for leasing plots to developers. - **Valuation Insight:** Two streams — **(i)** rent received during lease term, **(ii)** value of reverted building (less land value) at end of term. #### (e) Sub-Lease - **Concept:** The lessee (now becoming the sub-lessor) re-leases the property to a **third party** for a part or the whole of the remaining term. - **Why & How:** Common in commercial complexes; requires consent of the original lessor. - **Valuation Insight:** Sub-lessee's interest = unexpired portion of original lease; the difference between original and sub-lease rent is the sub-lessor's profit. --- ## 2.2 FORMS OF MORTGAGE — Detailed Analysis ### Standard KTU Classification of Mortgages #### (a) Simple Mortgage (Section 58, Transfer of Property Act) - **How it works:** Mortgagor binds himself personally to repay the loan and **agrees that, in default, the mortgagee may sell the property**, but does NOT deliver possession. - **Possession:** With **mortgagor** (owner-occupant). - **Redemption:** Mortgagor retains right to redeem at any time before sale. #### (b) Usufructuary Mortgage (Section 58) - **How it works:** Mortgagee takes **possession** of the property and receives **rents and profits** in lieu of interest, OR in satisfaction of both principal and interest. - **Possession:** With **mortgagee** (lender). - **Critical:** NO personal liability binds the mortgagor. #### (c) English Mortgage (Section 58) - **How it works:** Mortgagor binds himself to **repay the principal + interest on a specific date**, and provides that, in default, the property shall be **absolutely transferred** to the mortgagee. - **Possession:** With **mortgagor**. - **Critical:** Mortgagee can sue the mortgagor personally for the debt. #### (d) Equitable Mortgage (Section 58) - **How it works:** Created by **mere deposit of title deeds** with the mortgagee (no registered instrument, no possession transfer). - **Possession:** With **mortgagor**. - **Critical:** Right is enforceable only in **equity**, not at common law. Common in **commercial banking**. #### (e) Anomalous Mortgage - **How it works:** A mortgage that does **not fall under any of the above four categories** — governed by the terms agreed between the parties. #### (f) Puisne Mortgage - **How it works:** A **second/subsequent mortgage** created on a property that is already subject to a prior mortgage. The puisne mortgagee's claim is **subordinate** to the first mortgagee. --- ## 2.3 INCOME — Capitalization & Methods ### Core Terminology - **Net Annual Income (NAI):** Annual income available to the owner **after all outgoings** (taxes, repairs, insurance, management, sinking fund, depreciation). - **Years Purchase (YP):** The **multiplier** that converts a perpetual annual income into its present capital value. - **Capitalized Value (CV):** The present-day lump-sum value of future income streams. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Boundary Condition | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Years Purchase (Perpetuity) | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Income perpetual | Years | | Years Purchase (Limited Period) | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}$ | Income for n years | Years | | Years Purchase (Deferred n Years) | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r} \times \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | Perpetual income starting after n years | Years | | Net Income | $NI = GI - (\text{All Outgoings})$ | Standard | Currency/yr | | Capitalized Value (Perpetual) | $CV = NI \times YP$ | Perpetual income | Currency | | Capitalized Value (Term + Reversion) | $CV = (NI \times YP_{term}) + \dfrac{RV}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | Limited-period lease | Currency | | Sinking Fund Installment | $A = S \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | S = Future sum required | Currency/yr | | Sinking Fund Formula (YP) | $YP_{SF} = \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Equivalent YP replacing NI | Years | | Capitalized Value (Sinking Fund Method) | $CV = NI \times \dfrac{1}{r} - Sinking\ Fund\ Provision$ | Property reverts to lessor | Currency | > [!IMPORTANT] **SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT — Why two methods?** > - **Direct Capitalization** (NI × YP): Used when property is **perpetual / owned indefinitely**. > - **Sinking Fund Method**: Used when the property will **revert to the lessor** at the end of the term — the owner must accumulate a fund (sinking fund) to replace the building so that the lessor receives a *worn-out* property of equal replacement value. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Concept | Field Application | |---|---| | **Lease** | Contract documentation in **PPP (Public-Private Partnership)** projects, BOT (Build-Operate-Transfer) toll road concessions, railway land licensing, port land grants. | | **Mortgage** | **Real estate financing** in housing finance companies (HFCs), bank NPA (Non-Performing Asset) resolution, SARFAESI Act enforcement, foreclosure valuation. | | **Income Capitalization** | **Valuation of income-producing real estate** — shopping malls, IT parks, hotels, warehouses, commercial office buildings, agricultural land rental. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Years Purchase for Perpetuity ### Starting Premise Suppose a property yields a perpetual annual net income of $NI$. If the prevailing market rate of return (interest rate) is $r$ (per unit per year), then the capitalized value $CV$ must be such that: $$CV \times r = NI$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation Rearranging the basic income-equation for capitalized value: $$CV = \frac{NI}{r}$$ If we define **Years Purchase (YP)** as the multiplier that, when applied to a unit of annual income, gives the present capital value, then setting $NI = 1$: $$YP_{perpetuity} = \frac{1}{r}$$ **Interpretation:** When the rate of return is 8% (r = 0.08), the YP for a perpetual income is 12.5 years. This means a perpetual income of ₹1 lakh per year is equivalent to receiving ₹12.5 lakhs today. --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Years Purchase for Limited Period (Inwood's Table Foundation) ### Starting Premise A property yields a constant net income $NI$ for a finite period of $n$ years. After that, the income ceases (property fully reverts or income stops). ### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation The present value of an annuity of ₹1 per year for $n$ years at rate $r$ is the sum of present values of each year's income: $$PV = \frac{1}{(1+r)^{1}} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^{2}} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^{3}} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}$$ This is a **geometric progression** with first term $a = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$ and common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$. Using the geometric series sum formula $S_{n} = a \cdot \dfrac{1-q^{n}}{1-q}$: $$PV = \frac{1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+r}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r}}$$ Simplifying the denominator $1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r} = \dfrac{r}{1+r}$: $$PV = \frac{1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}}{\dfrac{r}{1+r}}$$ Cancelling $\dfrac{1}{1+r}$: $$PV = \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}}{r} = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}$$ Therefore, the **Years Purchase for a limited period** is: $$YP_{n} = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}$$ This is the foundation of the standard **Inwood's Tables** used in property valuation. --- ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Capitalized Value with Reversion ### Starting Premise A property is leased for $n$ years. The lessee pays an annual net rent of $NI$. At the end of the lease, the property (with its structure) reverts to the lessor. The **Reversionary Value (RV)** is the value of the property at the end of year $n$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Component 1:** Present value of the term income (a finite annuity): $$PV_{term} = NI \times \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}$$ **Component 2:** Present value of the reversionary value (a single future lump sum): $$PV_{reversion} = \frac{RV}{(1+r)^{n}}$$ **Total Capitalized Value (CV):** $$CV = NI \times \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}} + \frac{RV}{(1+r)^{n}}$$ **Interpretation:** This is the formula used for valuing **fixed-period (term) leases** and is critical in **building lease** valuation where the constructed building reverts to the landowner at the end of the lease. --- ## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Sinking Fund Method ### Starting Premise A property is on a **fixed-term lease** ($n$ years) and reverts to the lessor at the end. The lessee receives annual net income $NI$, but the building will have zero salvage value to the lessee at expiry. The lessee must therefore set aside an **annual Sinking Fund (SF) installment** that accumulates (at a safe rate $i$) to equal the replacement cost of the building $S$ at the end of year $n$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation The future value of a sinking fund of ₹1 deposited annually at compound interest rate $i$ for $n$ years is given by the standard annuity-due / ordinary annuity formula: $$FV = A \times \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$ Setting $FV = S$ (the replacement sum required at year $n$): $$S = A \times \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$ Solving for the annual installment $A$: $$A = S \times \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$ The **effective annual income** that can be capitalized is therefore: $$NI_{effective} = NI - A$$ The **capitalized value (Sinking Fund Method)**: $$CV = (NI - A) \times \frac{1}{r} = \frac{NI - A}{r}$$ **Equivalent YP form** (used in exam short-cuts): $$CV = NI \times \left[ \frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{i} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}} \right) \right]$$ The bracketed term is called the **"Net YP after deducting Sinking Fund"** and is a frequently tested item in valuation papers. --- ## 3.5 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Selection of Method | Property Situation | Applicable Income Method | Governing Condition | |---|---|---| | Freehold perpetual property | Direct capitalization: $CV = NI \times \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Ownership indefinite | | Term lease (no reversion) | Annuity: $CV = NI \times \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ | Income stops at year $n$ | | Term lease with reversion | Term + Reversion formula | Property returns to lessor | | Term lease (reversion + replacement) | Sinking Fund Method | Lessee must replace building | | Life lease | NI × YP based on **expectation of life** | Acts of life tables | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Lease, Mortgage & Income in Property Valuation ```mermaid graph TD A[REAL PROPERTY VALUATION ENGINE] --> B[LEASE INSTRUMENT] A --> C[MORTGAGE INSTRUMENT] A --> D[INCOME STREAM] B --> B1[Perpetual Lease] B --> B2[Fixed Period Lease] B --> B3[Life Lease] B --> B4[Building Lease] B --> B5[Sub-Lease] C --> C1[Simple Mortgage] C --> C2[Usufructuary Mortgage] C --> C3[English Mortgage] C --> C4[Equitable Mortgage] C --> C5[Anomalous Mortgage] C --> C6[Puisne Mortgage] D --> D1[Gross Income GI] D1 --> D2[Outgoings Deducted] D2 --> D3[Net Income NI] D3 --> D4[Years Purchase YP] D4 --> D5[Capitalized Value CV] B2 --> D4 B4 --> D5 C1 --> D3 C2 --> D3 style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style B fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D5 fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow Diagram — Selecting the Right Income Method ```mermaid flowchart TD S[START: Identify Property Type] --> Q1{Is the lease perpetual or freehold?} Q1 -- YES --> M1[Method 1: Direct Capitalization
CV = NI x YP at 1 divided by r] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Does income cease at end of lease term?} Q2 -- YES --> M2[Method 2: Annuity Capitalization
CV = NI x YP for n years] Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Does property revert to lessor at end of term?} Q3 -- YES --> Q4{Is the building to be replaced by lessee?} Q4 -- YES --> M3[Method 3: Sinking Fund Method
CV = NI - Sinking Fund times YP] Q4 -- NO --> M4[Method 4: Term + Reversion
CV = Term PV + Reversion PV] Q3 -- NO --> M1 style S fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style M1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style M2 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style M3 fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style M4 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Income Capitalization Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR P1[Gross Annual Rent] --> P2[Less: Municipal Tax] P2 --> P3[Less: Insurance Premium] P3 --> P4[Less: Repairs and Maintenance] P4 --> P5[Less: Management Charges] P5 --> P6[Less: Sinking Fund Installment] P6 --> P7[Less: Depreciation Provision] P7 --> P8[Net Annual Income NAI] P8 --> P9[Multiply by Years Purchase YP] P9 --> P10[Capitalized Value CV] style P1 fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P8 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P10 fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Forms of Mortgage (Possession vs. Personal Liability) | Mortgage Form | Possession With | Personal Liability | Sale on Default | |---|---|---|---| | Simple | Mortgagor | Yes | Yes (via court) | | Usufructuary | Mortgagee | No | No (rent adjusts debt) | | English | Mortgagor | Yes | Absolute transfer | | Equitable | Mortgagor | Yes | Equitable sale | | Anomalous | Per agreement | Per agreement | Per agreement | | Puisne | Mortgagor (typically) | Yes | Subject to first charge | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Distinguish between a Perpetual Lease and a Fixed-Period Lease.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | Perpetual Lease | Fixed-Period Lease | |---|---|---| | **Duration** | Indefinite — runs forever in theory | Definite period (e.g., 30, 99 years) | | **Reversion** | No reversion to lessor | Property reverts at term expiry | | **YP for valuation** | $\dfrac{1}{r}$ (Perpetuity) | $\dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ (Annuity) | | **Example** | Old land grants, agricultural tenure | Urban commercial lease of shop | *(1 mark for each distinguishing row = 3 marks)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define Usufructuary Mortgage. Who holds possession and what is the role of interest?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A usufructuary mortgage is one in which the mortgagee takes possession of the mortgaged property and receives the **rents and profits** in lieu of interest, or in satisfaction of both principal and interest. - **Possession (1 mark):** Possession lies with the **mortgagee** (lender), not the mortgagor. - **Role of Interest (1 mark):** The mortgagee does not have the right to sue the mortgagor personally for the loan; the rent/profit itself is treated as interest (and possibly principal recovery), so there is **no separate interest accrual in cash terms**. --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2024] **Q: (a) Explain the various forms of a mortgage with their distinguishing features.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The following are the **standard forms of mortgage** recognized under the Transfer of Property Act and tested in KTU valuation papers: **1. Simple Mortgage (1 mark)** - Mortgagor binds himself personally to repay the loan. - Agrees that, on default, the mortgagee may sell the property through court. - Possession remains with the **mortgagor**. - No transfer of possession — only a personal covenant to repay. **2. Usufructuary Mortgage (1 mark)** - Possession is delivered to the **mortgagee**. - Mortgagee collects rents/profits in lieu of interest (and possibly principal). - No personal liability binds the mortgagor. **3. English Mortgage (1 mark)** - Mortgagor agrees to repay principal + interest on a **specific date**. - On default, the property is **absolutely transferred** to the mortgagee. - Mortgagee retains a **personal right of action** against the mortgagor. **4. Equitable Mortgage (1 mark)** - Created by **mere deposit of title deeds** with the mortgagee. - No registered instrument, no possession transfer. - Right is enforceable only in **equity** (court of equity, not common law). **5. Anomalous Mortgage (1 mark)** - Any mortgage that does not fall within the four standard forms above. - Governed entirely by the **terms agreed** between the parties. **6. Puisne Mortgage (1 mark)** - A **second or subsequent mortgage** on a property already mortgaged. - Ranked **subordinate** to the prior (first) mortgage; the prior mortgagee's claim is settled first from the sale proceeds. **Concluding comparative remark (1 mark):** The choice of mortgage form affects (i) **who holds possession**, (ii) whether the **mortgagor has personal liability**, and (iii) the **mode of enforcement** on default. From a valuation perspective, the existence of a mortgage creates a **charge** on the property that reduces the mortgagor's equity (market value − outstanding loan). --- **Q: (b) A property yields a gross annual rent of ₹5,00,000. The following outgoings are estimated annually: Municipal Tax ₹60,000; Insurance ₹10,000; Repairs ₹40,000; Management 5% of gross rent; Sinking Fund 2% of gross rent; Depreciation 3% of gross rent. Calculate the Net Annual Income and the Capitalized Value at 8% interest (perpetual).** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate each outgoing (1 mark)** - Municipal Tax = ₹60,000 - Insurance = ₹10,000 - Repairs = ₹40,000 - Management (5% of ₹5,00,000) = ₹25,000 - Sinking Fund (2% of ₹5,00,000) = ₹10,000 - Depreciation (3% of ₹5,00,000) = ₹15,000 **Step 2: Total Outgoings (1 mark)** $$\text{Total Outgoings} = 60000 + 10000 + 40000 + 25000 + 10000 + 15000$$ $$\text{Total Outgoings} = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}000$$ **Step 3: Net Annual Income (1 mark)** $$NI = \text{Gross Rent} - \text{Total Outgoings} = 500000 - 160000$$ $$NI = \text{₹}3{,}40{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 4: Years Purchase for Perpetuity at 8% (1 mark)** $$YP = \frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5\ \text{years}$$ **Step 5: Capitalized Value (1 mark)** $$CV = NI \times YP = 340000 \times 12.5$$ $$CV = \text{₹}42{,}50{,}000$$ **Step 6: Final Answer Statement (1 mark)** > The **Net Annual Income is ₹3,40,000** and the **Capitalized Value of the property is ₹42,50,000** (i.e., ₹42.5 lakhs). --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: (a) Explain the different forms of lease with suitable examples and their valuation treatment.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Perpetual Lease (1 mark)** - Runs indefinitely; inheritable. - Example: Old agricultural land grants, hereditary tenancies. - **Valuation:** $CV = NI \times \dfrac{1}{r}$ (YP for perpetuity). **2. Fixed-Period (Term) Lease (1.5 marks)** - Defined period (e.g., 30 years, 99 years). - Example: Commercial office space lease, cinema hall land lease. - **Valuation:** $CV = NI \times YP_{n}$ for term; if reversion, add the reversionary value's present worth. **3. Life Lease (1 mark)** - Tied to a human lifespan; uses **actuarial life tables**. - Example: Estate leases granted "for the lifetime of X". - **Valuation:** $CV = NI \times YP_{life}$ where $YP_{life}$ depends on age and life expectancy of the lessee. **4. Building Lease (1.5 marks)** - Land given on lease for the lessee to **construct a building** within a period. - Example: Municipal plot leases, port trust land grants, railway colony plots. - **Valuation:** Two components — (i) rent during the term, (ii) the **value of the building (excluding land)** that reverts to the lessor at expiry. **5. Sub-Lease (1 mark)** - Lessee re-leases part/whole of the property to a third party. - Example: Anchor tenant in a mall sub-leasing a kiosk. - **Valuation:** Sub-lessee's interest = present value of rent differential over the unexpired term. **Concluding remark (1 mark):** The **valuation treatment differs** because of differences in income stream duration, reversion rights, and salvage considerations. --- **Q: (b) A building is constructed on a 30-year building lease. The annual ground rent is ₹1,20,000 and the outgoings are 40% of the ground rent. The reversionary value of the building (excluding land) at the end of 30 years is estimated at ₹20,00,000. Find the Capitalized Value of the lessee's interest at 8% interest rate.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Net Annual Income from the lease (1 mark)** $$NI = \text{Ground Rent} - \text{Outgoings} = 120000 - (0.40 \times 120000)$$ $$NI = 120000 - 48000 = \text{₹}72{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 2: Years Purchase for 30-year term at 8% (1 mark)** $$YP_{30} = \frac{(1+0.08)^{30} - 1}{0.08 \times (1+0.08)^{30}}$$ $$(1.08)^{30} = 10.0627\ (\text{standard table value})$$ $$YP_{30} = \frac{10.0627 - 1}{0.08 \times 10.0627} = \frac{9.0627}{0.8050}$$ $$YP_{30} = 11.258\ \text{years}$$ **Step 3: Present Value of the Term Income (1 mark)** $$PV_{term} = 72000 \times 11.258 = \text{₹}8{,}10{,}576$$ **Step 4: Present Value of the Reversion (1 mark)** $$PV_{reversion} = \frac{2000000}{(1.08)^{30}} = \frac{2000000}{10.0627}$$ $$PV_{reversion} = \text{₹}1{,}98{,}752$$ **Step 5: Total Capitalized Value (1 mark)** $$CV = PV_{term} + PV_{reversion} = 810576 + 198752$$ $$CV = \text{₹}10{,}09{,}328$$ **Step 6: Final Answer Statement (1 mark)** > The **Capitalized Value of the lessee's interest** in the 30-year building lease is **₹10,09,328** (approximately ₹10.09 lakhs). --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Valuation Numericals** > 1. **Forgetting the Sinking Fund provision** — In any term-lease problem, many students compute $CV = NI \times YP_{n}$ but forget that the property reverts. If the question says "**reverts to lessor**", you **MUST** subtract the sinking fund installment and apply the Sinking Fund Method, OR add the reversionary value's present worth. > 2. **Mixing up the rate of return and the sinking fund rate** — In Inwood's/sinking-fund method, the rate used for **capitalization** ($r$) and the rate used for **sinking fund accumulation** ($i$) are **not always the same**. Read the question carefully. > 3. **Using gross rent instead of net rent** in capitalization — A very common blunder. Capitalize **NET** income, not gross. > 4. **Confusing "ground rent" with "net income"** — Ground rent is the rent paid to the lessor; the lessee's net income is gross collectable rent **minus all outgoings**. > 5. **In the mortgage question**, students often write "English Mortgage" when the correct answer is "Simple Mortgage". Recall: **English = absolute transfer on default**; **Simple = conditional sale via court**. > 6. **Years Purchase units** — Always quote YP in "**years**" (not as a number) in the final statement. Examiner awards the concluding-step mark only for the unit. > 7. **Forgetting to discount the reversion** — Reversion is a **future** value. Always divide by $(1+r)^n$, never add it directly to the term present value. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Lease = temporary transfer of possession**; ownership stays with lessor. **Mortgage = security for loan**; possession usually stays with mortgagor. - **Perpetual Lease** → $CV = NI \times \dfrac{1}{r}$. - **Term Lease (no reversion)** → $CV = NI \times YP_{n}$. - **Term Lease (with reversion)** → $CV = NI \times YP_{n} + \dfrac{RV}{(1+r)^{n}}$. - **Sinking Fund Method** → $CV = (NI - A) \times \dfrac{1}{r}$, where $A = S \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$. - **Six mortgage forms**: Simple, Usufructuary, English, Equitable, Anomalous, Puisne — remember possession, personal liability, and enforcement mode. - **Three core lease forms to remember**: Perpetual, Fixed-Period, Life (with Building Lease and Sub-Lease as application-based variants). - **Inwood's Tables** are derived from the geometric series of discounted income and form the basis of all $YP_{n}$ lookups. - **Net Income = Gross Income − (Municipal Tax + Insurance + Repairs + Management + Sinking Fund + Depreciation)**. - **Capitalize NET, not GROSS.** - **Always check the question for two rates** — one for capitalization, one for sinking fund accumulation. - **Sinking fund** is essentially **self-insurance** for the lessor; it accumulates to a fund that replaces the building so the lessor gets a non-depreciated asset at reversion.

topics.title: Outgoings: Types and Sinking Fund
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Outgoings: Types and Sinking Fund

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Outgoings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outgoings** are the **recurring, periodic expenditures** that the owner of a property is legally or contractually bound to pay, which are **deducted from the gross rental income** to arrive at the **net effective income** used for capital valuation.

In the context of the **Years Purchase (YP) method** of valuation and the **Income Capitalisation Approach**, outgoings are the *liabilities of ownership* — the cost of *holding* the property, distinct from the cost of *acquiring* it. KTU 2024 Scheme specifically categorises them under the operational expenses module of property economics.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Net Salary Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of outgoings exactly like deductions from a gross salary.**
> Your **Gross Pay (₹1,00,000)** becomes **Take-Home (Net) Pay (₹78,000)** after deducting **PF, Tax, Insurance, Loan EMI**. For a property, the **Gross Rent** becomes the **Net Rent** (the *true earning capacity*) only after deducting all **outgoings** — tax, repairs, insurance, sinking fund, etc. **A valuer always values on Net Income, never Gross.**

### 1.3 Why Outgoings Matter in Valuation

The mathematical backbone of all rental-based valuation is:

$$\text{Valuation} = \text{Net Annual Income} \times \text{Years Purchase (YP)}$$

If outgoings are **underestimated**, the Net Income is **inflated**, leading to an **over-valuation** of the property — a fatal error in KTU board examinations and professional valuation practice.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants Used

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value (KTU Exam Default) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Rate of Interest on Sinking Fund | $i$ | **5% to 8% per annum** (commonly 6%) |
| Life of Building | $N$ | **60 to 100 years** |
| Scrap/Salvage Value | $S$ | **0% to 10%** of construction cost |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Outgoings

Outgoings are broadly classified into **two main categories**, which is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams:

### A. Revenue Outgoings (Recurring — paid annually/periodically)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **operational expenses** that occur *year after year* throughout the life of the property.

1. **Municipal & Local Body Taxes**
   - Property Tax / House Tax
   - Water Tax, Drainage Tax
   - Conservancy / Sanitation Tax
   - Street Lighting Tax, Fire Tax

2. **Repairs & Maintenance Charges**
   - Annual repairs (whitewashing, patchwork, plumbing)
   - Generally taken as **½% to 1% of the gross cost** of the building for *ordinary* repairs
   - *Special repairs* (e.g., re-roofing after 20 years) are handled via **Sinking Fund**, not as annual repair.

3. **Insurance Premium**
   - Fire insurance, earthquake insurance, structural insurance premium paid annually.

4. **Management & Collection Charges**
   - Salary of care-taker, rent collection costs, accountancy.
   - Usually **2% to 6% of gross rent**.

5. **Sinking Fund (SF)**
   - A *deliberate annual deposit* to accumulate a future lump sum (detailed in §2.2).

6. **Income Tax**
   - Tax on rental income (currently notional in KTU problems, unless specified).

7. **Legal Expenses**
   - Recurring litigation costs, attorney fees.

8. **Ground Rent**
   - If the land is on **leasehold**, the annual ground rent paid to the freeholder is an outgoing.

9. **Vacancy & Bad-Debts Allowance**
   - Provision for the property lying vacant between tenants (~5%–10% of gross rent).
   - Provision for tenants defaulting on rent.

### B. Non-Recurring / Capitalised Outgoings (One-time deductions)

- Arrears of taxes or ground rent.
- Outstanding mortgage interest at the time of purchase.
- Cost of *initial* repairs to bring the property to a rentable condition.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Board questions often test the difference between *Recurring* and *Non-Recurring* outgoings. Always tabulate them separately in your answer script.

---

## 2.2 The Sinking Fund — Conceptual Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A **Sinking Fund** is a *deliberate annual sum* set aside from the gross income of a property and invested at compound interest, so that on the expiry of the useful life of the building (or any specific component like a roof, lift, water tank), the **accumulated amount equals the cost of replacement**, without requiring a fresh capital outlay at that time.

### The 'Why' — Logical Reasoning Behind Sinking Fund

**Step 1:** A building *deteriorates* with time. A roof that lasts 20 years must be replaced.

**Step 2:** If no provision is made, the owner must suddenly find a large lump sum at year 20 — a financial shock.

**Step 3:** Instead, the owner deposits a *small* fixed amount *every year* in a compound-interest-bearing fund.

**Step 4:** By year 20, the accumulated corpus (Principal + Compound Interest) = Cost of new roof. This is the **Sinking Fund**.

**Step 5:** This annual deposit is treated as an **annual outgoing** in valuation.

### 'How' It Works — Mathematical Logic

If you deposit an amount $A$ at the end of every year at compound interest rate $i$ for $n$ years:

$$\text{Future Value of the Fund} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

The factor $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ is called the **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**.

If the **target corpus** required at year $n$ is $C$ (e.g., replacement cost of roof), then:

$$A = C \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

This $A$ is the **Annual Sinking Fund (Outgoing)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Module 1 — must be memorised verbatim.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Net Annual Income (NAI) | $\text{NAI} = \text{Gross Rent} - \text{All Outgoings}$ | Currency/year |
| 2 | Future Value of Sinking Fund | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | $F$ = corpus, $A$ = annual deposit |
| 3 | Annual Sinking Fund (given target) | $A = C \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $C$ = target amount |
| 4 | Annual Sinking Fund (depreciation equivalent) | $\text{SF} = (C - S) \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $C$ = original cost, $S$ = scrap value |
| 5 | Total Annual Outgoings | $\Sigma O = T_r + R + I + M + SF$ | Sum of taxes, repairs, insurance, mgmt., sinking fund |
| 6 | Valuation by Rental Method | $V = \text{NAI} \times \text{YP}$ | $V$ = property value |
| 7 | Years Purchase | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r}$ (for perpetual) | $r$ = rate of return |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** Sinking Fund ($A$) is **NOT the same as** Depreciation (Straight Line: $\frac{C-S}{n}$). They are mathematically different. Sinking Fund uses **compound interest**; Straight Line Depreciation does not. KTU examiners love to set a comparison question on this.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banks & NBFCs** use sinking fund methodology for *asset replacement reserves*.
- **Infrastructure** (bridges, dams, flyovers) — Government Public Works Departments compute SF for periodic overhaul.
- **Co-operative Housing Societies** maintain a *Sinking Fund Reserve* — legally mandatory in many states.
- **Insurance Valuation** — insurers insist on a sinking fund provision to ensure the *sum assured* matches *replacement cost*, not *market cost*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sinking Fund Formula

**Statement:** A sum of money $A$ is invested at the **end of every year** for $n$ years at compound interest rate $i$ per annum. Find the accumulated value at the end of $n$ years.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $A$ be the annual deposit made at the **end** of each year (this is the *ordinary annuity* assumption used in KTU).

**Year 1 deposit:** It remains invested for $(n-1)$ full years.
$$\text{Future Value of Year 1 deposit} = A(1+i)^{n-1}$$

**Year 2 deposit:** It remains invested for $(n-2)$ full years.
$$\text{Future Value of Year 2 deposit} = A(1+i)^{n-2}$$

**Year 3 deposit:**
$$\text{Future Value of Year 3 deposit} = A(1+i)^{n-3}$$

**General Year $k$ deposit:**
$$\text{Future Value of Year } k \text{ deposit} = A(1+i)^{n-k}$$

**Year $n$ deposit (last one):** It is deposited at the end of year $n$, so it earns **zero interest** (it is paid *into* the fund on the very day of withdrawal).
$$\text{Future Value of Year } n \text{ deposit} = A(1+i)^{0} = A$$

**Total Accumulated Fund $F$:**
$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}$$

This is a **Geometric Progression (G.P.)** with:
- First term $= a = A$
- Common ratio $= r = (1+i)$
- Number of terms $= n$

Sum of G.P. formula:
$$S_n = a \cdot \frac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}$$

Substituting:
$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$$

$$\boxed{F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}}$$

This is the **Sinking Fund Future Value Equation**.

---

### 3.2 Inverting the Formula — Solving for Annual Deposit $A$

If we know the **target corpus** $C$ required at the end of $n$ years, we set $F = C$:

$$C = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

Solving for $A$:

$$A = C \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

The factor $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ is called the **Annual Sinking Fund Factor** or **Capital Recovery Factor (Annuity form)**.

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Sinking Fund (Outgoing)} = C \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}}$$

---

### 3.3 Application in Building Depreciation

**Problem Setup:** A building is constructed for ₹ 20,00,000 with an expected life of 80 years and a scrap value of ₹ 2,00,000. Sinking Fund is maintained at 5% compound interest. Find the annual sinking fund contribution.

**Given:**
- Original Cost $C = 20,00,000$
- Scrap Value $S = 2,00,000$
- Life $n = 80$ years
- Interest $i = 5\% = 0.05$

**Step 1: Calculate the Depreciable Amount (Future Replacement Need)**
$$\text{Depreciable Amount} = C - S$$
$$\text{Depreciable Amount} = 20,00,000 - 2,00,000 = 18,00,000$$

This is the corpus $C_{target}$ that must be built up by the time the building is demolished/replaced.

**Step 2: Apply the Sinking Fund Formula**
$$A = (C - S) \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

$$A = 18,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{(1+0.05)^{80} - 1}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate $(1.05)^{80}$**

Using logarithms or standard tables:
$$(1.05)^{80} \approx 49.561$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Simplify**
$$A = 18,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{49.561 - 1}$$

$$A = 18,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{48.561}$$

$$A = 18,00,000 \cdot 0.0010296$$

$$\boxed{A \approx ₹ 18,533 \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 5: Verification by Working Backwards**
At ₹ 18,533 per annum for 80 years at 5% CI:
$$F = 18533 \times \frac{(1.05)^{80} - 1}{0.05} = 18533 \times 48.561 \approx 18,00,000 \checkmark$$

This matches the depreciable amount — derivation is verified.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Full Outgoings Calculation

**A residential building yields a gross monthly rent of ₹ 50,000.** Calculate the **net annual income** given the following outgoings:

| Outgoing | Amount/Year (₹) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Municipal Tax | 60,000 |
| Repairs @ 1% of construction cost (₹80,00,000) | 80,000 |
| Insurance Premium | 12,000 |
| Management @ 5% of Gross Rent | 30,000 |
| Sinking Fund (as derived in §3.3) | 18,533 |

**Step 1: Gross Annual Rent**
$$\text{Gross Rent} = 50,000 \times 12 = 6,00,000$$

**Step 2: Total Outgoings**
$$\Sigma O = 60,000 + 80,000 + 12,000 + 30,000 + 18,533 = 2,00,533$$

**Step 3: Net Annual Income**
$$\text{NAI} = 6,00,000 - 2,00,533 = 3,99,467$$

$$\boxed{\text{Net Annual Income} = ₹ 3,99,467 \text{ per annum}}$$

This NAI is the figure to be capitalised using an appropriate YP for valuation.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Sinking Fund vs. Straight Line Depreciation

| Parameter | Sinking Fund Method | Straight Line Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Interest on accumulated fund | **Compounded** | **Not considered** |
| Annual provision | Smaller (₹18,533 in §3.3) | Larger (₹22,500 = 18,00,000/80) |
| Corpus at year $n$ | Exactly equals replacement cost | Only the principal is recovered, no interest earned |
| Preferred when | Interest rates are significant | Interest rates are negligible |
| Use in valuation | Standard for *long-life* assets | Used for *short-life* assets |
| KTU exam weightage | **High** — 7 to 10 marks frequently | Medium — 3 to 5 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Outgoings

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OUTGOINGS IN VALUATION] --> B[RECURRING OUTGOINGS]
    A --> C[NON RECURRING OUTGOINGS]

    B --> B1[Municipal and Local Taxes]
    B --> B2[Repairs and Maintenance]
    B --> B3[Insurance Premium]
    B --> B4[Management Charges]
    B --> B5[Sinking Fund]
    B --> B6[Ground Rent]
    B --> B7[Vacancy Allowance]
    B --> B8[Legal Expenses]

    B5 --> B5A[Roof Replacement]
    B5 --> B5B[Building Renewal]
    B5 --> B5C[Special Repairs]

    C --> C1[Arrears of Tax]
    C --> C2[Initial Repair Cost]
    C --> C3[Outstanding Mortgage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above **functional block diagram** maps the complete taxonomy of outgoings as mandated in the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus. Note that **Sinking Fund is a sub-class of Recurring Outgoings**, but it is *qualitatively different* from routine outgoings — it represents *future obligation*, not present consumption.

---

## 4.2 Sinking Fund Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Property Owner] -->|Annually deposits| SF[Sinking Fund Account]
    SF -->|Earns compound interest at i percent| SF
    SF -->|At year n| R[Replacement of Asset]

    subgraph INCOMEFLOW[ANNUAL INCOME FLOW]
        GR[Gross Rent] -->|Minus| OUT[All Outgoings]
        OUT -->|Result| NAI[Net Annual Income]
    end

    OUT -->|Includes| SFCONTRIB[SF Contribution A]
    SFCONTRIB --> SF

    style SF fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style NAI fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Gross Rent** flows *downward* through deductions to become **Net Annual Income**. A portion of this NAI is *re-invested* as the **Annual Sinking Fund Contribution ($A$)**. The Sinking Fund Account **self-multiplies** via compound interest, and at year $n$ it **disburses** the full replacement cost. The owner thus avoids a future financial shock.

---

## 4.3 Cash-Flow Time Line of a Sinking Fund

```mermaid
gantt
    title Sinking Fund Accumulation Timeline (n = 20 years, i = 6 percent)
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat Y%Y

    section Yearly Deposits
    Year 1 Deposit  :a1, 2025, 1y
    Year 2 Deposit  :a2, 2026, 1y
    Year 3 Deposit  :a3, 2027, 1y
    Year 4 Deposit  :a4, 2028, 1y
    Year 5 Deposit  :a5, 2029, 1y
    Year 6 Deposit  :a6, 2030, 1y
    Year 7 Deposit  :a7, 2031, 1y
    Year 8 Deposit  :a8, 2032, 1y
    Year 9 Deposit  :a9, 2033, 1y
    Year 10 Deposit :a10, 2034, 1y
    Year 11 Deposit :a11, 2035, 1y
    Year 12 Deposit :a12, 2036, 1y
    Year 13 Deposit :a13, 2037, 1y
    Year 14 Deposit :a14, 2038, 1y
    Year 15 Deposit :a15, 2039, 1y
    Year 16 Deposit :a16, 2040, 1y
    Year 17 Deposit :a17, 2041, 1y
    Year 18 Deposit :a18, 2042, 1y
    Year 19 Deposit :a19, 2043, 1y
    Year 20 Deposit :a20, 2044, 1y

    section Compound Growth Phase
    Interest Accrual :b1, 2025, 20y

    section Corpus Withdrawal
    Replacement of Roof :crit, 2044, 1y
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Gantt chart** above illustrates the temporal mechanics: each year, a small deposit is added; meanwhile, the *entire accumulated balance* earns compound interest. At year 20, the corpus is withdrawn for *roof replacement* (marked as a critical milestone).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**"Distinguish between Revenue Outgoings and Sinking Fund as outgoings in property valuation."**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Revenue Outgoings | Sinking Fund |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Expenses for *current* consumption of services | Provision for *future* replacement |
| **Frequency** | Paid every year, recurring | Deposited every year, but used only once at year $n$ |
| **Purpose** | Maintain present-day usability of property | Re-build/replace the asset at end of life |
| **Examples** | Property tax, insurance, current repairs | Roof replacement fund, lift renewal fund |
| **Interest Treatment** | No interest component | Earns compound interest on accumulation |

*(A tabulated answer of 4–5 rows with brief examples is sufficient for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**"A property owner sets aside ₹ 25,000 every year as a Sinking Fund at 6% compound interest. Find the accumulated amount after 10 years."**

**Model Solution:**

$$\text{Given: } A = 25,000, \quad i = 0.06, \quad n = 10$$

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

$$F = 25,000 \cdot \frac{(1.06)^{10} - 1}{0.06}$$

$$(1.06)^{10} = 1.7908$$

$$F = 25,000 \cdot \frac{1.7908 - 1}{0.06} = 25,000 \cdot \frac{0.7908}{0.06}$$

$$F = 25,000 \times 13.18 = 3,29,500$$

$$\boxed{F = ₹ 3,29,500}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14-Mark Pattern]`**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`**

A building is constructed at a cost of **₹ 25,00,000**. Its expected life is **60 years** with a scrap value of **₹ 1,00,000** at the end of its life. The owner sets up a **Sinking Fund** at **5% compound interest** to accumulate the cost of replacement. The building fetches a **gross annual rent of ₹ 4,80,000**.

Other annual outgoings are:
- Municipal Tax: ₹ 36,000
- Repairs: ₹ 25,000
- Insurance: ₹ 8,000
- Management Charges: 4% of Gross Rent

### (a) Calculate the **Annual Sinking Fund contribution**. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify the target corpus.**

The building must be replaced at the end of 60 years. The amount that *needs to be accumulated* is the *depreciable cost* (since the scrap value is recovered separately at demolition).

$$C_{target} = \text{Original Cost} - \text{Scrap Value}$$
$$C_{target} = 25,00,000 - 1,00,000 = 24,00,000$$

**Step 2: Apply the Sinking Fund formula.**

$$A = C_{target} \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

$$A = 24,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{(1.05)^{60} - 1}$$

**Step 3: Compute $(1.05)^{60}$.**

$$(1.05)^{60} \approx 18.6792$$

**Step 4: Substitute.**

$$A = 24,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{18.6792 - 1} = 24,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{17.6792}$$

$$A = 24,00,000 \times 0.002828 = ₹ 6,787.20$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Sinking Fund} \approx ₹ 6,787 \text{ per annum}}$$

---

### (b) Determine the **Net Annual Income** and the **Valuation of the property** assuming a Years Purchase of 12. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Tabulate all annual outgoings.**

| Outgoing | Annual Amount (₹) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Municipal Tax | 36,000 |
| Repairs | 25,000 |
| Insurance | 8,000 |
| Management (4% of 4,80,000) | 19,200 |
| Sinking Fund (from part a) | 6,787 |
| **Total Outgoings ($\Sigma O$)** | **94,987** |

**Step 2: Compute Net Annual Income (NAI).**

$$\text{NAI} = \text{Gross Rent} - \Sigma O$$
$$\text{NAI} = 4,80,000 - 94,987 = 3,85,013$$

**Step 3: Compute Valuation.**

$$V = \text{NAI} \times \text{YP}$$
$$V = 3,85,013 \times 12 = 46,20,156$$

$$\boxed{\text{Valuation of the property} = ₹ 46,20,156}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

A property is yielding a **gross monthly rent of ₹ 35,000**. The following data is given:

- Life of building: **75 years**
- Construction cost: **₹ 60,00,000**
- Scrap value: **₹ 5,00,000**
- Sinking Fund interest rate: **5%** CI
- Other outgoings per year:
  - Municipal Tax = ₹ 24,000
  - Repairs = **0.75%** of construction cost
  - Insurance = ₹ 6,000
  - Management = **6%** of Gross Rent
  - Vacancy allowance = **2%** of Gross Rent

### (a) Compute the **Net Annual Income** of the property. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Gross Annual Rent.**

$$G = 35,000 \times 12 = 4,20,000$$

**Step 2: Compute percentage-based outgoings.**

- Repairs = $0.75\% \text{ of } 60,00,000 = 45,000$
- Management = $6\% \text{ of } 4,20,000 = 25,200$
- Vacancy = $2\% \text{ of } 4,20,000 = 8,400$

**Step 3: Compute Sinking Fund contribution.**

$$A = (60,00,000 - 5,00,000) \cdot \frac{0.05}{(1.05)^{75} - 1}$$

$$(1.05)^{75} \approx 38.8326$$

$$A = 55,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{37.8326} = 55,00,000 \times 0.0013216 = ₹ 7,269$$

**Step 4: Sum all outgoings.**

| Outgoing | ₹ |
| :--- | :---: |
| Municipal Tax | 24,000 |
| Repairs | 45,000 |
| Insurance | 6,000 |
| Management | 25,200 |
| Vacancy | 8,400 |
| Sinking Fund | 7,269 |
| **Total ($\Sigma O$)** | **1,15,869** |

**Step 5: Net Annual Income.**

$$\text{NAI} = 4,20,000 - 1,15,869 = 3,04,131$$

$$\boxed{\text{Net Annual Income} = ₹ 3,04,131 \text{ per annum}}$$

---

### (b) If the **rate of return** expected by the investor is **8%**, find the **valuation of the property**. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Years Purchase at 8%.**

$$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$

**Step 2: Apply Valuation formula.**

$$V = \text{NAI} \times \text{YP}$$
$$V = 3,04,131 \times 12.5$$

$$\boxed{V = ₹ 38,01,637.5 \approx ₹ 38,01,638}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Scrap Value with Replacement Cost.** Scrap is recovered at *demolition*; replacement cost is the *new construction cost*. Sinking Fund targets replacement cost **minus** scrap value.
> 2. **Do not use Straight Line Depreciation** ($\frac{C-S}{n}$) when the question explicitly states "Sinking Fund at CI rate". The two are different by a factor of $\frac{n \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
> 3. **Do not forget Vacancy & Bad-Debts Allowance** — they are outgoings, not optional deductions.
> 4. **Watch out for $(1+i)^n$** computation in long-life problems (75–100 years). Use logarithmic tables or a calculator — never write "approximately 1" for such large exponents.
> 5. **Always show the substitution step** before arriving at the numerical answer — KTU examiners award 2–3 marks for the *substitution* itself, even if the final computation has a small error.
> 6. **Convert all monthly rents to annual** by multiplying by 12 before subtracting outgoings.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Before the exam — read this list twice.**

- **Outgoings** = *Recurring periodic expenses* deducted from Gross Rent to obtain Net Income.
- **Two categories**: (i) Revenue (Recurring) and (ii) Non-Recurring.
- **Revenue Outgoings include**: Municipal taxes, repairs, insurance, management, sinking fund, ground rent, vacancy allowance.
- **Sinking Fund** is a *compound-interest-bearing annual deposit* to fund future asset replacement.
- **Key SF formula** (Future Value): $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$
- **Key SF formula** (Annual Deposit): $A = (C - S) \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$
- **Sinking Fund $\neq$ Depreciation.** SF uses compound interest; straight-line depreciation does not.
- **Net Annual Income** = Gross Rent $-$ *all* outgoings (including SF).
- **Valuation** = NAI $\times$ YP.
- **Years Purchase** = $\dfrac{1}{r}$ for perpetual income at return rate $r$.
- **Vacancy allowance** typically 2%–10% of gross rent.
- **Management charges** typically 2%–6% of gross rent.
- **Repairs** typically 0.5%–1% of construction cost (ordinary annual repairs only).
- **KTU default interest rate** on SF: **5% to 6%** per annum.
- **Remember** to convert all monthly data to **annual** before calculation.
- **Always** present outgoings in a **table** in the answer script for clarity and partial-mark safety.
- **Show every step of substitution** in long-answer SF problems — partial marks are awarded generously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Year's Purchase, Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Year's Purchase, Numerical Examples # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Year's Purchase (YP)** is defined as the capital sum required to be invested at a given rate of interest (rate of return) so as to produce a net annual income of **one unit of currency (Re. 1)**. It is the *multiplication factor* used by a valuer to convert the **Net Annual Income (NAI)** of a property into its **Capital Value (CV)**. Mathematically: $$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{r}$$ where $r$ is the rate of interest expressed as a decimal (i.e., $r = \dfrac{\text{Rate \%}}{100}$).
