The **fundamental valuation identity** is therefore: $$\text{Capital Value (CV)} = \text{Net Annual Income (NAI)} \times \text{YP}$$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Savings Account Lens" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you deposit money in a savings bank that pays you **Rs. 1 of interest every year, forever**. How much must you keep in that account today? > > If the bank pays **8% per annum**, then a principal of $P$ gives interest $= P \times 0.08$. To get exactly Re. 1, we need $P = \dfrac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$. > > That Rs. 12.5 is the **Year's Purchase at 8%**. In valuation, YP behaves like this multiplier: it tells you "how many years of net rent equals the lump-sum capital." | Real-world item | What it represents in valuation | |---|---| | Bank's **interest rate** (8 %) | The **rate of return** expected by an investor | | Annual **interest earned** (Re. 1) | The **Net Annual Income** of the property | | **Principal deposited** (Rs. 12.5) | The **Capital Value** of the property | So a higher interest rate $\Rightarrow$ lower YP $\Rightarrow$ lower capital value, and vice versa. --- ## 1.3 Why Year's Purchase is the Backbone of Valuation > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 scheme places YP at the heart of Module 1 because **every rental method of valuation (Direct Comparison, Belting, Standard Rent, etc.)** eventually needs the YP multiplier to translate *income* into *capital*. Without YP, the valuer cannot determine the present worth of a future stream of rents. Standard metrics used: - **Rate of interest (r):** Usually 6 % – 12 % in India for real estate. - **Holding period (n):** Lease tenure, life of structure, or deferment period. - **Net Annual Income (NAI):** Gross rent minus all outgoings (taxes, repairs, insurance, management, sinking fund, etc.). - **YP result:** A **dimensionless** number, multiplied by NAI to yield CV. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Logic of YP The derivation is rooted in the concept of *present value of a series of future payments*. Different *cases* of income lead to *different* YP formulas: ### Case-by-Case Logic 1. **Perpetuity (Freehold):** Income is *infinite* and constant. PV of a Re. 1 perpetuity $= \dfrac{1}{r}$. Hence YP $= \dfrac{1}{r}$. 2. **Ordinary Annuity (Lease of n years):** Income for n years, *first receipt at the end of year 1*. The present value is the geometric series sum, giving YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$. 3. **Annuity Due:** Income at the *start* of each year. Multiply the ordinary annuity YP by $(1+r)$: YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^{n-1}}$. 4. **Deferred Annuity:** Income starts *after d years* of waiting. Discount the ordinary annuity YP by $(1+r)^d$: YP $= \text{YP}_{annuity} \times (1+r)^{-d}$. 5. **Reversion (Year 2 onwards of a perpetuity):** The *first year's* income is excluded. YP $= \dfrac{1}{r} - 1 = \dfrac{1-r}{r}$. 6. **Sinking-Fund Method (Recovery of Capital):** The net income must cover both interest and the annual sinking-fund instalment. Mathematically, this collapses to the **same YP as the ordinary annuity** for n years: YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$. > [!NOTE] > **Why does Sinking Fund give the same YP as ordinary annuity?** > The annual Sinking Fund instalment to redeem Re. 1 in n years is $\dfrac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1}$. The total annual burden on Re. 1 of capital is: > $$\text{Interest} + \text{SF instalment} = r + \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1} = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$$ > Hence YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$, exactly the ordinary-annuity YP. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always quote the rate ($r$) as a decimal in the formula.** The YP result is dimensionless. The **NAI** carries the unit (Rs.) and imparts the rupee value to the capital sum. | Sl. No. | Case of YP | Formula | KTU Exam Hint (When to use) | |:---:|---|---|---| | 1 | Freehold / Perpetuity | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Income forever; freehold property | | 2 | Limited Period (Ordinary Annuity) | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ | Lease of $n$ years; income at year-end | | 3 | Annuity Due | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^{n-1}}$ | Income at year-*start*; rent in advance | | 4 | Deferred Annuity | $YP = \left[\dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}\right] \cdot (1+r)^{-d}$ | Vacancy / development period of $d$ years | | 5 | Reversion (perpetual, from year 2) | $YP = \dfrac{1-r}{r}$ | Right to perpetual income after year 1 | | 6 | Sinking Fund Method (finite life) | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ | Future life $= n$ years; capital must be recovered | | 7 | Freehold Ground Rent | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Perpetual ground rent | | 8 | Leasehold Ground Rent | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ | Ground rent for lease term $n$ | > [!WARNING] > Do **not** confuse YP with the *Sinking-Fund coefficient* itself. The SF coefficient is $\dfrac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1}$, while the YP for the Sinking-Fund method is its reciprocal adjusted for interest, i.e. the ordinary-annuity expression above. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice | Application | YP used | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Market valuation of a tenanted flat** | Perpetuity YP | To convert monthly rent into a sale price benchmark | | **Valuation of a leasehold interest (industrial plot)** | Limited-period YP | To find the lump-sum premium a lessee should pay today | | **Insurance valuation of a building (finite life)** | Sinking-Fund YP | To recover the depreciable cost over the building's residual life | | **Compensation for land under acquisition** | Deferred YP | To value rent that will commence after several years of development | | **Reverse calculation (income approach in mortgage)** | Any of the above | To determine *what rent* a given capital should fetch | The YP concept is thus the **lingua franca** between *builders, valuers, bankers, and arbitrators*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — YP of a Freehold (Perpetuity) Let the capital value be $C$ and the rate of interest per unit be $r$. Annual interest produced by the capital is $C \times r$. We require this annual interest to equal **Re. 1**: $$C \times r = 1$$ Solving for $C$: $$C = \frac{1}{r}$$ Hence, by definition, the capital sum that yields Re. 1 per annum in perpetuity is $\dfrac{1}{r}$, i.e., $$\boxed{\text{YP}_{\text{freehold}} = \frac{1}{r}}$$ **Capital Value identity:** $\text{CV} = \text{NAI} \times \dfrac{1}{r}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — YP of a Limited-Period Ordinary Annuity (n years) The income is Re. 1 receivable at the *end* of years 1, 2, 3, …, n. The present value is the sum of a geometric series: $$\text{PV} = \frac{1}{1+r} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+r)^n}$$ Factorising the first term and using the geometric series formula: $$\text{PV} = \frac{1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+r}\right)^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r}}$$ The denominator simplifies to $\dfrac{r}{1+r}$: $$\text{PV} = \frac{1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{\dfrac{r}{1+r}} = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+r)^n$: $$\text{PV} = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\text{YP}_{\text{annuity, n yrs}} = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — YP for a Deferred Annuity (d years' deferment) A *deferred annuity* of Re. 1 per year, deferred by $d$ years, has its first payment at the end of year $(d+1)$. The PV is the ordinary-annuity PV further discounted by $(1+r)^d$: $$\text{YP}_{\text{deferred}} = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n} \times \frac{1}{(1+r)^d} = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^{n+d}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — YP for Reversion (Year 2 onwards of a perpetuity) The total perpetuity YP from year 1 is $\dfrac{1}{r}$. Removing the first-year income (= 1) leaves: $$\text{YP}_{\text{reversion}} = \frac{1}{r} - 1 = \frac{1 - r}{r}$$ --- ## 3.5 Numerical Example Set — Carefully Solved > [!NOTE] > The following examples follow the **exact valuation style** required by KTU 2024 ESE. Every arithmetic step is shown; intermediate values are quoted to 4 decimal places before final rounding. --- ### **Example 1 (KTU 2024 model) — Freehold Capital Value** > **Problem:** A freehold property yields a Net Annual Income (NAI) of **Rs. 75,000**. The prevailing rate of interest is **8 %**. Find the Capital Value. **Step 1 — Convert rate to decimal:** $r = \dfrac{8}{100} = 0.08$. **Step 2 — Apply the perpetuity YP:** $$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Capital Value:** $$\text{CV} = \text{NAI} \times \text{YP} = 75{,}000 \times 12.5 = \text{Rs. } 9{,}37{,}500$$ **Answer:** $\text{CV} = \text{Rs. 9,37,500}$. --- ### **Example 2 — Limited Period (Lease of 30 years)** > **Problem:** A lease has 30 years remaining. The net annual rent is **Rs. 50,000**. The valuer's rate of interest is **8 %**. Find the present value of the lease. **Step 1 — Identify the case:** Ordinary annuity, $n = 30$, $r = 0.08$. **Step 2 — Compute $(1+r)^n$:** $$(1.08)^{30} = 10.0627 \quad \text{(using compound-interest tables)}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the formula:** $$\text{YP} = \frac{(1.08)^{30} - 1}{0.08 \times (1.08)^{30}} = \frac{10.0627 - 1}{0.08 \times 10.0627} = \frac{9.0627}{0.8050} = 11.2579$$ **Step 4 — Compute CV:** $$\text{CV} = 50{,}000 \times 11.2579 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}62{,}895$$ **Answer:** $\text{CV} = \text{Rs. 5,62,895 (approx.)}$. --- ### **Example 3 — Deferred Annuity** > **Problem:** A property is expected to give a net annual rent of **Rs. 40,000** for **20 years**, but the income will start only **after 5 years** (deferment). The rate of interest is **8 %**. Find the present value. **Step 1 — Required parameters:** $r = 0.08$, $n = 20$, $d = 5$. **Step 2 — Compute the YP for 20-year ordinary annuity:** $$(1.08)^{20} = 4.6610$$ $$\text{YP}_{20} = \frac{4.6610 - 1}{0.08 \times 4.6610} = \frac{3.6610}{0.3729} = 9.8181$$ **Step 3 — Discount for the 5-year deferment:** $$\text{YP}_{\text{deferred}} = \frac{9.8181}{(1.08)^5} = \frac{9.8181}{1.4693} = 6.6817$$ **Step 4 — Compute CV:** $$\text{CV} = 40{,}000 \times 6.6817 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}67{,}268$$ **Answer:** $\text{CV} = \text{Rs. 2,67,268 (approx.)}$. --- ### **Example 4 — Reversion (Perpetual)** > **Problem:** A reversionary interest gives a perpetual net annual income of **Rs. 1,00,000**, *but the first year yields no income* (i.e., the income is from year 2 onwards). Rate of interest is **10 %**. Find the present value of this reversion. **Step 1 — Identify the case:** Reversion of a perpetuity. **Step 2 — Apply the reversion YP formula:** $$\text{YP}_{\text{rev}} = \frac{1 - r}{r} = \frac{1 - 0.10}{0.10} = \frac{0.90}{0.10} = 9.0$$ **Step 3 — Compute CV:** $$\text{CV} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 9.0 = \text{Rs. } 9{,}00{,}000$$ **Cross-check:** The total perpetuity YP would be $1/0.10 = 10$. The first-year income is $1{,}00{,}000 \times 1 = 1{,}00{,}000$. So the reversion CV is $10{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000$. ✓ **Answer:** $\text{CV} = \text{Rs. 9,00,000}$. --- ### **Example 5 — Sinking Fund Method (Finite Life)** > **Problem:** A building has a future economic life of **25 years**. Its Net Annual Income is **Rs. 60,000**, and the rate of interest is **6 %**. The capital is to be fully recovered by the Sinking Fund method. Find the Capital Value. **Step 1 — Identify the case:** Sinking-fund method, $n = 25$, $r = 0.06$. **Step 2 — Compute $(1.06)^{25}$:** $$(1.06)^{25} = 4.2919$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Sinking-Fund YP formula (same as ordinary-annuity YP):** $$\text{YP} = \frac{(1.06)^{25} - 1}{0.06 \times (1.06)^{25}} = \frac{4.2919 - 1}{0.06 \times 4.2919} = \frac{3.2919}{0.2575} = 12.7845$$ **Step 4 — Compute CV:** $$\text{CV} = 60{,}000 \times 12.7845 = \text{Rs. } 7{,}67{,}070$$ **Answer:** $\text{CV} = \text{Rs. 7,67,070 (approx.)}$. > [!NOTE] > The SF method assumes the income is sufficient to (a) pay interest on capital AND (b) accumulate a sinking fund to recover the capital at the end of 25 years. Therefore the resulting YP is **lower than the perpetuity YP** (which would be $1/0.06 = 16.667$), giving a more conservative capital value. --- ### **Example 6 — Mixed Freehold + Leasehold Comparison (KTU-favourite type)** > **Problem:** A property yields a NAI of **Rs. 1,20,000**. > (a) Find the freehold capital value at **8 %**. > (b) Find the leasehold capital value for **40 years** at the same rate. > (c) State which value is greater and why. **(a) Freehold YP at 8 %:** $\text{YP}_{\text{free}} = \dfrac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$ $$\text{CV}_{\text{free}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 12.5 = \text{Rs. } 15{,}00{,}000$$ **(b) Leasehold (40 years):** $$(1.08)^{40} = 21.7245$$ $$\text{YP}_{40} = \frac{21.7245 - 1}{0.08 \times 21.7245} = \frac{20.7245}{1.7380} = 11.9246$$ $$\text{CV}_{\text{lease}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 11.9246 = \text{Rs. } 14{,}30{,}952$$ **(c) Comparison:** The freehold CV (Rs. 15,00,000) > leasehold CV (Rs. 14,30,952) because a freeholder enjoys the *reversion* after 40 years, which adds value. --- ## 3.6 Common Pitfalls When Solving Numerical Problems > [!WARNING] > - **Forgetting to convert % to decimal:** $r = 8\,\% \Rightarrow 0.08$, *not* $8$. > - **Using the perpetuity YP for a limited-period lease:** A frequent error that gives an inflated capital value. > - **Misapplying the deferment factor:** Remember, *both* $n$ (income period) and $d$ (deferment) appear in the denominator's exponent: $(1+r)^{n+d}$. > - **Confusing Annuity Due with Ordinary Annuity:** Annuity due is for rent paid *in advance*; ordinary annuity is for rent paid *in arrears*. > - **Sinking Fund method error:** The YP for SF equals the ordinary-annuity YP, not the Sinking-Fund coefficient. The SF coefficient is used inside the *annual instalment* calculation, not the YP itself. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Decision Map — "Which YP to Use?" ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start: Identify the nature of income]) --> B{Is income perpetual?} B -- Yes --> C[Use Perpetuity YP
YP = 1 divided by r] B -- No --> D{Is there a deferment period d?} D -- Yes --> E[Use Deferred Annuity YP
YP = Ordinary Annuity YP times 1 divided by 1 plus r to power d] D -- No --> F{Income for fixed n years?} F -- Yes --> G{Income at start or end of year?} G -- Start --> H[Use Annuity Due YP
YP equals 1 plus r to power n minus 1 over r times 1 plus r to power n minus 1] G -- End --> I[Use Ordinary Annuity YP
YP equals 1 plus r to power n minus 1 over r times 1 plus r to power n] F -- No --> J{Income from year 2 only?} J -- Yes --> K[Use Reversion YP
YP equals 1 minus r divided by r] J -- No --> L[Use Sinking Fund YP
YP equals 1 plus r to power n minus 1 over r times 1 plus r to power n] C --> M([End: Multiply NAI by YP to get Capital Value]) E --> M H --> M I --> M K --> M L --> M ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Freehold vs Leasehold ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Property] --> B1{Income type} B1 -- Perpetual --> C1[Freeholder receives income forever] B1 -- Limited period --> C2[Leaseholder receives income for n years] C1 --> D1[Freehold YP equals 1 divided by r] D1 --> E1[Capital Value equals NAI times Freehold YP] C2 --> D2[Leasehold YP equals 1 plus r to power n minus 1 over r times 1 plus r to power n] D2 --> E2[Capital Value equals NAI times Leasehold YP] C2 -. After n years .-> F1[Reversion returns to Freeholder] F1 --> D1 ``` > [!NOTE] > These Mermaid diagrams render as a clean flow chart in GitHub, VS Code preview, and most modern Markdown engines. If your renderer is older, the *text content* inside each node conveys the full logic. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Define Year's Purchase. State its formula for a freehold property.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Year's Purchase (YP)** is the capital sum required to be invested at a given rate of interest so as to produce a net annual income of Re. 1. For a **freehold property** (perpetual income), the formula is: > $$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{r}$$ > where $r$ is the rate of interest (as a decimal). For example, at 8 % interest, YP = 12.5. **[3 marks]** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between YP of a freehold property and a leasehold property (30 years) at 8 % interest. State the relevant formulas.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > | Aspect | Freehold | Leasehold (30 years) | > |---|---|---| > | Income nature | Perpetual | Limited (n = 30 years) | > | YP formula | $1/r$ | $\frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ | > | Numerical value at 8 % | $1 / 0.08 = 12.5$ | $11.258$ (approx.) | > | Conclusion | Higher YP, higher capital value | Lower YP, lower capital value | > > The leaseholder **does not** enjoy the reversion; this loss is reflected in the smaller YP. **[3 marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question is split into (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students attempt EITHER *Question A* OR *Question B* in full. --- ### **Question A (14 marks)** #### **(a) Derive the formula for YP of a freehold property. A freehold property yields a Net Annual Income of Rs. 60,000. The rate of interest is 6 %. Find the Capital Value.** [7 marks] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** Year's Purchase is the capital sum that yields Re. 1 as annual interest at a given rate $r$. **Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):** Let the capital value be $C$ and the annual interest be Re. 1. $$C \times r = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C = \frac{1}{r}$$ Hence $\text{YP}_{\text{freehold}} = \dfrac{1}{r}$. **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (3 marks):** - $r = 6\,\% = 0.06$ - $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{0.06} = 16.6667$ - $\text{CV} = 60{,}000 \times 16.6667 = \text{Rs. } 10{,}00{,}000$ **Answer:** Capital Value = **Rs. 10,00,000**. --- #### **(b) A lease of 30 years is to be granted on a property with an expected net annual rent of Rs. 50,000. The rate of interest is 7 %. Find the present value of the lease.** [7 marks] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the case:** Ordinary annuity, $n = 30$, $r = 0.07$. **Step 2 — Compute $(1+r)^n$ (2 marks):** $$(1.07)^{30} = 7.6123$$ **Step 3 — Compute YP (2 marks):** $$\text{YP}_{30} = \frac{7.6123 - 1}{0.07 \times 7.6123} = \frac{6.6123}{0.5329} = 12.4094$$ **Step 4 — Compute CV (2 marks):** $$\text{CV} = 50{,}000 \times 12.4094 = \text{Rs. } 6{,}20{,}470$$ **Answer:** Present value of the lease = **Rs. 6,20,470 (approx.)**. --- ### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) Explain the various cases of Year's Purchase used in property valuation. List the formulas.** [7 marks] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** > **Case 1 — Freehold / Perpetuity (1 mark):** Income forever. YP $= \dfrac{1}{r}$. > **Case 2 — Limited Period (Ordinary Annuity, n years) (1 mark):** Income at year-end. YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$. > **Case 3 — Annuity Due (1 mark):** Income at year-start. YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^{n-1}}$. > **Case 4 — Deferred Annuity (1 mark):** First income after $d$ years. YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^{n+d}}$. > **Case 5 — Reversion (1 mark):** Perpetual income from year 2 onwards. YP $= \dfrac{1-r}{r}$. > **Case 6 — Sinking-Fund Method (1 mark):** Capital recovered over $n$ years. YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ (same as ordinary annuity). > **Case 7 — Freehold / Leasehold Ground Rent (1 mark):** Freehold YP $= \dfrac{1}{r}$; Leasehold YP $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$. --- #### **(b) Find the present value of a property with the following data: NAI = Rs. 80,000, Rate = 8 %, Deferment = 5 years, Income period = 25 years.** [7 marks] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the case:** Deferred ordinary annuity, $r = 0.08$, $n = 25$, $d = 5$. **Step 2 — Compute $(1+r)^n$ and $(1+r)^d$ (2 marks):** $$(1.08)^{25} = 6.8485,\qquad (1.08)^{5} = 1.4693$$ **Step 3 — Compute ordinary-annuity YP for 25 years (2 marks):** $$\text{YP}_{25} = \frac{6.8485 - 1}{0.08 \times 6.8485} = \frac{5.8485}{0.5479} = 10.6748$$ **Step 4 — Apply deferment factor (1 mark):** $$\text{YP}_{\text{deferred}} = \frac{10.6748}{1.4693} = 7.2651$$ **Step 5 — Compute CV (2 marks):** $$\text{CV} = 80{,}000 \times 7.2651 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}81{,}208$$ **Answer:** Present value = **Rs. 5,81,208 (approx.)**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways students lose marks in YP numerical questions (KTU Board Examiner feedback):** > 1. **Not converting percentage to decimal:** Writing $r = 8$ instead of $r = 0.08$ is an instant 1-mark penalty. > 2. **Using the perpetuity YP for a finite lease:** Always read the problem carefully — if the lease has "30 years remaining", use the limited-period YP. > 3. **Forgetting the deferment factor:** A "deferred" annuity *must* be discounted by $(1+r)^d$ in the denominator; missing this gives a value that is too high. > 4. **Mixing up Sinking-Fund coefficient with YP:** The Sinking-Fund coefficient is $\dfrac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1}$, used to compute the *annual instalment*, NOT the YP. The YP for the SF method is the ordinary-annuity YP. > 5. **No units in the final answer:** Always state the result as "Rs. 5,62,895" (or in **Rs. in lakhs / crores** if the figure is large). Missing units costs ½ mark. > 6. **Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award step-marks. Show $(1+r)^n$ explicitly; do not jump to the final YP. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Year's Purchase — Rapid Revision Checklist** > - **Core identity:** $\text{CV} = \text{NAI} \times \text{YP}$. > - **Always** express $r$ as a **decimal** (e.g., $8\,\% \Rightarrow 0.08$). > - **Perpetuity (freehold) YP** $= 1/r$ — the simplest case. > - **Limited-period (lease) YP** $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ — most common KTU question. > - **Annuity Due YP** $= \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^{n-1}}$ — used when rent is paid *in advance*. > - **Deferred Annuity YP** $=$ ordinary-annuity YP $\times (1+r)^{-d}$ — watch the exponent $(n+d)$. > - **Reversion YP** $= (1-r)/r$ — second year onwards of a perpetuity. > - **Sinking-Fund YP** $=$ ordinary-annuity YP — capital recovery built into the income. > - **Freehold > Leasehold in value** for the same NAI and rate. > - **Step-marks rule:** Always show the calculation of $(1+r)^n$ before the final YP. > - **Units:** Final answer must carry the **rupee sign and the magnitude in words/figures**.

topics.title: Valuation Table: Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Valuation Table: Use

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tables** are pre-computed statistical reference charts that contain standard mathematical factors (multipliers) used by valuers to determine the **capitalized value (CV)** or **present worth (PW)** of an income stream (rent, profit, or benefit) expected from a real property over a specified time horizon at a stipulated rate of interest (yield).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, valuation tables are indispensable tools that convert a recurring income into a single lumpsum capital value. The fundamental logic behind every valuation table is rooted in the **time value of money** — *a rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received tomorrow.*

The general mathematical form that governs all valuation tables is:

$$
CV \text{ or } Value = \text{Annual Net Income} \times \text{Years Purchase (YP)}
$$

Where the **Years Purchase (YP)** is the multiplier retrieved from the appropriate table, and the **Annual Net Income** is the **multiplicand** (the actual rent/profit figure).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shortcut Multiplication" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Ready-Made Mix"**
> Imagine you are baking a cake. Instead of measuring flour, sugar, butter, and eggs separately every time, you buy a pre-mixed cake packet where the manufacturer has already done the math. You only need to add the main ingredient (milk/eggs) and bake. **Valuation tables work exactly like this pre-mixed packet** — the complex compound interest mathematics has already been "mixed" by actuarial scientists; the valuer only has to look up the correct factor and multiply it by the net income.

In the early 20th century, valuers had to compute compound interest manually using long division and logarithms. A single valuation of a 60-year lease could take hours. **Valuation tables eliminated this drudgery**, allowing professionals to perform dozens of valuations in a single working day with absolute accuracy.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The standard metrics embedded in every valuation table are:

- **Rate of Interest (r) or Yield**: Expressed as a **percentage per annum** (e.g., 5%, 6%, 8%, 10%, 12%, 15%). The KTU 2024 syllabus typically uses rates between **4% and 15%**.
- **Period (n)**: Expressed in **whole years** (1 to 100 years).
- **Income Frequency**: Standard tables assume **annual** income received at the **end of each year** (Ordinary Annuity / Annuity Regular). Special tables exist for **Annuity Due** (income at the beginning of the year).
- **Standard Multiplier Convention**: All factors are expressed to a minimum of **4 decimal places** for precision in professional valuation reports.

## 1.4 The Three Fundamental "Master" Valuation Tables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Three Tables Every KTU Student Must Master:**
> 1. **Years Purchase (YP) Single Rate Table** — Converts a uniform annual income into a present capital sum using a single rate of interest (covers both interest + capital recovery).
> 2. **Sinking Fund (SF) or Annuity tables** — Used when interest and capital recovery are to be calculated separately (dual rate method).
> 3. **Depreciation / Obsolescence Tables** — Used to compute the depreciated value of buildings, machinery, or structures over their useful life.

The **Single Rate YP Table** is the most frequently used in KTU board examinations, and this section focuses primarily on its application.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Valuation Tables Are Used — The Operational Logic

The use of valuation tables follows a rigid six-step operational protocol:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Income Stream**: Determine whether the property generates a **fixed (uniform) annual income** or a **varying (gradient) income**. Tables are designed primarily for **uniform income** scenarios.
- **Step 2 — Determine the Property Tenure**: Establish whether the property is held in **perpetuity (freehold)** or for a **limited period (leasehold)**.
- **Step 3 — Select the Appropriate Rate (r)**: This is the **expected rate of return** on the capital invested, considering the risk profile, location, and type of property.
- **Step 4 — Choose the Table Type**: Based on Steps 1, 2, and 3, select either the **YP Single Rate table**, **Sinking Fund table**, or **Depreciation table**.
- **Step 5 — Read the Multiplier (YP factor)**: From the table, locate the row corresponding to the **period (n)** and the column corresponding to the **rate (r)**. The intersection cell gives the Years Purchase factor.
- **Step 6 — Multiply and Arrive at the Capital Value**: Apply the formula:

$$
\text{Capital Value (CV)} = \text{Net Annual Income} \times \text{YP factor}
$$

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Step 3 is the Most Critical Decision in Valuation:**
> The choice of rate (r) is a **judgemental input**, not a mathematical one. A 1% error in the rate can change the property valuation by **15-20%** because of the exponential nature of the discount factor. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can justify their choice of rate logically (market yield, risk premium, opportunity cost).

> [!NOTE]
> **How Step 5 Works in a Real Table:**
> If a valuer requires the YP for a property yielding a uniform net income for 20 years at 8% interest, they find the row labelled "n = 20" and the column labelled "8%". The intersection reads **9.8181** (approximately). This means the buyer is willing to pay **9.8181 times the annual net income** to acquire the property.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **master formulas** that govern every valuation table you will encounter in KTU exams. Memorize these equations thoroughly.

| **S.No.** | **Scenario** | **Formula** | **Table Required** | **Typical Application** |
|:---------:|:-------------|:-----------:|:------------------:|:------------------------|
| 1 | Perpetuity (Freehold) — Income in perpetuity | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | YP Perpetuity Table | Freehold land, heritage estates |
| 2 | Limited Period — Single Rate (Annuity Regular) | $YP = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | YP Single Rate Table | Leasehold properties, fixed-term contracts |
| 3 | Limited Period — Single Rate (Annuity Due) | $YP = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \times (1+r)$ | Annuity Due Table | Rent received in advance |
| 4 | Deferred Annuity (Income starts after 'm' years) | $YP_{\text{deferred}} = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \times (1+r)^{-m}$ | YP + Discount Table | Property under development, future lease |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Factor | $SF = \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Sinking Fund Table | Dual Rate valuation, depreciation fund |
| 6 | Capital Recovery (CR) Factor | $CR = \dfrac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Table | Loan amortization, full annuity return |
| 7 | Present Worth Factor | $PW = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | Discount Table | Single future payment valuation |
| 8 | Straight-Line Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | Depreciation Table | Building age-based depreciation |
| 9 | Declining Balance Depreciation | $D_k = C \times \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}\right]^{k}$ | Declining Balance Table | Written Down Value (WDV) method |
| 10 | Compound Interest Factor | $CI = (1+r)^{n}$ | CI Table | Future value of single investment |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** In the table above, students often confuse the **Sinking Fund (SF) factor** with the **Capital Recovery (CR) factor**. Recall: **SF recovers only the capital**; **CR recovers capital + pays interest**. Hence CR = SF + r.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Property Practice

The valuation table methodology is used daily by:

- **Chartered Surveyors and Valuers** for property tax assessment and bank mortgage valuation.
- **Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs)** for portfolio capitalization.
- **Government Acquirers** (National Highways Authority, Railways, Smart City Corporations) for compensating landowners under the **Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013**.
- **Insurance Companies** for determining the insurable value of leased commercial properties.
- **Bank Loan Officers** for determining the Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio for housing finance.

The mastery of these tables is, therefore, not merely an academic exercise — it is a **core professional competency** for any civil engineer involved in real estate, infrastructure, or project finance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Years Purchase (YP) Factor for a Limited Period

Let us derive the single-rate YP formula from **first principles** using the concept of discounting.

**Assumption:** A property yields a uniform net annual income of **Rs. 1** at the end of each year for **n** years. We want to find the present capital value (PV) that is equivalent to receiving Rs. 1 per year for n years, discounted at rate **r** per annum.

The present value of Rs. 1 received at the end of Year 1 is:

$$
PV_1 = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{1}}
$$

The present value of Rs. 1 received at the end of Year 2 is:

$$
PV_2 = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{2}}
$$

Continuing this logic for n years, the total present value is the **sum of a geometric progression**:

$$
PV = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{1}} + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{3}} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

This is a geometric series with first term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$, common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$, and number of terms = n.

The sum of n terms of a geometric series is given by $S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - q^{n}}{1 - q}$.

Applying this:

$$
PV = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left[\dfrac{1}{(1+r)}\right]^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}}
$$

Simplifying the denominator $1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} = \dfrac{r}{(1+r)}$, we obtain:

$$
PV = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{\left[1 - (1+r)^{-n}\right]}{\left[\dfrac{r}{(1+r)}\right]}
$$

The $(1+r)$ in the numerator and denominator cancel out, leaving us with the **golden formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,YP = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the multiplier that converts **Rs. 1 per year for n years** into a **present capital sum**. Multiply this factor by the actual annual net income to obtain the property's market value.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1: Leasehold Flat Valuation**
> A 25-year leasehold flat yields a net monthly rent of Rs. 18,000. The owner expects an 8% return on capital. Using the YP table, calculate the capitalized value of the flat.

**Step 1 — Convert monthly rent to annual net income:**

$$
\text{Annual Net Income} = 18{,}000 \times 12 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}16{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the required table parameters:**

- Rate $r = 8\% = 0.08$
- Period $n = 25$ years
- Table required: **YP Single Rate Table, Annuity Regular**

**Step 3 — Read the YP factor from the table:**

From the standard YP table at n = 25 and r = 8%, the YP factor is **10.6748**.

**Step 4 — Compute the Capital Value:**

$$
CV = 2{,}16{,}000 \times 10.6748 = \text{Rs. } 23{,}05{,}756.80
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The fair market value of the 25-year leasehold flat is approximately **Rs. 23.06 Lakhs** based on the income approach to valuation.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Perpetuity Valuation with Reverting Lease

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2: Freehold Commercial Building**
> A freehold commercial building yields a net annual rent of Rs. 8,00,000 in perpetuity. The expected yield in the market is 6%. Using the valuation table, determine the capital value.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

- Tenure: **Perpetuity** (Freehold)
- Rate $r = 6\% = 0.06$
- Annual income = Rs. 8,00,000

**Step 2 — Read the YP factor for perpetuity:**

For perpetuity, the YP factor is calculated as:

$$
YP_{\text{perpetuity}} = \dfrac{1}{r} = \dfrac{1}{0.06} = 16.6667
$$

This factor (16.6667) is universally tabulated in perpetuity tables.

**Step 3 — Compute the Capital Value:**

$$
CV = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 16.6667 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}33{,}33{,}360
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The freehold commercial building is valued at approximately **Rs. 1.33 Crores**. The buyer is effectively paying 16.67 years' purchase of the annual rent to own the property forever.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Dual Rate Method Using Sinking Fund Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 3: Old Building with Sinking Fund**
> A building has a remaining useful life of 30 years. The net annual rent is Rs. 1,50,000. The expected return on capital is 5%, but the valuer wants to set aside 3% as a sinking fund to recover the capital. Calculate the value using the dual rate method.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

- $r_1 = 5\%$ (interest on capital)
- $r_2 = 3\%$ (sinking fund rate)
- $n = 30$ years
- Net income = Rs. 1,50,000

**Step 2 — Compute the YP for interest portion:**

The interest on capital is paid every year throughout the 30-year period, so its YP (treating interest as perpetuity) is:

$$
YP_{\text{interest}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20.0000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Sinking Fund factor:**

The sinking fund accumulates over 30 years at 3% interest. The Sinking Fund factor is:

$$
SF = \dfrac{r_2}{(1+r_2)^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{0.03}{(1.03)^{30} - 1}
$$

Computing the denominator:

$$
(1.03)^{30} = 2.4273 \text{ (read from compound interest table)}
$$

$$
SF = \dfrac{0.03}{2.4273 - 1} = \dfrac{0.03}{1.4273} = 0.02102
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total YP:**

The dual-rate YP combines the interest YP and the Sinking Fund YP:

$$
YP_{\text{dual}} = YP_{\text{interest}} + YP_{\text{sinking fund}} = \dfrac{1}{r_1} + \dfrac{1}{SF}
$$

$$
YP_{\text{dual}} = 20.0000 + \dfrac{1}{0.02102} = 20.0000 + 47.5737 = 67.5737
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Capital Value:**

$$
CV = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 67.5737 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}01{,}36{,}055
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Using the dual rate method, the building is valued at approximately **Rs. 1.01 Crores**. The much higher YP (67.57 vs. ~17.5 for single rate at 5% for 30 years) reflects the conservative dual-rate approach, which is preferred for **old buildings nearing the end of their economic life**.

## 3.5 Summary Table — When to Use Which Table

| **Property Condition** | **Recommended Table** | **Reason** |
|:-----------------------|:----------------------|:-----------|
| Freehold — Income forever | YP Perpetuity Table | Income is infinite, only discount rate matters |
| Long Lease (30+ years) | YP Single Rate Table | Single rate is acceptable approximation |
| Short Lease (< 30 years) | YP Single Rate Table | Higher accuracy needed, capital recovery critical |
| Old Building — Short life | Dual Rate + Sinking Fund Table | Separate interest and capital recovery for safety |
| Future Income (starts after delay) | Deferred Annuity Table | Discount both for the deferred period and annuity term |
| Insurance / Compensation | Declining Balance Depreciation Table | Reflects actual diminishing value of structure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Correct Valuation Table

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **decision-making workflow** that a valuer follows when selecting the appropriate valuation table for a real property.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA[Start: Property Income Data Available] --> B{Is the income perpetual or limited?}
    B -- Perpetual Freehold --> C[Use YP Perpetuity Table]
    C --> C1[YP factor = 1 divided by r]
    C1 --> D[Multiply by Net Annual Income]
    D --> ResultA[Capital Value of Freehold Property]
    B -- Limited Period --> E{Is income uniform or varying?}
    E -- Uniform --> F{Is it Single Rate or Dual Rate?}
    F -- Single Rate --> G[Use YP Single Rate Table]
    G --> G1[YP factor from 1 minus 1 plus r power n over r]
    G1 --> H[Multiply by Net Annual Income]
    H --> ResultB[Capital Value Single Rate Method]
    F -- Dual Rate --> I[Use Sinking Fund SF Table]
    I --> I1[YP interest = 1 by r1]
    I1 --> I2[YP sinking fund = 1 by SF factor]
    I2 --> I3[Add both to get Dual YP]
    I3 --> ResultC[Capital Value Dual Rate Method]
    E -- Varying --> J[Use Gradient Series Table]
    J --> ResultD[Capital Value Gradient Method]
    StartA --> K{Is the income deferred?}
    K -- Yes Deferred --> L[Use Deferred Annuity Table]
    L --> L1[Apply YP factor times discount factor]
    L1 --> ResultE[Present Value of Deferred Income]
    StartA --> M{Is the purpose depreciation?}
    M -- Yes Depreciation --> N{Which method?}
    N -- Straight Line --> O[Use SL Depreciation Table]
    N -- Declining Balance --> P[Use WDV Depreciation Table]
    O --> ResultF[Depreciated Value SL Method]
    P --> ResultG[Depreciated Value WDV Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** Begin at the top-left node "Start" and follow the arrows based on the characteristics of the property. Each branch ends at a "Capital Value" or "Depreciated Value" terminal node, which represents the final output that the valuer reports in the valuation report.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Standard YP Table

The following block diagram shows the **structural organization** of a typical Years Purchase (YP) table used in valuation practice.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HeaderA[Table Header: Years Purchase YP Table] --> ColHeaders[Column Headers: Rate of Interest r]
    ColHeaders --> R1[4 percent column]
    ColHeaders --> R2[5 percent column]
    ColHeaders --> R3[6 percent column]
    ColHeaders --> R4[8 percent column]
    ColHeaders --> R5[10 percent column]
    ColHeaders --> R6[12 percent column]
    ColHeaders --> R7[15 percent column]
    HeaderA --> RowHeaders[Row Headers: Period in Years n]
    RowHeaders --> N1[n equals 1]
    RowHeaders --> N2[n equals 5]
    RowHeaders --> N3[n equals 10]
    RowHeaders --> N4[n equals 20]
    RowHeaders --> N5[n equals 30]
    RowHeaders --> N6[n equals 50]
    RowHeaders --> N7[n equals 100]
    R3 --> Cell1[Intersection cell n=20 r=6 percent]
    N4 --> Cell1
    Cell1 --> ValueA[YP factor equals 11.4699]
    R4 --> Cell2[Intersection cell n=30 r=8 percent]
    N5 --> Cell2
    Cell2 --> ValueB[YP factor equals 11.2578]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Reading Instruction:** Locate the period (n) in the leftmost column, then trace horizontally to the column of the chosen rate (r). The cell at this intersection contains the YP factor to be multiplied by the annual net income.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Single Rate vs. Dual Rate Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MethodSelect{Choose Valuation Method} --> SR[Single Rate Method]
    MethodSelect --> DR[Dual Rate Method]
    SR --> SR1[Apply one combined rate r]
    SR1 --> SR2[Use YP Single Rate Table]
    SR2 --> SR3[Result: Simpler and faster]
    SR3 --> SRUse[Best for: Modern income properties with long leases]
    DR --> DR1[Split into two rates]
    DR1 --> DR2[Rate 1: Interest on capital r1]
    DR1 --> DR3[Rate 2: Sinking fund rate r2]
    DR2 --> DR4[YP interest = 1 by r1]
    DR3 --> DR5[SF factor from SF table]
    DR5 --> DR6[YP sinking fund = 1 by SF]
    DR4 --> DR7[Add YP interest plus YP sinking fund]
    DR6 --> DR7
    DR7 --> DR8[Result: Conservative and safe]
    DR8 --> DRUse[Best for: Old buildings, government acquisitions, insurance]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define "Years Purchase" (YP) and state its significance in the income approach to property valuation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Years Purchase (YP)** is a multiplier (dimensionless factor) obtained from a valuation table that, when multiplied by the **net annual income** of a property, gives its **capitalized market value**.

The formula is:

$$
\text{Value} = \text{Net Annual Income} \times \text{YP}
$$

**Significance:**
- It **simplifies complex compound interest calculations** into a single multiplication step.
- It provides a **standardized method** recognized across the valuation profession.
- It allows **comparative analysis** of properties with different income flows and lease durations.
- It is the **cornerstone of the income capitalization approach**, the most widely used method for income-generating real estate.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Single Rate and Dual Rate methods of valuation. When is the dual rate method preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Single Rate Method** | **Dual Rate Method** |
|:-----------|:-----------------------|:---------------------|
| Rates Used | One combined rate (covers interest + capital recovery) | Two separate rates (one for interest, one for sinking fund) |
| Tables Required | YP Single Rate Table | YP Table + Sinking Fund Table |
| Complexity | Simpler | More complex |
| Conservatism | Less conservative | More conservative |
| Accuracy for old buildings | Lower | Higher |
| Typical Use | New properties, long leases | Old buildings, short remaining life, government compensation |

> [!NOTE]
> The **dual rate method** is preferred when the property has a **limited remaining economic life**, when the valuer wishes to ensure that the **original capital is fully recovered** by the end of the property's life, and in **legal/government valuation** matters where conservativeness is mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**A leasehold property yields a uniform net annual rent of Rs. 4,80,000. The lease has a remaining term of 18 years. The valuer adopts a single rate of interest at 9% per annum. Using the Years Purchase (YP) table, calculate the capitalized value of the property. If the lease is extended by another 12 years under the same terms, what will be the revised capitalized value?**

#### Part (a) — Capitalized Value for Original 18-Year Lease (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Table Parameters:**

- Net Annual Income = Rs. 4,80,000
- Period $n = 18$ years
- Rate $r = 9\%$

**Step 2 — Read YP from Table:**

From the YP Single Rate Table at n = 18 and r = 9%:

$$
YP_{18, 9\%} = 8.7556
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Capital Value:**

$$
CV_{\text{original}} = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 8.7556 = \text{Rs. } 42{,}02{,}688
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer for Part (a):** The capitalized value of the 18-year leasehold property is **Rs. 42,02,688** (approximately Rs. 42.03 Lakhs).

#### Part (b) — Capitalized Value After 12-Year Extension (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate New Total Period:**

$$
n_{\text{new}} = 18 + 12 = 30 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Read New YP from Table:**

From the YP Single Rate Table at n = 30 and r = 9%:

$$
YP_{30, 9\%} = 10.2737
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Revised Capital Value:**

$$
CV_{\text{revised}} = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 10.2737 = \text{Rs. } 49{,}31{,}376
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Incremental Gain in Value:**

$$
\text{Increment} = 49{,}31{,}376 - 42{,}02{,}688 = \text{Rs. } 7{,}28{,}688
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer for Part (b):** The revised capitalized value is **Rs. 49,31,376**. The 12-year lease extension adds **Rs. 7,28,688** to the property's value, demonstrating the substantial impact of lease tenure on valuation.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**An old industrial building has a remaining useful life of 20 years. The net annual rental income is Rs. 3,60,000. The valuer wishes to use the dual rate method with an interest rate of 7% on the original capital and a sinking fund rate of 4% to recover the capital over 20 years. Calculate the capitalized value of the building using the sinking fund method.**

#### Part (a) — Compute the YP for Interest and the Sinking Fund Factor (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute YP for Interest (treated as perpetuity):**

$$
YP_{\text{interest}} = \dfrac{1}{r_1} = \dfrac{1}{0.07} = 14.2857
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Sinking Fund Factor:**

Using the SF table at n = 20 and rate = 4%:

$$
SF_{20, 4\%} = 0.03358
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the YP equivalent for Sinking Fund:**

$$
YP_{\text{sinking fund}} = \dfrac{1}{SF} = \dfrac{1}{0.03358} = 29.7796
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer for Part (a):** YP for interest = **14.2857**; Sinking Fund Factor = **0.03358**; YP equivalent for SF = **29.7796**.

#### Part (b) — Compute the Total Dual-Rate YP and the Capital Value (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Add the Two YP Components:**

$$
YP_{\text{dual}} = YP_{\text{interest}} + YP_{\text{sinking fund}}
$$

$$
YP_{\text{dual}} = 14.2857 + 29.7796 = 44.0653
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Capitalized Value:**

$$
CV = \text{Net Annual Income} \times YP_{\text{dual}}
$$

$$
CV = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 44.0653 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}58{,}63{,}508
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer for Part (b):** The capitalized value of the old industrial building using the dual rate method is **Rs. 1,58,63,508** (approximately Rs. 1.59 Crores). This is the conservative valuation that a valuer would defend in a court of law or before a government acquisition authority.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 2-3 Marks Each:**
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing Single Rate with Dual Rate:** Many students use a single rate table for problems that explicitly ask for the dual rate method (or vice versa). Always **read the question twice** and identify whether the problem mentions "interest on capital" and "sinking fund" separately.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting to Convert Monthly to Annual Income:** If a problem states "monthly rent of Rs. 10,000", you **must multiply by 12** to get Rs. 1,20,000 before applying the YP factor. KTU examiners deduct a full 1 mark for this oversight.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Using Perpetuity Formula for Limited Period:** The formula $YP = 1/r$ applies **only to perpetuity**. For a 30-year lease, you must use $YP = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$. Mixing these up leads to **massively inflated or deflated** valuations.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Not Showing the YP Factor Lookup:** Always **state the YP factor** you read from the table along with the rate and period. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for correctly identifying the table and reading the factor.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Forgetting Units in the Final Answer:** Always end with the final value in **Rs. (Rupees)** or **Lakhs/Crores** as appropriate. A bare number with no unit may attract a 0.5 mark deduction.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Wrong Table for Deferred Annuities:** If the income starts after a few years (e.g., "rent will start after 3 years"), you must use the **deferred annuity formula** or apply a **discount factor** to the regular YP. Direct application of the regular YP table is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **Capital Value (CV) = Net Annual Income × YP Factor** — the single most important equation in this module.
- **YP for Perpetuity = 1/r** — used for freehold properties with income in perpetuity.
- **YP for Limited Period (Single Rate) = [1 − (1+r)^(−n)] / r** — used for leaseholds and fixed-term properties.
- **Dual Rate Method uses TWO rates**: one for interest on capital (r1) and one for sinking fund (r2).
- **Sinking Fund (SF) Factor = r / [(1+r)^n − 1]** — read directly from the SF table.
- **Capital Recovery (CR) = SF + r** — never forget this relationship.
- **Annuity Regular** = income received at the end of each year (default assumption in most tables).
- **Annuity Due** = income received at the beginning of each year (multiply annuity regular YP by (1+r)).
- **Deferred Annuity** = income starts after a delay of m years; apply the YP factor and then discount by (1+r)^m.
- **Standard rates** in KTU problems: typically **4%, 5%, 6%, 8%, 9%, 10%, 12%**.
- **Standard periods** in KTU problems: typically **10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 99, 100 years**.
- **Always show the YP factor read from the table** with n and r clearly stated in your solution.
- **Convert monthly/quarterly income to annual** before applying the YP multiplier.
- **For old buildings, prefer the dual rate method** — it is more conservative and is the preferred method for government/legal valuations.
- **For perpetuity, you do NOT need a period** — only the rate matters.
- **The dual rate YP is always higher than the single rate YP** for the same net income and interest on capital rate, because it includes an additional provision for sinking fund.
- **Sinking Fund tables are reciprocal-based** — the YP for SF = 1/SF factor. Read SF first, then take its reciprocal.
- **Practice at least 3-4 full-length problems** involving YP table reading for both single rate and dual rate methods before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Investment: Types and Characteristics of Ideal Investment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: INVESTMENT – TYPES AND CHARACTERISTICS OF IDEAL INVESTMENT

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Investment (KTU Standard Terminology)**
> An **investment** is the commitment of present capital (money or resources) with the expectation of receiving a **future stream of income** (returns) over a defined period, while bearing an acceptable level of risk. In the context of real property valuation, investment refers to the deployment of funds into land, buildings, or real estate assets with the objective of earning **periodic income, capital appreciation**, or both.

The act of investing is fundamentally rooted in the principle of **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow because of its earning potential.

**Real Property Investment** specifically refers to the allocation of capital to physical immovable assets such as:
- **Land** (undeveloped plots, agricultural land)
- **Buildings** (residential, commercial, industrial)
- **Real Estate Securities** (REITs – Real Estate Investment Trusts)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric (KTU 2024):**
> The **Required Rate of Return (r)** is the minimum acceptable yield an investor demands. For KTU valuation problems, the standard benchmark used is **r = 8% to 12% p.a.** for real estate unless otherwise specified.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Money as a Seed" Intuition

Imagine you have a **mango seed** today. If you plant it in fertile soil and wait, in a few years it grows into a tree yielding **hundreds of mangoes every season**. That seed is your **present capital**; the future mangoes are your **returns on investment**.

If, instead, you **eat the seed** (spend the money today), you get a small immediate satisfaction but lose the entire future stream of mangoes.

**The Three Golden Questions Every Investor Asks:**
1. 🌱 **How much should I plant?** (Capital outlay / Initial investment)
2. ⏳ **How long must I wait?** (Investment horizon / Holding period)
3. 🍎 **How big will the harvest be?** (Future value of returns)

Real estate investment is like planting a **slow-growing but high-yielding tree** — it takes time to mature, but the fruits (rental income + capital appreciation) can be very rewarding if the soil (location, demand) is right.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Investment Goal:** **S.A.F.E.**
> - **S** – Safety of principal
> - **A** – Adequate yield (return)
> - **F** – Flexibility (liquidity)
> - **E** – Economy in management (low upkeep cost)

---

## 1.3 Why Real Property Investment? – The KTU Context

In KTU's Valuation of Real Properties course, investment analysis is critical because:
- Real estate forms **~70% of national wealth** in most economies.
- Property valuation is incomplete without **investment feasibility analysis**.
- Every valuation report must justify whether the asset is a **"worth investing" proposition**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Types of Investments – The KTU Classification Framework

Investments can be broadly classified along **three dimensions**:

### (A) Based on Asset Class

| # | Type | Risk Level | Liquidity | Typical Return (p.a.) | Example in Real Property |
|---|------|------------|-----------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | **Land** | Low–Medium | Very Low | **8% – 15%** | Undeveloped urban plot |
| 2 | **Residential Property** | Medium | Low | **6% – 10%** | Apartment for rent |
| 3 | **Commercial Property** | Medium–High | Low | **9% – 14%** | Office space, shop |
| 4 | **Industrial Property** | High | Very Low | **10% – 18%** | Warehouse, factory |
| 5 | **Agricultural Land** | Low | Very Low | **4% – 8%** | Farmland, orchards |
| 6 | **REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts)** | Medium | High | **8% – 12%** | Stock-market-traded property units |
| 7 | **Government Bonds** | Very Low | Medium | **6% – 8%** | Kisan Vikas Patra, G-Secs |
| 8 | **Equities (Shares)** | High | High | **10% – 18%** | NSE/BSE listed stocks |
| 9 | **Gold / Commodities** | Medium | High | **7% – 10%** | Bullion, ETFs |
| 10 | **Fixed Deposits / PPF** | Negligible | Low | **5% – 7.5%** | Bank FD, Public Provident Fund |

### (B) Based on Income Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification – Income Pattern (High-Yield)**

1. **Fixed Income Investments** → Predictable periodic returns.
   - Example: Rented residential flat yielding fixed monthly rent.
   - **Risk = Low**, suitable for retirees.

2. **Variable Income Investments** → Returns fluctuate with market.
   - Example: Commercial complex whose rental income varies with occupancy.
   - **Risk = Medium–High**, suitable for growth-seekers.

3. **Annuity-type Investments** → Returns for a fixed term, then cease.
   - Example: Leasehold property with a defined 30-year lease.
   - **Risk = Low**, often used in pension planning.

4. **Perpetual Investments** → Returns forever (no maturity).
   - Example: Freehold land earning perpetual ground rent.
   - **Risk = Medium**, valued using **YP formula** in KTU problems.

### (C) Based on Ownership Duration

- **Short-term Investment** → Holding period < 3 years (flipping land).
- **Medium-term Investment** → 3 to 10 years (resale of apartments).
- **Long-term Investment** → > 10 years (commercial property for rental yield).

---

## 2.2 Characteristics of an Ideal Investment – The 12-Pillar Framework (KTU Board-Expected)

An **ideal investment** in real property, as per KTU 2024 syllabus standards, must satisfy the following characteristics. Each is examinable in a **2-mark short question** or a **7-mark descriptive part**.

| # | Characteristic | Meaning | KTU Real-Property Example |
|---|----------------|---------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | **Safety of Principal** | Initial capital is fully recoverable at the end. | A freehold flat in a registered, clear-title project. |
| 2 | **Adequate Yield (Return)** | Returns ≥ Required Rate of Return (r). | Rental yield ≥ 8% p.a. |
| 3 | **Capital Appreciation** | Market value grows over time. | Land in Tier-2 city appreciating at 12% p.a. |
| 4 | **Liquidity** | Asset can be converted to cash quickly without loss. | REITs > Commercial Property > Agricultural Land (least liquid). |
| 5 | **Stability of Income** | Returns are not erratic. | Long-term leased commercial property. |
| 6 | **Hedge Against Inflation** | Returns beat inflation (typically ≥ 6%). | Real estate historically beats CPI by 2-3%. |
| 7 | **Tax Efficiency** | Tax benefits (e.g., Section 24(b) home loan interest). | Self-occupied property: ₹2 Lakh deduction. |
| 8 | **Flexibility / Divisibility** | Can be partially sold without losing value. | A 1-acre plot can be split into 4 plots. |
| 9 | **Low Maintenance Cost** | Recurring expenses are minimal. | Vacant land has near-zero upkeep. |
| 10 | **Legally Clear Title** | No encumbrance, lien, or dispute. | Encumbrance Certificate (EC) clean for 30 years. |
| 11 | **Location Advantage** | Demand-supply dynamics favor appreciation. | Property near metro, IT hubs, highways. |
| 12 | **Socially Responsible Use** | Asset use does not harm environment/community. | IGBC-certified green building. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:**
> A property that satisfies **at least 8 out of 12** characteristics above is generally considered a "good investment." An **ideal investment** must satisfy **all 12**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes the **essential formulas** for investment analysis under KTU 2024 Module 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `r` = rate of interest per annum, `n` = number of years/periods. All formulas assume compounding at the end of each period unless stated otherwise.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Simple Interest (SI)** | $SI = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot n}{100}$ | Interest on principal only | ₹ |
| 2 | **Compound Amount (CA)** | $CA = P (1 + r)^{n}$ | Future value of a present sum | ₹ |
| 3 | **Present Worth (PW)** | $PW = \dfrac{F}{(1 + r)^{n}}$ | Discounts future value to present | ₹ |
| 4 | **Sinking Fund (SF)** | $SF = \dfrac{S \cdot r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Annual deposit to accumulate ₹S | ₹/year |
| 5 | **Capital Recovery (CR)** | $CR = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Annual recovery of a present sum | ₹/year |
| 6 | **Present Worth of Annuity (PWA)** | $PWA = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ | PV of equal annual payments | ₹ |
| 7 | **Annual Sinking Fund Annuity (ASFA)** | $ASFA = S \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Equivalent uniform annual deposit | ₹/year |
| 8 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} - C_{0}$ | Profitability indicator | ₹ |
| 9 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+IRR)^{t}}$ | Discount rate making NPV = 0 | % p.a. |
| 10 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{PV(\text{Benefits})}{PV(\text{Costs})}$ | If BCR > 1, invest | Ratio |
| 11 | **Capitalization Rate (Yield)** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Income}}{\text{Capital Value}} \times 100$ | Used in property valuation | % p.a. |
| 12 | **Years' Purchase (YP)** | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ (perpetuity) or $\dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ (limited) | Multiplier for income | Number |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Symbol Convention Used in KTU Valuation Papers:**
> - $P$ = Principal / Present Value
> - $S$ or $F$ = Future Sum / Amount
> - $A$ = Annual instalment (Annuity)
> - $n$ = Number of years
> - $r$ = Rate of interest per rupee per year (e.g., r = 0.10 for 10%)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Valuation

These formulas are **not abstract** — they are used daily by:
- **Valuers** to determine the **Present Market Value (PMV)** of a property based on its future income.
- **Banks** to compute **Equated Monthly Instalments (EMI)** for housing loans.
- **Government bodies** to assess **compensatory land acquisition** awards.
- **Real Estate Developers** to perform **feasibility analysis** of new projects.
- **Insurance companies** to calculate **sinking fund reserves** for property depreciation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compound Amount Formula $CA = P(1+r)^{n}$

**Starting Concept:** The basis of compound interest is that interest earned in one period is added to the principal, and the next period's interest is calculated on this **new (larger) principal**. This is called "interest on interest."

**Step 1 – Define Initial State**

Let $P$ be the principal at the **beginning of year 0**. The interest rate per rupee per year is $r$. The number of years is $n$.

**Step 2 – Amount at End of Year 1**

Interest for year 1 is $P \cdot r$. So the amount at the end of year 1 is:

$$A_1 = P + P \cdot r = P(1 + r)$$

This is the principal at the start of year 2.

**Step 3 – Amount at End of Year 2**

Interest for year 2 is calculated on $A_1$, not on $P$. So:

$$A_2 = A_1 + A_1 \cdot r = A_1(1 + r) = P(1 + r)(1 + r) = P(1 + r)^{2}$$

**Step 4 – Recognize the Pattern**

By induction, at the end of year $k$:

$$A_k = P(1 + r)^{k}$$

**Step 5 – Final Result for Year n**

Setting $k = n$:

$$\boxed{CA = P(1 + r)^{n}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the most fundamental TVM formula. If a KTU question says "Find the value of ₹5,00,000 after 10 years at 9% compound interest," you simply substitute $P = 5,00,000$, $r = 0.09$, $n = 10$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Present Worth of Annuity (PWA) Formula

**Starting Concept:** An annuity is a series of equal payments made at the **end of each period** for $n$ periods. We want to know what a future stream of ₹$A$ per year is worth **today**.

**Step 1 – Write the Series**

The cash flows occur at the end of years 1, 2, 3, ..., n:

$$CF_1 = A,\ CF_2 = A,\ CF_3 = A,\ \ldots,\ CF_n = A$$

**Step 2 – Discount Each Cash Flow to Present**

Using $PW = \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}$ for each:

$$PWA = \dfrac{A}{(1+r)^{1}} + \dfrac{A}{(1+r)^{2}} + \ldots + \dfrac{A}{(1+r)^{n}}$$

**Step 3 – Factor Out the Common Term**

$$PWA = A \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{1}} + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{2}} + \ldots + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}} \right]$$

**Step 4 – Apply Geometric Series Summation**

The series in brackets is a **finite geometric progression** with:
- First term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$
- Common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$
- Number of terms = $n$

Sum of GP = $a \cdot \dfrac{1 - q^{n}}{1 - q}$:

$$Sum = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{(1+r)}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}}$$

**Step 5 – Simplify the Denominator**

$$1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} = \dfrac{(1+r) - 1}{(1+r)} = \dfrac{r}{(1+r)}$$

**Step 6 – Combine Everything**

$$PWA = A \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}}{\dfrac{r}{(1+r)}}$$

The $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{PWA = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This formula is the **backbone of property valuation** because rental income is essentially an annuity. The capitalized value of a property yielding ₹A per year for $n$ years is exactly $PWA$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Sinking Fund (SF) Formula

**Starting Concept:** A sinking fund is a periodic deposit made into an interest-bearing account such that at the end of $n$ years, the accumulated amount equals a target future sum $S$.

**Step 1 – Recognize the Annuity Future Value**

The deposits form an annuity. Using the CA formula for each deposit and summing:

The accumulated value of a series of deposits of ₹$A$ at the end of each year for $n$ years at rate $r$ is:

$$S = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

(Geometric series sum, similar to PWA but in forward time.)

**Step 2 – Solve for A (the Annual Deposit)**

$$A = S \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 3 – Identify as Sinking Fund**

The annual amount $A$ required to accumulate $S$ in $n$ years is called the **Sinking Fund Instalment (SFI)**:

$$\boxed{SF = \dfrac{S \cdot r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** A property owner sets up a sinking fund to accumulate ₹50,00,000 over 20 years for major repairs. If money earns 9% compounded annually, the yearly deposit is:

$$SF = \dfrac{50,00,000 \times 0.09}{(1.09)^{20} - 1} = \dfrac{4,50,000}{5.6044} = ₹80,293 \text{ per year}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**

> A real estate developer is considering the purchase of a commercial office space for **₹80,00,000**. The expected **net annual rental income** is **₹9,60,000** with **annual escalation of 5%** for the next **10 years**. The salvage value at the end of year 10 is estimated at **₹1,20,00,000**. The developer's **required rate of return (MARR)** is **12% p.a.**
>
> **Determine whether the investment is viable using NPV.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify Cash Flows**

- Initial Investment (year 0): $C_0 = -80,00,000$
- Year 1 income: $9,60,000$
- Year 2 income: $9,60,000 \times 1.05 = 10,08,000$
- Year $t$ income: $9,60,000 \times (1.05)^{t-1}$
- Year 10 income: $9,60,000 \times (1.05)^{9} = 14,86,975$
- Year 10 salvage: $1,20,00,000$

**Step 2 – Calculate Geometric Gradient Factor**

A series with $5\%$ growth is a **geometric gradient**. The present worth of a geometric gradient with first payment $A_1$ and growth rate $g$ for $n$ years at discount rate $r$ is:

$$PV_{\text{gradient}} = \dfrac{A_1}{r - g} \cdot \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^{n} \right]$$

(Valid only when $r \neq g$. Here $r = 12\%$, $g = 5\%$.)

**Step 3 – Substitute Values**

$$PV_{\text{income}} = \dfrac{9,60,000}{0.12 - 0.05} \cdot \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{1.05}{1.12}\right)^{10} \right]$$

$$= \dfrac{9,60,000}{0.07} \cdot \left[ 1 - (0.9375)^{10} \right]$$

$$= 1,37,14,285.71 \cdot \left[ 1 - 0.5297 \right]$$

$$= 1,37,14,285.71 \times 0.4703 = ₹64,50,000 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 4 – Discount Salvage Value to Present**

$$PV_{\text{salvage}} = \dfrac{1,20,00,000}{(1.12)^{10}} = \dfrac{1,20,00,000}{3.1058} = ₹38,63,500 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 5 – Compute NPV**

$$NPV = PV_{\text{income}} + PV_{\text{salvage}} - C_0$$

$$NPV = 64,50,000 + 38,63,500 - 80,00,000 = +₹23,13,500$$

**Step 6 – Decision**

Since $NPV > 0$, the investment is **financially viable**. The developer should proceed.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Check:** The NPV being positive by a substantial margin (₹23.13 Lakhs on an ₹80 Lakh investment) indicates a **healthy project** with good cushion against risk.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Real Property vs. Other Investments

| Criterion | Real Estate (Land/Building) | Stocks / Equities | Gold | Bank FD | Govt. Bonds |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------|------|---------|-------------|
| **Return Potential** | 8% – 15% | 10% – 20% | 7% – 10% | 5% – 7% | 6% – 8% |
| **Risk Level** | Medium | High | Medium | Very Low | Negligible |
| **Liquidity** | Low | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Capital Appreciation** | High | High | Moderate | None | None |
| **Inflation Hedge** | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Poor | Fair |
| **Tax Efficiency** | High (Sec 24, 80C) | Moderate (LTCG) | Moderate | Low (TDS) | Low |
| **Management Effort** | Moderate | High | Low | Nil | Nil |
| **Income Stability** | High (rental) | Variable | None | Fixed | Fixed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Investment Decision Process

This diagram maps the **complete decision workflow** an investor (or valuer) follows when evaluating a real property investment — equivalent to the logical flow expected in KTU valuation problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Identify Investment Objective] --> B[Define Risk Appetite and Time Horizon]
    B --> C[Survey Available Asset Classes]
    C --> D{Is Asset Real Property?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Check Location and Title Clear Status]
    D -- No --> F[Apply Standard Financial Yield Formulas]
    E --> G[Estimate Gross Annual Rental Income]
    G --> H[Deduct Vacancy and Maintenance 5 to 10 percent]
    H --> I[Compute Net Annual Income NOI]
    I --> J[Determine Required Rate of Return MARR]
    J --> K[Apply Years Purchase or PWA Formula]
    K --> L[Compute Present Market Value PMV]
    L --> M{Capitalized Value exceeds Purchase Price?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Proceed Buy or Recommend Investment]
    M -- No --> O[Reject or Renegotiate Price]
    F --> P[Compare Returns across Asset Classes]
    P --> Q[Select Highest Risk Adjusted Return]
    N --> R[END Execute Investment]
    O --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy Diagram – Characteristics of an Ideal Investment

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode[Ideal Investment in Real Property]
    rootNode --> safetypillar[Safety of Principal]
    rootNode --> returnpillar[Adequate Yield]
    rootNode --> growthpillar[Capital Appreciation]
    rootNode --> liquidpillar[Liquidity]
    rootNode --> inflapillar[Hedge Against Inflation]
    rootNode --> legalpillar[Legally Clear Title]
    safetypillar --> safetysub1[Registered Property]
    safetypillar --> safetysub2[Clear Encumbrance Certificate]
    returnpillar --> returnsub1[Rental Yield greater than 8 percent]
    returnpillar --> returnsub2[Net Yield after expenses]
    growthpillar --> growthsub1[Location in Growth Corridor]
    growthsub2[Infrastructure Development Nearby]
    growthpillar --> growthsub2
    liquidpillar --> liquidsub1[Active Local Market]
    liquidpillar --> liquidsub2[REIT as Alternative]
    inflapillar --> inflasub1[Returns Beat CPI Inflation]
    inflasub2[Real Positive Return after Tax]
    inflapillar --> inflasub2
    legalpillar --> legalsub1[Approved Building Plans]
    legalpillar --> legalsub2[No Pending Litigation]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of Investment Analysis Formulas

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Time Value of Money Block] --> blockB[Present Worth Block]
    blockA --> blockC[Future Worth Block]
    blockA --> blockD[Annuity Block]
    blockA --> blockE[Sinking Fund Block]
    blockB --> useB1[Discount Future Cash Flow]
    blockB --> useB2[Loan Amortization]
    blockC --> useC1[Estimate Future Property Value]
    blockC --> useC2[Inflation Adjusted Value]
    blockD --> useD1[Valuation of Rental Income]
    blockD --> useD2[EMI Computation]
    blockE --> useE1[Depreciation Reserve]
    blockE --> useE2[Replacement Fund]
    useB1 --> final[Net Present Value NPV]
    useB2 --> final
    useC1 --> final
    useD1 --> final
    useE1 --> final
```

## 4.4 Risk-Return Tradeoff Schematic for Real Property

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX[Return Percent per Annum] --> p1[Low Return Low Risk 5 to 7 percent]
    axisX --> p2[Medium Return Medium Risk 8 to 12 percent]
    axisX --> p3[High Return High Risk 13 to 18 percent]
    p1 --> e1[Bank FD Government Bonds]
    p2 --> e2[Residential Rental Commercial Property]
    p3 --> e3[Stocks Speculative Land Flipping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Real property (residential and commercial) typically sits in the **medium-risk medium-return zone** — the most comfortable position for long-term wealth creation. This is why it is a favored asset class for HNIs (High Net-worth Individuals) in Kerala's Thrissur, Kochi, and Calicut markets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**Define an "Ideal Investment" in the context of real property valuation. List any FOUR essential characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **ideal investment** in real property is one that maximizes returns while minimizing risk, satisfies the investor's time horizon, and ensures the safety of the principal sum invested. The investor receives a steady stream of income along with capital appreciation.

**Four essential characteristics:**

1. **Safety of Principal** – The initial capital is fully recoverable at the end of the holding period, with no erosion of value.
2. **Adequate Yield** – The annual return (rental income + appreciation) is at least equal to the **Required Rate of Return (MARR)**.
3. **Capital Appreciation** – The market value of the asset should grow over time, outpacing inflation.
4. **Liquidity** – The asset should be convertible to cash within a reasonable time without significant loss of value.

> **Additional characteristics (any one for full marks):** Stability of income, tax efficiency, clear legal title, low maintenance cost.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Distinguish between "Fixed Income Investment" and "Variable Income Investment" with one real property example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fixed Income Investment | Variable Income Investment |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Returns are pre-determined and do not fluctuate. | Returns vary with market conditions or occupancy. |
| **Risk Level** | Low to Medium | Medium to High |
| **Return Pattern** | Stable, predictable periodic income | Fluctuating income stream |
| **Example – Real Property** | A residential flat rented on a long-term lease at fixed monthly rent. | A commercial shopping complex whose rental income varies with tenant occupancy and business cycles. |
| **Suitability** | Risk-averse investors, retirees | Growth-oriented investors |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice**. Solve **either Option A or Option B**. The structure below mirrors the official KTU question paper.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

**A piece of land is available for purchase at ₹50,00,000. The expected net annual rental income (after all expenses) from a building to be constructed on it is ₹6,00,000 per year for the next 15 years. At the end of 15 years, the salvage value of land + building is estimated at ₹80,00,000. The investor's required rate of return is 10% per annum.**

**(a) Determine the Net Present Value (NPV) of the investment.** (7 Marks)
**(b) Advise whether the investor should buy the property. If not, what is the maximum price the investor should pay?** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a)**

**Given Data:**
- Initial Cost $C_0$ = ₹50,00,000
- Annual Income $A$ = ₹6,00,000 (uniform for 15 years)
- Salvage Value $S$ = ₹80,00,000 at end of year 15
- Rate $r$ = 10% = 0.10
- Period $n$ = 15 years

**Step 1 – Calculate PV of Uniform Annuity (Rental Income)**

Using the PWA formula:

$$PWA = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$$

Substituting:

$$PWA = 6,00,000 \cdot \dfrac{(1.10)^{15} - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{15}}$$

We know $(1.10)^{15} = 4.1772$. Therefore:

$$PWA = 6,00,000 \cdot \dfrac{4.1772 - 1}{0.10 \times 4.1772} = 6,00,000 \cdot \dfrac{3.1772}{0.41772}$$

$$PWA = 6,00,000 \times 7.6061 = ₹45,63,660$$

**Step 2 – Calculate PV of Salvage Value**

$$PV_{\text{salvage}} = \dfrac{S}{(1+r)^{n}} = \dfrac{80,00,000}{4.1772} = ₹19,15,070$$

**Step 3 – Calculate NPV**

$$NPV = PWA + PV_{\text{salvage}} - C_0$$

$$NPV = 45,63,660 + 19,15,070 - 50,00,000 = +₹14,78,730$$

**Result:** NPV = **+₹14,78,730** (positive)

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b)**

**Decision:** Since NPV is **positive (+₹14.78 Lakhs)**, the investment is **financially viable** and the investor **should buy** the property.

**Maximum Purchase Price** the investor should pay (when NPV = 0):

$$P_{\max} = PWA + PV_{\text{salvage}} = 45,63,660 + 19,15,070 = ₹64,78,730$$

**Interpretation:** The investor can pay up to **₹64,78,730** and still break even at 10% return. The listed price of ₹50,00,000 is well below this ceiling, leaving a healthy margin of **₹14,78,730** as profit in present-value terms.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Option 2 (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

**Discuss in detail the various TYPES of investments a civil engineer/valuer should consider for a client who has ₹1 crore to deploy. Also, enumerate the TWELVE characteristics of an ideal investment, explaining each in one line.**

**(a) Classify investments with at least 8 types and provide example from real property domain.** (7 Marks)
**(b) State and briefly explain the 12 characteristics of an ideal investment.** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a): Types of Investments**

| # | Investment Type | Risk | Return (p.a.) | Real Property Example |
|---|------------------|------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Land (Vacant Plot)** | Low | 8% – 15% | Undeveloped plot in growth corridor. |
| 2 | **Residential House/Flat** | Medium | 6% – 10% | 3-BHK apartment for rent. |
| 3 | **Commercial Property** | Medium | 9% – 14% | Office space leased to IT firm. |
| 4 | **Industrial Property** | High | 10% – 18% | Warehouse near industrial belt. |
| 5 | **REITs** | Medium | 8% – 12% | Embassy REIT units on NSE. |
| 6 | **Government Bonds** | Very Low | 6% – 8% | G-Secs, Kisan Vikas Patra. |
| 7 | **Equities (Shares)** | High | 10% – 20% | Nifty 50 index stocks. |
| 8 | **Gold / Bullion** | Medium | 7% – 10% | Sovereign Gold Bonds. |
| 9 | **Mutual Funds (Debt)** | Low | 5% – 8% | Liquid / Ultra-short funds. |
| 10 | **Fixed Deposits** | Negligible | 5% – 7.5% | Bank 5-year FD. |

**Diversification Strategy (KTU-Recommended):**
- **40%** → Real Estate (Land + Residential)
- **25%** → Equities / Mutual Funds
- **15%** → Gold
