- **10%** → Fixed Income (FD, Bonds)
- **10%** → Liquid / Emergency Reserve

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b): 12 Characteristics of Ideal Investment**

1. **Safety of Principal** – Original capital is fully protected and recoverable.
2. **Adequate Yield** – Return ≥ required rate (MARR), typically 8% – 12%.
3. **Capital Appreciation** – Market value rises with time, beating inflation.
4. **Liquidity** – Asset can be sold quickly without major value loss.
5. **Stability of Income** – Returns are not erratic or highly seasonal.
6. **Hedge Against Inflation** – Real returns remain positive after inflation.
7. **Tax Efficiency** – Legitimate tax savings (e.g., Section 24, 80C).
8. **Flexibility / Divisibility** – Asset can be partially sold or repurposed.
9. **Low Maintenance Cost** – Annual upkeep expenses are minimal.
10. **Legally Clear Title** – No encumbrance, lien, or litigation.
11. **Location Advantage** – Demand-supply dynamics support growth.
12. **Socially Responsible Use** – Investment does not harm environment or community.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `r` and `n` units** – If the problem says "12% per annum compounded semi-annually," then $r = 0.06$ and $n = 2 \times \text{years}$. Many students forget to convert. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Sign convention in NPV** – Initial investment is a **cash outflow** (negative). Many students add it instead of subtracting. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting the salvage value** – In valuation problems, salvage value must be discounted to present and added to NPV. Skipping it leads to wrong decisions. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 4. **Using simple interest instead of compound interest** – KTU 2024 valuation questions always assume **compound interest** unless explicitly stated otherwise. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not stating the units** in final answer – Always write "₹" symbol clearly. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 6. **Mixing up YP formulas** – Years Purchase for perpetuity is $\frac{1}{r}$, but for limited period is $\frac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$. Read the question carefully. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🔑 Last-Minute KTU Revision – 60-Second Summary**

- ✅ **Investment** = Present sacrifice for future benefit, governed by **Time Value of Money**.
- ✅ **Types of Investment**: Real property (land, residential, commercial, industrial), REITs, bonds, equities, gold, FD.
- ✅ **Income patterns**: Fixed, Variable, Annuity, Perpetual.
- ✅ **12 Characteristics of Ideal Investment** (mnemonic **S.A.F.E.T.Y. C.A.P.I.T.A.L.**):
  - **S**afety
  - **A**dequate yield
  - **F**lexibility
  - **E**conomy
  - **T**itle clarity
  - **Y**ield stability
  - **C**apital appreciation
  - **A**ppreciation potential
  - **P**roper location
  - **I**nflation hedge
  - **T**ax efficiency
  - **A**lignment with social good
  - **L**iquidity
- ✅ **Must-know formulas**:
  - $CA = P(1+r)^{n}$
  - $PWA = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$
  - $SF = \dfrac{S \cdot r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$
  - $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$
  - $Y = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{\text{Capital Value}}$
- ✅ **Decision rule for NPV**:
  - $NPV > 0$ → **INVEST** ✅
  - $NPV = 0$ → Indifferent (break-even)
  - $NPV < 0$ → **REJECT** ❌
- ✅ **Real property** is **medium-risk, medium-return**, with high **inflation-hedge** ability and **low liquidity** compared to financial assets.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Standard Rate of Return** assumption: **10% p.a.** unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ Always **show the formula**, **substitute the values**, and **box the final answer** to score full marks in valuation problems.
- ✅ In **perpetual income** problems, use $YP = \frac{1}{r}$ (i.e., 10 years' purchase for 10% rate, 12.5 YP for 8% rate).
- ✅ For **limited-period income**, use the PWA multiplier (7.6061 at 10%, 15 years).

> **End of Module 2 – Investment: Types and Characteristics of Ideal Investment**
> **Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcomes | CO2: Analyse Investment Feasibility in Real Property**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appraisal Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Appraisal Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Appraisal Techniques** in real property valuation are the **systematic, mathematically defined, and standardized methods** employed by a *Valuer* to estimate the *Market Value*, *Rental Value*, *Investment Value*, or *Fair Value* of an immovable asset (land, building, or both) at a given date, taking into account the **purpose of valuation**, the **nature of the property**, and the **prevailing economic conditions**.

According to the **RICS (Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors) Red Book** and the **IVS (International Valuation Standards)**, an appraisal must be supported by one or more of the following recognised approaches to value. KTU expects students to know the *theory*, *applicability*, *formulae*, and *limitations* of each technique.

### Classification of Appraisal Techniques (KTU Module-2 Core)

| Sl. No. | Primary Approach | Sub-Method | Best-Suited Property Type |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Market / Sales Comparison** | Direct Comparison | Residential houses, vacant plots |
| 2 | **Income Approach** | Direct Capitalisation | Commercial offices, shops |
| 3 | **Income Approach** | Yield Cap. (DCF) | Multi-tenanted income assets |
| 4 | **Cost Approach** | Replacement/Reproduction | Specialised properties, schools, hospitals |
| 5 | **Residual / Hypothetical** | Development Method | Bare land to be developed |
| 6 | **Profits / Accounts** | Trading Potential | Hotels, cinemas, theatres |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Three Lenses of a Valuer")

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a valuer as a doctor diagnosing a property's value from three different clinical angles:**
>
> - **Lens 1 — The "Twin House" Lens (Market Approach):** *“What did the neighbour’s identical house sell for last month?”* — You compare and adjust.
> - **Lens 2 — The "Cash Machine" Lens (Income Approach):** *“How much rent will this building throw at me every month, capitalised at the market yield?”*
> - **Lens 3 — The "Brick & Mortar" Lens (Cost Approach):** *“If I demolish it and rebuild from scratch, what will it cost me, minus how old it already is?”*

The final *Market Value* is the **reconciled, weighted average** of the values indicated by the most applicable methods.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Appraisal

- **Standard Lot Size (India Urban):** 1 Cent = **435.6 sq.ft** ; 1 Ground ≈ 2,400 sq.ft
- **Standard Depreciation Methods:** *Straight-Line* and *Written-Down Value (WDV)*
- **Standard Referred Publications:** RICS Red Book, IVS 2022, IS 6571, IS 15449
- **Capitalisation Rate (i):** Always expressed as a *decimal* (e.g., 8% → 0.08)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always quote the *purpose* of valuation (*Mortgage, Sale, Insurance, Taxation, Compulsory Acquisition*) before selecting the technique — the same property can have **three different values** under three different purposes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Market / Sales Comparison Approach

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. Identify **at least three** recent, arm’s-length transactions of *comparable* properties in the **same micro-market**.
2. Adjust the transaction price of each comparable for differences in:
   - **Time** (market movement index)
   - **Location** (frontage, road width)
   - **Physical attributes** (area, age, condition, floor, view)
3. Apply the *net adjusted value* of the comparables to the **subject property**.
4. Reject outliers (statistical rejection of ± 2σ deviations).

### Key Formula
$$
\boxed{\; V_{\text{subject}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{P_k + \sum A_{k,j}}{n} \;}
$$

Where $P_k$ is the transaction price of comparable $k$, and $A_{k,j}$ are the unit adjustments (₹/sq.ft or ₹ per unit) for attributes $j$ (time, location, size, etc.).

## 2.2 The Income Capitalisation Approach

This is the **most heavily-tested** approach in KTU examinations. It rests on the concept of **Years' Purchase (YP)**.

### 2.2.1 Years' Purchase (YP) — Complete Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Income Pattern | YP Formula | Use Case |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Perpetual fixed income** | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{i}$ | Freehold ground rent |
| 2 | **Annual income for $n$ years** | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Leasehold interest |
| 3 | **Deferred perpetuity ($t$ yrs)** | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{i(1+i)^{t}}$ | Reversion in $t$ years |
| 4 | **Sinking Fund (Redeemable)** | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Recover capital + interest |
| 5 | **Growing perpetuity (rate $g$)** | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{i-g}$ | Rent escalating with inflation |
| 6 | **Double Rate (Reversion)** | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{i+\dfrac{1}{n}} \text{ or } \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{s}}$ | Reciprocals of Inwood tables |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TRAP:** The *Years' Purchase* and the *Years' Purchase with Reversion* have **identical algebraic forms** but represent **opposite cash flows**. Students frequently mix them up and lose 4–5 marks per question.

### 2.2.2 Direct Capitalisation
$$
\boxed{\; V = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Income (NAI)}}{\text{Capitalisation Rate } (i)} = \text{NAI} \times \text{YP} \;}
$$

The NAI is the *gross rent* minus **all** outgoings (vacancy, management, repairs, taxes, insurance).

### 2.2.3 Discounted Cash Flow (DCF / Yield Capitalisation)
$$
V = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^{t}} \; + \; \dfrac{\text{Terminal Value}_{n}}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

The Terminal Value uses a *reversion yield* $i_{rev}$ (typically 25–50 bps higher than the initial yield $i$ to reflect the open-market risk at exit).

## 2.3 The Cost Approach

$$
\boxed{\; V_{\text{property}} = V_{\text{land}} \; + \; (RCN - D_{\text{physical}} - D_{\text{functional}} - D_{\text{economic}}) \;}
$$

- **RCN** = Replacement Cost New (₹/sq.ft × plinth area)
- **D** = Total Depreciation (sum of physical, functional, and economic)
- **Land value** is **never depreciated**.

## 2.4 The Residual / Development Method (Hypothetical)

Used when *bare land* is to be redeveloped. The land value is the *residue* after subtracting all costs and profit from the gross development value (GDV).

$$
\boxed{\; V_{\text{land}} = \text{GDV} - (\text{Build Cost} + \text{Finance Cost} + \text{Prof. Fee} + \text{Statutory} + \text{Contingency} + \text{Developer's Profit}) \;}
$$

Developer's profit is typically **15%–25% of GDV** in India for residential projects.

## 2.5 The Profits / Accounts Method

For *specialised* trading properties (hotels, theatres, hospitals) where comparable sales don't exist.

$$
\boxed{\; V = \dfrac{\text{Adjusted Trading Profit} \times 100}{\text{All Risks Yield (\%)} } \;}
$$

The All Risks Yield (ARY) is normally higher (8%–10% for hotels) than commercial yields (7%–8%) to reflect **trading risk**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Banks (Mortgage Valuation):** Use the **Market Approach** as primary, Cost Approach as cross-check.
> - **Insurance:** Strictly the **Cost Approach** (replacement cost new).
> - **NHAI / Land Acquisition:** **Belting method** + **Market Approach**.
> - **Income-producing REIT assets:** **DCF / Yield Cap.** with sensitivity of ±100 bps.
> - **Statutory Authorities (BMC, KMDA, KIIFB):** **Land & Building Method** as per IS 6571.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Years' Purchase (Perpetuity) from First Principles

The **Years' Purchase (YP)** at a given rate $i$ is defined as the *capital sum* whose annual interest (at that rate) equals **one rupee of net income**.

**Step 1 — Capital sum of ₹1 in perpetuity:**
$$
\text{Let capital} = C, \quad \text{Annual interest} = C \times i
$$

**Step 2 — Equate the interest to ₹1 (the unit of net income):**
$$
C \times i = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $C$:**
$$
C = \frac{1}{i}
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, the Years' Purchase for a perpetuity is:**
$$
\boxed{\; \text{YP}_{\infty} = \frac{1}{i} \;}
$$

For an income of ₹$R$ per annum in perpetuity:
$$
V = R \times \text{YP}_{\infty} = \frac{R}{i}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: YP for an Annuity of $n$ Years (Deferred / Annuity-Immediate)

The capital value is the sum of $n$ discounted annual payments of ₹1 each.

**Step 1 — Discount each future rupee back to present (time 0):**
$$
V = \frac{1}{(1+i)^{1}} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{2}} + \dots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 2 — This is a geometric progression with first term $a = \frac{1}{1+i}$ and common ratio $r = \frac{1}{1+i}$. Sum of GP:**
$$
S = a \, \frac{1-r^{n}}{1-r}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $a$ and $r$:**
$$
V = \frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator:**
$$
1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the factor $\frac{1}{1+i}$:**
$$
V = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^{n}$:**
$$
\boxed{\; \text{YP}_{n} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \;}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: DCF Terminal Value (Reversion Yield)

In a multi-year DCF, the **Terminal Value** at the end of year $n$ is the present value of *all future cash flows* from year $n+1$ to infinity, discounted at the **reversion yield** $i_{rev}$.

**Step 1 — Assume net income in year $n+1$ is $\text{CF}_{n+1}$:**
$$
\text{TV}_{n} = \frac{\text{CF}_{n+1}}{i_{rev}}
$$

**Step 2 — Discount this terminal value back to year 0:**
$$
\text{PV of TV} = \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}} \cdot \frac{\text{CF}_{n+1}}{i_{rev}}
$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the present value of the property under DCF is:**
$$
\boxed{\; V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^{t}} \; + \; \frac{\text{CF}_{n+1}}{i_{rev}(1+i)^{n}} \;}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

> **Question:** A freehold shop yields a net annual rent of **₹1,20,000** in perpetuity. The redemption rate of capital is **8%** p.a. The property also has a *reversion* clause where rent will reduce to ₹90,000 p.a. after 10 years for perpetuity. Find the present value of the property.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Capitalised value of the initial perpetuity (₹1,20,000 @ 8%):**
$$
V_1 = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{0.08} = \text{₹}15{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Capitalised value of the reversion (₹90,000 in perpetuity from year 11):**
$$
V_2 = \frac{90{,}000}{0.08} = \text{₹}11{,}25{,}000 \quad \text{(value at year 10)}
$$

**Step 3 — Discount this reversion back 10 years at 8% (i.e., the YP for a deferred perpetuity):**
$$
V_2^{PV} = \frac{11{,}25{,}000}{(1.08)^{10}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $(1.08)^{10}$:**
$$
(1.08)^{10} = 2.1589
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the present value of reversion:**
$$
V_2^{PV} = \frac{11{,}25{,}000}{2.1589} = \text{₹}5{,}21{,}073
$$

**Step 6 — Add the two capital values:**
$$
V_{\text{total}} = 15{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}21{,}073 = \boxed{\text{₹}20{,}21{,}073}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Situation | Most Suitable Technique | Reason |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Vacant residential plot in a developed colony | **Market Approach** | Active sales data available |
| 30-year-old commercial building on prime road | **Income Cap.** | Generates measurable rent |
| Specialised school building (no sales data) | **Cost Approach** | Limited market; replacement cost reliable |
| Bare agricultural land marked for township | **Residual Method** | Value is *what it can become* |
| 100-room hotel | **Profits Method** | Value = capitalised trading profit |
| Statutory valuation for property tax | **Cost + Land (BMC)** | Mandated by law |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Appraisal Techniques

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Appraisal Techniques for Real Property] --> M1[Market Comparison Approach]
    root --> M2[Income Approach]
    root --> M3[Cost Approach]
    root --> M4[Residual Method]
    root --> M5[Profits Method]
    root --> M6[Belting Method]

    M1 --> M1a[Direct Comparison of Sales]
    M1 --> M1b[Adjustment Grid]

    M2 --> M2a[Direct Capitalisation]
    M2 --> M2b[Yield Capitalisation DCF]
    M2a --> M2a1[Perpetuity YP]
    M2a --> M2a2[Annuity YP]
    M2a --> M2a3[Deferred YP]
    M2b --> M2b1[Explicit Forecast Period]
    M2b --> M2b2[Terminal Value with Reversion]

    M3 --> M3a[Replacement Cost]
    M3 --> M3b[Depreciation Analysis]
    M3b --> M3b1[Physical Deterioration]
    M3b --> M3b2[Functional Obsolescence]
    M3b --> M3b3[Economic Obsolescence]

    M4 --> M4a[Development Method]
    M4 --> M4b[Land Residual Method]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Income-Capitalisation Appraisal

```mermaid
graph LR
    input[Step 1 Gross Rent Data] --> vchk[Step 2 Deduct Vacancy]
    vchk --> oth[Step 3 Deduct Other Outgoings]
    oth --> nai[Step 4 Compute Net Annual Income NAI]
    nai --> choose{Step 5 Income Pattern}
    choose --> perp[Perpetuity]
    choose --> annu[Annuity n Years]
    choose --> def[Deferred n Years]
    choose --> dcf[DCF Multi Year]
    perp --> yp1[YP equals 1 divided by i]
    annu --> yp2[YP equals annuity formula]
    def --> yp3[YP equals deferred formula]
    dcf --> dcfstep[Discount each cash flow plus terminal value]
    yp1 --> mult[Step 6 Multiply NAI by YP]
    yp2 --> mult
    yp3 --> mult
    dcfstep --> sum[Step 7 Sum all PVs to get Market Value]
    mult --> sum
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Workflow of a Residual Method Valuation

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Step 1 Identify Highest and Best Use of Land] --> s2[Step 2 Estimate Gross Development Value GDV]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Estimate Total Development Cost]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 Add Professional Fees and Finance Cost]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5 Add Statutory and Contingency Charges]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6 Add Developers Profit 15 to 25 percent of GDV]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7 Compute Land Value as Residue]
    s7 --> s8[Step 8 Cross-Check with Comparable Land Sales]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain briefly the Market Data Comparison Approach of valuation. When is it preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Market Data Comparison Approach** (a.k.a. *Sales Comparison Approach*) estimates the value of a property by **comparing it with similar properties that have been recently sold** in the same locality. Adjustments are made to the comparable sales for differences in *time, location, size, condition, and amenities* to arrive at an indicative value of the subject property.
**Preferred when:** the property is **commonly transacted** in an active market (e.g., residential flats, vacant urban plots), sufficient **recent arm’s-length comparable data** is available, and the property is **not income-producing** or specialised. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define “Years’ Purchase” (YP) and state its formula for a perpetuity and a deferred perpetuity of $t$ years.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition:** *Years’ Purchase (YP)* is the **capital sum** whose annual interest at the prevailing redemption rate equals **one rupee of net income**. It is the **multiplier** that converts annual net income into capital value.
- **For a perpetuity:** $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{i}$
- **For a perpetuity deferred by $t$ years:** $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{i(1+i)^{t}}$
where $i$ is the rate of interest (decimal). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice — Select EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A freehold commercial building yields a net annual rent of ₹6,00,000 for the first 5 years and ₹9,00,000 thereafter in perpetuity. The capitalisation rate is 9% p.a. Determine the present capital value of the property.**

**Sub-part (a) — Compute the YP for the first 5 years and capitalise ₹6,00,000. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the YP formula for an annuity of 5 years at 9%:**
$$
\text{YP}_{5} = \frac{(1.09)^{5} - 1}{0.09 \times (1.09)^{5}}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $(1.09)^{5}$:**
$$
(1.09)^{5} = 1.5386
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$
\text{YP}_{5} = \frac{1.5386 - 1}{0.09 \times 1.5386} = \frac{0.5386}{0.1385} = 3.8897
$$

**Step 4 — Capitalise ₹6,00,000:**
$$
V_1 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 3.8897 = \text{₹}23{,}33{,}820
$$

**Sub-part (b) — Compute the YP for the deferred perpetuity and total capital value. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Capitalise the perpetuity of ₹9,00,000 at 9%:**
$$
V_{\text{perp}} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{0.09} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(value at year 5)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the YP for a 5-year deferment:**
$$
\text{YP}_{\text{deferred}} = \frac{1}{0.09 \times (1.09)^{5}} = \frac{1}{0.1385} = 7.2205
$$

**Step 3 — Discount the reversion to year 0:**
$$
V_2 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1385 = \text{₹}13{,}85{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Total capital value of the property:**
$$
V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 = 23{,}33{,}820 + 13{,}85{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹}37{,}18{,}820}
$$

---

### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A bare land is proposed to be developed as a residential layout of 50 plots of 3 cents each. The expected sale price is ₹25 lakh per plot. Total construction/development cost is ₹3.5 crore, professional fees 8% of cost, finance cost 12% of cost, statutory charges ₹25 lakh, contingency 3% of cost, and developer’s profit 20% of GDV. Find the value of the land by the Residual Method.**

**Sub-part (a) — Compute the Gross Development Value (GDV) and total development cost. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute GDV:**
$$
\text{GDV} = 50 \text{ plots} \times \text{₹}25{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}125 \text{ Crore (₹}1{,}25{,}00{,}00{,}000\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Base construction cost:** ₹3.5 Cr = ₹3,50,00,000

**Step 3 — Professional fees (8% of cost):**
$$
0.08 \times 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}28{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Finance cost (12% of cost):**
$$
0.12 \times 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}42{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Statutory charges:** ₹25,00,000 (given)

**Step 6 — Contingency (3% of cost):**
$$
0.03 \times 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}10{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 7 — Total Development Cost:**
$$
3{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 28{,}00{,}000 + 42{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹}4{,}55{,}50{,}000
$$

**Sub-part (b) — Compute the developer’s profit and the residual value of the land. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Developer’s Profit (20% of GDV):**
$$
0.20 \times 1{,}25{,}00{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}25{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Total deductions from GDV:**
$$
\text{Total} = 4{,}55{,}50{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}29{,}55{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Residual Land Value:**
$$
V_{\text{land}} = 1{,}25{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 29{,}55{,}50{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹}95{,}44{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Step 4 — Per-plot value (sanity check):**
$$
\frac{95{,}44{,}50{,}000}{50} = \text{₹}1{,}90{,}89{,}000 \text{ per plot} \; \checkmark \text{(Less than GDV ₹25 L, hence reasonable)}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks (Module 2 — Appraisal Techniques):**
> 1. **Mixing YP annuity and YP reversion formulas** — They are *algebraically the same* but represent *different cash-flow directions*. (–4 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to discount the reversion back to year 0** when calculating the deferred perpetuity. (–3 marks)
> 3. **Including land depreciation** in the Cost Approach — Land is *never* depreciated. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Using gross rent instead of net rent** in the Income Approach — Always deduct *vacancy, management, repairs, taxes, and insurance*. (–3 marks)
> 5. **Confusing "perpetuity" and "perpetuity deferred"** YP expressions. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Not stating the *purpose* of valuation** before applying the method — the *purpose* determines the *basis of value*. (–2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Module 2: Appraisal Techniques**

- **Six recognised techniques:** Market, Income (Direct Cap. + DCF), Cost, Residual, Profits, Belting.
- **Years’ Purchase (YP)** is the *heart* of Module 2 — memorise the **six** YP variants in the cheat-sheet table.
- **Perpetuity YP** = $1/i$ ; **Annuity YP** = $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ ; **Deferred perpetuity YP** = $\frac{1}{i(1+i)^t}$.
- **Direct Cap. Formula:** $\text{Value} = \dfrac{\text{NAI}}{i}$ — used for *stable* income properties.
- **DCF Formula:** $V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^t} + \dfrac{\text{CF}_{n+1}}{i_{rev}(1+i)^n}$ — used for *fluctuating* income.
- **Cost Approach:** $\text{Value} = \text{Land} + (\text{RCN} - \text{Total Depreciation})$.
- **Residual Method:** $\text{Land} = \text{GDV} - (\text{All Costs} + \text{Developer's Profit})$.
- **Profits Method:** $\text{Value} = \dfrac{\text{Adjusted Profit}}{\text{ARY}}$; ARY is **higher** than normal yields (8%–10% for hotels).
- **Land is never depreciated** in any method.
- **Net Annual Income (NAI)** = Gross Rent − (Vacancy + Outgoings + Management + Repairs + Insurance + Taxes).
- **Reversion yield** > **Initial yield** (typically by 25–50 basis points).
- **Developer’s profit** in India: 15%–25% of GDV (residential), 18%–22% of GDV (commercial).
- **Purpose of valuation** dictates the method — always state it first.
- **RICS Red Book & IVS 2022** are the **international standard-bearers** — quote them for full marks.
- **Cap rate $i$ is in decimal** (8% = 0.08, not 8).
- **Inwood’s tables** give YP for annuity cases; **Investors’ tables** give the same for single-payment (reversion) cases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Present Value (NPV) by Discounted Cash Flow Method (DCF), Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Net Present Value (NPV) by Discounted Cash Flow Method (DCF), Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Net Present Value (NPV) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Present Value (NPV)** is the algebraic sum of the present worth of **all future cash inflows** (rental income and reversion/sale proceeds) and the **present worth of all future cash outflows** (acquisition cost, development expenditure, periodic outflows) over a defined holding/analysis period, **discounted at an appropriate yield rate (discount rate)** that reflects the time value of money, risk profile, and opportunity cost of capital tied up in the real estate asset.

In the KTU 2024 valuation framework, NPV is the **primary investment decision criterion** under the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) technique. A property is considered financially viable when **NPV ≥ 0** at the chosen discount rate, and **maximization of NPV** is the rational investor's objective when comparing alternative real estate opportunities.

### 1.2 Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the annualised **discount rate at which the present value of all expected future cash inflows exactly equals the present value of all expected cash outflows**, i.e., the rate that drives the NPV of the investment to **zero**. Mathematically, IRR is the solution `r*` of the equation `NPV(r*) = 0`.

IRR is the **true yield** earned by an investor over the holding period on a real estate asset, considering both periodic income (rentals) and capital appreciation (reversion). It is expressed as a **percentage per annum** and is used widely because it allows comparison of investments of differing scales, durations, and risk profiles on a common footing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a fruit-bearing tree. Each year it gives you mangoes (rentals) and at the end of 5 years you cut and sell the wood (reversion). However, money today is *worth more* than the same money tomorrow — a ₹100 note today can be deposited in a bank to earn interest, so ₹100 next year is actually worth less than ₹100 today.

**NPV** answers: *"If I discount every mango and the wood-sale money back to today's value and add them up, do they exceed the price I paid to plant/buy the tree?"* If yes → buy; if no → don't.

**IRR** answers: *"At what rate of return (interest rate) does the present value of all my future mangoes + wood money exactly equal the price I paid for the tree?"* That rate is the true yield your tree is delivering. If the bank offers 9 % and your tree's IRR is 13 %, the tree is the better investment.

### 1.4 The DCF Method — Intuitive Picture

The **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** method is the most rigorous income-capitalisation technique recognised by RICS, IVS, and the KTU valuation syllabus. It explicitly models:
- Year-by-year cash flows (instead of a single year's income).
- The **reversion** (terminal sale value) at the end of the holding period.
- The **time value of money** via mathematical discounting.
- The **risk-adjusted discount rate** specific to the asset class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** The DCF method is *not* an annual rental multiplier; it is a multi-period simulation of an investor's cash flow timeline, brought back to a single present-day number.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Highlighted in KTU Valuation

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Discount Rate / Yield | $r$ | **% per annum** |
| Holding Period | $n$ | **years** |
| Net Operating Income | $\text{NOI}$ | **₹/year** |
| Reversion (Terminal Value) | $R$ | **₹** |
| Net Present Value | $\text{NPV}$ | **₹** |
| Internal Rate of Return | $\text{IRR}$ | **% per annum** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Real Estate Cash Flow Stream

A KTU-standard DCF model assumes the following timeline for a single real estate investment:

1. **Year 0 (t = 0):** Acquisition outlay (purchase price + stamp duty + registration + legal fees + initial repairs). This is a **cash outflow** and is *not* discounted (it is already at present value).
2. **Years 1 to n (periodic cash flows):** Net Operating Income (NOI) each year, computed as:
   $$\text{NOI}_t = \text{Gross Rental Income}_t - \text{Operating Expenses}_t - \text{Vacancy \& Bad-debt Allowance}_t$$
3. **Year n (Reversion):** Sale price of the property net of selling expenses (brokerage, disposal cost).

### 2.2 Logical Steps in the DCF / NPV Procedure

The KTU Board Examiner expects the following sequential logic. Each step has a *why* and a *how*:

- **Step 1 — Estimate Gross Income (GI):** Project market rent × lettable area for each year. *Why:* Income determines return. *How:* Use comparable rental evidence (CIR, RICS Red Book).
- **Step 2 — Deduct Operating Expenses:** Property tax, insurance, repairs, management charges, sinking-fund provisions. *Why:* Investor receives only the *net* amount.
- **Step 3 — Compute NOI:** The cash flow available for debt service and equity return.
- **Step 4 — Project Holding Period (n):** Usually 5, 7, 10, or 15 years. *Why:* The investor will eventually sell.
- **Step 5 — Estimate Reversion (R):** The expected future sale price at year `n`, derived from next-year NOI divided by an **exit yield** $Y_e$ (the All-Risks Yield at exit). *Why:* Capital value is the capitalised future income.
- **Step 6 — Choose Discount Rate (r):** Reflects risk-free rate + property risk premium + illiquidity premium + growth assumptions. *Why:* Money has a time-value; risk demands compensation.
- **Step 7 — Discount all cash flows to Present Value (PV):** Apply the discount factor $\frac{1}{(1+r)^t}$.
- **Step 8 — Sum and Subtract Initial Outlay:** $\text{NPV} = \sum \text{PV(inflows)} - \text{Initial Investment}$.

### 2.3 The Core NPV Equation

The most general KTU-grade NPV equation for a real property with variable cash flows is:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{NOI}_t}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{R_n}{(1+r)^n} - C_0$$

where $C_0$ is the initial cash outlay at $t = 0$, and $R_n$ is the reversion at year $n$.

For a **constant NOI** scenario where $\text{NOI}_t = A$ (an annuity), this simplifies using the **Present Worth of Annuity Factor (PWAF)**:

$$\text{NPV} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^n} + \frac{R_n}{(1+r)^n} - C_0$$

### 2.4 The Reversion Equation

The future sale value of the property is almost always capitalised from the **next-year NOI** at the **exit yield** $Y_e$:

$$R_n = \frac{\text{NOI}_{n+1}}{Y_e}$$

This embodies the *in-perpetuity* nature of real estate ownership — the next buyer effectively buys a perpetual income stream capitalised at the market's prevailing yield at the time of exit.

### 2.5 The IRR Equation

IRR is the root $r = \text{IRR}$ of:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{NOI}_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} + \frac{R_n}{(1+\text{IRR})^n} - C_0 = 0$$

This is a **polynomial of degree $n$** and generally has no closed-form solution for $n \geq 2$. Therefore, KTU students use:
- **Trial-and-Error / Tabular method**, or
- **Linear Interpolation** between two bounding rates.

### 2.6 The Linear Interpolation Formula (KTU Favourite)

If $r_1$ and $r_2$ are two trial rates giving NPVs of opposite sign ($\text{NPV}_1 > 0$ and $\text{NPV}_2 < 0$):

$$\text{IRR} \approx r_1 + \frac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2} \cdot (r_2 - r_1)$$

This is derived from the **secant method** of root-finding, assuming a linear NPV-vs-rate relationship between $r_1$ and $r_2$ (a reasonable approximation when $r_2 - r_1$ is small, typically ≤ 2 %).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{NOI}_t}{(1+r)^t} + \dfrac{R_n}{(1+r)^n} - C_0$ | General NPV for variable NOI | Default in DCF problems |
| 2 | $\text{NPV} = A \cdot \text{PWAF}(r,n) + \dfrac{R_n}{(1+r)^n} - C_0$ | NPV for constant NOI $A$ | When NOI is uniform |
| 3 | $\text{PWAF}(r,n) = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ | Present Worth Annuity Factor | Annuity discount |
| 4 | $\text{YP}_n = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}$ | Years' Purchase (in perpetuity equivalent) | Single-year cap for `n`-year income |
| 5 | $R_n = \dfrac{\text{NOI}_{n+1}}{Y_e}$ | Reversion via exit yield | All reversion calculations |
| 6 | $\text{SPPWF} = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ | Single Payment Present Worth Factor | Lump-sum discount |
| 7 | $\text{IRR} \approx r_1 + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2}(r_2 - r_1)$ | Linear interpolation IRR | Trial-and-error problems |
| 8 | $\text{NPV}(r=\text{IRR}) = 0$ | Defining equation of IRR | Conceptual questions |
| 9 | $\text{PV} = \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | Present value of future sum $F$ | Reversion discount |
| 10 | $r_{\text{real}} \approx r_{\text{nominal}} - g$ | Approx. real discount rate | Inflation-adjusted DCF (advanced) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Discipline (KTU Examiner's Eye):** All monetary values in the same currency unit (₹ or ₹ lakhs). All discount rates in **% p.a.** compounded **annually** unless stated otherwise. The KTU Board does **not** use monthly compounding in valuation problems.

### 2.8 Engineering / Real-Estate Utility

DCF-NPV and IRR are used by:
- **REITs and institutional investors** to value Grade-A office and commercial portfolios.
- **Banks (HFCs and NBFCs)** in mortgage underwriting and loan-to-value analysis.
- **Government agencies** (NBCC, CPWD, state housing boards) for build-operate-transfer (BOT) and PPP project appraisal.
- **Individual investors** evaluating rental residential and commercial purchases.
- **Tax authorities** in estate duty and capital gains computations where the **discounted future earnings** method is adopted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Present Value of a Single Future Cash Flow

Consider a sum $F$ receivable after $n$ years. If the prevailing interest (discount) rate is $r$ per annum, the present value $\text{PV}$ is the amount which, if invested today at rate $r$, would grow to $F$ in $n$ years.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Future Value Equation:} \quad F &= \text{PV} \cdot (1+r)^n \\[4pt]
\text{Solve for PV:} \quad \text{PV} &= \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic (text row 1):** Money grows geometrically at rate $r$; therefore the *inverse* operation brings future money to the present.

**Conversion logic (text row 2):** The factor $(1+r)^{-n}$ is the **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)**, tabulated in KTU-recommended valuation tables.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Present Value of a Constant Annuity (Years' Purchase)

Consider a constant annual income $A$ receivable at the **end** of each year for $n$ years, with discount rate $r$. The total present value is the sum of `n` discounted terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV} &= \frac{A}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+r)^3} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+r)^n} \\[4pt]
\text{PV} &= A \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+r)^t} \\[4pt]
\text{PV} &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \quad \text{(geometric series identity)} \\[4pt]
\text{PV} &= A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^n} \\[4pt]
\text{PV} &= A \cdot \text{PWAF}(r,n)
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic (text row 1):** Each year's income is brought to present value individually.

**Conversion logic (text row 2):** Factor out the common constant $A$ to reveal a geometric series.

**Conversion logic (text row 3):** Use the identity $\sum_{t=1}^{n} x^t = \dfrac{x(1-x^n)}{1-x}$ with $x = (1+r)^{-1}$.

**Conversion logic (text row 4):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+r)^n$ to obtain the standard textbook form.

**Conversion logic (text row 5):** Recognise the result as the *Present Worth Annuity Factor* $\text{PWAF}(r,n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Annuity factors assume **end-of-year** receipts (ordinary annuity). If rentals are received **monthly** or **in advance**, the factor must be modified. KTU problems default to **annual, end-of-year** unless specified.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reversion Capitalisation

The terminal sale value at the end of year $n$, denoted $R_n$, is what the next buyer will pay. The next buyer capitalises the **next year's** NOI at the prevailing market yield $Y_e$ (in perpetuity):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \text{NOIn} \times \text{YP}_\infty \quad \text{(at the exit yield)} \\[4pt]
\text{YP}_\infty \text{ at rate } Y_e &= \frac{1}{Y_e} \quad \text{(perpetuity)} \\[4pt]
\therefore R_n &= \frac{\text{NOI}_{n+1}}{Y_e}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic (text row 1):** A perpetuity's value is income divided by yield.

**Conversion logic (text row 2):** The Years' Purchase in perpetuity is $1/Y_e$ (e.g., 10 % yield → YP = 10).

**Conversion logic (text row 3):** Substitute and obtain the reversion formula.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Linear Interpolation for IRR

We assume that within a small interval $[r_1, r_2]$, the function $\text{NPV}(r)$ behaves approximately linearly. Then, by similar triangles:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR} - r_1 &= \frac{0 - \text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_2 - \text{NPV}_1} \cdot (r_2 - r_1) \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= r_1 + \frac{-\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_2 - \text{NPV}_1} \cdot (r_2 - r_1) \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= r_1 + \frac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2} \cdot (r_2 - r_1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic (text row 1):** Set up the linear-interpolation ratio of NPV changes to rate changes.

**Conversion logic (text row 2):** Solve for the unknown `IRR` (the rate at which NPV = 0).

**Conversion logic (text row 3):** Rearrange signs to the standard textbook form.

> [!WARNING]
> The interpolation is **only an approximation**. For high-accuracy KTU problems, keep the trial rate gap $r_2 - r_1 \leq 2$ % p.a. to minimise curvature error.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — NPV by DCF

**Problem Statement (Model Question):** A property is being acquired for **₹ 50,00,000** (inclusive of acquisition costs). The expected Net Operating Income for Year 1 is **₹ 4,00,000** and is projected to grow at **5 % per annum** for 5 years. The expected sale price at the end of Year 5 (reversion) is **₹ 65,00,000**. Using a discount rate of **12 % p.a.**, determine the NPV. Advise the investor.

**Solution — Step 1: Project the NOI for each year.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NOI}_1 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 1.05 = 4{,}20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_3 &= 4{,}20{,}000 \times 1.05 = 4{,}41{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_4 &= 4{,}41{,}000 \times 1.05 = 4{,}63{,}050 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_5 &= 4{,}63{,}050 \times 1.05 = 4{,}86{,}202.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute the discount factors at 12 % p.a.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \\[4pt]
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\[4pt]
(1.12)^3 &= 1.404928 \\[4pt]
(1.12)^4 &= 1.57351936 \\[4pt]
(1.12)^5 &= 1.7623416832
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute the Present Value of each year's NOI.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_1) &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.1200} = 3{,}57{,}142.86 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_2) &= \frac{4{,}20{,}000}{1.2544} = 3{,}34{,}895.41 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_3) &= \frac{4{,}41{,}000}{1.404928} = 3{,}13{,}883.29 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_4) &= \frac{4{,}63{,}050}{1.57351936} = 2{,}94{,}266.00 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_5) &= \frac{4{,}86{,}202.50}{1.7623416832} = 2{,}75{,}887.96
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 4: Compute the Present Value of the Reversion.**

$$\text{PV}(R_5) = \frac{65{,}00{,}000}{1.7623416832} = 36{,}88{,}038.27$$

**Solution — Step 5: Sum the present values of inflows and subtract initial investment.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total PV of inflows} &= 3{,}57{,}142.86 + 3{,}34{,}895.41 + 3{,}13{,}883.29 + 2{,}94{,}266.00 + 2{,}75{,}887.96 + 36{,}88{,}038.27 \\[4pt]
&= 52{,}64{,}113.79 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV} &= 52{,}64{,}113.79 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV} &= 2{,}64{,}113.79
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** NPV is positive (₹ 2,64,114 approximately). The investment is **financially viable** at a 12 % discount rate. The investor should proceed.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — IRR by Linear Interpolation

**Continuing the same example**, we already have:

- At $r_1 = 12$ %: $\text{NPV}_1 = + 2{,}64{,}113.79$
- We need to find a rate $r_2$ at which NPV becomes negative. Try $r_2 = 14$ %.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute discount factors at 14 % p.a.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.14)^1 &= 1.1400 \\[4pt]
(1.14)^2 &= 1.2996 \\[4pt]
(1.14)^3 &= 1.481544 \\[4pt]
(1.14)^4 &= 1.68896016 \\[4pt]
(1.14)^5 &= 1.9254145824
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute the Present Value of NOIs and Reversion at 14 %.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_1) &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.1400} = 3{,}50{,}877.19 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_2) &= \frac{4{,}20{,}000}{1.2996} = 3{,}23{,}176.36 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_3) &= \frac{4{,}41{,}000}{1.481544} = 2{,}97{,}655.91 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_4) &= \frac{4{,}63{,}050}{1.68896016} = 2{,}74{,}147.94 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_5) &= \frac{4{,}86{,}202.50}{1.9254145824} = 2{,}52{,}517.27 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(R_5) &= \frac{65{,}00{,}000}{1.9254145824} = 33{,}76{,}094.31
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute NPV at 14 %.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total PV at 14\%} &= 3{,}50{,}877.19 + 3{,}23{,}176.36 + 2{,}97{,}655.91 + 2{,}74{,}147.94 + 2{,}52{,}517.27 + 33{,}76{,}094.31 \\[4pt]
&= 48{,}74{,}468.98 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV}_2 &= 48{,}74{,}468.98 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= -1{,}25{,}531.02
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 4: Apply Linear Interpolation Formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR} &= r_1 + \frac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2} \cdot (r_2 - r_1) \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= 12 + \frac{2{,}64{,}113.79}{2{,}64{,}113.79 - (-1{,}25{,}531.02)} \cdot (14 - 12) \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= 12 + \frac{2{,}64{,}113.79}{3{,}89{,}644.81} \cdot 2 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= 12 + (0.67788) \cdot 2 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= 12 + 1.3558 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &\approx 13.36 \text{ \% p.a.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The property delivers an **IRR of approximately 13.36 % p.a.**, which is **greater than** the investor's required yield of 12 %. Therefore, the deal is **acceptable**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DCF / NPV Valuation Flowchart

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** depicting the procedural sequence of a KTU-grade DCF valuation, including feedback loops and the IRR branch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Property Identification] --> A1[Step 1: Collect Market Data - Rents, Vacancy, Expenses]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Estimate Gross Income Each Year]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Deduct Operating Expenses]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Compute NOI_t for t = 1 to n]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Define Holding Period n years]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Estimate Reversion R_n via Exit Yield Y_e]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Select Discount Rate r - Risk adjusted]
    A7 --> B1[Step 8: Compute PV of NOI and Reversion]
    B1 --> B2[Step 9: Sum PV inflows - Subtract C_0]
    B2 --> C1{NPV Decision}
    C1 --> C2[NPV > 0: ACCEPT Investment]
    C1 --> C3[NPV = 0: INDIFFERENT]
    C1 --> C4[NPV < 0: REJECT Investment]
    B2 --> D1[Parallel Branch: Compute IRR]
    D1 --> D2[Try rate r_1: NPV positive]
    D2 --> D3[Try rate r_2: NPV negative]
    D3 --> D4[Apply Linear Interpolation]
    D4 --> D5[Output: IRR percentage]
    D5 --> E1{IRR Decision}
    E1 --> E2[IRR > MARR: ACCEPT]
    E1 --> E3[IRR = MARR: INDIFFERENT]
    E1 --> E4[IRR < MARR: REJECT]
```

### 4.2 Cash Flow Timeline Diagram

The following Mermaid block visualises the temporal positioning of cash flows along the time axis, which is essential for understanding the NPV discounting logic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Year 0: t = 0]:::neg -->|Outflow C0 downward| N1[Year 1: NOI1]
    N1 -->|Discount at r| N2[Year 2: NOI2]
    N2 --> N3[Year 3: NOI3]
    N3 --> N4[Year 4: NOI4]
    N4 --> N5[Year 5: NOI5 plus Reversion R5]
    N5 -->|Discount all back| PV[Sum of Present Values]
    PV --> NPV[NPV = PV inflows - C0]
    classDef neg fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Discounted Cash Flow Decision Matrix

| Condition on NPV | Condition on IRR | Investment Decision | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| NPV > 0 at hurdle rate | IRR > hurdle rate (MARR) | **ACCEPT** | Adds value above opportunity cost |
| NPV = 0 at hurdle rate | IRR = hurdle rate | **INDIFFERENT** | Break-even; meets but does not exceed target |
| NPV < 0 at hurdle rate | IRR < hurdle rate | **REJECT** | Destroys value; not worth the capital tie-up |
| Mutually exclusive projects | Choose the one with **highest NPV** (for value-maximising investor) | **ACCEPT larger NPV** | NPV is the superior ranking criterion for scale-different projects |
| Mutually exclusive projects | Choose the one with **highest IRR** (only for identical scale and timing) | **ACCEPT larger IRR** | IRR is acceptable as a *first screen* only |

### 4.4 NPV-vs-Rate Profile (Conceptual Graph)

The following ASCII diagram illustrates the **characteristic shape of the NPV function** versus discount rate — a key conceptual requirement for IRR questions.

```
 NPV (₹)
   |                                          NPV = 0  ←── IRR (root)
   |                                        *
   |                                      *
   |                                    *
   |                                 *
   |                              *
   |                          *
   |                     *
   |                *
   |          *
   |     *
   | *
   |*
   +-------------------------------------------------------> r (%)
        5%   8%   10%   12%   13.36%   14%   16%
                                          ↑
                                     IRR point
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the graph:** NPV starts high (low discount rate means future cash is valued near its face value) and decreases monotonically as $r$ increases. The point where the curve crosses the horizontal axis is the **IRR**. Beyond the IRR, NPV becomes negative, meaning the discount rate is so high that the property no longer covers its cost.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Net Present Value (NPV) in the context of real property valuation. State the decision rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Net Present Value is the difference between the present worth of all future cash inflows (net rental income and reversion) and the present worth of all future cash outflows (acquisition cost and development expenditure), discounted at an appropriate yield rate that reflects the time value of money and risk. The decision rule is:

- **Accept** the property investment if **NPV ≥ 0**.
- **Reject** the property investment if **NPV < 0**.

When comparing mutually exclusive projects, the alternative with the **highest NPV** is preferred, as it maximises wealth addition to the investor.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of a real estate investment? How is it computed using linear interpolation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Internal Rate of Return is the annualised discount rate at which the present value of all expected future cash inflows (rentals and reversion) equals the present value of all cash outflows (acquisition cost). In other words, it is the rate that makes the NPV of the investment exactly **zero**.

It is the root of the equation:

$$\text{NPV}(r) = 0$$

Because the NPV function is a polynomial of degree equal to the holding period, IRR has no simple closed-form solution and is generally computed by the **linear interpolation method** between two trial rates $r_1$ and $r_2$ giving NPVs of opposite sign:

$$\text{IRR} \approx r_1 + \frac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2} \cdot (r_2 - r_1)$$

The smaller the gap between $r_1$ and $r_2$, the more accurate the estimate.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** A commercial property is offered for sale at **₹ 75,00,000**. The expected Net Operating Income (NOI) is **₹ 6,00,000 in Year 1** and is expected to grow uniformly at **6 % per annum** for **6 years**. The estimated sale value at the end of Year 6 is **₹ 95,00,000**. Determine the **NPV** using a discount rate of **13 % p.a.**, and state whether the investor should buy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the same data, determine the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of the investment by linear interpolation. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — NPV Calculation**

**Step 1: Project NOI for years 1 to 6.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NOI}_1 &= 6{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_2 &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 1.06 = 6{,}36{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_3 &= 6{,}36{,}000 \times 1.06 = 6{,}74{,}160 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_4 &= 6{,}74{,}160 \times 1.06 = 7{,}14{,}609.60 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_5 &= 7{,}14{,}609.60 \times 1.06 = 7{,}57{,}486.18 \\[4pt]
\text{NOI}_6 &= 7{,}57{,}486.18 \times 1.06 = 8{,}02{,}935.35
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute discount factors at 13 % p.a.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.13)^1 &= 1.1300 \\[4pt]
(1.13)^2 &= 1.2769 \\[4pt]
(1.13)^3 &= 1.442897 \\[4pt]
(1.13)^4 &= 1.63047361 \\[4pt]
(1.13)^5 &= 1.84243518 \\[4pt]
(1.13)^6 &= 2.08195175
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute PV of each year's NOI.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_1) &= 6{,}00{,}000 / 1.1300 = 5{,}30{,}973.45 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_2) &= 6{,}36{,}000 / 1.2769 = 4{,}98{,}080.20 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_3) &= 6{,}74{,}160 / 1.442897 = 4{,}67{,}212.07 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_4) &= 7{,}14{,}609.60 / 1.63047361 = 4{,}38{,}218.06 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_5) &= 7{,}57{,}486.18 / 1.84243518 = 4{,}11{,}04.95 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_6) &= 8{,}02{,}935.35 / 2.08195175 = 3{,}85{,}672.65
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute PV of Reversion.**

$$\text{PV}(R_6) = 95{,}00{,}000 / 2.08195175 = 45{,}63{,}081.84$$

**Step 5: Sum and Compute NPV.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total PV of inflows} &= 5{,}30{,}973.45 + 4{,}98{,}080.20 + 4{,}67{,}212.07 + 4{,}38{,}218.06 + 4{,}11{,}04.95 + 3{,}85{,}672.65 + 45{,}63{,}081.84 \\[4pt]
&\approx 72{,}93{,}343.22 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV} &= 72{,}93{,}343.22 - 75{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV} &\approx -2{,}06{,}656.78
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion for Part (a):** NPV is **negative** (₹ −2,06,657). The investment is **not viable** at 13 % discount rate. The investor should **not buy** at the asking price.

---

**Part (b) — IRR Calculation**

Since at 13 % the NPV is negative, we already have a candidate $r_2 = 13$ % with $\text{NPV}_2 \approx -2{,}06{,}657$. Try a lower rate, $r_1 = 10$ %.

**Step 1: Discount factors at 10 % p.a.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^1 &= 1.10 \\[4pt]
(1.10)^2 &= 1.21 \\[4pt]
(1.10)^3 &= 1.331 \\[4pt]
(1.10)^4 &= 1.4641 \\[4pt]
(1.10)^5 &= 1.61051 \\[4pt]
(1.10)^6 &= 1.771561
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: PVs at 10 %.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_1) &= 6{,}00{,}000 / 1.10 = 5{,}45{,}454.55 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_2) &= 6{,}36{,}000 / 1.21 = 5{,}25{,}619.83 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_3) &= 6{,}74{,}160 / 1.331 = 5{,}06{,}506.39 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_4) &= 7{,}14{,}609.60 / 1.4641 = 4{,}88{,}085.86 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_5) &= 7{,}57{,}486.18 / 1.61051 = 4{,}70{,}331.05 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(\text{NOI}_6) &= 8{,}02{,}935.35 / 1.771561 = 4{,}53{,}194.41 \\[4pt]
\text{PV}(R_6) &= 95{,}00{,}000 / 1.771561 = 53{,}62{,}690.07
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Total PV and NPV at 10 %.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total PV at 10\%} &\approx 5{,}45{,}454.55 + 5{,}25{,}619.83 + 5{,}06{,}506.39 + 4{,}88{,}085.86 + 4{,}70{,}331.05 + 4{,}53{,}194.41 + 53{,}62{,}690.07 \\[4pt]
&\approx 83{,}51{,}882.16 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV}_1 &\approx 83{,}51{,}882.16 - 75{,}00{,}000 = +8{,}51{,}882.16
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply Linear Interpolation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR} &\approx r_1 + \frac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2} \cdot (r_2 - r_1) \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &\approx 10 + \frac{8{,}51{,}882.16}{8{,}51{,}882.16 - (-2{,}06{,}656.78)} \cdot (13 - 10) \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &\approx 10 + \frac{8{,}51{,}882.16}{10{,}58{,}538.94} \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &\approx 10 + 0.80495 \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &\approx 10 + 2.4149 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &\approx 12.41 \text{ \% p.a.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion for Part (b):** The IRR is approximately **12.41 % p.a.** If the investor's **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) is 13 %**, then the deal is **not acceptable** (IRR < MARR). The NPV-based conclusion of Part (a) is therefore corroborated by the IRR analysis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) method** of property valuation. List its assumptions, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential flat is available for **₹ 60,00,000**. The annual rent is **₹ 4,80,000**, expected to remain constant for **5 years**, with annual expenses at **20 % of the gross rent**. The expected resale value at the end of Year 5 is **₹ 78,00,000**. Find the **NPV** at a discount rate of **11 % p.a.** and determine the **IRR** by trial and interpolation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — DCF Method — Conceptual Framework**

> [!NOTE]
> The DCF method, in its rigorous form, is the present-value-of-future-cash-flows technique. It explicitly models the *time* and *risk* dimensions of a real estate investment.

**Assumptions of the DCF method:**

1. Cash flows occur at **discrete intervals** (typically annually, end-of-year).
2. The **discount rate (yield)** is constant over the holding period and is commensurate with the risk of the property class.
3. The investor has a **finite holding period** $n$, after which the property is sold (reversion).
4. Cash flows are **deterministic** (point estimates) or, in advanced models, are replaced by **expected values** under uncertainty.
5. The capital market is **efficient** in the sense that the discount rate correctly prices time and risk.

**Advantages of the DCF method:**

- It is **theoretically rigorous** and aligns with the modern finance principle of time value of money.
- It explicitly incorporates the **reversion value** and the **holding period**, unlike the direct-capitalisation (rent multiplier) method.
- It allows **year-by-year modelling** of income and expense variations (rent escalations, vacancy, capital expenditure cycles).
- It accommodates **non-uniform cash flows** and **growth assumptions**.
- It is the **internationally accepted standard** (RICS, IVS) for income-property valuation.

**Limitations of the DCF method:**

- The output is **highly sensitive to the discount rate** chosen — small changes in $r$ produce large changes in NPV.
- Estimating the **future sale price (reversion)** is itself a forecasting exercise and carries significant uncertainty.
- It requires **detailed input data** (rents, vacancy, expenses, growth rates) which may not be readily available for all property categories.
- It is a **single-scenario model** in its basic form; risk and uncertainty need to be addressed through Monte Carlo simulation or sensitivity analysis.
- It assumes a **rational, single-objective investor** (wealth maximisation) and may not capture behavioural or strategic factors.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Compute Net Income (NOI) per year.**

Annual gross rent = ₹ 4,80,000. Expenses = 20 % × 4,80,000 = ₹ 96,000. Therefore, NOI per year:

$$A = 4{,}80{,}000 - 96{,}000 = 3{,}84{,}000 \text{ per year (constant)}$$

**Step 2: Compute discount factors at 11 % p.a.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.11)^1 &= 1.11 \\[4pt]
(1.11)^2 &= 1.2321 \\[4pt]
(1.11)^3 &= 1.367631 \\[4pt]
(1.11)^4 &= 1.51807041 \\[4pt]
(1.11)^5 &= 1.68505816
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute PV of NOI (using annuity factor, since constant).**

$$\text{PWAF}(11\%, 5) = \frac{1.11^5 - 1}{0.11 \times 1.11^5} = \frac{0.685058}{0.185356} = 3.69590$$

$$\text{PV of NOI inflows} = 3{,}84{,}000 \times 3.69590 = 14{,}19{,}225.60$$

**Step 4: Compute PV of Reversion.**

$$\text{PV}(R_5) = 78{,}00{,}000 / 1.68505816 = 46{,}28{,}853.74$$

**Step 5: Compute NPV at 11 %.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total PV of inflows} &= 14{,}19{,}225.60 + 46{,}28{,}853.74 = 60{,}48{,}079.34 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV} &= 60{,}48{,}079.34 - 60{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV} &\approx +48{,}079.34
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** NPV is **marginally positive** at 11 % discount rate. The investment is just viable; investor may proceed with caution.

**Step 6: Determine IRR by trial and interpolation.**

We need a rate at which NPV becomes negative. Try $r_2 = 12$ %.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PWAF}(12\%, 5) &= \frac{1.12^5 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.12^5} = \frac{0.762342}{0.211481} = 3.60478 \\[4pt]
\text{PV of NOI at 12\%} &= 3{,}84{,}000 \times 3.60478 = 13{,}84{,}235.52 \\[4pt]
\text{PV of Reversion at 12\%} &= 78{,}00{,}000 / 1.762342 = 44{,}26{,}024.36 \\[4pt]
\text{Total PV at 12\%} &= 13{,}84{,}235.52 + 44{,}26{,}024.36 = 58{,}10{,}259.88 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV}_2 &= 58{,}10{,}259.88 - 60{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}89{,}740.12
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Apply Linear Interpolation** (using $r_1 = 11$ %, $\text{NPV}_1 = +48{,}079.34$ and $r_2 = 12$ %, $\text{NPV}_2 = -1{,}89{,}740.12$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR} &= 11 + \frac{48{,}079.34}{48{,}079.34 - (-1{,}89{,}740.12)} \cdot (12 - 11) \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= 11 + \frac{48{,}079.34}{2{,}37{,}819.46} \cdot 1 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &= 11 + 0.2022 \\[4pt]
\text{IRR} &\approx 11.20 \text{ \% p.a.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion for Part (b):** The property delivers an **IRR of approximately 11.20 % p.a.**, which is just above the 11 % discount rate, confirming the marginal viability of the deal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to discount the reversion** at the same rate as the cash flows. The reversion is a future lump sum; it MUST be discounted by $(1+r)^n$.
> 2. **Confusing the discount rate with the growth rate.** Many students apply the growth rate (5–6 %) where the discount rate (12–14 %) should be used. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Using the wrong year for reversion capitalisation.** Reversion at year `n` uses the discount factor $(1+r)^n$, **not** $(1+r)^{n-1}$ or $(1+r)^{n+1}$.
> 4. **Sign errors in interpolation.** The denominator of the interpolation formula is $\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2$ (not $\text{NPV}_1 + \text{NPV}_2$).
> 5. **Omitting acquisition/stamp duty/registration costs** from the initial outlay $C_0$. If the question states "inclusive of all costs," use that figure; otherwise, add 6–10 % of purchase price for Indian stamp duty and registration.
> 6. **Compound growth misapplication.** NOI growing at $g$ % for $t$ years gives $\text{NOI}_t = \text{NOI}_1 (1+g)^{t-1}$ (not $(1+g)^t$ — Year 1 is the base).
> 7. **Not stating units** of the final NPV (₹ lakhs vs. ₹) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for ambiguous currency units.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Board-Exam Quick Recall):**

- **NPV** = Σ PV(inflows) − Initial Outlay $C_0$. A **positive NPV** means the property generates value above the hurdle rate and should be **acquired**.
- **IRR** is the discount rate that makes NPV = 0. It is the **true yield** of the property; compare it with the **MARR** (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).
- **DCF = multi-period, time-value-aware.** Don't confuse it with the direct capitalisation (rent multiplier) method, which is single-period.
- **Annuity Factor formula:** $\text{PWAF}(r,n) = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ — use when NOI is **constant**.
- **Variable NOI:** Discount each year's NOI individually and sum — use when NOI grows or changes.
- **Reversion $R_n$** at year `n` is discounted by $(1+r)^n$, NOT by $(1+r)^{n-1}$ or $(1+r)^{n+1}$.
- **Linear Interpolation** works best when $r_2 - r_1 \leq 2$ %. Use the formula: $\text{IRR} = r_1 + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2}(r_2 - r_1)$.
- **Decision Rule (NPV):** Accept if NPV ≥ 0; Reject if NPV < 0.
- **Decision Rule (IRR):** Accept if IRR ≥ MARR; Reject if IRR < MARR.
- **For mutually exclusive projects**, use **NPV (not IRR)** as the primary ranking criterion because IRR ignores the *scale* of investment.
- **Sensitivity:** A change of 1 % in the discount rate can shift NPV by 5–10 % — always test sensitivity in valuation reports.
- **Units:** Discount rate in **% p.a.**, NOI in **₹/year**, Reversion in **₹**, NPV in **₹**, IRR in **% p.a.**.
- **KTU default:** Annual, end-of-year cash flows; annual compounding; no monthly adjustments.
- **Comparison with Rent Multiplier:** DCF captures *time* and *risk* explicitly; rent multiplier is a *single-period* proxy and is unsuitable for income-producing commercial properties.
- **Real Discount Rate (advanced):** $r_{\text{real}} \approx r_{\text{nominal}} - g_{\text{inflation}}$; use when cash flows are quoted in constant (real) terms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Numerical Examples" in Valuation?

In the context of **PECET862 – Valuation of Real Properties (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, "Numerical Examples" refers to the **quantitative, arithmetic problem-solving exercises** that translate theoretical valuation principles (rental method, direct comparison, annuity, depreciation, belting, etc.) into computable answers involving **Years' Purchase (YP)**, **Annual Rent (AR)**, **Sinking Fund (SF)**, **Capitalized Value (CV)**, **Depreciated Value (DV)**, and **Land–Building allocation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Numerical valuation* is the application of standardized financial-mathematical formulae to estimate the **Present-Day Market Value (PDMV)**, **Capitalized Value**, or **Book Value** of a real property, by combining its **Net Annual Income (NAI)** with a **Years' Purchase factor** derived from the property's **economic life**, **rate of interest**, and **sinking-fund contribution**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you own a small shop that gives you **₹1,00,000 net profit every year** (after paying taxes, electricity, staff). If a buyer asks *"How much will you take for the entire shop as a going concern?"*, you cannot just multiply by 10 or 20 — you must consider:

1. **How many years will the shop keep earning?** (Life of building)
2. **What return does the buyer want on his capital?** (Interest rate)
3. **Should the buyer also set aside a small amount every year to rebuild the shop when it becomes old?** (Sinking Fund)

The "magic number" that combines all three is called the **Years' Purchase (YP)**. Multiplying the **Net Annual Rent** by this YP gives the **Capitalized Value** — the single lump-sum price a willing buyer should pay today. This is exactly what Module-2 numericals teach you to compute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant (KTU 2024 Standard):** Although interest rates vary, the I.S. 2741 Part IV and most KTU numericals default to a **standard rate of interest i = 9% per annum** (sometimes 6%, 8%, 10% or 12% as specified per problem) when not explicitly stated. The **Sinking Fund coefficient** is read from standard valuation tables (Arora & Rangwala).

### 1.3 Why this topic matters in KTU Exams

Numerical problems form **40–50 % of marks** in Part B questions of PECET862. The examiner expects you to:

- Identify the *correct method* (Rental / Annuity / Depreciation / Direct Comparison).
- Apply the *right formula* with consistent units (years, %, ₹).
- Show the **substitution of values** and **tabular YP-factor usage** clearly.
- Carry units of currency (₹) consistently in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Logic Flow

The valuation pipeline for every Module-2 numerical follows this **five-stage logic**:

1. **Step 1 – Identify the Income Stream**  
   Gross Rent → Less Outgoings (taxes, repairs, insurance, management) = **Net Annual Rent (NAR)**.

2. **Step 2 – Determine Economic Life (n)**  
   Life of the building in years (typically 60–100 for RCC, 40–60 for load-bearing, 20–40 for temporary).

3. **Step 3 – Pick the Capitalization Method**  
   - *Single-Rate YP Method* (no sinking fund)  
   - *Annuity Method* (interest + sinking fund)  
   - *Direct Comparison with known sales*  
   - *Depreciation Method* (for built-up property)

4. **Step 4 – Read YP from Tables / Compute It**  
   For Single-Rate: $YP = \dfrac{1}{i}$ (perpetuity) or $YP = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$ (for limited life with interest only).  
   For Annuity: $YP = \dfrac{1}{i + \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}}$ (or read directly from annuity tables).

5. **Step 5 – Compute Capitalized / Depreciated Value**  
   $CV = NAR \times YP$, or $DV = C - \left( \dfrac{C-S}{n} \right) \times t$, etc.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use the table below as the first-page reference during numerical solving. Memorize the boxed items.**

| # | Method | Governing Formula | Symbol Key | Units |
|---|--------|-------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | **Single-Rate YP (Perpetuity)** | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i}$ | $i$ = rate of interest (decimal) | years |
| 2 | **Single-Rate YP (Limited Life)** | $YP = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$ | $n$ = economic life | years |
| 3 | **Annuity YP** | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i + \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}}$ | combines interest + sinking fund | years |
| 4 | **Capitalized Value** | $CV = NAR \times YP$ | $NAR$ = Net Annual Rent | ₹ |
| 5 | **Sinking Fund Installment** | $SFI = C \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $C$ = capital to be recovered | ₹/yr |
| 6 | **Straight-Line Depreciation (Annual)** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $C$ = original cost, $S$ = scrap value, $n$ = life | ₹/yr |
| 7 | **Book Value after 't' years (SLM)** | $BV = C - D \cdot t$ | $t$ = elapsed years | ₹ |
| 8 | **Declining-Balance Method** | $BV_{n} = C \cdot (1 - r)^{n}$ | $r$ = constant % of BV written off | ₹ |
| 9 | **Land–Building Split by Ratio** | $\text{Land share} = \dfrac{L}{L+B} \times CV$ | $L$, $B$ = guided unit rates | ₹ |
| 10 | **Gross Multiplier (Direct Comparison)** | $Value = \text{Gross Rent} \times \text{GM}$ | $GM$ = Sales $\div$ Gross Rent | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol convention used everywhere below:**  
> $i$ = rate of interest per rupee per year (i.e., 9 % = 0.09)  
> $n$ = total economic life in years  
> $t$ = age already elapsed in years  
> $C$ = original/replacement cost  
> $S$ = scrap/salvage value at end of life

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banks & Housing Finance Companies (HFCs):** Use these exact YP/annuity formulae to decide *loan-to-value* ratios for mortgages.
- **Insurance Companies:** Use Straight-Line and Declining-Balance depreciation to settle fire/structural-damage claims.
- **Government Valuers (PWD, CPWD, KSEB, KWA):** Apply Annuity Method to value long-lease properties.
- **Income-Tax & Stamp Duty Departments:** Use these numericals to test whether the *circle-rate-declared value* matches the *income-capitalized value* — major source of property-tax litigation in Kerala.
