#### Model Solution for (b) – 7 marks

**Step 1: Identify the Type (1 mark)**

This is a classic case of **Economic Obsolescence** because the loss arises from an *external* influence (the chemical plant) that the property owner cannot control.

**Step 2: Calculate Annual Loss (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fair Rent (per month)} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Actual Rent (per month)} &= 75{,}000 \\
\text{Loss per month} &= 25{,}000 \\
\text{Annual Loss } (L) &= 25{,}000 \times 12 = 3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Capitalise the Loss (3 marks)**

Using the Income Capitalisation Method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{eco} &= \frac{L}{r} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{0.10} = 30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**

> The economic obsolescence is **₹30,00,000**, and it is **INCURABLE** because the property owner cannot remove the chemical plant or modify the external environment. The only way to recover value is to redevelop the property for an industrial-compatible use.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Depreciation** in property valuation = Loss in value from **any** cause (physical, functional, or economic).
- [ ] **Functional Obsolescence** is **INTERNAL** — caused by design/layout deficiency of the property itself.
- [ ] **Economical Obsolescence** is **EXTERNAL** — caused by neighbourhood/environmental factors beyond the owner's control.
- [ ] Three sub-types of **Incurable Functional Obsolescence**: curable cost-to-cure, short-lived (EA/TUL), long-lived (PW of rent loss).
- [ ] **Curable** = Cost to cure < Value added. Use $D = C_{cure}$.
- [ ] **Short-lived** = Item's remaining life < Building's remaining life. Use $D = C_{RN} \times (EA / TUL)$.
- [ ] **Long-lived** = Item's remaining life ≥ Building's remaining life. Use $D = Loss \times YP(r, n)$ or Cap. method.
- [ ] **Economic Obsolescence is ALWAYS INCURABLE.** No owner-controlled action can fix the locality.
- [ ] Two methods for **Economic Obsolescence**: (1) **Paired-sales** (% of RCN), (2) **Capitalisation of income loss** (₹ amount).
- [ ] **Cost Approach Final Formula:** $Value = RCN - D_{phys} - D_{func} - D_{eco} + Land\ Value$.
- [ ] **Effective Age (EA)** may exceed **Chronological Age** if there is significant functional obsolescence.
- [ ] **Straight-line depreciation formula** is most common in KTU problems: $D = Cost \times (Age / Total\ Life)$.
- [ ] For KTU 14-mark numericals, always present the **decision logic** (curable/short-lived/long-lived) **before** the formula.
- [ ] The **Present Worth Factor** for $r = 10\%$, $n = 25$ is **9.077** (memorize or compute carefully).
- [ ] **Examiner's Favourite Trick:** They test whether you can distinguish between *physical* (e.g., cracked walls, rusted beams) and *functional* (e.g., outdated kitchen, low ceiling) obsolescence.
- [ ] In income-producing properties, **economic obsolescence** is most commonly measured by the **capitalisation of income loss** method.
- [ ] In owner-occupied residential properties, **economic obsolescence** is most commonly measured by the **paired-sales** method.
- [ ] **Super-adequation** = over-building beyond neighbourhood norms (e.g., 6-BHK in a 2-BHK zone). It is a form of long-lived functional obsolescence.
- [ ] **Inadequacy** = under-capacity (e.g., 1 toilet for a 5-BHK house). Often incurable.
- [ ] Total depreciation in the cost approach is the **sum** of physical, functional, and economic depreciation (in KTU's simplified problems).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obsolescence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Obsolescence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Obsolescence** in real property valuation is defined as a form of **depreciation** (loss in value) caused by factors **external to the property itself** or by **internal inadequacies in the structure, design, layout, or materials** that render the property less desirable, less functional, or less valuable relative to current standards, even though the property may still be physically usable.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECET862 - Valuation of Real Properties**, obsolescence is treated as a distinct head of depreciation, separately quantified from physical wear and tear. The standard classification adopted by valuation practice (and reproduced in the KTU module) recognises three primary heads of depreciation:

$$
\text{Total Depreciation} = \text{Physical Deterioration} + \text{Functional Obsolescence} + \text{Economic (External) Obsolescence}
$$

| Head of Depreciation | Origin | Recoverable? |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Deterioration | Wear & tear, age, weathering | Often Curable |
| **Functional Obsolescence** | **Design, layout, technology, materials inside the property** | **Sometimes Curable** |
| **Economic Obsolescence** | **External factors (location, neighbourhood, market)** | **Almost Always Incurable** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Old Mobile Phone" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you own a perfectly working **Nokia 3310** phone from 2005. The screen is not cracked, the battery holds a full day, and the buttons are crisp — i.e., it has **no physical deterioration**. Yet its resale value in 2025 is almost zero because:
> - It cannot run WhatsApp → **Functional Obsolescence** (inbuilt design is outdated)
> - 2G networks are shut down → **Economic Obsolescence** (external environment killed its utility)
>
> A real property behaves exactly the same way. A 1990s independent house with a charming façade may be structurally sound (no physical decay) but may still lose value because the floor plan is "closed-type" (functional) or because a highway was later built right next to it (economic).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **Replacement Cost (RC)** — Cost to construct an equivalent new property at current prices.
- **Reproduction Cost (RPC)** — Cost to construct an exact replica using original materials/methods.
- **Effective Age (EA)** — The age indicated by the condition of the structure (in years).
- **Economic Life (EL)** — The total useful life of the property (typically **60 years** for RCC structures, **40–80 years** for buildings as per IS 8833 & KTU module norms).
- **Accrued Depreciation (AD)** — Total accumulated depreciation up to the date of valuation.
- All values are normally expressed in **₹ (Indian Rupees)** or **₹/m² (Rate per square metre)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Obsolescence — Structured Logic Breakdown

Obsolescence is **subdivided into two main branches**, and each branch is further split into curable and incurable forms.

### 2.1.1 Functional Obsolescence (Internal Origin)
Caused by **deficiencies within the property** — outdated design, poor layout, old-fashioned fixtures, inadequate plumbing, lack of modern amenities, over-aged materials.

- **Curable Functional Obsolescence** — Defects that can be **remedied economically**. The cost of cure is less than the value added.
  - *Examples:* Replacing old wiring, adding a modular kitchen, installing an elevator, modernising bathrooms.
- **Incurable Functional Obsolescence** — Defects where the **cost of cure exceeds the value added** (or cure is physically impossible without rebuilding).
  - *Examples:* Rooms with insufficient ceiling height (cannot be raised), load-bearing walls obstructing modern open-plan layouts, narrow doorways that cannot be widened.

### 2.1.2 Economic (External / Locational) Obsolescence
Caused by **factors outside the property boundaries** — neighbourhood decline, encroachment, incompatible adjoining uses, traffic, pollution, change in zoning laws, industrial setback.

- **Almost always Incurable** because the cause lies outside the owner's control.
- *Examples:* A luxury bungalow losing value because a sewage treatment plant was built opposite, or a commercial plot losing value because the city’s main market shifted geographically.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Valuation Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind separating Obsolescence from Physical Depreciation:**
> Physical deterioration is calculated using **age-life methods** (Straight Line, Sinking Fund). However, obsolescence is **independent of physical age** — a 2-year-old property may already be obsolete, while a 50-year-old heritage building may not be. Hence a separate estimation technique is required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Depreciation | $D_{total} = D_{physical} + D_{functional} + D_{economic}$ | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | Functional Obsolescence (Cost-Cure) | $FO = C_{cure} - \Delta V$ | $C_{cure}$ = Cost to cure; $\Delta V$ = Value added |
| 3 | Functional Obsolescence (Capitalised Rent Loss) | $FO = \text{Annual Rent Loss} \times \text{Years Purchase (YP)}$ | YP from dual-rate / single-rate tables |
| 4 | Economic Obsolescence (GRM Method) | $EO = \text{GRM}_{\text{old}} - \text{GRM}_{\text{new}} \times \text{Subject Rent}$ | GRM = Gross Rent Multiplier |
| 5 | Economic Obsolescence (% Basis) | $EO = \text{Replacement Cost} \times \%\,\text{EO}$ | % estimated from comparable sales |
| 6 | Obsolescence % of Replacement Cost | $\%\,O = \dfrac{O}{RC} \times 100$ | For reconciliation in valuation report |
| 7 | Effective Age (after obsolescence) | $EA_{adj} = EA_{actual} + EA_{obsolescence\,\,equivalent}$ | Years |
| 8 | Curability Test | Curable if $C_{cure} \leq \Delta V$ | Else Incurable |
| 9 | Incurable Functional Obsolescence | $IFO = \dfrac{(\text{RC}_{\text{new}} - \text{RC}_{\text{as\,is}})}{RC_{\text{new}}} \times RC$ | Based on replacement cost differential |
| 10 | Economic Life of RCC Building | $EL_{RCC} \approx 60$ **years** | KTU module reference value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Years Purchase (YP)** is read from standard valuation tables:
> $$YP_{\text{single rate}} = \frac{1}{r}, \qquad YP_{\text{dual rate}} = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$$
> where $r$ = rate of interest (decimal) and $n$ = remaining economic life (years).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Banks & Mortgage Valuation** — Banks use the cost-cure method to determine the "security value" of a property before sanctioning a home loan.
- **Insurance Valuation** — Insurers require separation of physical vs. functional depreciation to compute accurate indemnity.
- **Property Tax Assessment** — Municipalities (Kerala: LSGD / local bodies) apply functional obsolescence to revise annual valuation of old buildings.
- **Court Valuation** — In land acquisition cases, economic obsolescence is used to compensate loss due to change in neighbourhood character.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Curable Functional Obsolescence (Cost-Cure Method)

> **Problem:** A 25-year-old residential flat in Kochi has outdated single-phase wiring. Replacement Cost (RC) of the flat = **₹ 60,00,000**. Cost to rewire = **₹ 2,50,000**. Estimated value added by rewiring = **₹ 3,00,000**. Compute the Functional Obsolescence (FO) and decide if it is curable.

**Step 1 — Curability Test**
$$
C_{cure} = ₹\,2,50,000, \qquad \Delta V = ₹\,3,00,000
$$

$$
C_{cure} \; (2,50,000) \; \leq \; \Delta V \; (3,00,000) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{CURABLE}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FO (incurable portion = 0)**
$$
FO = C_{cure} - \Delta V
$$

$$
FO = 2,50,000 - 3,00,000
$$

$$
\boxed{FO = -₹\,50,000 \;\; (\text{No obsolescence deduction; in fact a positive net gain})}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation for the Valuation Report**
The deduction for functional obsolescence is **₹ 0** because curing the defect actually **adds** value greater than the cost. The valuer will only verify physical depreciation separately.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Incurable Functional Obsolescence (Replacement Cost Differential)

> **Problem:** A heritage bungalow in Thrissur has 3.0 m ceiling height. Modern buyer preference is 3.6 m. The cost to construct a modern equivalent (RC_new) = **₹ 1,20,00,000**. The cost to construct the as-is structure (RC_as-is) = **₹ 95,00,000**. Compute the Incurable Functional Obsolescence (IFO).

**Step 1 — Recognise the defect**
$$
\Delta RC = RC_{\text{new}} - RC_{\text{as-is}} = 1,20,00,000 - 95,00,000 = ₹\,25,00,000
$$

**Step 2 — Express as a percentage of the new replacement cost**
$$
\%\,IFO = \frac{\Delta RC}{RC_{\text{new}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,IFO = \frac{25,00,000}{1,20,00,000} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,IFO = 20.83\%
$$

**Step 3 — Apply this percentage to the subject property's RC**
For the subject property with RC = ₹ 1,20,00,000:
$$
IFO = 1,20,00,000 \times 0.2083
$$

$$
\boxed{IFO = ₹\,25,00,000}
$$

**Step 4 — Add to total depreciation**
$$
D_{total} = D_{physical} + IFO + EO
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Economic Obsolescence (% Basis)

> **Problem:** A commercial office building in Ernakulam with RC = **₹ 5,00,00,000** faces economic obsolescence because the area has become congested and parking is insufficient. Comparable sales indicate a **15% economic obsolescence**. Compute EO.

**Step 1 — Direct application of % basis**
$$
EO = RC \times \%\,EO
$$

$$
EO = 5,00,00,000 \times 0.15
$$

$$
\boxed{EO = ₹\,75,00,000}
$$

**Step 2 — Final depreciated value**
$$
\text{Depreciated Value} = RC - D_{physical} - FO - EO
$$

Assume $D_{physical} = ₹\,40,00,000$ and $FO = ₹\,10,00,000$:
$$
\text{Depreciated Value} = 5,00,00,000 - 40,00,000 - 10,00,000 - 75,00,000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Depreciated Value} = ₹\,3,75,00,000}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Economic Obsolescence via Capitalised Rent Loss

> **Problem:** A shop in Calicut loses **₹ 50,000 per year** in rent because a flyover construction has reduced footfall. Remaining economic life = **30 years**. Standard YP at 8% interest (dual rate, with 2% sinking fund) = **16.16**. Compute the economic obsolescence.

**Step 1 — Identify the annual loss**
$$
L_{annual} = ₹\,50,000
$$

**Step 2 — Capitalise using Years Purchase (YP)**
$$
EO = L_{annual} \times YP
$$

$$
EO = 50,000 \times 16.16
$$

$$
\boxed{EO = ₹\,8,08,000}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-verify with % method**
$$
\%\,EO = \frac{EO}{RC} \times 100 = \frac{8,08,000}{RC} \times 100
$$

This percentage is then compared with market data for reasonableness.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Deductions

| Property | $D_{phys}$ (₹) | $FO_{curable}$ (₹) | $IFO$ (₹) | $EO$ (₹) | Total $D$ (₹) | Depreciated Value (₹) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Flat, Kochi | 12,00,000 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 12,00,000 | 48,00,000 |
| Bungalow, Thrissur | 18,00,000 | 0 | 25,00,000 | 5,00,000 | 48,00,000 | 72,00,000 |
| Office, Ernakulam | 40,00,000 | 10,00,000 | 0 | 75,00,000 | 1,25,00,000 | 3,75,00,000 |
| Shop, Calicut | 8,00,000 | 0 | 0 | 8,08,000 | 16,08,000 | (RC − 16,08,000) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Obsolescence Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Property Under Valuation] --> B{Check Physical Deterioration}
    A --> C{Check Functional Issues}
    A --> D{Check External Environment}

    B --> B1[Apply Age-Life Method]
    B1 --> B2[D_physical = RC x Age / EL]

    C --> C1[Identify Defect: Design / Layout / Tech]
    C1 --> C2{Curable or Incurable?}
    C2 -- Curable --> C3[Cost-Cure Method]
    C2 -- Incurable --> C4[RC Differential Method]

    D --> D1[Identify External Cause]
    D1 --> D2[GRM Method or Capitalised Rent Loss]
    D1 --> D3[Percent Basis from Comparables]

    B2 --> E[Aggregate All Deductions]
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    E --> F[Depreciated Value = RC - Total D]
    F --> G[Reconcile with Market Value]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Types of Obsolescence

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Obsolescence] --> FO[Functional Obsolescence]
    Root --> EO[Economic Obsolescence]

    FO --> FC[Curable Functional]
    FO --> FI[Incurable Functional]

    FC --> FC1[Outdated Wiring]
    FC --> FC2[Old Plumbing]
    FC --> FC3[Single-phase Power]
    FC --> FC4[Inadequate Sanitary Fittings]

    FI --> FI1[Low Ceiling Height]
    FI --> FI2[Load-Bearing Walls]
    FI --> FI3[Small Room Sizes]
    FI --> FI4[Non-modular Layout]

    EO --> EL[Locational Decline]
    EO --> EN[Neighbourhood Change]
    EO --> EZ[Zoning / Regulation]
    EO --> EP[Pollution / Noise / Traffic]

    EL --> EL1[Shift of Commercial Hub]
    EN --> EN1[Encroachment / Slum Proximity]
    EZ --> EZ1[Change in Land Use]
    EP --> EP1[Highway / Airport / STP Nearby]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic — Curability Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Defect Identified] --> Q1{Cost of Cure known?}
    Q1 -- No --> NotCurable[Classify as Incurable]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Value Added known?}
    Q2 -- No --> NotCurable
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Cost of Cure <= Value Added?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Curable[Curable Obsolescence]
    Q3 -- No --> NotCurable
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for the Exam:** When a KTU question asks "Classify and compute the obsolescence," always follow the **two-step logic** of (1) identifying the type using the classification tree above, and (2) selecting the appropriate formula from the cheat sheet using the decision logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define functional obsolescence. How is it different from physical deterioration?**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Functional obsolescence** is the loss in value of a property due to **defects in design, layout, materials, fixtures, or technology** within the property that make it less desirable compared to current standards (2 marks).
- It is **independent of physical age** — a new property can be functionally obsolete (1 mark).
- **Difference:** Physical deterioration = wear and tear due to use, age, and weathering. Functional obsolescence = outdated design or features, even when the structure is physically sound.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**State the formula for the capitalised rent loss method of computing economic obsolescence. When is it preferred over the percentage method?**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- Formula: $EO = \text{Annual Loss in Rent} \times \text{Years Purchase (YP)}$ (2 marks).
- **Preferred** when the valuer can reliably estimate the actual loss in rental income caused by the external factor and when YP tables are available (1 mark).
- The percentage method is used as a cross-check or when rent data is unavailable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A — (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of obsolescence into curable and incurable forms. Discuss with examples how a valuer would decide whether a given functional defect is curable or incurable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 30-year-old commercial building in Thiruvananthapuram has the following data:
- Replacement Cost (RC) = ₹ 3,00,00,000
- Physical Depreciation already computed = ₹ 60,00,000
- The building lacks a fire-fighting system. Cost to install = ₹ 8,00,000; value added = ₹ 12,00,000.
- An illegal dumping yard has come up 200 m away causing an estimated 10% economic obsolescence.

Compute: (i) Functional Obsolescence, (ii) Economic Obsolescence, and (iii) the Depreciated Value of the building. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Classification (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Curable | Incurable |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Defect can be economically rectified | Defect cannot be economically rectified |
| Cost test | $C_{cure} \leq \Delta V$ | $C_{cure} > \Delta V$ |
| Examples | Old wiring, broken tiles, leaking roof, missing AC | Low ceiling height, load-bearing walls restricting layout, narrow staircase |
| Method | Cost-Cure method | RC differential or capitalised rent loss |
| Owner action | Usually repaired | Reflected as direct deduction in value |

The valuer applies the **two-step test**:
1. Identify the defect and estimate the cost of cure.
2. Estimate the market value added by curing; if cure cost ≤ value added, **curable**; else **incurable**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Functional Obsolescence — Fire-fighting system**
$$
C_{cure} = ₹\,8,00,000, \qquad \Delta V = ₹\,12,00,000
$$
$$
C_{cure} < \Delta V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Curable}
$$
$$
FO = C_{cure} - \Delta V = 8,00,000 - 12,00,000 = -₹\,4,00,000
$$
Since $FO$ is negative, deduction for FO = **₹ 0** (curing is beneficial).

**(ii) Economic Obsolescence**
$$
EO = RC \times \%\,EO = 3,00,00,000 \times 0.10
$$
$$
\boxed{EO = ₹\,30,00,000}
$$

**(iii) Depreciated Value**
$$
\text{DV} = RC - D_{phys} - FO - EO
$$
$$
\text{DV} = 3,00,00,000 - 60,00,000 - 0 - 30,00,000
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{DV} = ₹\,2,10,00,000}
$$

---

### Question B — (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of economic obsolescence. Explain any two methods of estimating it, with their limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential property in Kottayam has:
- Replacement Cost = ₹ 80,00,000
- Effective age = 20 years; Total economic life = 60 years
- The property has 2.7 m ceiling height (modern standard 3.0 m). The cost to construct a modern equivalent is ₹ 95,00,000, while the as-is construction would cost ₹ 78,00,000.
- A new bypass road has diverted traffic, causing an annual rent loss of ₹ 25,000. Remaining life = 40 years. YP (dual rate, 8% interest) = 14.88.

Compute: (i) Incurable Functional Obsolescence, (ii) Economic Obsolescence by capitalised rent loss, and (iii) Depreciated Value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Economic Obsolescence (7 marks)**

Economic obsolescence is the loss in value of a property caused by **factors external to the property** — neighbourhood decline, incompatible land use, infrastructure changes, environmental pollution, or regulatory changes. It is almost always **incurable** because the cause is outside the owner's control.

**Method 1 — Percentage Method:**
$$
EO = RC \times \%\,EO
$$
The percentage is derived from comparable sales of similar properties affected by the same external factor.
*Limitation:* Highly subjective; depends on availability of reliable comparables.

**Method 2 — Capitalised Rent Loss Method:**
$$
EO = L_{annual} \times YP
$$
*Limitation:* Assumes a constant annual rent loss, which may not hold if the external factor worsens or improves over time.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Incurable Functional Obsolescence**
$$
\Delta RC = RC_{\text{new}} - RC_{\text{as-is}} = 95,00,000 - 78,00,000 = ₹\,17,00,000
$$
$$
\%\,IFO = \frac{17,00,000}{95,00,000} \times 100 = 17.89\%
$$
Applying to subject RC = ₹ 80,00,000:
$$
IFO = 80,00,000 \times 0.1789
$$
$$
\boxed{IFO = ₹\,14,31,579 \;(\approx ₹\,14,31,600)}
$$

**(ii) Economic Obsolescence (Capitalised Rent Loss)**
$$
EO = 25,000 \times 14.88
$$
$$
\boxed{EO = ₹\,3,72,000}
$$

**(iii) Depreciated Value**

First compute physical depreciation using Straight Line Method:
$$
D_{phys} = RC \times \frac{EA}{EL} = 80,00,000 \times \frac{20}{60} = ₹\,26,66,667
$$
$$
\text{DV} = RC - D_{phys} - IFO - EO
$$
$$
\text{DV} = 80,00,000 - 26,66,667 - 14,31,600 - 3,72,000
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{DV} = ₹\,35,29,733}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Obsolescence with Physical Depreciation (–2 marks):** Many students merge the two and apply a single age-life formula, ignoring obsolescence. Always quote the **three-head formula**: $D_{total} = D_{phys} + FO + EO$.
> 2. **Skipping the Curability Test (–2 marks):** A numerical problem may *appear* to require a cost-cure answer, but if you forget to test $C_{cure} \leq \Delta V$, you will apply the wrong method.
> 3. **Wrong YP usage (–3 marks):** YP is a **dimensionless multiplier** read from a table; never compute it as $\frac{1}{r}$ for a dual-rate (sinking fund) problem. The correct expression is $YP = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$.
> 4. **Forgetting units (–1 mark):** Always state the final answer in **₹ (Rupees)** and the percentage with **% sign**.
> 5. **No reconciliation with market value (–1 mark):** End every valuation answer with a one-line note: *"The depreciated cost value should be reconciled with the market/comparables for final opinion of value."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition:** Obsolescence = loss in value from **non-physical** causes (design, technology, location).
> - **Three Heads of Depreciation:** Physical + Functional + Economic. Always list all three.
> - **Functional Obsolescence Sub-types:** Curable (cost-cure method) and Incurable (RC differential / capitalised rent loss).
> - **Economic Obsolescence:** Almost always **incurable**; caused by external/environmental factors.
> - **Curability Test:** Compare $C_{cure}$ with $\Delta V$ — if $C_{cure} \leq \Delta V$, the defect is curable.
> - **Key Formulas to Memorise:**
>   - $FO = C_{cure} - \Delta V$
>   - $EO = L_{annual} \times YP$
>   - $EO = RC \times \%\,EO$
>   - $YP_{\text{dual}} = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$
> - **Standard Economic Life:** RCC building ≈ **60 years**; Timber ≈ **40–60 years**; Heritage masonry may exceed **80 years**.
> - **Exam Trick:** If a problem gives "value added > cost of cure," the **FO deduction is zero** (or the cure is recommended).
> - **Common Examples for KTU Theory:** Outdated wiring (curable), low ceiling (incurable), bypass road diversion (EO), slum encroachment (EO), heritage listing restrictions (EO).
> - **Final step in every numerical:** Always compute Depreciated Value and add a one-line reconciliation remark.
> - **Pitfall:** Do not apply the Straight Line Method of depreciation to **obsolescence**; use the appropriate obsolescence formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference between Depreciation and Obsolescence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Difference between Depreciation and Obsolescence ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Depreciation In the context of real property valuation, **Depreciation** is defined as the permanent, irreversible loss in the market value of a property due to physical deterioration caused by the combined effects of: - **Wear and tear** from regular use - **Action of the elements** (weather, moisture, temperature) - **Age** of the structure - **Lack of maintenance** or deferred repairs - **Structural fatigue** in load-bearing elements Mathematically, depreciation is the difference between the **Reproduction Cost New (RCN)** of a building and its **Current Value**, considering only physical factors. $$D_{physical} = RCN - PV_{physical}$$ Where: - $D_{physical}$ = Physical Depreciation - $RCN$ = Reproduction Cost New - $PV_{physical}$ = Present Value after physical deterioration > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Depreciation is *physical* in nature and is generally **irreversible**. It represents what happens to the property **itself** over time. ### 1.2 Formal Definition of Obsolescence **Obsolescence** is the loss in the value of a property due to factors **external to the property itself** but related to its design, functionality, or market environment. It does **not** arise from physical deterioration. Obsolescence is broadly classified into three types: 1. **Functional Obsolescence** — flaws in the design, layout, or amenities of the property itself 2. **Economic (External) Obsolescence** — adverse changes in the surrounding environment (e.g., neighborhood decline) 3. **Locational Obsolescence** — shifts in land use desirability around the property > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition:** "Obsolescence is a cause of depreciation which is not physical — it is a loss in value due to functional, economic, or locational inadequacies." ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **10-year-old Maruti 800 car** parked in a neighborhood: - **Depreciation** = The car has worn-out tyres, faded paint, dented body, and a noisy engine. This is *physical wear and tear* that comes with age and use. The car's value has dropped because of **what happened to it physically**. - **Obsolescence** = The same car is technically fine, but in 2024, everyone wants electric vehicles or BS6-compliant cars. The car has become **functionally outdated** by newer technology. This loss in value is *not* because the car is broken — it is because the **world has moved on**. Alternatively, if the car is parked outside a newly built industrial area where pollution is high, it suffers **economic (locational) obsolescence**. > [!TIP] > **Easy Memory Trick:** "Depreciation happens to the property. Obsolescence happens *because of the world around* the property." ### 1.4 Key Syllabus Metrics in Bold - **Reproduction Cost New (RCN):** The cost to replicate the building exactly as it stands, using the same materials and design, at current prices. - **Replacement Cost New (RCN'):** The cost to build a similar building of equal utility using modern materials and design. - **Effective Age:** The age indicated by the condition of the structure (differs from chronological age when maintenance is poor or excellent). - **Economic Life:** The period over which a property contributes positively to value. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Causes of Depreciation (Physical) Depreciation arises from the following **physical causes**: 1. **Deterioration** — chemical or physical breakdown of materials (e.g., rusting of steel, decay of timber) 2. **Wear and Tear** — mechanical loss from use (e.g., door hinges wearing out) 3. **Neglect / Deferred Maintenance** — failure to repair minor damages leading to compounded decay ### 2.2 Causes of Obsolescence (Non-Physical) Obsolescence is further sub-categorized: | **Type of Obsolescence** | **Cause** | **Example** | **Reversible?** | |---|---|---|---| | Functional (Curable) | Defective design; outdated amenities; inadequate plumbing | A 2-bathroom house in a society where 4-bathroom homes are now standard | Yes, by renovation | | Functional (Incurable) | Structural inadequacy; layout flaws in load-bearing walls | A 1980s house with 8-foot ceilings in a market demanding 12-foot ceilings | No, without major reconstruction | | Economic / External | Neighborhood decline, pollution, traffic congestion, encroachment | A residential plot in an area now declared an industrial zone | Generally No | | Locational | Loss of neighborhood prestige, change in land use pattern | A bungalow in a high-rise apartment dominated locality | Generally No | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Aspect** | **Depreciation** | **Obsolescence** | |---|---|---| | Nature of loss | Physical | Functional / Economic / Locational | | Origin | Property itself | External environment / design flaw | | Measured by | Age-life method, Straight-line, Sinking fund, etc. | Capitalized rent loss / paired sales analysis | | Common formula | $D = \frac{Age}{Total\ Life} \times 100\%$ | $O = \frac{Annual\ Income\ Loss}{Capitalization\ Rate}$ | | Reversibility | Usually **Irreversible** | Sometimes **Reversible** (curable) | | Recovery option | Major reconstruction only | Renovation, remodeling, or marketing fix | | Time factor | Direct, time-dependent | Often time-independent or sudden | | Effect on building life | Reduces economic life | May or may not affect structural life | | Typical % range | 0% to 100% of RCN | Variable (can be 0% to 50%+) | | Influenced by maintenance | Yes, strongly | No | ### 2.4 Mathematical Models for Depreciation **1. Straight-Line Method:** $$D = \frac{C \times (A - S)}{L}$$ Where: - $C$ = Original Cost (Reproduction Cost New) - $A$ = Effective Age of the building - $S$ = Salvage Value (often 10% of $C$ in KTU problems) - $L$ = Total Economic Life of the building **2. Declining Balance Method:** $$D_t = C \times (1 - r)^{t-1} \times r$$ Where: - $r$ = Rate of depreciation (decimal) - $t$ = Year number - $C$ = Original Cost **3. Obsolescence Capitalization Method:** $$O = \frac{L_{annual}}{R_c}$$ Where: - $L_{annual}$ = Annual loss in rental income due to obsolescence - $R_c$ = Capitalization rate (decimal, e.g., 0.08 or 0.10) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** In KTU valuation practice, distinguishing depreciation from obsolescence is critical for **insurance valuation** (which considers physical depreciation) versus **market valuation** (which considers all forms of value loss including obsolescence). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Total Depreciation Formula Derivation The **Total Depreciation (T.D.)** of a property is the sum of physical depreciation and all forms of obsolescence. We derive the standard KTU formulation: **Step 1:** Start with the Reproduction Cost New (RCN) as the base value of the property. **Step 2:** Subtract the **Physical Depreciation** to account for wear, tear, and deterioration. **Step 3:** Subtract the **Functional Obsolescence** to account for design and utility inadequacies. **Step 4:** Subtract the **Economic (External) Obsolescence** to account for environmental and locational factors. **Step 5:** The final residue is the **Depreciated Value (D.V.)** of the property, also called the **Current Market Value** when the obsolescence is properly quantified. $$ \begin{aligned} RCN_{depreciated} &= RCN - D_{physical} - O_{functional} - O_{economic} \\[6pt] \text{Total Depreciation } (TD) &= D_{physical} + O_{functional} + O_{economic} \\[6pt] D.V. &= RCN - TD \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Example — Straight-Line Depreciation vs. Obsolescence > **Problem:** A residential building was constructed 25 years ago at a cost of ₹25,00,000. The total economic life of the building is 100 years, with a salvage value of ₹2,50,000. The building suffers from a curable functional obsolescence of ₹1,00,000 and an incurable economic obsolescence estimated to cause a perpetual annual rental loss of ₹20,000 capitalized at 10%. > > **Required:** Calculate the total depreciation and the present value of the building. **Step 1: Calculate Physical Depreciation (Straight-Line Method)** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{physical} &= \frac{C \times (A - S)}{L} \\[6pt] &= \frac{25,00,000 \times (25 - 2,50,000/25,00,000 \times 100)}{100} \\[6pt] &= \frac{25,00,000 \times (25 - 10)}{100} \\[6pt] &= \frac{25,00,000 \times 15}{100} \\[6pt] &= ₹3,75,000 \end{aligned} $$ > **Note on Step 1:** The salvage value $S$ as a percentage of $C$ is $2,50,000 / 25,00,000 = 10\%$. Therefore, the depreciable base is 100% − 10% = 90%. Effective age = 25 years, total life = 100 years. **Step 2: Curable Functional Obsolescence (Given)** $$ O_{functional, curable} = ₹1,00,000 $$ **Step 3: Incurable Economic Obsolescence (Capitalized Rental Loss)** $$ \begin{aligned} O_{economic} &= \frac{L_{annual}}{R_c} \\[6pt] &= \frac{20,000}{0.10} \\[6pt] &= ₹2,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Total Depreciation (TD)** $$ \begin{aligned} TD &= D_{physical} + O_{functional} + O_{economic} \\[6pt] &= 3,75,000 + 1,00,000 + 2,00,000 \\[6pt] &= ₹6,75,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Depreciated Value (D.V.)** $$ \begin{aligned} D.V. &= RCN - TD \\[6pt] &= 25,00,000 - 6,75,000 \\[6pt] &= ₹18,25,000 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Each Concept | **Condition Identified in Valuation** | **Apply Depreciation?** | **Apply Obsolescence?** | |---|---|---| | Cracked plaster, damp walls, broken flooring | ✅ Yes (Physical) | ❌ No | | 2-washroom house in 4-washroom market | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Functional) | | Industrial smoke near residential building | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Economic) | | Roof leakage from aged waterproofing | ✅ Yes (Physical) | ❌ No | | Old fuse-box wiring in a smart-home market | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Functional) | | Building near newly built flyover (noise) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Locational) | | Termite-damaged wooden beams | ✅ Yes (Physical) | ❌ No | | Carpet area far below current market norms | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Functional) | > [!TIP] > **Examiner Trick:** If a question states *"the property is in good condition but has poor design"* → It is **obsolescence, not depreciation**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Total Value Loss in Real Property ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Reproduction Cost New
RCN of Building"] --> B["Total Value Loss
Depreciation Component"] B --> C["Physical Depreciation
Dphysical"] B --> D["Functional Obsolescence
Ofunctional"] B --> E["Economic or Locational
Obsolescence Oeconomic"] C --> C1["Wear and Tear"] C --> C2["Deterioration"] C --> C3["Deferred Maintenance"] D --> D1["Design Flaw"] D --> D2["Outdated Amenities"] D --> D3["Inadequate Layout"] E --> E1["Neighborhood Decline"] E --> E2["Change in Land Use"] E --> E3["Environmental Pollution"] C1 --> F["Depreciated Value
DV of Property"] C2 --> F C3 --> F D1 --> F D2 --> F D3 --> F E1 --> F E2 --> F E3 --> F ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Decision Logic for Valuer ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Inspect Building
for Value Loss"] --> B{"Is the Loss
Physical?"} B -- "Yes" --> C["Apply DEPRECIATION
Methods"] B -- "No" --> D{"Is it due to
Design or Layout?"} D -- "Yes" --> E["Apply FUNCTIONAL
OBSOLESCENCE"] D -- "No" --> F{"Is it due to
Surroundings?"} F -- "Yes" --> G["Apply ECONOMIC
or LOCATIONAL
OBSOLESCENCE"] F -- "No" --> H["No Value Loss
Identified"] C --> I["Compute Total
Depreciation TD"] E --> I G --> I H --> I I --> J["Subtract from RCN
to get DV"] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Depreciation vs. Obsolescence ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DepreciationPath["DEPRECIATION"] DP1["Cause: Property Itself"] --> DP2["Type: Physical"] DP2 --> DP3["Reversible: Generally No"] DP3 --> DP4["Measured by: Age-Life Method"] end subgraph ObsolescencePath["OBSOLESCENCE"] OP1["Cause: External / Design"] --> OP2["Type: Functional, Economic, Locational"] OP2 --> OP3["Reversible: Sometimes Yes"] OP3 --> OP4["Measured by: Capitalized Loss / Paired Sales"] end DP4 --> Final["Total Depreciation TD"] OP4 --> Final ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`DP1`, `OP4`, etc.) to comply with the engine's safeguard against reserved keywords. Labels are kept in clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define depreciation in the context of real property valuation. Mention any two physical causes.** **Model Answer:** Depreciation in real property valuation refers to the **permanent loss in the market value of a building** due to physical deterioration, wear and tear, and the action of natural elements over time. It is the difference between the Reproduction Cost New (RCN) and the Present Value of the property, considered from a purely physical standpoint. **Two physical causes:** 1. **Deterioration** — chemical or physical breakdown of building materials (e.g., rusting of steel reinforcement, decay of timber). 2. **Wear and Tear** — mechanical damage from regular use (e.g., worn-out flooring, damaged door hinges). *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two causes)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **What is functional obsolescence? How is it different from physical deterioration?** **Model Answer:** **Functional Obsolescence** is a form of value loss in a property that arises from **defects in the design, layout, size, or amenities** of the structure itself, making it less desirable compared to current market standards. It is *not* caused by physical wear. **Difference from physical deterioration:** | **Aspect** | **Functional Obsolescence** | **Physical Deterioration** | |---|---|---| | Cause | Design/utility flaw | Use, age, weather | | Nature | Inadequacy | Wear and tear | | Visible as | Outdated features | Cracks, decay, breakage | *(3 marks: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for distinction)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the various **causes of depreciation** in buildings. Differentiate clearly between **physical depreciation and functional obsolescence** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A building was constructed 30 years ago at a cost of ₹40,00,000. Its expected total life is 80 years, and the salvage value is ₹4,00,000. The building suffers from: - Curable functional obsolescence: ₹1,50,000 - Economic obsolescence causing an annual rental loss of ₹30,000, capitalized at 8% Calculate the **total depreciation** and the **depreciated value (D.V.)** of the building. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Causes of Depreciation & Differentiation:** **Causes of physical depreciation:** 1. Deterioration — chemical/physical decay of materials 2. Wear and tear — usage-based damage 3. Deferred maintenance / neglect 4. Structural fatigue in load-bearing elements **Key differences:** | **Parameter** | **Physical Depreciation** | **Functional Obsolescence** | |---|---|---| | Origin | Inside the property | Design/amenity inadequacy | | Time factor | Increases with age | Independent of age | | Measurement | Age-life, sinking fund | Capitalized rent loss, cost-to-cure | | Examples | Cracked walls, rusted beams | Outdated 2-washroom layout, no smart wiring | *(7 marks: 3 marks for causes + 4 marks for differentiation with examples)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Physical Depreciation (Straight-Line)** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{physical} &= \frac{C \times (A - S)}{L} \\[6pt] &= \frac{40,00,000 \times (30 - 4,00,000 \times 100 / 40,00,000)}{80} \\[6pt] &= \frac{40,00,000 \times (30 - 10)}{80} \\[6pt] &= \frac{40,00,000 \times 20}{80} \\[6pt] &= ₹10,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Functional Obsolescence** $$ O_{functional, curable} = ₹1,50,000 $$ **Step 3: Economic Obsolescence (Capitalization Method)** $$ \begin{aligned} O_{economic} &= \frac{L_{annual}}{R_c} \\[6pt] &= \frac{30,000}{0.08} \\[6pt] &= ₹3,75,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Total Depreciation** $$ \begin{aligned} TD &= D_{physical} + O_{functional} + O_{economic} \\[6pt] &= 10,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 3,75,000 \\[6pt] &= ₹15,25,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Depreciated Value** $$ \begin{aligned} D.V. &= RCN - TD \\[6pt] &= 40,00,000 - 15,25,000 \\[6pt] &= ₹24,75,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** Total Depreciation = **₹15,25,000**; Depreciated Value = **₹24,75,000** *(7 marks: 2 marks for physical dep + 1.5 for functional + 1.5 for economic + 2 for total and DV)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Discuss the **types of obsolescence** in real property valuation with examples. How is **curable obsolescence** different from **incurable obsolescence**? *(7 marks)* **(b)** A property has a Reproduction Cost New (RCN) of ₹60,00,000. It has suffered: - Physical depreciation of ₹12,00,000 - Functional obsolescence (curable) of ₹2,00,000 - Economic obsolescence (incurable) of ₹3,00,000 Calculate the **total accrued depreciation** and the **depreciated value** of the property. Comment on the **percentage loss in value**. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Types of Obsolescence:** **1. Functional Obsolescence:** Loss in value due to the property's own design deficiencies. - *Example:* A 2-bedroom house in a market where 3-bedroom units are the norm. **2. Economic (External) Obsolescence:** Loss due to adverse external conditions. - *Example:* A residential property in an area declared as an industrial zone. **3. Locational Obsolescence:** Loss due to shift in neighborhood prestige or land use. - *Example:* A standalone villa surrounded by high-rise apartments that block sunlight. **Curable vs. Incurable:** | **Aspect** | **Curable Obsolescence** | **Incurable Obsolescence** | |---|---|---| | Cost of cure | Less than the value added | Exceeds the value added | | Feasibility | Economically feasible | Not economically viable | | Example | Adding a new bathroom | Rebuilding foundation | | Recovery | Possible by renovation | Cannot recover economically | *(7 marks: 4 marks for types + 3 marks for curable vs incurable)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Total Accrued Depreciation** $$ \begin{aligned} TD &= D_{physical} + O_{functional} + O_{economic} \\[6pt] &= 12,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 3,00,000 \\[6pt] &= ₹17,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Depreciated Value** $$ \begin{aligned} D.V. &= RCN - TD \\[6pt] &= 60,00,000 - 17,00,000 \\[6pt] &= ₹43,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Percentage Loss in Value** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{ Loss} &= \frac{TD}{RCN} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{17,00,000}{60,00,000} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 28.33\% \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** Total Depreciation = **₹17,00,000**; Depreciated Value = **₹43,00,000**; **Percentage Loss = 28.33%** *(7 marks: 3 marks for total dep + 2 marks for DV + 2 marks for % loss with comment)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "salvage value" with "scrap value":** Salvage value is what the property yields at the end of its useful life; scrap value is purely the material recovery. Using them interchangeably costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Ignoring functional obsolescence:** Many students calculate only physical depreciation and miss functional and economic components, leading to a wrong Depreciated Value. > 3. **Wrong unit for capitalization rate:** The rate must be in **decimal** (e.g., 0.08 for 8%), not as a percentage. This is a frequent calculation error. > 4. **Effective Age vs. Chronological Age:** If a building is well-maintained, its effective age is less than its chronological age. Always read the problem carefully. > 5. **Not stating units:** Always write "₹" or "Rs." or appropriate currency unit in the final answer. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Depreciation = Physical loss in value** due to wear, tear, age, and deterioration. - **Obsolescence = Non-physical loss in value** due to design flaws, outdated amenities, neighborhood decline, or locational shifts. - **Three types of obsolescence:** Functional, Economic (External), and Locational. - **Curable obsolescence** is economically feasible to fix; **incurable obsolescence** is not. - **Key formula:** $D.V. = RCN - (D_{physical} + O_{functional} + O_{economic})$ - **Straight-line depreciation formula:** $D = \frac{C \times (A - S)}{L}$ - **Capitalized obsolescence formula:** $O = \frac{L_{annual}}{R_c}$ - **Salvage value** is typically **10% of the original cost** unless specified. - **Effective age** may differ from chronological age based on maintenance quality. - **Always identify causes separately** before applying formulas in KTU numerical problems. - **Examiner's mantra:** "Physical = Depreciation; Design/External = Obsolescence." ---

topics.title: Method of Estimating Cost Depreciation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method of Estimating Cost Depreciation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Depreciation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depreciation (KTU 2024 Definition):** Depreciation is the **permanent, continuous, and progressive loss in the value of a property** due to its use, wear and tear, decay, obsolescence, structural deterioration, or any other cause. It represents the *difference between the present value (or replacement cost) of a building/property* and its *depreciated value (or scrap/salvage value)* at a given point in its useful life.

In real property valuation, depreciation is **not merely physical decay**; it is a **composite loss** arising from four fundamental causes recognized in KTU syllabus:

1. **Physical Deterioration** – Wear and tear from use, exposure to weather, material fatigue, and structural decay.
2. **Functional Obsolescence** – Loss of utility due to outdated design, outdated amenities, or poor functional layout.
3. **Economic (External) Obsolescence** – Loss of value due to external factors like neighborhood decline, change in zoning laws, or market shifts.
4. **Age-Related Wear (Curable vs. Incurable)** – Curable depreciation can be repaired; incurable must be accepted as a loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Depreciation** specifically refers to the *estimated monetary loss* in the original/construction cost of a property over time, calculated using standardized engineering methods.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Car" Intuition

Imagine you buy a brand-new car for **₹10,00,000**. After 5 years, you cannot sell it for the same price — even if it still runs. Why?

- The engine has worn → **Physical Depreciation**
- Newer models have better mileage, touchscreen, and safety features → **Functional Obsolescence**
- The neighborhood you live in has congestion, making your car less desirable → **Economic Obsolescence**

The reduction in value (₹10,00,000 – Resale Price) is the **depreciation**.
A **valuation engineer** must *estimate* this loss using a *method* — these methods are the **Methods of Estimating Depreciation**.

> **In property valuation, the engineer treats the building like a "long-lived asset" and the "car" is replaced by a "real estate building" with a useful life of 50–100 years.**

---

## 1.3 Why Methods Matter in KTU Valuation

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Valuation Accuracy** | Determines market price for sale, mortgage, taxation, insurance. |
| **Legal Compliance** | Required for rent fixation, property tax, wealth tax. |
| **Investment Decision** | Helps investors compute ROI, NPV of real estate. |
| **Accounting Standard** | Conforms to Indian Accounting Standards for tangible assets. |
| **Insurance Claims** | Determines "Actual Cash Value" at the time of loss. |

---

## 1.4 Standard Terminology (KTU Notation Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Original Cost / Replacement Cost | ₹ |
| $S$ | Salvage / Scrap Value at end of life | ₹ |
| $n$ | Estimated Useful Life | Years |
| $t$ | Elapsed Age (period for which depreciation is computed) | Years |
| $V_t$ | Depreciated / Present Value after $t$ years | ₹ |
| $D_t$ | Annual Depreciation in year $t$ | ₹/year |
| $d$ | Depreciation rate (fraction) | Dimensionless |
| $i$ | Rate of Interest (sinking fund, annuity) | Fraction per year |
| $A$ | Annual installment for sinking fund | ₹/year |
| $R$ | Rate of annual sinking fund contribution | % |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six KTU-Recognized Methods of Depreciation

The KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET862) recognizes **six principal methods** for estimating depreciation of buildings and real property:

1. **Straight Line Method** (Equal Annual Instalment Method)
2. **Declining Balance Method** (Written Down Value / Diminishing Balance Method)
3. **Sinking Fund Method**
4. **Annuity Method** (Capitalized Cost Method)
5. **Replacement Cost Method** (Replacement of Parts Method)
6. **Service Life or Life Table Method** (Insurance/Tax Tables)

Each method is rooted in a different economic philosophy — we analyze each below.

---

## 2.2 Method 1 — Straight Line Method (SLM)

### Conceptual Foundation
A *fixed, equal amount* is written off every year, assuming the property loses value **uniformly** over its life.

### Logic Steps
- Calculate the total depreciable amount: $C - S$.
- Divide it equally by the useful life $n$.
- The annual depreciation is a constant $D = (C - S)/n$.
- Present value at age $t$: $V_t = C - t \cdot D$.

### Why It Works
- Simplest method; best for **uniformly used** properties (e.g., residential houses with regular maintenance).
- Suitable when obsolescence is **not rapid**.

### Why It Fails
- Ignores the fact that buildings typically lose value *faster* in early years (when functional obsolescence kicks in).

---

## 2.3 Method 2 — Declining Balance Method (DBM / WDV)

### Conceptual Foundation
A *fixed percentage* is charged each year on the **diminishing book value**. The asset loses more value early, less value later.

### Logic Steps
- Let $d$ = fixed rate of depreciation per year (fraction).
- Book value at end of year $t$: $V_t = C(1-d)^t$.
- Annual depreciation in year $t$: $D_t = C(1-d)^{t-1} \cdot d$.

### Why It Works
- Matches the **actual wear pattern** of most buildings (rapid early obsolescence, slower later decay).
- Preferred in **Indian Income Tax** and **Company Law** accounting.

### Why It Fails
- Never fully reduces to salvage value $S$ unless $d$ is precisely calibrated.
- More complex than SLM.

### Special Variant — Double Declining Balance
$d = \dfrac{2}{n}$ (i.e., twice the straight-line rate).

---

## 2.4 Method 3 — Sinking Fund Method (SFM)

### Conceptual Foundation
The owner sets aside a **fixed annual amount** that, with compound interest, accumulates to the depreciation amount at the end of the property's life.

### Logic Steps
- Annual sinking fund contribution: $A = \dfrac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- At end of year $n$, the accumulated fund equals $C - S$, which replaces the property.
- Each year, the property is assumed to depreciate by the depreciation amount, but the **interest earned** on the sinking fund also adds to the income from the property.

### Why It Works
- Accounts for the **time value of money** — money saved early earns interest.
- Realistic for long-life public buildings (dams, bridges, heritage structures).
- The accumulated fund equals the **replacement cost** at the end of life.

### Why It Fails
- Ignores actual maintenance of the structure.
- The owner must actually invest the annual installment.

---

## 2.5 Method 4 — Annuity Method (Capitalized Cost Method)

### Conceptual Foundation
The **most rigorous** method. It assumes the owner is *losing* the entire capital investment over the property's life and must recover both:
- (a) The annual depreciation (equal installments to recover $C - S$)
- (b) The **interest** on the original capital
- (c) A **sinking fund contribution** to replace the property at end of life

### Annual Amount Recoverable (Annuity)
$$A = \text{Depreciation/yr} + \text{Interest on Capital} + \text{Sinking Fund Instalment}$$

$$A = \frac{C - S}{n} + \frac{(C + S)}{2} \cdot i + \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The middle term uses the **average capital** $(C + S)/2$ because capital is gradually being returned through depreciation.

### Why It Works
- Most scientific and economically correct.
- Best suited for **leasehold, rental, and yield-based valuations**.

### Why It Fails
- Requires accurate $i$ and $n$ — sensitive to input errors.
- Computationally heavy (no simple spreadsheet).

---

## 2.6 Method 5 — Replacement Cost Method (Replacement of Parts)

### Conceptual Foundation
The property's value equals the **present cost of replacing it** *minus* the depreciation that has occurred. Uses current prices, not historical prices.

### Logic Steps
- Estimate current replacement cost $C_{\text{rep}}$ of the *new* building using PWD/CPWD schedules.
- Estimate depreciation = $\dfrac{t}{n} \times (C_{\text{rep}} - S)$.
- Present Value $V_t = C_{\text{rep}} - \text{Depreciation}$.

### Why It Works
- Accurate in **inflationary periods** because it uses current prices.
- Required for **insurance valuations** and **balance sheet revaluation**.

### Why It Fails
- Difficult to estimate *exact* replacement cost for old buildings.
- Ignores intangible value (e.g., heritage, location premium).

---

## 2.7 Method 6 — Service Life / Life Table Method

### Conceptual Foundation
Based on **actuarial/insurance life tables**. Depreciation rate is a *statistical average* derived from observations of similar properties.

### Logic Steps
- Each component (roof, flooring, plaster, etc.) has a separately tabulated life.
- Depreciation = (age / total life) × replacement cost of that component.
- Total depreciation = sum of component depreciations.

### Why It Works
- Highly accurate for **insurance valuations** and **government properties**.
- Recognizes that different parts depreciate at different rates.

### Why It Fails
- Requires extensive statistical data.
- Component-wise calculation is tedious.

---

## 2.8 Master Formula Cheat Sheet (KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU ESE. Every numerical question derives from these formulas.**

| # | Method | Present Value $V_t$ | Annual Depreciation $D_t$ | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Straight Line** | $V_t = C - \dfrac{t(C-S)}{n}$ | $D = \dfrac{C-S}{n}$ | Uniform annual loss |
| 2 | **Declining Balance (WDV)** | $V_t = C(1-d)^t$ | $D_t = C(1-d)^{t-1} \cdot d$ | Constant % of book value |
| 3 | **Sinking Fund** | $V_t = C - A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^t - 1}{i}$ | $A = \dfrac{(C-S)i}{(1+i)^n-1}$ | Compounded fund replaces asset |
| 4 | **Annuity** | $V_t = C - \dfrac{t(C-S)}{n} - \text{Interest Component}$ | $A = \dfrac{C-S}{n} + \dfrac{(C+S)i}{2} + \dfrac{(C-S)i}{(1+i)^n-1}$ | Recovery of capital + interest + sinking fund |
| 5 | **Replacement Cost** | $V_t = C_{\text{rep}} - \dfrac{t(C_{\text{rep}} - S)}{n}$ | $D = \dfrac{C_{\text{rep}}-S}{n}$ | Current cost basis |
| 6 | **Service Life** | $V_t = \sum_i \left[1 - \dfrac{t}{n_i}\right] C_{\text{rep},i}$ | Component-wise | Statistically observed lives |

---

## 2.9 Comparison Matrix — When to Use Each Method

| Method | Best Suited For | KTU Favourite Topic? | Difficulty Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| SLM | Simple residential, uniform-use assets | Yes (Part A & B) | ★ |
| DBM/WDV | Income tax, accounting, commercial | Yes (frequent numerical) | ★★ |
| Sinking Fund | Long-life public, heritage buildings | Yes (Part A definition) | ★★★ |
| Annuity | Lease, yield-based, commercial | Yes (Part B 14-mark) | ★★★★ |
| Replacement Cost | Insurance, revaluation | Sometimes | ★★ |
| Service Life | Insurance, government, heritage | Rare | ★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Straight Line Method (Exhaustive)

> **Problem Setup:** A building costs ₹20,00,000. Salvage value after 50 years is ₹2,00,000. Find the depreciated value after 20 years.

### Step 1: Identify variables

$$C = ₹20,00,000, \quad S = ₹2,00,000, \quad n = 50 \text{ years}, \quad t = 20 \text{ years}$$

### Step 2: Compute depreciable amount (total loss over life)

$$\text{Total Depreciation} = C - S = 20,00,000 - 2,00,000 = 18,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 3: Compute annual depreciation

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{18,00,000}{50} = 36,000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

### Step 4: Compute accumulated depreciation over 20 years

$$\text{Accumulated Depreciation} = D \times t = 36,000 \times 20 = 7,20,000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 5: Compute present value

$$V_t = C - \text{Accumulated Depreciation} = 20,00,000 - 7,20,000 = ₹12,80,000$$

### Step 6: Verify using direct formula

$$V_{20} = C - \frac{t(C-S)}{n} = 20,00,000 - \frac{20 \times 18,00,000}{50} = 20,00,000 - 7,20,000 = ₹12,80,000 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Result:** Depreciated Value after 20 years = **₹12,80,000**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Declining Balance Method (Exhaustive)

> **Problem:** A structure cost ₹10,00,000. Depreciation rate is **5% p.a. on WDV**. Find value after 4 years and the depreciation in the 5th year.

### Step 1: Identify variables

$$C = ₹10,00,000, \quad d = 0.05, \quad t = 4 \text{ years}$$

### Step 2: Apply WDV formula

$$V_t = C(1-d)^t = 10,00,000 \times (1 - 0.05)^4$$

### Step 3: Compute $(0.95)^4$

$$(0.95)^2 = 0.9025, \quad (0.95)^4 = (0.9025)^2 = 0.81450625$$

### Step 4: Compute present value

$$V_4 = 10,00,000 \times 0.81450625 = ₹8,14,506.25$$

### Step 5: Compute depreciation in 5th year

$$D_5 = C(1-d)^{4} \cdot d = 10,00,000 \times 0.81450625 \times 0.05 = ₹40,725.31$$

### Step 6: Alternative check

$$D_5 = V_4 \times d = 8,14,506.25 \times 0.05 = ₹40,725.31 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Result:** Value after 4 years = **₹8,14,506.25**; 5th-year depreciation = **₹40,725.31**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sinking Fund Method (Exhaustive)

> **Problem:** A building costs ₹15,00,000 with salvage ₹3,00,000 after 40 years. Sinking fund rate is **6% p.a. compound interest**. Find the annual sinking fund installment.

### Step 1: Identify variables

$$C = ₹15,00,000, \quad S = ₹3,00,000, \quad n = 40, \quad i = 0.06$$

### Step 2: Apply the Sinking Fund formula

$$A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

### Step 3: Compute $(1.06)^{40}$

$$(1.06)^{10} = 1.7908, \quad (1.06)^{20} = 1.7908^2 \approx 3.2071$$
$$(1.06)^{40} = 3.2071^2 \approx 10.2857$$

### Step 4: Substitute

$$A = \frac{(15,00,000 - 3,00,000) \times 0.06}{10.2857 - 1} = \frac{12,00,000 \times 0.06}{9.2857}$$

### Step 5: Compute numerator and denominator

$$\text{Numerator} = 72,000, \quad \text{Denominator} = 9.2857$$

$$A = \frac{72,000}{9.2857} = ₹7,754.10 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 6: Verify accumulated value at year 40

$$FV = A \times \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} = 7,754.10 \times \frac{10.2857 - 1}{0.06} = 7,754.10 \times 154.7620 = ₹12,00,000 \checkmark$$

> **Result:** Annual Sinking Fund Instalment = **₹7,754.10**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Annuity Method (The "King" 14-Mark Method)

> **Problem:** A property costs ₹25,00,000. Salvage value ₹5,00,000. Life 30 years. Sinking fund rate 5%. Find the annual amount to be recovered by the owner.

### Step 1: Identify the three components

| Component | Formula | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Depreciation | $\dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $D$ |
| Interest on Capital | $\dfrac{C + S}{2} \cdot i$ | $I$ |
| Sinking Fund Instalment | $\dfrac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $A$ |

### Step 2: Compute each component

**Component 1 — Annual Depreciation**

$$D = \frac{25,00,000 - 5,00,000}{30} = \frac{20,00,000}{30} = ₹66,666.67 \text{/year}$$

**Component 2 — Interest on Capital (Average)**

$$I = \frac{25,00,000 + 5,00,000}{2} \times 0.05 = 15,00,000 \times 0.05 = ₹75,000 \text{/year}$$

**Component 3 — Sinking Fund Instalment**

First, $(1.05)^{30} = ?$

$$(1.05)^{10} \approx 1.6289, \quad (1.05)^{30} = (1.6289)^3 \approx 4.322$$

$$A = \frac{20,00,000 \times 0.05}{4.322 - 1} = \frac{1,00,000}{3.322} = ₹30,102.35 \text{/year}$$

### Step 3: Total annual annuity

$$\text{Annual Recovery} = D + I + A = 66,666.67 + 75,000 + 30,102.35 = ₹1,71,769.02$$

### Step 4: Interpretation for KTU

> **The owner must recover ₹1,71,769 every year** for the property to remain a viable investment — covering depreciation, interest on locked capital, and the sinking fund.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table — Same Property, Six Methods

> **Common Inputs:** $C = ₹10,00,000, S = ₹1,00,000, n = 20 \text{ years}, t = 10 \text{ years}, i = 5\%$.

| Method | Computation | Depreciated Value $V_{10}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **SLM** | $10,00,000 - \dfrac{10 \times 9,00,000}{20}$ | **₹5,50,000** |
| **WDV (d=10%)** | $10,00,000 \times (0.9)^{10} = 10,00,000 \times 0.3487$ | **₹3,48,678** |
| **Sinking Fund** | $10,00,000 - A \cdot \dfrac{(1.05)^{10}-1}{0.05}$ where $A = \dfrac{9,00,000 \times 0.05}{1.6295-1} = 71,517$ | ₹7,38,012 |
| **Annuity** | $10,00,000 - 4,50,000 - 3,15,000$ (capital & interest) | ≈ **₹2,35,000** |
| **Replacement Cost** | $C_{\text{rep}}(1) - 0.5(C_{\text{rep}} - S)$ | **0.5$C_{\text{rep}}$ + 0.5S** |
| **Service Life** | Component-weighted | Varies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The **Annuity Method gives the lowest depreciated value** because it accounts for the *time value of money* rigorously. **SLM gives the highest** because it ignores interest.

---

## 3.6 Special Numerical — KTU 14-Mark Problem Type

> **Question:** A building was constructed for ₹18,00,000. After 15 years, its value was estimated at ₹10,00,000 by the Straight Line Method. Find: (a) Total life of the building. (b) Scrap value. (c) Annual depreciation.

### Solution

**Given:** $C = 18,00,000, V_{15} = 10,00,000, t = 15 \text{ years}$.

**Step 1 — Depreciation over 15 years**

$$\text{Total Depreciation} = C - V_{15} = 18,00,000 - 10,00,000 = ₹8,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Annual depreciation**

$$D = \frac{8,00,000}{15} = ₹53,333.33 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 — If total life = $n$ and scrap = $S$:**

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} \Rightarrow \frac{C - S}{n} = 53,333.33 \Rightarrow C - S = 53,333.33 \cdot n$$

Without additional info, $n$ cannot be uniquely determined. However, **assuming scrap value = 10% of $C$**:

$$S = 1,80,000, \quad \text{then } n = \frac{18,00,000 - 1,80,000}{53,333.33} = \frac{16,20,000}{53,333.33} \approx 30.4 \text{ years}$$

> **Result:** Total life ≈ **30.4 years**; Annual depreciation = **₹53,333.33**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Selecting the Right Depreciation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Property Valuation Required] --> B{Type of Asset?}
    B -->|Residential/Uniform| C[Straight Line Method]
    B -->|Commercial/Rapid Obsolescence| D[Declining Balance WDV]
    B -->|Long-life Public/Heritage| E[Sinking Fund Method]
    B -->|Leasehold/Rental Yield| F[Annuity Method]
    B -->|Insurance/Revaluation| G[Replacement Cost Method]
    B -->|Component-wise Audit| H[Service Life Method]

    C --> I[Output: Vt = C - tD]
    D --> J[Output: Vt = C times 1-d^t]
    E --> K[Output: Sinking Fund Accumulation]
    F --> L[Output: Depreciation + Interest + Sinking Fund]
    G --> M[Output: Crep minus Depreciation]
    H --> N[Output: Component Sum]

    I --> O[Final Depreciated Value]
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O

    O --> P[KTU ESE Report: Vt with full derivation]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Annuity Method (The Most Complex)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Cost C] --> B[Capital Recovery Layer]
    A --> C[Interest on Capital Layer]
    A --> D[Sinking Fund Layer]

    B --> B1[Annual Depreciation Component: C minus S over n]
    C --> C1[Average Capital times Interest Rate: C plus S over 2 times i]
    D --> D1[SF Instalment: C minus S times i over 1+i^n minus 1]

    B1 --> E[Total Annual Annuity A]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E

    E --> F[Owner Recovers Per Year]
    F --> G[At Year n: Property Replaced via Sinking Fund]
```

---

## 4.3 WDV Depreciation Pattern (Graphical Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Year 0: Book Value = C] --> Y1[Year 1: V1 = C times 1-d]
    Y1 --> Y2[Year 2: V2 = C times 1-d squared]
    Y2 --> Y3[Year 3: V3 = C times 1-d cubed]
    Y3 --> Yn[Year n: Vn = C times 1-d^n]

    Y1 --> D1[D1 = C times 1-d to power 0 times d]
    Y2 --> D2[D2 = C times 1-d to power 1 times d]
    Y3 --> D3[D3 = C times 1-d to power 2 times d]
    Yn --> Dn[Dn = C times 1-d to power n-1 times d]

    D1 --> Note[Each year depreciation shrinks]
    D2 --> Note
    D3 --> Note
    Dn --> Note
```

---

## 4.4 Component-wise Schematic — Service Life Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Total Building] --> R1[Roof: Life 25 years]
    P --> R2[Plaster: Life 15 years]
    P --> R3[Flooring: Life 20 years]
    P --> R4[Doors and Windows: Life 30 years]
    P --> R5[Plumbing: Life 18 years]

    R1 --> A1[Dep1 = t/25 times C1]
    R2 --> A2[Dep2 = t/15 times C2]
    R3 --> A3[Dep3 = t/20 times C3]
    R4 --> A4[Dep4 = t/30 times C4]
    R5 --> A5[Dep5 = t/18 times C5]

    A1 --> SUM[Total Depreciation = Dep1 + Dep2 + Dep3 + Dep4 + Dep5]
    A2 --> SUM
    A3 --> SUM
    A4 --> SUM
    A5 --> SUM

    SUM --> V[Present Value Vt = C minus Total Depreciation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Depreciation" in the context of real property valuation. List the four principal causes of depreciation recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Depreciation is the *gradual, permanent, and continuous loss in the value of a real property* (building/structure) due to use, wear and tear, decay, structural deterioration, or obsolescence. It is the difference between the original/replacement cost and the present market value.
>
> **Four Causes (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. **Physical Deterioration** — Wear, decay, weathering, structural fatigue.
> 2. **Functional Obsolescence** — Outdated design, amenities, or layout.
> 3. **Economic (External) Obsolescence** — Negative neighborhood changes, zoning changes.
> 4. **Age-Life Wear (Curable/Incurable)** — Repairs needed or accepted as loss.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between the Straight Line Method and the Declining Balance Method of depreciation. State one merit and one demerit of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Straight Line | Declining Balance |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of Depreciation** | Equal annual amount | Fixed % of diminishing value |
| **Annual Depreciation** | Constant = $(C-S)/n$ | Decreasing = $C(1-d)^{t-1} \cdot d$ |
| **Merit** | Simple, predictable | Matches actual wear pattern |
| **Demerit** | Ignores early obsolescence | Never reaches salvage $S$ exactly |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> A commercial building was constructed 25 years ago at a cost of **₹30,00,000**. The expected total life is **60 years** and the salvage value is **₹2,00,000**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the present value of the building using the **Straight Line Method** and the **Declining Balance Method** (rate = 5% p.a.). (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** If the sinking fund interest rate is **6% p.a.**, calculate the **annual sinking fund installment** required to replace the property at the end of its life. Also compute the value after 25 years under the sinking fund approach. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

### Part (a) — SLM and WDV Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $C = 30,00,000, S = 2,00,000, n = 60, t = 25$.

**Step 1 — SLM Present Value (3.5 Marks)**

$$D_{\text{SLM}} = \frac{30,00,000 - 2,00,000}{60} = \frac{28,00,000}{60} = ₹46,666.67 \text{ per year}$$

$$V_{25}^{\text{SLM}} = C - t \cdot D = 30,00,000 - 25 \times 46,666.67 = 30,00,000 - 11,66,666.67$$

$$\boxed{V_{25}^{\text{SLM}} = ₹18,33,333.33}$$

**Step 2 — WDV Present Value (3.5 Marks)**

$$V_{25}^{\text{WDV}} = 30,00,000 \times (1 - 0.05)^{25}$$

$$(0.95)^{25} = ?$$

Using log table or successive squaring:
$$(0.95)^{10} \approx 0.5987, \quad (0.95)^{20} \approx 0.3585, \quad (0.95)^{25} = (0.95)^{20} \times (0.95)^5$$
$$(0.95)^5 = 0.7738 \Rightarrow (0.95)^{25} \approx 0.3585 \times 0.7738 \approx 0.2774$$

$$V_{25}^{\text{WDV}} = 30,00,000 \times 0.2774 = \boxed{₹8,32,200}$$

**Comparison:** WDV gives a *lower* value because it depreciates faster in early years.

---

### Part (b) — Sinking Fund Method (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sinking Fund Instalment (3.5 Marks)**

$$A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{28,00,000 \times 0.06}{(1.06)^{60} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.06)^{60}$:
$$(1.06)^{10} \approx 1.7908, \quad (1.06)^{20} \approx 3.2071, \quad (1.06)^{30} \approx 5.7435$$
$$(1.06)^{60} = (1.06)^{30} \times (1.06)^{30} \approx 5.7435^2 \approx 32.988$$

$$A = \frac{28,00,000 \times 0.06}{32.988 - 1} = \frac{1,68,000}{31.988} = ₹5,252.34$$

**Step 2 — Accumulated value at $t = 25$ (3.5 Marks)**

$$V_{25}^{\text{SF}} = C - A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{25} - 1}{i}$$

Compute $(1.06)^{25}$:
$$(1.06)^{20} \approx 3.2071, \quad (1.06)^{5} \approx 1.3382$$
$$(1.06)^{25} = 3.2071 \times 1.3382 \approx 4.2919$$

$$\text{SF Accumulated} = 5,252.34 \times \frac{4.2919 - 1}{0.06} = 5,252.34 \times 54.865 = ₹2,88,170$$

$$V_{25}^{\text{SF}} = 30,00,000 - 2,88,170 = \boxed{₹27,11,830}$$

**Interpretation:** Sinking Fund gives the *highest* value because the depreciation is offset by accumulated interest.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

> A residential property is purchased for **₹50,00,000** with an estimated life of **40 years** and a salvage value of **₹5,00,000**. The owner wishes to recover his investment along with interest at **5% p.a.**
>
> **(a)** Using the **Annuity Method**, determine the **annual amount the owner must recover** so that the property can be replaced at the end of its life. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Compare the four methods (SLM, WDV, Sinking Fund, Annuity) for the same property after **20 years** and identify which method yields the lowest present value. State one reason. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

### Part (a) — Annuity Method Annual Recovery (7 Marks)

**Given:** $C = 50,00,000, S = 5,00,000, n = 40, i = 0.05$.

**Component 1 — Annual Depreciation (2 Marks)**

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{50,00,000 - 5,00,000}{40} = \frac{45,00,000}{40} = ₹1,12,500$$

**Component 2 — Interest on Capital (Average) (2 Marks)**

$$I = \frac{C + S}{2} \cdot i = \frac{50,00,000 + 5,00,000}{2} \times 0.05 = 27,50,000 \times 0.05 = ₹1,37,500$$

**Component 3 — Sinking Fund Instalment (3 Marks)**

$$A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{45,00,000 \times 0.05}{(1.05)^{40} - 1}$$

$(1.05)^{40} = ((1.05)^{10})^4 \approx (1.6289)^4$

$(1.6289)^2 = 2.6533, \quad (1.6289)^4 = (2.6533)^2 = 7.0400$

$$A = \frac{2,25,000}{7.04 - 1} = \frac{2,25,000}{6.04} = ₹37,251.66$$

**Total Annual Annuity**

$$\text{Annual Recovery} = 1,12,500 + 1,37,500 + 37,251.66 = \boxed{₹2,87,251.66}$$

---

### Part (b) — Comparison of Four Methods at $t = 20$ (7 Marks)

**Method 1 — SLM (2 Marks)**

$$V_{20}^{\text{SLM}} = 50,00,000 - \frac{20 \times 45,00,000}{40} = 50,00,000 - 22,50,000 = ₹27,50,000$$

**Method 2 — WDV (rate = 1/40 × 100 = 2.5%) (2 Marks)**

$$V_{20}^{\text{WDV}} = 50,00,000 \times (1 - 0.025)^{20} = 50,00,000 \times (0.975)^{20}$$

$(0.975)^{20} \approx 0.6027$

$$V_{20}^{\text{WDV}} = 50,00,000 \times 0.6027 = ₹30,13,500$$

Wait — WDV here is *higher* because $d$ is small. This is normal.

**Method 3 — Sinking Fund (1.5 Marks)**

Accumulated SF at $t=20$: $A \cdot \dfrac{(1.05)^{20} - 1}{0.05} = 37,251.66 \times 29.778 = ₹11,09,180$

$$V_{20}^{\text{SF}} = 50,00,000 - 11,09,180 = ₹38,90,820$$

**Method 4 — Annuity (1.5 Marks)**

Capital recovered in 20 years via depreciation: $20 \times 1,12,500 = ₹22,50,000$

Interest on average capital over 20 years: $20 \times 1,37,500 = ₹27,50,000$

But the present value of remaining property at $t=20$ after recovery:
$$V_{20}^{\text{Annuity}} = 50,00,000 - 22,50,000 - 11,09,180 \approx ₹16,40,820$$

**Comparison Table**

| Method | Value at $t=20$ | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Sinking Fund | ₹38,90,820 | Highest |
| WDV | ₹30,13,500 | 2nd |
| SLM | ₹27,50,000 | 3rd |
| **Annuity** | **₹16,40,820** | **Lowest** |

> **Conclusion:** The **Annuity Method** yields the **lowest** present value because it accounts for *interest on locked capital* and *time value of money* — giving the most conservative (economically rigorous) estimate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> 1. **Don't forget the average capital term** in the Annuity Method: students often write $\frac{C}{2} \cdot i$ instead of $\frac{C+S}{2} \cdot i$. This loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Don't confuse WDV with SLM**: WDV's annual depreciation is *not* constant — it shrinks each year.
> 3. **Units consistency**: rate $i$ must be in decimal (0.05), not percentage (5%).
> 4. **Order of operations**: For Sinking Fund, compute $(1+i)^n$ *first* using log tables; don't round intermediate values to 2 decimals.
> 5. **Salvage value matters** only in SLM, Sinking Fund, and Annuity — **not in WDV** (where it falls out of the formula).

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Depreciation** = permanent loss in property value due to physical, functional, economic, or age-life causes.
- **Salvage Value (S)** = residual value at end of useful life.
- **Book Value (V_t)** = present value after $t$ years of depreciation.

### 🧠 Six Methods — Quick Recall
1. **SLM**: $V_t = C - t(C-S)/n$ — uniform loss.
2. **WDV**: $V_t = C(1-d)^t$ — exponential decay.
3. **Sinking Fund**: Annual contribution with compound interest.
4. **Annuity**: Depreciation + Interest + Sinking Fund = total recovery.
5. **Replacement Cost**: Use *current* prices, not historical.
6. **Service Life**: Component-wise statistical depreciation.

### 🎯 KTU Exam Strategy
- **Part A (3 marks)**: Definition + 4 causes of depreciation. Memorize verbatim.
- **Part B (14 marks)**: At least **one of the methods is guaranteed** — practice numericals.
- **Sub-part (a)**: 7 marks → usually one method's calculation.
- **Sub-part (b)**: 7 marks → comparison or sinking fund/annuity.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- Using $C \cdot i$ instead of $\frac{C+S}{2} \cdot i$ in Annuity Method.
- Forgetting to compute $(1+i)^n$ for long $n$ — use log tables.
- Confusing depreciation rate with interest rate.
- Treating $S$ as irrelevant in WDV.

### 💡 Real-World Applications
- **Income Tax**: WDV method (Companies Act 2013, Schedule II).
- **Insurance Valuation**: Replacement Cost Method.
- **Government Properties**: Service Life Method.
- **Lease/Rent Fixation**: Annuity Method.
- **Mortgage/Loan**: SLM (simple, transparent).
- **Heritage Buildings**: Sinking Fund (preserves capital for restoration).

### 📚 Standard Formulas — Final Memory Bank
$$\boxed{D_{\text{SLM}} = \frac{C - S}{n}} \quad \boxed{V_t^{\text{WDV}} = C(1-d)^t}$$
$$\boxed{A_{\text{SF}} = \frac{(C-S)i}{(1+i)^n-1}} \quad \boxed{A_{\text{Annuity}} = \frac{C-S}{n} + \frac{(C+S)i}{2} + \frac{(C-S)i}{(1+i)^n-1}}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Numerical Examples" in Valuation?

In the context of **PECET862 – Valuation of Real Properties (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, "Numerical Examples" refers to the **quantitative, arithmetic problem-solving exercises** that translate theoretical valuation principles (rental method, direct comparison, annuity, depreciation, belting, etc.) into computable answers involving **Years' Purchase (YP)**, **Annual Rent (AR)**, **Sinking Fund (SF)**, **Capitalized Value (CV)**, **Depreciated Value (DV)**, and **Land–Building allocation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
