> *Numerical valuation* is the application of standardized financial-mathematical formulae to estimate the **Present-Day Market Value (PDMV)**, **Capitalized Value**, or **Book Value** of a real property, by combining its **Net Annual Income (NAI)** with a **Years' Purchase factor** derived from the property's **economic life**, **rate of interest**, and **sinking-fund contribution**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you own a small shop that gives you **₹1,00,000 net profit every year** (after paying taxes, electricity, staff). If a buyer asks *"How much will you take for the entire shop as a going concern?"*, you cannot just multiply by 10 or 20 — you must consider:

1. **How many years will the shop keep earning?** (Life of building)
2. **What return does the buyer want on his capital?** (Interest rate)
3. **Should the buyer also set aside a small amount every year to rebuild the shop when it becomes old?** (Sinking Fund)

The "magic number" that combines all three is called the **Years' Purchase (YP)**. Multiplying the **Net Annual Rent** by this YP gives the **Capitalized Value** — the single lump-sum price a willing buyer should pay today. This is exactly what Module-2 numericals teach you to compute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant (KTU 2024 Standard):** Although interest rates vary, the I.S. 2741 Part IV and most KTU numericals default to a **standard rate of interest i = 9% per annum** (sometimes 6%, 8%, 10% or 12% as specified per problem) when not explicitly stated. The **Sinking Fund coefficient** is read from standard valuation tables (Arora & Rangwala).

### 1.3 Why this topic matters in KTU Exams

Numerical problems form **40–50 % of marks** in Part B questions of PECET862. The examiner expects you to:

- Identify the *correct method* (Rental / Annuity / Depreciation / Direct Comparison).
- Apply the *right formula* with consistent units (years, %, ₹).
- Show the **substitution of values** and **tabular YP-factor usage** clearly.
- Carry units of currency (₹) consistently in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Logic Flow

The valuation pipeline for every Module-2 numerical follows this **five-stage logic**:

1. **Step 1 – Identify the Income Stream**  
   Gross Rent → Less Outgoings (taxes, repairs, insurance, management) = **Net Annual Rent (NAR)**.

2. **Step 2 – Determine Economic Life (n)**  
   Life of the building in years (typically 60–100 for RCC, 40–60 for load-bearing, 20–40 for temporary).

3. **Step 3 – Pick the Capitalization Method**  
   - *Single-Rate YP Method* (no sinking fund)  
   - *Annuity Method* (interest + sinking fund)  
   - *Direct Comparison with known sales*  
   - *Depreciation Method* (for built-up property)

4. **Step 4 – Read YP from Tables / Compute It**  
   For Single-Rate: $YP = \dfrac{1}{i}$ (perpetuity) or $YP = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$ (for limited life with interest only).  
   For Annuity: $YP = \dfrac{1}{i + \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}}$ (or read directly from annuity tables).

5. **Step 5 – Compute Capitalized / Depreciated Value**  
   $CV = NAR \times YP$, or $DV = C - \left( \dfrac{C-S}{n} \right) \times t$, etc.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use the table below as the first-page reference during numerical solving. Memorize the boxed items.**

| # | Method | Governing Formula | Symbol Key | Units |
|---|--------|-------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | **Single-Rate YP (Perpetuity)** | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i}$ | $i$ = rate of interest (decimal) | years |
| 2 | **Single-Rate YP (Limited Life)** | $YP = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$ | $n$ = economic life | years |
| 3 | **Annuity YP** | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i + \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}}$ | combines interest + sinking fund | years |
| 4 | **Capitalized Value** | $CV = NAR \times YP$ | $NAR$ = Net Annual Rent | ₹ |
| 5 | **Sinking Fund Installment** | $SFI = C \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $C$ = capital to be recovered | ₹/yr |
| 6 | **Straight-Line Depreciation (Annual)** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $C$ = original cost, $S$ = scrap value, $n$ = life | ₹/yr |
| 7 | **Book Value after 't' years (SLM)** | $BV = C - D \cdot t$ | $t$ = elapsed years | ₹ |
| 8 | **Declining-Balance Method** | $BV_{n} = C \cdot (1 - r)^{n}$ | $r$ = constant % of BV written off | ₹ |
| 9 | **Land–Building Split by Ratio** | $\text{Land share} = \dfrac{L}{L+B} \times CV$ | $L$, $B$ = guided unit rates | ₹ |
| 10 | **Gross Multiplier (Direct Comparison)** | $Value = \text{Gross Rent} \times \text{GM}$ | $GM$ = Sales $\div$ Gross Rent | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol convention used everywhere below:**  
> $i$ = rate of interest per rupee per year (i.e., 9 % = 0.09)  
> $n$ = total economic life in years  
> $t$ = age already elapsed in years  
> $C$ = original/replacement cost  
> $S$ = scrap/salvage value at end of life

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banks & Housing Finance Companies (HFCs):** Use these exact YP/annuity formulae to decide *loan-to-value* ratios for mortgages.
- **Insurance Companies:** Use Straight-Line and Declining-Balance depreciation to settle fire/structural-damage claims.
- **Government Valuers (PWD, CPWD, KSEB, KWA):** Apply Annuity Method to value long-lease properties.
- **Income-Tax & Stamp Duty Departments:** Use these numericals to test whether the *circle-rate-declared value* matches the *income-capitalized value* — major source of property-tax litigation in Kerala.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

The following **five illustrative numericals** are exhaustive, KTU-exam-grade, and progressively complex. Each is solved with explicit substitution, intermediate steps, and final boxed answers.

---

### 📌 Numerical Example 1 — Single-Rate YP (Limited Life)

**Problem:**  
A residential building has a remaining economic life of **50 years**. The expected net annual rent is **₹ 1,20,000**. The investor expects a return of **9 % per annum** on his capital. Calculate the capitalized value of the property. *(Use single-rate YP formula.)*

**Given Data:**  
$NAR = ₹\,1,20,000$ ; $n = 50$ years ; $i = 0.09$

**Step 1 – Write the formula.**

$$YP_{\text{limited}} \;=\; \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$$

**Step 2 – Compute $(1+i)^{n}$.**

$$(1+0.09)^{50} \;=\; (1.09)^{50}$$

Using the standard valuation table for 9 % at 50 years:

$$(1.09)^{50} \;\approx\; 74.3575$$

**Step 3 – Substitute in numerator.**

$$(1+i)^{n} - 1 \;=\; 74.3575 - 1 \;=\; 73.3575$$

**Step 4 – Substitute in denominator.**

$$i \cdot (1+i)^{n} \;=\; 0.09 \times 74.3575 \;=\; 6.6922$$

**Step 5 – Compute YP.**

$$YP \;=\; \dfrac{73.3575}{6.6922} \;\approx\; 10.9615 \;\approx\; 10.96 \text{ years}$$

**Step 6 – Compute Capitalized Value.**

$$CV \;=\; NAR \times YP \;=\; 1,20,000 \times 10.96 \;=\; ₹\,13,15,200$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** **Capitalized Value = ₹ 13,15,200 (≈ ₹ 13.15 lakh)**

---

### 📌 Numerical Example 2 — Annuity Method (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:**  
A commercial property fetches a **gross monthly rent of ₹ 50,000**. The annual outgoings are estimated as:  
- Municipal tax = ₹ 40,000  
- Repairs = ₹ 25,000  
- Insurance = ₹ 5,000  
- Management = 10 % of gross rent  

The building has a **life of 60 years** and the investor wants **9 % return on capital** with a **sinking fund to recover the capital at 5 %** (combined sinking-fund interest rate). Find the **capitalized value of the building only**.

**Given Data:**  
Gross rent = $50,000 \times 12 = ₹\,6,00,000$  
$i_{\text{interest}} = 0.09$ ; $i_{sf} = 0.05$ ; $n = 60$ years

**Step 1 – Compute total outgoings.**

$$\text{Municipal tax} + \text{Repairs} + \text{Insurance} \;=\; 40,000 + 25,000 + 5,000 \;=\; ₹\,70,000$$

**Step 2 – Compute management charge.**

$$\text{Management} \;=\; 0.10 \times 6,00,000 \;=\; ₹\,60,000$$

**Step 3 – Compute Net Annual Rent (NAR).**

$$NAR \;=\; 6,00,000 - 70,000 - 60,000 \;=\; ₹\,4,70,000$$

**Step 4 – Read annuity YP from table (i = 0.09, n = 60).**

For pure 9 % interest over 60 years (single rate):

$$YP_{\text{interest only}} \;=\; \dfrac{(1.09)^{60}-1}{0.09 \times (1.09)^{60}} \;\approx\; 11.0480$$

(Standard table value: at 9 %, 60 yrs → YP = 11.05)

**Step 5 – Compute sinking-fund instalment coefficient.**

$$SFC \;=\; \dfrac{i_{sf}}{(1+i_{sf})^{n}-1} \;=\; \dfrac{0.05}{(1.05)^{60}-1}$$

From the sinking-fund table: $\dfrac{0.05}{(1.05)^{60}-1} \approx 0.002893$

**Step 6 – Combine interest + sinking fund rates to get Annuity YP.**

$$YP_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.09 + 0.002893} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.092893} \;\approx\; 10.7647$$

(Rounded to **10.76** from standard tables.)

**Step 7 – Compute Capitalized Value.**

$$CV \;=\; NAR \times YP_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; 4,70,000 \times 10.76 \;=\; ₹\,50,57,200$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** **Capitalized Value (Annuity Method) = ₹ 50,57,200 (≈ ₹ 50.57 lakh)**

---

### 📌 Numerical Example 3 — Straight-Line Depreciation Method

**Problem:**  
A godown was constructed 15 years ago at a cost of **₹ 25,00,000**. Its estimated total life is **50 years** and the scrap value at the end of life is **₹ 2,50,000**. Find (a) the annual depreciation, (b) the book value today, and (c) the book value after 30 years.

**Given:** $C = 25,00,000$ ; $S = 2,50,000$ ; $n = 50$ ; $t = 15$ (today), $t = 30$ (forecast).

**Step 1 – Annual depreciation (D).**

$$D \;=\; \dfrac{C - S}{n} \;=\; \dfrac{25,00,000 - 2,50,000}{50} \;=\; \dfrac{22,50,000}{50} \;=\; ₹\,45,000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 – Book Value today (t = 15).**

$$BV_{15} \;=\; C - D \times t \;=\; 25,00,000 - 45,000 \times 15 \;=\; 25,00,000 - 6,75,000 \;=\; ₹\,18,25,000$$

**Step 3 – Book Value after 30 years.**

$$BV_{30} \;=\; C - D \times 30 \;=\; 25,00,000 - 45,000 \times 30 \;=\; 25,00,000 - 13,50,000 \;=\; ₹\,11,50,000$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $D = ₹\,45,000$/yr ; $BV_{15} = ₹\,18,25,000$ ; $BV_{30} = ₹\,11,50,000$

---

### 📌 Numerical Example 4 — Declining-Balance (WDV) Method

**Problem:**  
A commercial building has an original cost of **₹ 40,00,000**. A constant rate of **5 % of the Written-Down Value (WDV)** is depreciated every year. Find the value of the building after (a) 5 years, (b) 10 years, and (c) what fraction of original cost remains after 20 years.

**Given:** $C = 40,00,000$ ; $r = 5\% = 0.05$ ; $t$ variable.

**Step 1 – Apply WDV formula.**

$$BV_{t} \;=\; C \cdot (1 - r)^{t}$$

**Step 2 – After 5 years.**

$$BV_{5} \;=\; 40,00,000 \times (0.95)^{5} \;=\; 40,00,000 \times 0.7738 \;=\; ₹\,30,95,200$$

**Step 3 – After 10 years.**

$$BV_{10} \;=\; 40,00,000 \times (0.95)^{10} \;=\; 40,00,000 \times 0.5987 \;=\; ₹\,23,94,800$$

**Step 4 – Fraction remaining after 20 years.**

$$(0.95)^{20} \;\approx\; 0.3585$$

So remaining fraction = **0.3585** (≈ 35.85 % of original cost).

$$BV_{20} \;=\; 40,00,000 \times 0.3585 \;=\; ₹\,14,34,000$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $BV_{5} = ₹\,30,95,200$ ; $BV_{10} = ₹\,23,94,800$ ; $BV_{20} = ₹\,14,34,000$

---

### 📌 Numerical Example 5 — Land & Building Split (Composite Valuation)

**Problem:**  
A property consists of **400 m² of land** and a **two-storey building with total plinth area 240 m²** (Ground + First floor = 120 m² each). Comparable sales in the locality give:  
- **Land rate = ₹ 8,000 / m²**  
- **Building (new) rate = ₹ 18,000 / m²** (without depreciation)  

The building is **20 years old**, total life **80 years**, scrap value **10 %** of new cost. Calculate the **present value of the property**.

**Step 1 – Land value.**

$$V_{\text{land}} \;=\; 400 \times 8,000 \;=\; ₹\,32,00,000$$

**Step 2 – Replacement cost of building (new).**

$$C_{\text{bldg}} \;=\; 240 \times 18,000 \;=\; ₹\,43,20,000$$

**Step 3 – Depreciation (SLM, n = 80, S = 10 % of C, t = 20).**

$$D \;=\; \dfrac{C - S}{n} \;=\; \dfrac{43,20,000 - 4,32,000}{80} \;=\; \dfrac{38,88,000}{80} \;=\; ₹\,48,600 \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 4 – Total accumulated depreciation for 20 years.**

$$\text{Depreciation}_{\text{20 yrs}} \;=\; 48,600 \times 20 \;=\; ₹\,9,72,000$$

**Step 5 – Present depreciated value of building.**

$$V_{\text{bldg}} \;=\; 43,20,000 - 9,72,000 \;=\; ₹\,33,48,000$$

**Step 6 – Total property value.**

$$V_{\text{total}} \;=\; V_{\text{land}} + V_{\text{bldg}} \;=\; 32,00,000 + 33,48,000 \;=\; ₹\,65,48,000$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** **Total Present Value of Property = ₹ 65,48,000**

---

### 📌 Numerical Example 6 — Gross-Multiplier (Direct Comparison) Method

**Problem:**  
Three comparable residential flats in the same locality were recently sold for **₹ 42,00,000**, **₹ 45,00,000** and **₹ 48,00,000**, fetching gross monthly rents of **₹ 35,000**, **₹ 37,500** and **₹ 40,000** respectively. The subject flat has a **gross monthly rent of ₹ 38,000**. Estimate its value by the Gross-Multiplier method.

**Step 1 – Compute Gross Multiplier (GM) for each comparable.**

$$GM_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{42,00,000}{35,000 \times 12} \;=\; \dfrac{42,00,000}{4,20,000} \;=\; 10.00$$

$$GM_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{45,00,000}{37,500 \times 12} \;=\; \dfrac{45,00,000}{4,50,000} \;=\; 10.00$$

$$GM_{3} \;=\; \dfrac{48,00,000}{40,000 \times 12} \;=\; \dfrac{48,00,000}{4,80,000} \;=\; 10.00$$

**Step 2 – Average GM.**

$$\overline{GM} \;=\; \dfrac{10.00 + 10.00 + 10.00}{3} \;=\; 10.00$$

**Step 3 – Apply to subject property's annual gross rent.**

$$\text{Annual Gross Rent} \;=\; 38,000 \times 12 \;=\; ₹\,4,56,000$$

$$V_{\text{subject}} \;=\; \overline{GM} \times \text{Annual Gross Rent} \;=\; 10 \times 4,56,000 \;=\; ₹\,45,60,000$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** **Estimated Value of Subject Flat = ₹ 45,60,000**

---

### 📌 Numerical Example 7 — Belting Method (Frontage-Based Land Valuation)

**Problem:**  
A rectangular plot of land has a **frontage of 20 m** and **depth of 30 m**. The standard depth adopted for the locality is **25 m**, and the rate of land is **₹ 12,000 / m²**. The **mean depth of the standard plot** is **24 m** (i.e., frontage = depth for the standard). Calculate the value of the plot using the **Belting (or Central-Frontage) method**, given the depth-variation table for 25 m standard depth:

| Depth zone | Width (m) | Index (%) |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| Up to 12.5 m | 12.5 | 120 % |
| 12.5 – 25.0 m | 12.5 | 100 % |
| Beyond 25 m | — | 80 % |

**Step 1 – Identify belts (each 12.5 m wide up to standard depth).**

- **Belt 1 (0 – 12.5 m):** Area = $20 \times 12.5 = 250\ \text{m}^{2}$ at 120 %.
- **Belt 2 (12.5 – 25.0 m):** Area = $20 \times 12.5 = 250\ \text{m}^{2}$ at 100 %.
- **Belt 3 (25.0 – 30.0 m):** Area = $20 \times 5.0 = 100\ \text{m}^{2}$ at 80 %.

**Step 2 – Compute weighted rate for each belt.**

$$\text{Belt 1 rate} \;=\; 12,000 \times 1.20 \;=\; ₹\,14,400 / \text{m}^{2}$$

$$\text{Belt 2 rate} \;=\; 12,000 \times 1.00 \;=\; ₹\,12,000 / \text{m}^{2}$$

$$\text{Belt 3 rate} \;=\; 12,000 \times 0.80 \;=\; ₹\,9,600 / \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 – Compute belt-wise value.**

$$V_{1} \;=\; 250 \times 14,400 \;=\; ₹\,36,00,000$$

$$V_{2} \;=\; 250 \times 12,000 \;=\; ₹\,30,00,000$$

$$V_{3} \;=\; 100 \times 9,600 \;=\; ₹\,9,60,000$$

**Step 4 – Total plot value.**

$$V_{\text{total}} \;=\; 36,00,000 + 30,00,000 + 9,60,000 \;=\; ₹\,75,60,000$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** **Plot Value (Belting Method) = ₹ 75,60,000**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Valuation Workflow (Mermaid)

The following flow chart shows **how a typical Module-2 numerical is decoded** step-by-step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the Problem] --> B{Is it a Rented Property?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute Gross Rent and Outgoings]
    C --> D[Net Annual Rent NAR = Gross - Outgoings]
    D --> E{Single-rate YP or Annuity YP?}
    E -- Single-rate --> F[Use YP = 1/i or Limited-life YP formula]
    E -- Annuity --> G[Compute SFC then YP = 1/(i + SFC)]
    F --> H[Capitalized Value = NAR x YP]
    G --> H
    B -- No --> I{Is it an Owner-Occupied Building?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Use Replacement Cost minus Depreciation]
    J --> K{SLM or WDV Method?}
    K -- SLM --> L[D = C - S / n ; BV = C - D x t]
    K -- WDV --> M[BV = C x 1 - r to power t]
    L --> N[Present Value = Land + Depreciated Building]
    M --> N
    I -- No --> O{Is it a Land-Only Sale?}
    O -- Yes --> P{Belting or Direct Comparison?}
    P -- Belting --> Q[Split Plot into Belts using Local Index Table]
    Q --> R[Sum Belt Areas x Belt Rates]
    P -- Direct --> S[Gross Multiplier Method: GM = Sale / Annual Gross Rent]
    S --> T[Value = GM x Subject Annual Gross Rent]
    R --> U[End: Final Capitalized Value]
    T --> U
    H --> U
    N --> U
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of "Capitalized Value" Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Property] --> L[Land Component]
    P --> B[Building Component]
    L --> LR[Land Rate x Area]
    LR --> LV[Land Value]
    B --> RC[Replacement Cost New]
    RC --> D[Less: Depreciation]
    D --> BV[Depreciated Building Value]
    BV --> CV[Capitalized Value]
    LV --> CV
    CV --> F[Final Market Value]
```

### 4.3 Choice-of-Method Decision Tree (Exam Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given Data?] --> Q2{Is Net Annual Rent given?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Use Rental Method]
    R1 --> R2{Life given?}
    R2 -- Yes --> R3[Annuity YP]
    R2 -- No --> R4[Single-rate YP at Perpetuity]
    Q2 -- No --> S1{Is Replacement Cost given?}
    S1 -- Yes --> T1[Use Depreciation Method]
    T1 --> T2{Equal annual write-off?}
    T2 -- Yes --> T3[Straight Line Method]
    T2 -- No --> T4[Declining Balance WDV]
    S1 -- No --> U1{Frontage Depth given?}
    U1 -- Yes --> V1[Belting Method]
    U1 -- No --> W1[Use Comparable Sales / Direct Comparison]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `Q1`, `R3`, `V1`, etc.) — no reserved keywords used. All labels are raw uppercase text without bold/italic markers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECET862, Module 2]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define **Years' Purchase (YP)**. State its formula for a property of **limited life** at rate of interest *i* per rupee per annum.

**Model Answer:**  
Years' Purchase (YP) is the **capitalized value of a rupee of net annual income** received for a stipulated number of years. It is the multiplier that converts Net Annual Rent into Capitalized Value.

$$YP \;=\; \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$$

where *i* = rate of interest (decimal) and *n* = economic life in years. *[(3 Marks – definition 1, formula 2)]*

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** Distinguish between the **Straight-Line Method** and the **Written-Down Value Method** of depreciation with one formula each.

**Model Answer:**  
In the **Straight-Line Method (SLM)**, an equal amount $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ is written off every year, so annual depreciation is constant but book value decreases linearly.  

In the **Written-Down Value (WDV) / Declining-Balance Method**, a *fixed percentage* of the *diminishing book value* is written off each year, so depreciation is high initially and tapers off: $BV_{t} = C \cdot (1-r)^{t}$. *[(3 Marks – SLM 1.5, WDV 1.5)]*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style, Module 2]*

**Q (A). (a)** A property is expected to yield a **net annual rent of ₹ 1,80,000**. The investor requires an **interest return of 8 %** and a **sinking fund at 4 %** to be created for replacement after the **economic life of 60 years**. Calculate the **capitalized value** of the property using the **Annuity Method**. *[(7 Marks)]*

**Model Solution:**

Given: $NAR = ₹\,1,80,000$ ; $i = 0.08$ ; $i_{sf} = 0.04$ ; $n = 60$ yrs.

**Step 1 – Interest-only YP at 8 %, 60 yrs.**

$$YP_{\text{int}} \;=\; \dfrac{(1.08)^{60}-1}{0.08 \times (1.08)^{60}} \;\approx\; 12.376$$

**Step 2 – Sinking Fund Coefficient at 4 %, 60 yrs.**

$$SFC \;=\; \dfrac{0.04}{(1.04)^{60}-1} \;\approx\; 0.004202$$

**Step 3 – Annuity YP.**

$$YP_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.08 + 0.004202} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.084202} \;\approx\; 11.876$$

**Step 4 – Capitalized Value.**

$$CV \;=\; 1,80,000 \times 11.876 \;=\; ₹\,21,37,680$$

> ✅ **Answer:** **CV = ₹ 21,37,680 (≈ ₹ 21.38 lakh)**

---

**Q (A). (b)** An industrial shed was constructed at a cost of **₹ 18,00,000**. Its **scrap value** is **₹ 1,00,000** and useful life is **40 years**. Using the **Straight-Line Method**, compute the **book value at the end of 12 years** and the **annual sinking-fund instalment** required to replace the building (interest on sinking fund = 5 %). *[(7 Marks)]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Annual depreciation (SLM).**

$$D \;=\; \dfrac{18,00,000 - 1,00,000}{40} \;=\; \dfrac{17,00,000}{40} \;=\; ₹\,42,500/\text{yr}$$

**Step 2 – Book Value at t = 12.**

$$BV_{12} \;=\; 18,00,000 - 42,500 \times 12 \;=\; 18,00,000 - 5,10,000 \;=\; ₹\,12,90,000$$

**Step 3 – Sinking-Fund Instalment to recover capital ₹ 18,00,000 in 40 yrs at 5 %.**

$$SFI \;=\; 18,00,000 \times \dfrac{0.05}{(1.05)^{40}-1}$$

From the SFC table: at 5 %, 40 yrs, SFC ≈ 0.00828.

$$SFI \;=\; 18,00,000 \times 0.00828 \;\approx\; ₹\,14,904/\text{yr}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $BV_{12} = ₹\,12,90,000$ ; $SFI = ₹\,14,904/\text{yr}$ *(≈ ₹ 14,900/yr)*

---

#### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Module 2]*

**Q (B). (a)** A property is to be valued by the **Rental Method**. The following particulars are given:

- Gross monthly rent = **₹ 75,000**  
- Municipal tax = **12 %** of gross rent  
- Repairs & maintenance = **₹ 50,000 p.a.**  
- Insurance premium = **₹ 12,000 p.a.**  
- Management charges = **8 %** of gross rent  
- YP (annuity) at 9 % interest and 3 % sinking fund, life 70 years = **10.65**  

Compute the **capitalized value** of the property. *[(7 Marks)]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Compute gross annual rent.**

$$GR \;=\; 75,000 \times 12 \;=\; ₹\,9,00,000$$

**Step 2 – Compute outgoings.**

$$\text{Municipal tax} \;=\; 0.12 \times 9,00,000 \;=\; ₹\,1,08,000$$

$$\text{Management} \;=\; 0.08 \times 9,00,000 \;=\; ₹\,72,000$$

$$\text{Total outgoings} \;=\; 1,08,000 + 50,000 + 12,000 + 72,000 \;=\; ₹\,2,42,000$$

**Step 3 – Net Annual Rent.**

$$NAR \;=\; 9,00,000 - 2,42,000 \;=\; ₹\,6,58,000$$

**Step 4 – Capitalized Value.**

$$CV \;=\; 6,58,000 \times 10.65 \;=\; ₹\,70,07,700$$

> ✅ **Answer:** **CV = ₹ 70,07,700 (≈ ₹ 70.08 lakh)**

---

**Q (B). (b)** The owner of a plot measuring **25 m × 40 m** wants to value it using the **Belting Method**. The standard depth for the locality is **25 m**, and the rate of land is **₹ 6,000 / m²**. The depth-index table (per cent of standard) is:

| Depth zone | Index |
|------------|-------|
| First quarter (up to 6.25 m) | 130 % |
| Second quarter (6.25 – 12.5 m) | 120 % |
| Third quarter (12.5 – 18.75 m) | 110 % |
| Fourth quarter (18.75 – 25.0 m) | 100 % |
| Beyond 25 m | 80 % |

Calculate the **value of the plot**. *[(7 Marks)]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify belts.** Plot depth = 40 m. Standard depth = 25 m. So plot extends 15 m beyond the standard.

| Belt | Depth range | Width (m) | Area (25 m × w) (m²) | Index | Rate (₹/m²) | Value (₹) |
|------|-------------|-----------|----------------------|-------|-------------|-----------|
| I    | 0 – 6.25    | 6.25      | $25 \times 6.25 = 156.25$  | 1.30  | 7,800 | 12,18,750 |
| II   | 6.25 – 12.5 | 6.25      | $25 \times 6.25 = 156.25$  | 1.20  | 7,200 | 11,25,000 |
| III  | 12.5 – 18.75| 6.25      | $25 \times 6.25 = 156.25$  | 1.10  | 6,600 | 10,31,250 |
| IV   | 18.75 – 25.0| 6.25      | $25 \times 6.25 = 156.25$  | 1.00  | 6,000 |  9,37,500 |
| V    | 25 – 40     | 15.0      | $25 \times 15.0 = 375.0$   | 0.80  | 4,800 | 18,00,000 |

**Step 2 – Sum the values.**

$$V_{\text{total}} \;=\; 12,18,750 + 11,25,000 + 10,31,250 + 9,37,500 + 18,00,000 \;=\; ₹\,61,12,500$$

> ✅ **Answer:** **Plot Value (Belting) = ₹ 61,12,500 (≈ ₹ 61.13 lakh)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚩 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert monthly rent to annual rent** (multiply by 12) — costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Using gross rent instead of net rent** in the Capitalized-Value step (forgetting to subtract all outgoings) — costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up interest rate and sinking-fund rate** in Annuity Method — must be kept **distinct**; final YP denominator is $(i + SFC)$.
> 4. **Not stating units (₹, years, m²)** in the final answer — **½ mark deducted per answer**.
> 5. **Skipping the "Given Data" line** at the start of the solution — board examiners consider this a **mark-losing habit** in valuation papers.
> 6. **Using `|` symbol for absolute value in YP tables** — only on the answer sheet, not in the answer booklet tables. In *your* notes, use `\vert`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🟢 **Capitalized Value = Net Annual Rent × Years' Purchase** (the master equation for Module 2).
- 🟢 **Net Annual Rent = Gross Rent − All Outgoings** (tax + repairs + insurance + management + vacancy).
- 🟢 **Single-Rate YP (perpetuity) = 1/i** — use when *no* life is given.
- 🟢 **Single-Rate YP (limited life) = [(1+i)ⁿ − 1] / [i·(1+i)ⁿ]** — use when life is given and *no* sinking fund is needed.
- 🟢 **Annuity YP = 1 / (i + SFC)** — the KTU default when sinking fund is mentioned.
- 🟢 **SFC = i_sf / [(1+i_sf)ⁿ − 1]** — read directly from standard tables for 4 %, 5 %, 6 % etc.
- 🟢 **SLM Depreciation = (C − S)/n** — linear, equal annual write-off.
- 🟢 **WDV Depreciation: BV_t = C·(1 − r)^t** — geometric decline.
- 🟢 **Belting Method** divides the plot into horizontal strips of *standard quarter depth* and applies a **frontage index**.
- 🟢 **Gross Multiplier Method** uses the formula $GM = \dfrac{\text{Comparable Sale Price}}{\text{Annual Gross Rent}}$; the *average* GM is then multiplied by the subject's annual gross rent.
- 🟢 **Always read the problem twice** to identify: (i) method, (ii) given life, (iii) interest & sinking-fund rates, (iv) gross vs. net rent.
- 🟢 **State all assumptions** (e.g., *outgoings are constant*, *rent is collected regularly*) — earns **viva-style grace marks** even in written exams.
- 🟢 **Practice valuation tables** for 6 %, 8 %, 9 %, 10 % and 12 % at common lives (40, 50, 60, 80, 100 yrs) for quick substitution.
- 🟢 **Cross-check the answer** with the *Cost Method* and *Market Method* if data permits — multi-method answers fetch higher marks in KTU 2024 Scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building FSI, Plot Coverage, Types of Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Building FSI, Plot Coverage, Types of Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Floor Space Index (FSI) — The Building Density Compass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSI (Floor Space Index)**, also called **FAR (Floor Area Ratio)** in many international codes, is the **ratio of the total built-up area of all floors** to the **total area of the plot** on which the building stands.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
FSI is a regulatory tool used by urban local bodies to control the **bulk and density of construction** permissible on a given plot of land.

$$
\text{FSI} = \frac{\text{Total Built-up Area of All Floors (sq.m)}}{\text{Total Plot Area (sq.m)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> - FSI is **dimensionless** (a pure ratio).
> - Higher FSI = More construction allowed = More density.
> - FSI is **NOT** the same as the *number of floors* — it depends on floor plate sizes.

## 1.2 Plot Coverage (Ground Coverage) — The Land-Footprint Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plot Coverage** is the percentage of the **plot area that can be physically covered** by the building footprint at the **ground / plinth level**.

$$
\text{Plot Coverage (\%)} = \frac{\text{Ground Floor Built-up Area}}{\text{Total Plot Area}} \times 100
$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🍕 Analogy — The Pizza & Box Method:**
> - Imagine a **cardboard box (your plot)** and you are allowed to place **stacks of pizzas (floors)** inside it.
> - **Plot Coverage** = the size of the *base pizza* relative to the box bottom.
> - **FSI** = the *total area of all pizzas stacked* divided by the box bottom.
> - If the municipality says "FSI = 2.0", and your box is 600 sq.m, you can stack pizzas whose **total area = 1200 sq.m**. You can do this with 2 floors of 600 sq.m each, or 3 floors of 400 sq.m each — your choice!

## 1.4 Types of Building Structures — The Skeletal Classification

| S.No | Structure Type | Load Path | Common Use |
|------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | **Load Bearing Structure** | Walls → Foundation | Low-rise (≤ 2 floors) |
| 2 | **RCC Framed Structure** | Slab → Beam → Column → Foundation | Mid to High-rise (3–30 floors) |
| 3 | **Steel Framed Structure** | Slab → Beam → Steel Column → Foundation | Industrial, Warehouses, High-rise |
| 4 | **Composite Structure** | Steel + RCC hybrid | Modern high-rises |
| 5 | **Pre-engineered / Prefabricated** | Modular panels | Industrial sheds, Site offices |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Built-up Area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Built-up area for FSI calculation typically includes:
> 1. **Plinth area** of all floors
> 2. **Staircase rooms**, lift wells, machine rooms
> 3. **Basement floor area** (in some municipal rules)
> 4. **Balconies** (counted at 50% in many Kerala municipalities)
> 5. **Garages, servant quarters** (often counted)

**Deductions** (NOT counted in FSI):
> - Open-to-sky terraces
> - Loft areas less than 1.5 m height
> - Parking areas in basements (in select cities)
> - Pump rooms, meter rooms (service areas)

## 2.2 Factors Influencing FSI Permission

### 2.2.1 Road Width (The Single Biggest Factor)
In Kerala (KMBR / Town & Country Planning rules), FSI is **directly linked** to the width of the **abutting road**:

| Road Width (m) | Permissible FSI (Residential) | Permissible FSI (Commercial) |
|:--------------:|:----------------------------:|:----------------------------:|
| Below 4.0      | 1.00                         | 1.20                         |
| 4.0 – 6.0      | 1.30                         | 1.50                         |
| 6.0 – 9.0      | 1.75                         | 2.00                         |
| 9.0 – 12.0     | 2.00                         | 2.25                         |
| 12.0 – 15.0    | 2.25                         | 2.50                         |
| 15.0 – 24.0    | 2.50                         | 2.75                         |
| Above 24.0     | 3.00                         | 3.50                         |

### 2.2.2 Premium FSI
> [!NOTE]
> **Premium FSI** is the **additional FSI** that a developer can purchase from the municipal authority (by paying a premium charge, often 25–50% of the land value per extra unit of FSI). This is a revenue-generation tool for local bodies.

$$
\text{Premium Charge} = \text{Additional FSI} \times \text{Plot Area} \times \text{Land Rate} \times \text{Premium \%}
$$

### 2.2.3 TDR (Transferable Development Rights)
- TDR is generated when land is **acquired by the government** for public purpose.
- The owner receives **certificates** that can be **sold** in the open market, allowing buyers to use that extra FSI on their own plots.
- TDR is **NOT** consumed in the area from which it originates.

## 2.3 Plot Coverage Rules (Kerala KMBR Indicative Values)

| Land Use | Permissible Plot Coverage |
|----------|:------------------------:|
| Residential (Single Dwelling) | **60%** |
| Residential (Apartment) | **45–50%** |
| Commercial | **50%** |
| Industrial | **40%** |
| Public / Semi-Public | **35%** |

> [!WARNING]
> - Coverage **decreases** with increasing road width for narrow plots to ensure light & ventilation.
> - Remaining area must be left as **open space** for setbacks, landscaping, parking.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $FSI = \dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | Floor Space Index |
| 2 | $GC(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Ground Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}} \times 100$ | Ground Coverage |
| 3 | $\text{Max. Total Floor Area} = FSI \times \text{Plot Area}$ | Permissible built-up |
| 4 | $\text{Max. No. of Floors} = \dfrac{FSI \times \text{Plot Area}}{\text{Ground Floor Area}}$ | Possible floor count |
| 5 | $\text{Open Area Required} = \text{Plot Area} - \text{Ground Floor Area}$ | Mandatory open space |
| 6 | $\text{Premium FSI Cost} = \Delta FSI \times \text{Plot Area} \times \text{Rate}$ | TDR/Premium FSI cost |
| 7 | $\text{Setback} = f(\text{Plot depth, road width, height})$ | Building line distance |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Valuation

> [!NOTE]
> In **real property valuation**, the *maximum permissible FSI* directly determines:
> 1. **Highest & Best Use (HBU)** of a plot.
> 2. **Replacement Cost** of buildings.
> 3. **Land value** — higher FSI permission = higher land value.
> 4. **Feasibility & Rent Multiplication** for commercial properties.

A valuer must always check the **current local Building Rules** (KMBR 2019 for Kerala) since FSI norms are revised periodically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Basic FSI & Coverage Calculation

> **Given Data:**
> - Plot size: **20 m × 30 m** = 600 sq.m
> - Road width: **12 m** (abutting road)
> - Proposed construction: **Ground + 3 Floors**, each of plinth area = **180 sq.m**
> - Local rules: FSI for 12 m road (residential) = **2.25**, Max coverage = **60%**

### Step 1 — Calculate Total Plot Area

$$
A_{plot} = 20 \times 30 = 600 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Total Built-up Area Proposed

$$
A_{built} = \text{Ground Floor} + \text{First} + \text{Second} + \text{Third}
$$
$$
A_{built} = 180 + 180 + 180 + 180 = 720 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Actual FSI Consumed

$$
FSI_{actual} = \frac{A_{built}}{A_{plot}} = \frac{720}{600} = 1.20
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Actual Plot Coverage

$$
GC = \frac{180}{600} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Permissible Maximum Built-up

$$
A_{max} = FSI_{permitted} \times A_{plot} = 2.25 \times 600 = 1350 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Maximum Possible Floors

$$
n_{max} = \frac{A_{max}}{A_{gf}} = \frac{1350}{180} = 7.5 \;\Rightarrow\; 7 \text{ floors}
$$

### Step 7 — Check Setback Compliance

> [!NOTE]
> For 600 sq.m plot with 12 m road in Kerala (KMBR 2019 indicative):
> - **Front setback** = 5 m (for height up to 15 m)
> - **Side setback** = 1.5 m (each side, with min 3 m total)
> - **Rear setback** = 2 m
> - **Buildable footprint** = (20 − 3) × (30 − 7) = 17 × 23 = 391 sq.m
>
> **Status**: Proposed 180 sq.m ≤ 391 sq.m ✅ COMPLIANT

### Step 8 — Verdict

| Parameter | Proposed | Permissible | Status |
|-----------|:--------:|:-----------:|:------:|
| FSI | 1.20 | 2.25 | ✅ OK |
| Coverage | 30% | 60% | ✅ OK |
| Footprint | 180 sq.m | 391 sq.m | ✅ OK |

**Conclusion:** The proposal is **fully compliant** and the developer can even construct **3 more floors** (subject to height & structural stability) using the unutilized FSI of (2.25 − 1.20) = **1.05** units.

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Premium FSI Calculation

> **Given:**
> - Plot area = 1000 sq.m
> - Normal permitted FSI = 2.0
> - Developer wants to use FSI = 2.5
> - Guideline value of land = ₹25,000 / sq.m
> - Premium charge = 30% of guideline value

### Step 1 — Additional FSI Required

$$
\Delta FSI = 2.5 - 2.0 = 0.5
$$

### Step 2 — Additional Floor Area Permitted

$$
A_{extra} = \Delta FSI \times A_{plot} = 0.5 \times 1000 = 500 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

### Step 3 — Premium Charge Calculation

$$
C_{premium} = A_{extra} \times \text{Rate} \times \text{Premium \%}
$$
$$
C_{premium} = 500 \times 25000 \times 0.30 = 37,50,000
$$

### Step 4 — Final Cost Payable

$$
C_{premium} = \text{₹ 37.5 Lakhs}
$$

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Comparative Structure Cost Analysis

> A 4-storey residential building (G+3) of 400 sq.m / floor, plot 1000 sq.m, in Kochi.

**Cost of construction (2024 indicative KSRB rates for Kerala):**

| S.No | Structure Type | Rate (₹ / sq.m of built-up) | Total Built-up = 1600 sq.m | Total Cost (₹ Cr) |
|:----:|----------------|:---------------------------:|:--------------------------:|:-----------------:|
| 1 | Load Bearing (Brick) | ₹ 18,000 | 1600 × 18000 | **₹ 2.88 Cr** |
| 2 | RCC Framed | ₹ 22,500 | 1600 × 22500 | **₹ 3.60 Cr** |
| 3 | Steel Framed | ₹ 28,000 | 1600 × 28000 | **₹ 4.48 Cr** |
| 4 | Pre-engineered | ₹ 20,000 | 1600 × 20000 | **₹ 3.20 Cr** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuer's Insight:** While Load Bearing is the cheapest for low-rise, it is **structurally limited to G+1/G+2** in seismic zones. RCC Framed is the **most common** for residential and gives maximum FSI utilization because of thinner walls (higher carpet-to-built-up ratio).

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix — Choosing the Right Structure

| Criterion | Load Bearing | RCC Framed | Steel Framed | Prefab |
|-----------|:------------:|:----------:|:------------:|:------:|
| Max. Floors | 2 | 30+ | 50+ | 4 |
| Wall Thickness | 230–300 mm | 115–150 mm | 100–125 mm | 100 mm |
| Carpet Area Efficiency | Low | High | Very High | Medium |
| Construction Speed | Slow | Medium | Fast | Very Fast |
| Seismic Resistance | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good |
| Cost per sq.m (₹) | Lowest | Medium | High | Medium |
| Maintenance | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| Future Modification | Difficult | Easy | Easy | Difficult |
| Typical Use | Old houses | Apartments | Industries | Site offices |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — FSI Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plot Dimensions] --> B[Calculate Plot Area]
    B --> C[Identify Abutting Road Width]
    C --> D[Lookup Permissible FSI from KMBR Table]
    D --> E[Calculate Max Permissible Floor Area]
    E --> F[Check Proposed Construction Plan]
    F --> G[Calculate Actual FSI Consumed]
    G --> H{Actual FSI vs Permissible FSI}
    H -->|Within Limit| I[Compliant]
    H -->|Exceeds| J[Apply for Premium FSI or TDR]
    J --> K[Pay Premium Charge]
    K --> L[Revised Compliance Check]
    I --> M[Proceed for Plan Approval]
    L --> M
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Structure Selection Decision Engine

    A[Project Inputs: Height, Span, Budget, Time] --> B{Number of Floors?}
    B -->|1 to 2| C[Load Bearing Structure]
    B -->|3 to 15| D[RCC Framed Structure]
    B -->|15 to 50| E{Use Type?}
    B -->|Above 50| F[Steel or Composite Structure]
    E -->|Residential| D
    E -->|Commercial Office| G[RCC or Composite]
    E -->|Industrial| H[Steel Portal Frame]
    C --> I[Validate: Soil Bearing Capacity]
    D --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    F --> I
    I --> J[Final Structural Design]
```

## 4.3 Layered Diagram — Building Hierarchy for FSI

        P1[Plot Area: 600 sq.m]
    end

        U1[Floor 1: 360 sq.m]

        U3[Floor 3: 360 sq.m]

    G1 --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> U3
```

## 4.4 Comparative Mapping — Structure Type vs FSI Realization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load Bearing\nMax 2 Floors] -->|Realises ~50% of permitted FSI| X[Lower FSI Utilisation]
    B[RCC Framed\nMax 30 Floors] -->|Realises ~90% of permitted FSI| Y[Optimal FSI Realisation]
    C[Steel Framed\nMax 50 Floors] -->|Realises ~95% of permitted FSI| Y
    D[Prefab\nMax 4 Floors] -->|Realises ~70% of permitted FSI| X
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Floor Space Index (FSI). Explain its significance in property valuation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** FSI is the ratio of total built-up area of all floors to the total plot area, expressed as a dimensionless number.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $FSI = \dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$
- **Significance in valuation (1 mark):** FSI determines the **maximum construction potential** of a plot. Higher permissible FSI increases the **highest and best use value**, **replacement cost**, and **resale value** of the property. It is a critical input in the **market comparison approach** and **development method** of valuation.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Plot Coverage and FSI with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Plot Coverage (1 mark):** It is the ratio of the *ground floor built-up area* to the *plot area*, expressed as a percentage. It controls the building footprint and ensures open space, light, and ventilation.
- **FSI (1 mark):** It is the ratio of *total built-up area of all floors* to the *plot area*. It controls the total bulk/volume of construction.
- **Example (1 mark):** On a 500 sq.m plot with 60% coverage and FSI 1.5, ground floor = 300 sq.m, and total permissible built-up = 750 sq.m (can be distributed over 2 to 3 floors).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A rectangular plot of size 25 m × 40 m abuts a 15 m wide road. The Kerala Municipal Building Rules permit FSI of 2.50 and a maximum plot coverage of 55% for residential buildings in this zone. Calculate:
(i) Maximum permissible total built-up area.
(ii) Maximum permissible ground floor area.
(iii) Maximum number of floors possible (with equal floor plate size).
(iv) Unutilized FSI in terms of floor area.

**(b) [7 marks)** Explain the **five major types of building structures** used in Kerala. Compare RCC framed and Load bearing structures with respect to **cost, height, carpet area efficiency, and seismic resistance** in a tabular form.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** Plot = 25 × 40 = 1000 sq.m, Road = 15 m, FSI = 2.50, Max Coverage = 55%

**(i) Maximum permissible total built-up area:**

$$
A_{max} = FSI \times A_{plot} = 2.50 \times 1000 = 2500 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

**(ii) Maximum permissible ground floor area:**

$$
A_{gf} = 55\% \times 1000 = 550 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

**(iii) Maximum number of floors (equal floor plate):**

$$
n = \frac{A_{max}}{A_{gf}} = \frac{2500}{550} = 4.545 \;\Rightarrow\; 4 \text{ full floors}
$$

**(iv) Unutilized FSI in floor area:**

$$
A_{unutilized} = 2500 - (4 \times 550) = 2500 - 2200 = 300 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Five Major Building Structures in Kerala:**
1. **Load Bearing** — Brick / Stone walls carry floor & roof loads. Used up to G+2.
2. **RCC Framed** — Reinforced Cement Concrete columns & beams form skeleton. Most common in apartments and high-rises.
3. **Steel Framed** — Rolled steel sections carry loads. Used in warehouses, industrial sheds, and high-rise commercial buildings.
4. **Composite** — RCC + Steel combination for large spans and high-rises.
5. **Pre-engineered (PEB) / Prefabricated** — Factory-made components assembled on site. Used in industrial buildings.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Load Bearing | RCC Framed |
|-----------|:------------:|:----------:|
| Construction Cost | Lower (~₹18,000/sq.m) | Medium (~₹22,500/sq.m) |
| Maximum Height | 2 floors | 30+ floors |
| Wall Thickness | 230–300 mm | 115–150 mm |
| Carpet Area Efficiency | ~70% | ~82–85% |
| Seismic Resistance | Poor (brittle walls) | Good (ductile frame) |
| Construction Time | Slow | Medium |
| Future Modification | Difficult | Easy |

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A developer owns a 800 sq.m plot on a 12 m road. Permitted FSI = 2.25, normal coverage = 60%. The developer wants to construct G+4 with each floor having plinth area of 200 sq.m. Check the proposal for compliance. If non-compliant, calculate the **premium FSI cost** at 30% of guideline value of ₹20,000 / sq.m.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **step-by-step methodology** a valuer follows to determine the **maximum construction potential** of a given plot in Kerala.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** Plot = 800 sq.m, Road = 12 m, FSI = 2.25, Coverage = 60%, Floors = G+4 (5 floors), each = 200 sq.m, Guideline value = ₹20,000/sq.m, Premium = 30%.

**Step 1 — Total Built-up Proposed:**

$$
A_{prop} = 5 \times 200 = 1000 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual FSI Consumed:**

$$
FSI_{act} = \frac{1000}{800} = 1.25
$$

**Step 3 — Permissible FSI:**

$$
FSI_{perm} = 2.25 \quad (\text{for 12 m road})
$$

**Step 4 — Permissible Built-up:**

$$
A_{max} = 2.25 \times 800 = 1800 \; \text{sq.m}
$$

**Step 5 — Plot Coverage Check:**

$$
GC = \frac{200}{800} \times 100 = 25\% \quad \leq 60\% \;\; ✅
$$

**Step 6 — Compliance Verdict:**
- FSI 1.25 ≤ 2.25 ✅ **COMPLIANT**
- Unutilized FSI = 2.25 − 1.25 = 1.00 unit = 1.00 × 800 = **800 sq.m of additional construction possible**.

**Step 7 — Hypothetical Premium FSI Case (if developer wanted G+8, each 200 sq.m = 1800 sq.m):**

$$
\Delta FSI = \frac{1800 - 1440}{800} = 0.45 \; \text{units}
$$

(Were 1440 = 2.25 × 800 = permitted built-up)

> [!NOTE]
> Since the proposal here is **already compliant**, the premium FSI cost is **NOT** required. If the developer had wanted to construct beyond 1800 sq.m of total area, then:
> $$
> C_{premium} = 100 \times 20000 \times 0.30 = ₹ 6,00,000
> $$
> (for an additional 100 sq.m of construction).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block Diagram — Valuer's Step-by-Step Methodology:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Plot Boundaries and Area] --> B[Step 2: Identify Abutting Road Width]
    B --> C[Step 3: Refer to Local KMBR 2019 Rules]
    C --> D[Step 4: Extract Permissible FSI for Zone and Road]
    D --> E[Step 5: Extract Permissible Plot Coverage]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply Height Restrictions and Setback Norms]
    F --> G[Step 7: Calculate Max Permissible Total Floor Area]
    G --> H[Step 8: Calculate Max Permissible Footprint]
    H --> I[Step 9: Derive Maximum Number of Floors]
    I --> J[Step 10: Compute Maximum Construction Potential in Rs.]
    J --> K[Step 11: Compare with Existing Construction]
    K --> L[Step 12: Determine Highest and Best Use]
    L --> M[Final Valuation Report]
```

**Verbal Explanation:**
1. The valuer first measures the plot using revenue records / physical survey.
2. The abutting road width is ascertained from the municipality / PWD records.
3. The applicable Building Rules (KMBR 2019 for Kerala) are identified.
4. From Schedule 1 of KMBR, FSI is picked based on road width and zone.
5. Plot coverage and setback norms are noted.
6. The maximum total floor area = FSI × Plot Area.
7. The maximum number of floors = (Max floor area) / (Ground floor area).
8. The total construction cost is then estimated using current PWD/KSRB rates.
9. The valuation is finally derived using the **development method** or **comparable sale method**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing FSI with number of floors** — Many students write "FSI = 4" meaning 4 floors. Wrong! FSI is the *ratio*, not the count. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting to include ALL floors** — In multi-floor problems, every floor (including basement in some rules) must be summed up for FSI. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Not verifying the road-width-based FSI table** — Kerala rules are *road-width dependent*, not a flat 1.5 or 2.0. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Plot Coverage expressed as decimal instead of %** — Always write coverage in percentage, not decimal. (Lose 0.5 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up Load Bearing with RCC** — These are fundamentally different structural systems; examiners test this frequently. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **No units in final answer** — Always write **sq.m** for area, **%** for coverage, and the FSI as a pure number. (Lose 0.5 mark per instance)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Building FSI, Plot Coverage, Types of Structure**

- ✅ **FSI = Total Built-up Area / Plot Area** (dimensionless, NOT in %)
- ✅ **Plot Coverage = Ground Floor Area / Plot Area × 100** (always in %)
- ✅ FSI in Kerala is **road-width based** — wider road = higher FSI permitted
- ✅ Typical Kerala residential FSI: **1.5 to 3.0** depending on road width
- ✅ Typical residential plot coverage: **45–60%**
- ✅ **Premium FSI** allows extra construction by paying a fee
- ✅ **TDR (Transferable Development Rights)** allows shifting of unused FSI to another plot
- ✅ **Load Bearing** = walls carry load, max 2 floors, brick/stone
- ✅ **RCC Framed** = columns and beams carry load, most common in modern Kerala apartments
- ✅ **Steel Framed** = high-rises, industrial, warehouses
- ✅ **Composite** = hybrid RCC + Steel for high-rises
- ✅ **Pre-engineered / Prefab** = fast construction, mostly industrial
- ✅ RCC has **higher carpet-area efficiency** due to thinner walls
- ✅ Steel framed is **most seismic-resistant**
- ✅ Valuer must always consult **current KMBR / Town Planning Rules** for FSI norms
- ✅ Unutilized FSI directly impacts **Highest & Best Use (HBU)** in valuation
- ✅ Common valuation mistake: confusing **Plinth Area** with **Carpet Area** (Plinth > Carpet by ~15–25%)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Valuation for Open Land: Comparative, Abstractive and Belting Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Valuation for Open Land: Comparative, Abstractive and Belting Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Open Land Valuation

**Open land** (also called *undeveloped land*, *bare land*, or *virgin land*) refers to a parcel of land that has not been improved with permanent structures such as buildings, roads, or utility infrastructure. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, open land primarily includes agricultural plots, vacant residential plots, undeveloped commercial sites, and rural parcels awaiting future development.

The valuation of open land is a foundational skill in the real-estate profession because:
- It establishes the **base value** from which developed property values are derived.
- It is heavily relied upon for **stamp-duty assessment**, **land acquisition compensation**, **agricultural lending**, and **capital gains tax** computations.
- It is a frequent 14-mark KTU ESE question under Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** *Valuation of open land is the art of estimating the present fair market value of an undeveloped parcel using scientific methods, based on the principle of substitution, anticipation, and comparison.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Principal Methods

The KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET862) explicitly prescribes three classical techniques for the valuation of open land:

| # | Method | Core Logic |
|---|--------|------------|
| 1 | **Comparative Method** (Direct Comparison) | Value is derived from recent, verifiable sale prices of **similar neighbouring plots** |
| 2 | **Abstractive Method** (Abstractive Development / Hypothetical Subdivision) | Value of developed land is **abstracted** (subtracted) by the cost of future development, leaving the bare-land value |
| 3 | **Belting Method** | An irregular or deep plot is divided into **concentric belts/strips** of varying depth from the road, with each belt weighted by its relative utility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** In KTU 2024 ESE, examiners often ask: *"Which method is most appropriate when no recent comparable sales are available?"* — The answer is the **Abstractive Method** (because it depends on the *potential* value, not *historical* sales). Conversely, *"Which method is best suited for large agricultural holdings with road frontage?"* — The answer is the **Belting Method**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine three friends trying to guess the price of an unmarked t-shirt in a thrift store:

- **Friend 1 (Comparative Method):** Walks around, finds three *similar* t-shirts with visible price tags, and **averages** their prices. *"This shirt looks like those — so it should cost about the same."*
- **Friend 2 (Abstractive Method):** Knows the t-shirt *will* be sold at a boutique next month for ₹2,000. He subtracts the shop's likely *markup and refurbishment cost* of ₹500 to estimate the *raw cloth* is worth about ₹1,500. *"The future is known — work backwards."*
- **Friend 3 (Belting Method):** Recognises the shirt is a *long* piece of cloth where the *front portion* (next to the collar) is more valuable than the *tail end*. He prices the shirt in **strips** — each strip gets a per-metre rate that *decreases* with distance from the collar. *"Position matters — closer to the road = more valuable."*

These three mental models precisely mirror the three KTU-prescribed open-land valuation techniques.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical & Economic Metrics (Bolded Constants)

The following standard metrics are universally used in KTU valuation problems and must be memorised:

- **1 Ground = 2,400 sq.ft** (used in Kerala real estate)
- **1 Cent = 435.6 sq.ft** (1/100 of an acre)
- **1 Acre = 43,560 sq.ft = 100 cents = 4,840 sq.yards**
- **1 Hectare = 2.471 acres = 10,000 m²**
- **Standard plot depth for residential use = 60 ft to 90 ft** (beyond which a "rear land" devaluation is applied)
- **Typical belting widths** = 30 ft, 60 ft, 90 ft, 120 ft (or as determined by the valuer based on road frontage)
- **Fair Return on Land (FROL)** = 5% to 6% per annum of market value (used in the Belting Method)
- **Year of Purchase (YOP)** vs **Year of Valuation (YOV)** — appreciation rates typically range from **5% to 15% per annum** depending on locality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — The Comparative Method (Direct Comparison)

### 2.1.1 Theory & Guiding Principles

The Comparative Method rests on the **Principle of Substitution**: *"A rational buyer will not pay more for a property than the cost of acquiring an equally desirable substitute."*

**Operational Logic (Bullet-Structured):**
- Identify **at least 3 to 6 recent transactions** of *similar* open plots in the *same* or *comparable* locality.
- Adjust each comparable for differences in **size, shape, road frontage, soil type, accessibility, zoning, time of sale, and seller motivation**.
- Apply **weighted averaging** (most recent & most similar transactions carry the highest weight).
- Arrive at a **unit rate per sq.ft (or per cent or per acre)** which is then applied to the subject plot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule of Thumb:** Sales older than **2 years** from the date of valuation should be escalated (compounded) to the present value using a documented local appreciation rate. Sales within the last 6 months generally require **no time adjustment** if market is stable.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Procedure

1. **Inspection of Subject Property** — Note location, area, frontage, depth, topography, road width, accessibility.
2. **Survey of Comparables** — Collect data from sub-registrar records, broker ledgers, and direct enquiries.
3. **Physical Verification** — Visit each comparable; verify it has *similar* attributes.
4. **Adjustment Grid** — Construct a comparison table adjusting each comparable for differences.
5. **Time Adjustment** — Escalate older sales to the valuation date.
6. **Final Rate Determination** — Compute the indicative unit rate.
7. **Apply to Subject** — Multiply unit rate × area of subject plot.

### 2.1.3 Key Formulas

Let:
- $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$ = Sale price of each comparable
- $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ = Area of each comparable
- $r$ = annual appreciation rate (in decimal)
- $n_i$ = number of years between comparable sale and date of valuation
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to comparable $i$ (with $\sum w_i = 1$)

**Escalation formula (compound):**

$$S_i^{\text{(present)}} = S_i \times (1 + r)^{n_i}$$

**Weighted unit rate (₹ per sq.ft):**

$$\text{Unit Rate} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{S_i^{\text{(present)}}}{A_i}$$

**Total value of subject plot:**

$$V_{\text{subject}} = \text{Unit Rate} \times A_{\text{subject}}$$

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — The Abstractive Method (Abstractive Development Method)

### 2.2.1 Theory

The Abstractive Method is the **"value-after-development minus development-cost"** approach. It is most useful when:
- No recent comparable sales are available (rural/greenfield areas), **or**
- The land has clear, identifiable development potential (e.g., a residential layout approval).

**The Abstractive Equation (the heart of this method):**

$$V_{\text{bare land}} = V_{\text{developed land}} - C_{\text{development}}$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{developed land}}$ = estimated selling price of the *fully developed* property (e.g., per-plinth-area rate × total buildable area).
- $C_{\text{development}}$ = total cost incurred to bring the land to the developed state — includes **landscaping, road laying, drainage, electricity, water supply, compound wall, registration, brokerage, and developer's profit**.

### 2.2.2 Subdivision Approach (Hypothetical Subdivision)

A common KTU application is the **Hypothetical Subdivision Method**:
1. Hypothetically divide the plot into a certain number of *smaller* building plots (as per local building rules).
2. Estimate the **post-development sale price** of each smaller plot.
3. Sum all such post-development prices to get the **gross development value (GDV)**.
4. Subtract **all development costs and the developer's margin (typically 15%–25% of GDV)** to obtain the **bare-land value**.

### 2.2.3 Key Formulas

**Bare land value (per plot):**

$$V_{\text{land}} = \text{GDV} - C_{\text{dev}} - D_{\text{profit}}$$

**Developer's profit** (often expressed as a percentage of GDV):

$$D_{\text{profit}} = p \times \text{GDV} \quad \text{where } p = 0.15 \text{ to } 0.25$$

**Per-area bare-land value:**

$$\text{Unit Rate (₹/sq.ft)} = \frac{V_{\text{land}}}{A_{\text{subject}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to **deduct the developer's profit and risk margin**. The valuation is *not* just GDV minus construction cost. Deducting only the construction cost will **over-estimate** the land value, leading to a 2-3 mark loss in ESE.

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — The Belting Method (Belting or Depth Method)

### 2.3.1 Theory

The Belting Method addresses a real-world fact: **land value is not uniform across a plot**. The strip of land *fronting the road* is far more valuable than a strip at the rear.

**Operational Logic:**
- The plot is divided into **a number of parallel belts (strips)** of equal width, parallel to the road.
- Each belt is assigned a **value percentage** (relative to the first belt's value).
- A *first belt* (or *plinth belt*) — usually the front-most 60 to 90 feet — is taken as **100%**.
- Subsequent belts are valued at **progressively decreasing percentages** (e.g., 80%, 60%, 40%, 20%).

### 2.3.2 Standard Belting Percentages (RICS / KTU Reference)

A widely used KTU-recommended set of percentages is:

| Belt Number | Depth from Road | Value (% of First Belt) |
|:-----------:|:----------------|:-----------------------:|
| 1st Belt | 0 to 60 ft (or 0 to 90 ft) | **100%** |
| 2nd Belt | 60 to 120 ft | **80%** |
| 3rd Belt | 120 to 180 ft | **60%** |
| 4th Belt | 180 to 240 ft | **40%** |
| 5th Belt | 240 to 300 ft | **20%** |
| 6th Belt | 300 ft onwards | **Negligible / NIL** |

> [!NOTE]
> These percentages are *not universal*; the KTU examiner expects you to state clearly that *"the belting percentages depend on locality, road width, and intended use."*

### 2.3.3 Key Formula

Let:
- $L$ = total road frontage (in ft) of the plot
- $w_b$ = width of belt $b$ (in ft)
- $p_b$ = value percentage of belt $b$ (in decimal, e.g., 0.80)
- $R$ = unit rate of the first belt (₹ per sq.ft)

**Area of belt $b$:**

$$A_b = L \times w_b$$

**Value of belt $b$:**

$$V_b = A_b \times p_b \times R$$

**Total land value:**

$$V_{\text{land}} = R \times L \times \sum_{b=1}^{n} (p_b \cdot w_b)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Core Equation | Typical Use Case | Critical Adjustment |
|:------:|:--------------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| **Comparative** | $V = \text{Unit Rate} \times A$ | Urban/semi-urban residential | Time-escalation $(1+r)^{n}$ |
| **Abstractive** | $V_{\text{land}} = \text{GDV} - C_{\text{dev}} - D_{\text{profit}}$ | Greenfield/development potential | Deduct developer's margin (15–25%) |
| **Belting** | $V = R \times L \times \sum (p_b \cdot w_b)$ | Deep, irregular plots | First-belt = 100%, decreasing tail |
| **Escalation** | $S_{\text{now}} = S_{\text{past}} \times (1+r)^{n}$ | Used in Comparative | $r = 0.05$ to $0.15$ |
| **Yields/FROL** | Net Yield = $\dfrac{\text{Net Annual Rent}}{\text{Land Value}}$ | Cross-check only | $5\% \text{ to } 6\%$ of land value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Tip:** Memorise the three core equations and the *standard belting percentages* — these appear in nearly every KTU Module 3 question.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Comparative Method

### Problem Statement
A valuer is asked to estimate the present market value of a **vacant residential plot of 10 cents (4,356 sq.ft)** in a suburb of Kochi, with road frontage of 40 ft. The following comparable sales are gathered:

| Comparable | Sale Price (₹) | Area (sq.ft) | Sale Date |
|:----------:|:--------------:|:------------:|:---------:|
| C1 | 65,00,000 | 4,800 | 18 months ago |
| C2 | 78,00,000 | 5,200 | 8 months ago |
| C3 | 55,00,000 | 4,000 | 30 months ago |

Assume annual appreciation = **8% per annum**. Weight the most recent sale (C2) most heavily. Use weights $w_1 = 0.30, w_2 = 0.50, w_3 = 0.20$.

### Step 1 — Time Escalation (Compound)

Apply $S_i^{\text{present}} = S_i \times (1.08)^{n_i}$ where $n_i$ is years from sale to valuation date (assume valuation is *today*).

**For C1** (1.5 years ago):

$$S_1^{\text{present}} = 65,00,000 \times (1.08)^{1.5}$$

$$(1.08)^{1.5} = e^{1.5 \ln 1.08} = e^{1.5 \times 0.07696} = e^{0.11544} \approx 1.1224$$

$$S_1^{\text{present}} = 65,00,000 \times 1.1224 = 72,95,600$$

**For C2** (0.667 years ago):

$$S_2^{\text{present}} = 78,00,000 \times (1.08)^{0.667}$$

$$(1.08)^{0.667} = e^{0.667 \times 0.07696} = e^{0.05133} \approx 1.0527$$

$$S_2^{\text{present}} = 78,00,000 \times 1.0527 = 82,11,060$$

**For C3** (2.5 years ago):

$$S_3^{\text{present}} = 55,00,000 \times (1.08)^{2.5}$$

$$(1.08)^{2.5} = 1.08^2 \times 1.08^{0.5} = 1.1664 \times 1.0392 \approx 1.2122$$

$$S_3^{\text{present}} = 55,00,000 \times 1.2122 = 66,67,100$$

### Step 2 — Compute Escalated Unit Rates

$$\text{Unit Rate}_1 = \frac{72,95,600}{4,800} = 1,519.92 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

$$\text{Unit Rate}_2 = \frac{82,11,060}{5,200} = 1,579.05 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

$$\text{Unit Rate}_3 = \frac{66,67,100}{4,000} = 1,666.78 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

### Step 3 — Weighted Average Unit Rate

$$\text{Unit Rate}_{\text{subject}} = 0.30 \times 1519.92 + 0.50 \times 1579.05 + 0.20 \times 1666.78$$

$$= 455.98 + 789.53 + 333.36 = 1,578.87 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

$$\text{Unit Rate}_{\text{subject}} \approx 1,579 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

### Step 4 — Value of Subject Plot

$$V_{\text{subject}} = 1,579 \times 4,356 = 68,78,124$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{subject}} \approx \text{₹ 68.78 Lakhs}}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Abstractive Method

### Problem Statement
A developer owns **1 acre (43,560 sq.ft)** of agricultural land on the outskirts of Thrissur. Approval has been received to develop a **residential layout of 24 plots**, each plot being 1,200 sq.ft with internal roads, drainage, electricity, and water. The expected post-development **sale price is ₹ 2,500 per sq.ft** of plot area. Estimated development costs are **₹ 30,00,000** for infrastructure, and the developer expects a **20% margin** on GDV. Find the bare-land value.

### Step 1 — Compute Gross Development Value (GDV)

$$\text{GDV} = 24 \text{ plots} \times 1,200 \text{ sq.ft} \times 2,500 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

$$\text{GDV} = 24 \times 1,200 \times 2,500 = 7,20,00,000$$

$$\text{GDV} = \text{₹ 7.20 Crores}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Developer's Profit

$$D_{\text{profit}} = 0.20 \times 7,20,00,000 = 1,44,00,000$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Abstractive Formula

$$V_{\text{land}} = \text{GDV} - C_{\text{dev}} - D_{\text{profit}}$$

$$V_{\text{land}} = 7,20,00,000 - 30,00,000 - 1,44,00,000$$

$$V_{\text{land}} = 5,46,00,000$$

### Step 4 — Per-Acre and Per-Cent Value

$$\text{Per acre} = 5,46,00,000 \div 1 = 5,46,00,000$$

$$\text{Per cent} = 5,46,00,000 \div 100 = 5,46,000$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{land}} = \text{₹ 5.46 Crores (i.e., ₹ 5.46 Lakhs per cent)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Belting Method

### Problem Statement
A rectangular plot in Thiruvananthapuram has dimensions **200 ft (frontage, along road) × 300 ft (depth)**. The standard rate for the **first belt (0–60 ft depth)** in the locality is **₹ 3,000 per sq.ft**. Use the standard KTU belting percentages: 100%, 80%, 60%, 40%, 20%. Compute the total value of the plot.

### Step 1 — Determine Belt Widths

The depth of 300 ft divided into belts of 60 ft width gives **5 belts**.

| Belt | Depth Range | Width (ft) | % Value |
|:----:|:-----------:|:----------:|:-------:|
| 1st | 0–60 | 60 | 100% |
| 2nd | 60–120 | 60 | 80% |
| 3rd | 120–180 | 60 | 60% |
| 4th | 180–240 | 60 | 40% |
| 5th | 240–300 | 60 | 20% |

### Step 2 — Compute Area of Each Belt

Frontage $L = 200$ ft. Each belt area:

$$A_b = 200 \times 60 = 12,000 \text{ sq.ft}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Value of Each Belt

$$V_1 = 12,000 \times 1.00 \times 3,000 = 3,60,00,000$$

$$V_2 = 12,000 \times 0.80 \times 3,000 = 2,88,00,000$$

$$V_3 = 12,000 \times 0.60 \times 3,000 = 2,16,00,000$$

$$V_4 = 12,000 \times 0.40 \times 3,000 = 1,44,00,000$$

$$V_5 = 12,000 \times 0.20 \times 3,000 = 72,00,000$$

### Step 4 — Sum All Belts

$$V_{\text{land}} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + V_4 + V_5$$

$$V_{\text{land}} = 3,60,00,000 + 2,88,00,000 + 2,16,00,000 + 1,44,00,000 + 72,00,000$$

$$V_{\text{land}} = 10,80,00,000$$

### Step 5 — Cross-Check with Simple Formula

$$V_{\text{land}} = R \times L \times \sum (p_b \cdot w_b)$$

$$\sum (p_b \cdot w_b) = (1.00 \times 60) + (0.80 \times 60) + (0.60 \times 60) + (0.40 \times 60) + (0.20 \times 60)$$

$$= 60 + 48 + 36 + 24 + 12 = 180 \text{ ft (effective)}$$

$$V_{\text{land}} = 3,000 \times 200 \times 180 = 10,80,00,000 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{land}} = \text{₹ 10.80 Crores}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Depth Concept:** The "effective depth" of 180 ft (vs. actual 300 ft) reflects the diminishing marginal utility of land with distance from the road. This is a powerful KTU concept often asked in viva voce.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Scenario | Best Method | Reason |
|:---------|:-----------:|:-------|
| Dense urban area, plenty of recent sales | **Comparative** | Reliable market data is available |
| Rural agricultural land, no recent sales | **Abstractive** | Uses *potential* GDV rather than historical sales |
| Deep plot with narrow road frontage | **Belting** | Accounts for depth-based depreciation |
| Plot intended for immediate development | **Abstractive** | Best captures development economics |
| Government acquisition of small urban plot | **Comparative** | Stamp-duty data is the legal benchmark |
| Long, narrow, irregular plot | **Belting** | Only method that handles geometric irregularity |
| Mixed-use area with multiple comparables | **Comparative + Belting hybrid** | Use Comparative to set first-belt rate, then Belting for the rest |
