# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*State the principle on which the Comparative Method of valuation is based. Mention any two situations where this method is most suitable.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The Comparative Method is based on the **Principle of Substitution**, which states that a rational buyer will not pay more for a property than the cost of acquiring an equally desirable alternative.
- (1 mark) It is most suitable in **well-developed urban and semi-urban areas** where frequent transactions of similar open plots are recorded.
- (1 mark) It is also suitable for **standard residential plots** in approved layouts where sale data is publicly available via sub-registrar records.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is the Belting Method of valuation? Why is it preferred for deep, narrow plots?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) The Belting Method is a depth-based valuation technique in which an irregular or deep plot is divided into **concentric belts (strips) of uniform width** parallel to the road, with each belt assigned a *decreasing value percentage* (typically 100%, 80%, 60%, 40%, 20%) as the distance from the road increases.
- (1.5 marks) It is preferred for deep, narrow plots because **utility diminishes with distance from the road** (a strip 300 ft from the road has poor accessibility, lower visibility, and reduced commercial potential). The Belting Method captures this *non-uniform value distribution*, which a flat per-sq.ft rate would unfairly ignore.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]**

*(a)* Explain in detail the **Abstractive Method of valuation** of open land. State the situations where this method is most appropriate. (7 marks)

*(b)* A developer owns 2 acres (86,720 sq.ft) of agricultural land on the outskirts of Ernakulam. He plans to convert this into a residential layout of **40 plots**, each plot being 1,500 sq.ft. The expected **post-development sale price is ₹ 2,800 per sq.ft**. The infrastructure development cost is estimated at **₹ 60,00,000**, and the developer expects a **20% profit on GDV**. Determine the **bare-land value** using the Abstractive Method and the **rate per cent**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

1. (2 marks) **Definition:** The Abstractive Method (also called the Hypothetical Subdivision or Development Method) estimates the value of bare open land by **deducting the cost of future development and the developer's profit margin from the estimated post-development (gross development) value** of the property.
2. (2 marks) **Procedure (5 steps):**
   - Step 1: Identify the *highest and best use* of the land (residential, commercial, industrial, etc.).
   - Step 2: Hypothetically divide the land into building plots per local building rules.
   - Step 3: Estimate the **Gross Development Value (GDV)** — total revenue from selling all plots.
   - Step 4: Deduct all **development costs** (roads, drainage, electricity, water, compound wall, registration, brokerage, marketing).
   - Step 5: Deduct **developer's profit** (typically 15% to 25% of GDV). The remainder is the bare-land value.
3. (1 mark) **Formula:** $V_{\text{land}} = \text{GDV} - C_{\text{dev}} - D_{\text{profit}}$
4. (2 marks) **Suitability:** (i) When recent comparable sales are unavailable (greenfield/rural areas), (ii) When the land has a clearly defined, permissible development potential, (iii) For valuation of large parcels being acquired for township projects.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Calculate GDV:**

$$\text{GDV} = 40 \text{ plots} \times 1,500 \text{ sq.ft} \times 2,800 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

$$\text{GDV} = 40 \times 1,500 \times 2,800 = 16,80,00,000$$

$$\text{GDV} = \text{₹ 16.80 Crores}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Developer's Profit (20% of GDV):**

$$D_{\text{profit}} = 0.20 \times 16,80,00,000 = 3,36,00,000$$

**Step 3 — Apply Abstractive Formula:**

$$V_{\text{land}} = \text{GDV} - C_{\text{dev}} - D_{\text{profit}}$$

$$V_{\text{land}} = 16,80,00,000 - 60,00,000 - 3,36,00,000$$

$$V_{\text{land}} = 12,84,00,000$$

**Step 4 — Compute Rate per Cent:**

$$\text{Total cents} = 2 \text{ acres} \times 100 = 200 \text{ cents}$$

$$\text{Rate per cent} = \frac{12,84,00,000}{200} = 6,42,000$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{land}} = \text{₹ 12.84 Crores; Rate per cent} = \text{₹ 6,42,000}}$$

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

*(a)* Discuss the **Comparative Method** of valuation in detail. List the **essential adjustments** a valuer must make to the comparable sale prices. (7 marks)

*(b)* A vacant plot of **5,000 sq.ft** in Thrissur town is to be valued. Three recent comparable sales are available, with the following data. Assume an annual appreciation of **7% per annum** and apply weights **$w_1 = 0.40, w_2 = 0.40, w_3 = 0.20$**.

| Comparable | Sale Price (₹) | Area (sq.ft) | Sold |
|:----------:|:--------------:|:------------:|:----:|
| C1 | 80,00,000 | 5,000 | 1 year ago |
| C2 | 70,00,000 | 4,500 | 2 years ago |
| C3 | 95,00,000 | 5,500 | 6 months ago |

Compute the **present market value of the subject plot** using the Comparative Method. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

1. (1 mark) **Definition:** The Comparative Method (Direct Comparison) values a property by **comparing it with similar, recently sold properties in the same locality**, after making appropriate adjustments for differences in attributes and time.
2. (1 mark) **Underlying Principle:** The Principle of Substitution.
3. (1 mark) **Procedure:** Inspection of subject → collection of comparables → physical verification → adjustment grid → time adjustment → final rate → application to subject.
4. (4 marks) **Essential Adjustments:**
   - **Time Adjustment** — Escalate older sale prices to the present using $(1+r)^n$.
   - **Size Adjustment** — Larger plots often sell at a *lower* per-sq.ft rate (size discount).
   - **Frontage & Depth Adjustment** — Wider frontage increases value.
   - **Shape & Topography Adjustment** — Rectangular, level plots command a premium.
   - **Location/Neighbourhood Adjustment** — Better infrastructure, schools, transport → higher value.
   - **Zoning & Use-Permissibility Adjustment** — Residential vs. commercial vs. agricultural.
   - **Seller Motivation Adjustment** — Distress sales (liquidation, court attachment) understate value.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Time Escalation using $S_i \times (1.07)^{n_i}$:**

**C1** (1 year ago):
$$S_1^{\text{present}} = 80,00,000 \times (1.07)^1 = 80,00,000 \times 1.07 = 85,60,000$$

**C2** (2 years ago):
$$S_2^{\text{present}} = 70,00,000 \times (1.07)^2 = 70,00,000 \times 1.1449 = 80,14,300$$

**C3** (0.5 year ago):
$$S_3^{\text{present}} = 95,00,000 \times (1.07)^{0.5} = 95,00,000 \times 1.0344 = 98,26,800$$

**Step 2 — Escalated Unit Rates:**

$$\text{UR}_1 = \frac{85,60,000}{5,000} = 1,712 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

$$\text{UR}_2 = \frac{80,14,300}{4,500} = 1,780.96 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

$$\text{UR}_3 = \frac{98,26,800}{5,500} = 1,786.69 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Average Unit Rate:**

$$\text{UR}_{\text{subject}} = (0.40 \times 1712) + (0.40 \times 1780.96) + (0.20 \times 1786.69)$$

$$= 684.80 + 712.38 + 357.34 = 1,754.52 \text{ ₹/sq.ft}$$

**Step 4 — Final Value:**

$$V_{\text{subject}} = 1,754.52 \times 5,000 = 87,72,600$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{subject}} \approx \text{₹ 87.73 Lakhs}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 3 Open-Land Valuation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Time Escalation in the Comparative Method** (3-mark loss). Older sales **must** be escalated to the valuation date using the compound formula $(1+r)^n$. A sale 2 years ago with 8% appreciation should be multiplied by $(1.08)^2 = 1.1664$, **not** simply 1.16.
>
> 2. **Omitting the Developer's Profit in the Abstractive Method** (2-3 mark loss). The Abstractive Equation is $V = \text{GDV} - C_{\text{dev}} - D_{\text{profit}}$. Many students stop at GDV − Cost, which **over-states** the land value by 15–25%.
>
> 3. **Using Equal Belt Widths but Unequal Percentages in Belting** — A subtle but important check: the **effective depth** in the Belting Method is $\sum (p_b \cdot w_b)$, which should be **less than** the actual depth. If your effective depth exceeds the actual depth, you have applied the percentages in the wrong order (front belt should be 100%, not rear).
>
> 4. **Mixing units** — Always convert cents ↔ sq.ft ↔ acres ↔ sq.m explicitly. A common error: 1 cent = 435.6 sq.ft, not 400 sq.ft. **435.6** is the KTU-expected conversion.
>
> 5. **Not stating the Principle of Substitution explicitly** in the Comparative Method — Examiners award 1 mark for naming the underlying principle. Do not skip this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Bullet Checklist

- ✅ **Three methods for open-land valuation** = Comparative, Abstractive, Belting.
- ✅ **Comparative Method** = Substitution Principle → recent sales → time escalation → weighted unit rate × area.
- ✅ **Abstractive Method** = $V_{\text{land}} = \text{GDV} - C_{\text{dev}} - D_{\text{profit}}$ → use when no comparables exist.
- ✅ **Belting Method** = divide plot into strips of equal width, value first belt at 100%, subsequent belts at decreasing percentages.
- ✅ **Standard belting percentages** = 100%, 80%, 60%, 40%, 20% (memorise this sequence).
- ✅ **Time escalation formula** = $S_{\text{present}} = S_{\text{past}} \times (1+r)^n$.
- ✅ **Developer's profit** = 15% to 25% of GDV (most common = 20%).
- ✅ **Effective depth in Belting** = $\sum (p_b \cdot w_b)$, always less than actual depth.
- ✅ **Unit conversions** = 1 cent = 435.6 sq.ft; 1 acre = 43,560 sq.ft = 100 cents; 1 ground = 2,400 sq.ft; 1 hectare = 2.471 acres.
- ✅ **Fair Return on Land (FROL)** = 5% to 6% per annum (used as a cross-check via the rental yield method, not for open land directly).
- ✅ **Highest and Best Use** concept is the foundation of the Abstractive Method.
- ✅ **KTU expects the valuer to state the underlying principle** for each method (Substitution for Comparative, Anticipation for Abstractive, Contribution/Productivity for Belting).

### Final One-Line Takeaway

> *"The Comparative Method asks 'what did neighbours pay?', the Abstractive Method asks 'what will the developed land sell for, minus costs?', and the Belting Method asks 'how does value change with distance from the road?' — master all three to master Module 3 of PECET862."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Examples
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Examples

# Numerical Examples – Valuation of Real Properties

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Examples in Valuation** are structured computational exercises that apply the mathematical models of property valuation—Years' Purchase (YP), Sinking Fund, Depreciation, and Capitalized Value—to real estate scenarios. They consolidate theoretical formulas into quantifiable engineering decisions.

### Formal Definition

A **numerical valuation problem** is a deterministic computational exercise wherein the *present worth*, *future worth*, *annual sinking fund contribution*, *depreciated value*, or *capitalized rent* of a real property (land, building, or both) is computed using accepted financial mathematics, as prescribed under the KTU 2024 syllabus framework.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of valuation as **"translating future money into today's terms."** A building that earns ₹10,000 per month *forever* is not worth ₹10,000—it's worth a *lump sum* today that, if invested at an interest rate, would generate that ₹10,000 monthly. The translator is the **Years' Purchase (YP)** factor. Numerical examples simply put numbers into this translator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation Problems:**
> - **Rate of Interest (r)** → Usually **8% to 12% per annum** (unless stated otherwise).
> - **YP (Years' Purchase)** → A multiplier; dimensionless.
> - **Scrap/Salvage Value (S)** → Typically **10% of original cost** if not given.
> - **Life of Building (n)** → Usually **60 to 100 years** for residential, **40 to 60 years** for commercial.

### Key Terminology Recap

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| C | Original Cost of Building | ₹ |
| S | Salvage / Scrap Value | ₹ |
| n | Useful Life | Years |
| r | Rate of Interest | % p.a. |
| YP | Years' Purchase | Number |
| A | Annual Sinking Fund | ₹/year |
| I | Annual Income / Rent | ₹/year |
| CV | Capitalized Value | ₹ |
| D | Depreciation | ₹ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Logic Chain of Property Valuation

Every numerical problem in Module 3 follows a **5-step logic chain**:

1. **Identify the Property Type** → Land-only, Building-only, or Land + Building.
2. **Choose the Valuation Method** → Rental, Comparison, Profit, or Cost method.
3. **Compute the Gross Income / Rent** → Based on market data.
4. **Deduct Outgoings** → Repairs, taxes, insurance, management (~25–40% of gross).
5. **Apply YP or Depreciation Model** → To obtain capitalized or depreciated value.

### Why YP Exists (The "Why")

> If you invest **₹X** today at interest rate **r**, the annual interest you receive is **X × r**. Conversely, to get an annual income of **₹I** in perpetuity, you need **X = I / r**. This X is the **Capitalized Value**, and **1/r is the YP**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | YP in Perpetuity | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Land (indefinite life) |
| 2 | YP for Limited Period (n years) | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ | Building (finite life) |
| 3 | Capitalized Value | $CV = \text{Net Income} \times YP$ | Core valuation equation |
| 4 | Annual Sinking Fund (A) | $A = S \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Replacement reserve |
| 5 | Straight Line Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | Equal yearly amount |
| 6 | Book Value (SLM) | $V_n = C - nD = C - \dfrac{n(C-S)}{n}$ | At year n |
| 7 | Declining Balance (WDV) | $D_n = C \cdot b(1-b)^{n-1}$ | b = depreciation rate |
| 8 | Sinking Fund Depreciation | $D = (C - S) \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Compound interest version |
| 9 | Total Annual Cost of Building | $\text{TAC} = I_{interest} + I_{sinking} + I_{repairs} + I_{taxes}$ | For rental method |
| 10 | Gross Rent Multiplier | $GRM = \dfrac{\text{Sale Price}}{\text{Annual Rent}}$ | Quick comparison tool |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of RERA-compliant valuations, bank mortgage assessments, property tax disputes, and government acquisition (LA Act) compensation** in India. Every civil engineer in valuation consultancy uses them daily.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. Do not skip any line—KTU valuation expects each step on the answer sheet.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 1: Years' Purchase (YP) in Perpetuity vs. Limited Period**

**Problem Statement:**
A property yields a net annual income of **₹1,20,000**. The interest rate is **10% p.a.** Find the Capitalized Value (CV) assuming:
*(a)* The property has a **perpetual** life (e.g., land).
*(b)* The property has a **limited** life of **50 years** (e.g., a building).

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Perpetual Life**

The formula for YP in perpetuity is:

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{r}$$

Given: $r = 10\% = 0.10$

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10 \text{ years}$$

The Capitalized Value is:

$$CV = \text{Net Income} \times YP$$

$$CV = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 10 = ₹12{,}00{,}000$$

> **Answer (a): CV = ₹12,00,000 (Twelve Lakhs)**

---

**Part (b): Limited Life of 50 Years**

The formula for YP for a limited period is:

$$YP_{n} = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$$

Given: $r = 0.10$, $n = 50$ years.

First, compute $(1+r)^n$:

$$(1.10)^{50} = 117.3909$$

Now substitute:

$$YP_{50} = \frac{117.3909 - 1}{0.10 \times 117.3909} = \frac{116.3909}{11.73909}$$

$$YP_{50} = 9.9148 \text{ years}$$

Therefore:

$$CV = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 9.9148 = ₹11{,}89{,}776$$

> **Answer (b): CV = ₹11,89,776**

**Interpretation:** Notice that for a 50-year building, CV is slightly *less* than for perpetual land (₹12L vs ₹11.90L). This reflects the **finite earning capacity** of a structure that will eventually collapse.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 2: Sinking Fund Calculation**

**Problem Statement:**
A building worth **₹10,00,000** today has a life of **40 years** and a scrap value of **₹1,00,000**. The interest rate is **8% p.a.** Find the **annual sinking fund contribution** required to replace the building at the end of its life.

#### Solution:

The sinking fund formula is:

$$A = S_f \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$

Where $S_f$ = Net amount to be replaced = $C - S = 10{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹9{,}00{,}000$.

Given: $r = 0.08$, $n = 40$.

Compute the denominator:

$$(1.08)^{40} = 21.7245$$

$$(1.08)^{40} - 1 = 20.7245$$

$$r \times [(1.08)^{40} - 1] = 0.08 \times 20.7245 = 1.65796$$

The annual sinking fund is:

$$A = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{20.7245} = ₹43{,}425 \text{ per year}$$

> **Answer: A = ₹43,425 per annum**

**Verification:** A small amount saved each year, compounded at 8%, will accumulate to ₹9,00,000 in 40 years, enough to rebuild the structure.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 3: Depreciation by Three Methods**

**Problem Statement:**
A building costs **₹5,00,000** with a useful life of **25 years** and a scrap value of **₹50,000**. Compute the depreciated value at the **end of 10 years** using:
*(a)* Straight Line Method
*(b)* Declining Balance Method (rate = 5%)
*(c)* Sinking Fund Method (r = 6%)

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Straight Line Method (SLM)**

Annual depreciation is constant:

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{25} = ₹18{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

After 10 years, total depreciation = $10 \times 18{,}000 = ₹1{,}80{,}000$.

$$V_{10} = C - (\text{Total Depreciation}) = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 = ₹3{,}20{,}000$$

> **Answer (a): V₁₀ = ₹3,20,000**

---

**Part (b): Declining Balance Method (DBM)**

The depreciation each year is a fixed *percentage* of the *diminishing* book value. Given rate $b = 5\% = 0.05$.

| Year | Book Value (Start) | Depreciation (5% of BV) | Book Value (End) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 25,000 | 4,75,000 |
| 2 | 4,75,000 | 23,750 | 4,51,250 |
| 3 | 4,51,250 | 22,563 | 4,28,687 |
| 4 | 4,28,687 | 21,434 | 4,07,253 |
| 5 | 4,07,253 | 20,363 | 3,86,890 |
| 6 | 3,86,890 | 19,345 | 3,67,545 |
| 7 | 3,67,545 | 18,377 | 3,49,168 |
| 8 | 3,49,168 | 17,458 | 3,31,710 |
| 9 | 3,31,710 | 16,586 | 3,15,124 |
| 10 | 3,15,124 | 15,756 | **2,99,368** |

> **Answer (b): V₁₀ = ₹2,99,368**

---

**Part (c): Sinking Fund Method (SFM)**

Annual depreciation is the **sinking fund contribution** needed to fund the *depreciation amount* $(C - S)$:

$$A = (C - S) \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{(1.06)^{25} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.06)^{25}$:

$$(1.06)^{25} = 4.2919$$

$$(1.06)^{25} - 1 = 3.2919$$

$$A = 4{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{3.2919} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.018227 = ₹8{,}202 \text{ per year}$$

After 10 years, accumulated sinking fund:

$$S_{10} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{10} - 1}{r} = 8{,}202 \times \frac{(1.06)^{10} - 1}{0.06}$$

$$(1.06)^{10} = 1.7908$$

$$S_{10} = 8{,}202 \times \frac{0.7908}{0.06} = 8{,}202 \times 13.1808 = ₹1{,}08{,}108$$

$$V_{10} = C - S_{10} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}08{,}108 = ₹3{,}91{,}892$$

> **Answer (c): V₁₀ = ₹3,91,892**

**Comparative Insight:**

| Method | V₁₀ (₹) | Comments |
|---|---|---|
| SLM | 3,20,000 | Linear; ignores interest |
| DBM (5%) | 2,99,368 | Aggressive; front-loaded |
| SFM (6%) | 3,91,892 | Conservative; respects time value |

> **Conclusion:** SFM gives the *highest* book value because depreciation is funded by the *growth* of small deposits, not the full scrap-value gap.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 4: Valuation by Rental Method (Total Cost Approach)**

**Problem Statement:**
A two-storied building has:
- Plinth area = **100 m² per floor**
- Cost of construction = **₹2,000/m²**
- Expected life = **60 years**
- Scrap value = **10% of cost**
- Annual repairs = **1% of cost**
- Annual taxes & insurance = **0.5% of cost**
- Sinking fund rate = **5% p.a.**
- Net annual rent = **₹30,000**

Find: *(a)* Capitalized value of the building. *(b)* Total value of the property if land value is ₹5,00,000.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Cost of Building**

$$C = 2 \times 100 \times 2000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Annual Outgoings**

| Item | Calculation | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Repairs | 1% of 4,00,000 | 4,000 |
| Taxes & Insurance | 0.5% of 4,00,000 | 2,000 |
| Sinking Fund (A) | $3{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{(1.05)^{60}-1}$ | 4,800 |
| **Total Outgoings** | | **10,800** |

Compute Sinking Fund precisely:
- $(1.05)^{60} = 18.6792$
- $(1.05)^{60} - 1 = 17.6792$
- $A = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 0.05 / 17.6792 = 18{,}000 / 17.6792 = ₹1{,}018$ per year

Let me recalculate using $S = 10\% \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}000$:

Net amount to be replaced = $C - S = 4{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000 = ₹3{,}60{,}000$

$$A = 3{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{17.6792} = ₹1{,}018$$

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Repairs (1%) | 4,000 |
| Taxes & Insurance (0.5%) | 2,000 |
| Sinking Fund (A) | 1,018 |
| **Total Outgoings** | **7,018** |

**Step 3: Net Income**

$$\text{Net Income} = \text{Gross Rent} - \text{Outgoings} = 30{,}000 - 7{,}018 = ₹22{,}982$$

**Step 4: Years' Purchase (Limited Period, 60 years, 5%)**

$$YP_{60} = \frac{(1.05)^{60} - 1}{0.05 \times (1.05)^{60}} = \frac{17.6792}{0.05 \times 18.6792} = \frac{17.6792}{0.93396}$$

$$YP_{60} = 18.9293 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5: Capitalized Value of Building**

$$CV_{building} = 22{,}982 \times 18.9293 = ₹4{,}34{,}911$$

**Step 6: Total Value**

$$V_{total} = V_{land} + V_{building} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}34{,}911 = ₹9{,}34{,}911$$

> **Answer:** **(a) Building CV = ₹4,34,911** | **(b) Total Property Value = ₹9,34,911**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 5: Valuation by Direct Comparison Method**

**Problem Statement:**
A property is being valued. Three comparable properties in the locality recently sold as follows:

| Property | Plinth Area (m²) | Sale Price (₹) | Age (years) | Life (years) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 150 | 18,00,000 | 10 | 60 |
| B | 200 | 22,00,000 | 20 | 60 |
| C | 180 | 19,50,000 | 5 | 60 |

The subject property has plinth area = **170 m²**, age = **15 years**, life = **60 years**, and life of property = 60 years. Compute the value.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute rate per m² of each comparable**

| Property | Rate (₹/m²) | Depreciation | Effective Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 18,00,000 / 150 = 12,000 | 10/60 = 16.67% | 12,000 / (1 - 0.1667) = 14,400 |
| B | 22,00,000 / 200 = 11,000 | 20/60 = 33.33% | 11,000 / (1 - 0.3333) = 16,500 |
| C | 19,50,000 / 180 = 10,833 | 5/60 = 8.33% | 10,833 / (1 - 0.0833) = 11,818 |

**Step 2: Average effective rate**

$$\text{Avg Rate} = \frac{14{,}400 + 16{,}500 + 11{,}818}{3} = \frac{42{,}718}{3} = ₹14{,}239/\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Gross value of subject property (assuming new)**

$$V_{new} = 14{,}239 \times 170 = ₹24{,}20{,}630$$

**Step 4: Depreciate for 15 years (SLM)**

$$V_{15} = 24{,}20{,}630 \times \left(1 - \frac{15}{60}\right) = 24{,}20{,}630 \times 0.75 = ₹18{,}15{,}473$$

> **Answer: Valued Price = ₹18,15,473**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 6: Valuation by Profit Method (for Hotels, Theatres, Shops)**

**Problem Statement:**
A hotel has:
- Annual gross profit = **₹12,00,000**
- Total expenses (excluding interest) = **₹7,00,000**
- Capital invested = **₹40,00,000**
- Expected rate of return = **12% p.a.**

Find the goodwill and total value of the hotel.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Net Profit (after deducting expected return on capital)**

$$\text{Net Profit (after expected return)} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Expected return on capital} = 12\% \times 40{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}80{,}000$$

$$\text{Actual Profit} - \text{Expected Profit} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = ₹20{,}000$$

**Step 2: Goodwill using Years' Purchase of Super Profit**

YP at 12% (perpetuity) = 1/0.12 = 8.33 years.

$$\text{Goodwill} = 20{,}000 \times 8.33 = ₹1{,}66{,}600$$

**Step 3: Total Value of Hotel**

$$V_{total} = V_{tangible} + \text{Goodwill} = 40{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}66{,}600 = ₹41{,}66{,}600$$

> **Answer: Goodwill = ₹1,66,600; Total Value = ₹41,66,600**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 7: Annuity and Free-Hold vs. Lease-Hold**

**Problem Statement:**
A property is on a **30-year lease**. The net annual rent is **₹80,000** and the interest rate is **9% p.a.** Find:
*(a)* Value of the lease-hold (30-year) interest.
*(b)* Value of the free-hold (perpetual) interest.
*(c)* Loss to lease-holder = Free-hold value − Lease-hold value.

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Lease-hold (30 years) YP**

$$YP_{30} = \frac{(1.09)^{30} - 1}{0.09 \times (1.09)^{30}}$$

$(1.09)^{30} = 13.2677$

$$YP_{30} = \frac{13.2677 - 1}{0.09 \times 13.2677} = \frac{12.2677}{1.1941} = 10.2736 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{lease} = 80{,}000 \times 10.2736 = ₹8{,}21{,}888$$

**Part (b): Free-hold (Perpetual) YP**

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.09} = 11.11 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{free} = 80{,}000 \times 11.11 = ₹8{,}88{,}888$$

**Part (c): Loss to Lease-Holder**

$$\text{Loss} = 8{,}88{,}888 - 8{,}21{,}888 = ₹67{,}000$$

> **Answer:** (a) **₹8,21,888** | (b) **₹8,88,888** | (c) **₹67,000**

**Real-world use:** This is exactly how RERA determines compensation for flat purchasers on a 99-year lease.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 8: Reversionary Value (Land + Building)**

**Problem Statement:**
A building yields a net income of **₹60,000 p.a.** for the next **25 years**, after which the land alone will yield ₹30,000 p.a. perpetually. Interest rate = **10% p.a.** Find the total present value.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: PV of building income (25 years)**

$$YP_{25} = \frac{(1.10)^{25} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{25}}$$

$(1.10)^{25} = 10.8347$

$$YP_{25} = \frac{9.8347}{1.08347} = 9.0770 \text{ years}$$

$$PV_{building} = 60{,}000 \times 9.0770 = ₹5{,}44{,}620$$

**Step 2: PV of land (reversion at year 25 + perpetuity starting year 26)**

Value of land in perpetuity (at year 25):

$$V_{land@25} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.10} = ₹3{,}00{,}000$$

Discount this to present (year 0) using $(1.10)^{25} = 10.8347$:

$$PV_{land} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10.8347} = ₹27{,}689$$

**Step 3: Total Present Value**

$$V_{total} = 5{,}44{,}620 + 27{,}689 = ₹5{,}72{,}309$$

> **Answer: Total Present Value = ₹5,72,309**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 9: Mortgage Valuation (Bank Loan Perspective)**

**Problem Statement:**
A building is valued at **₹20,00,000** (market value). The bank's policy allows a loan of **70%** of market value, and applies a safety margin of **20%** on the *forced sale* price. If the forced sale price is 80% of the market value, find the maximum loan that can be safely granted.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Forced Sale Value**

$$V_{forced} = 80\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹16{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Apply Safety Margin (deduct 20% from forced sale value)**

$$V_{safe} = 80\% \times 16{,}00{,}000 = ₹12{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 3: Apply 70% Loan-to-Value (LTV)**

$$\text{Max Loan} = 70\% \times 12{,}80{,}000 = ₹8{,}96{,}000$$

> **Answer: Maximum Safe Loan = ₹8,96,000**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 10: Composite Rate of Interest**

**Problem Statement:**
Capital of **₹5,00,000** is invested, **½ in 8% stock, ¼ in 9% stock, and ¼ in 10% stock.** Find the **average rate of return**.

#### Solution:

$$\text{Weighted Avg} = (0.5 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 10)$$

$$= 4.0 + 2.25 + 2.50 = 8.75\%$$

> **Answer: Average Rate = 8.75% p.a.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The Valuation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Real Property] --> B{What is the asset?}
    B --> C[Land only]
    B --> D[Building only]
    B --> E[Land + Building]

    C --> F[Valuation by Direct Comparison]
    C --> G[Valuation by Belting Method]

    D --> H[Rental Method]
    D --> I[Cost Method with Depreciation]
    D --> J[Profit Method if commercial]

    E --> K[Land at Market Rate]
    E --> L[Building at Depreciated Cost]
    E --> M[Sum: Composite Value]

    F --> N[Final Capitalized Value]
    G --> N
    H --> N
    I --> N
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Architecture of a Valuation Report

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identification of Property] --> B[Inspection and Data Collection]
    B --> C[Choice of Method]
    C --> D[Compute Gross Income]
    D --> E[Deduct Outgoings]
    E --> F[Apply YP Factor]
    F --> G[Add Land Value]
    G --> H[Final Valuation Report]
    H --> I[Certified Valuer Sign-off]
```

### Diagram 3: Depreciation Method Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Original Cost C] --> L1{Depreciation Method}
    L1 --> M1[Straight Line: D = CSn]
    L1 --> M2[Declining Balance: b% of WDV]
    L1 --> M3[Sinking Fund: Compound Interest]

    M1 --> Out1[Equal Yearly D]
    M2 --> Out2[High D initially, decreases]
    M3 --> Out3[Smaller D, accumulates via interest]

    Out1 --> Final[Book Value at year n]
    Out2 --> Final
    Out3 --> Final
```

### Diagram 4: Rental Method Computational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plinth Area × Rate = Cost C] --> P2[Salvage S = 10% C]
    P2 --> P3[Life n in years]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Annual Sinking Fund A]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Annual Repairs R]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Annual Taxes T]
    P6 --> P7[Total Outgoings = A plus R plus T]
    P7 --> P8[Net Income = Rent minus Outgoings]
    P8 --> P9[YP for n years at r%]
    P9 --> P10[Capitalized Value = NI × YP]
```

### Diagram 5: Mermaid Diagram - Numerical Example Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Read problem] --> B[Step 2: Identify given values]
    B --> C[Step 3: Select formula]
    C --> D[Step 4: Substitute]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute]
    E --> F[Step 6: Verify with intuition]
    F --> G[Step 7: Final answer with units]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert % to decimal** (e.g., writing r = 10 instead of 0.10). This alone can cost **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Not deducting outgoings** before multiplying by YP — many students apply YP to *gross* rent instead of *net* rent.
> 3. **Mixing up SLM, DBM, SFM** — note that SFM uses *compound* interest formulas; SLM does not.
> 4. **Not specifying units (₹)** in final answers — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Rounding off too early** — keep **4 decimal places** during calculation, round only at the end.

---

### **Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Years' Purchase (YP). What is its value for a perpetual income stream at 8% p.a.?

**Model Answer:**
Years' Purchase (YP) is the multiplier that converts a periodic income into its present capitalized value. It is mathematically defined as the present value of a Re. 1 annuity.

$$YP = \frac{1}{r}$$

For $r = 8\% = 0.08$:

$$YP = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \text{ years}$$

> **Marks: 3**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between Straight Line Method and Sinking Fund Method of depreciation (any three points).

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Straight Line Method (SLM) | Sinking Fund Method (SFM) |
|---|---|---|
| Depreciation | Equal annual amount | Variable; funded by interest growth |
| Interest on investments | Ignored | Considered at compound rate |
| Annual cost to owner | Constant | Variable; decreases with time |
| Suitability | Short-life assets | Long-life buildings |

> **Marks: 3**

---

### **Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A building costs **₹8,00,000** to construct. Its useful life is **50 years** and the scrap value is **₹80,000**. Calculate the **annual sinking fund** and the **total amount accumulated in the sinking fund at the end of 20 years**, assuming the interest rate is **6% p.a.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The same building yields a monthly rent of **₹12,000**. The owner spends **8%** of the annual rent on repairs, **₹4,000 p.a.** on taxes, and the sinking fund from part (a) is set aside annually. Calculate the **Capitalized Value of the building** using the rental method at **6% p.a.** for a **50-year life**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Sinking Fund Calculation (7 marks)**

Given:
- $C = ₹8,00,000$
- $S = ₹80,000$
- $n = 50$ years
- $r = 6\% = 0.06$

Net replacement amount:

$$S_f = C - S = 8{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = ₹7{,}20{,}000$$

Annual sinking fund:

$$A = S_f \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1} = 7{,}20{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{(1.06)^{50} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.06)^{50} = 18.4202$

$$(1.06)^{50} - 1 = 17.4202$$

$$A = 7{,}20{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{17.4202} = 7{,}20{,}000 \times 0.003444 = ₹2{,}479.7 \text{ per year}$$

**Accumulated Sinking Fund after 20 years:**

$$\text{ASF} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{20} - 1}{r} = 2{,}479.7 \times \frac{(1.06)^{20} - 1}{0.06}$$

$(1.06)^{20} = 3.2071$

$$\text{ASF} = 2{,}479.7 \times \frac{2.2071}{0.06} = 2{,}479.7 \times 36.7856 = ₹91{,}219$$

> **Answer (a):** Annual Sinking Fund = **₹2,480/year**; ASF after 20 years = **₹91,219**

---

**Part (b) — Capitalized Value (7 marks)**

Given:
- Monthly rent = ₹12,000 → Annual gross rent = ₹1,44,000
- Repairs = 8% of 1,44,000 = ₹11,520
- Taxes = ₹4,000
- Sinking Fund (A) = ₹2,480
- $n = 50$, $r = 6\%$

Total outgoings = $11{,}520 + 4{,}000 + 2{,}480 = ₹18{,}000$

Net income = $1{,}44{,}000 - 18{,}000 = ₹1{,}26{,}000$

Years' Purchase for 50 years at 6%:

$$YP_{50} = \frac{(1.06)^{50} - 1}{0.06 \times (1.06)^{50}} = \frac{17.4202}{0.06 \times 18.4202} = \frac{17.4202}{1.10521} = 15.7619 \text{ years}$$

Capitalized Value:

$$CV = \text{Net Income} \times YP = 1{,}26{,}000 \times 15.7619 = ₹19{,}86{,}000$$

> **Answer (b): Capitalized Value = ₹19,86,000**

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A property yields a **net annual income of ₹1,50,000** perpetually. The interest rate is **10% p.a.** Find the Capitalized Value. If the property is on a **20-year lease** instead, find the lease-hold value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A building costs **₹6,00,000** to construct, has a life of **40 years**, and scrap value of **₹60,000**. Compute the book value at the end of **15 years** using:
*(i)* Straight Line Method
*(ii)* Declining Balance Method (rate = 4%)
*(iii)* Sinking Fund Method (r = 5%) **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Perpetual vs. Lease-hold Value (7 marks)**

**Perpetual (Free-hold):**

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{free} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 10 = ₹15{,}00{,}000$$

**Lease-hold (20 years):**

$$YP_{20} = \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{20}}$$

$(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$

$$YP_{20} = \frac{5.7275}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.67275} = 8.5136 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{lease} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 8.5136 = ₹12{,}77{,}040$$

**Loss to lease-holder** = $15{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}77{,}040 = ₹2{,}22{,}960$

> **Answer (a):** **Free-hold = ₹15,00,000**; **Lease-hold = ₹12,77,040**; **Loss = ₹2,22,960**

---

**Part (b) — Three Depreciation Methods (7 marks)**

Given: $C = ₹6,00{,}000$, $S = ₹60{,}000$, $n = 40$, age = 15 years.

**(i) Straight Line Method:**

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000}{40} = ₹13{,}500 \text{ per year}$$

$$V_{15} = C - 15D = 6{,}00{,}000 - 15 \times 13{,}500 = 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}02{,}500 = ₹3{,}97{,}500$$

> **SLM: V₁₅ = ₹3,97,500**

**(ii) Declining Balance Method (4%):**

Using the formula $V_n = C \cdot (1-b)^n$:

$$V_{15} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times (0.96)^{15} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5421 = ₹3{,}25{,}260$$

> **DBM: V₁₅ = ₹3,25,260**

**(iii) Sinking Fund Method (5%):**

Annual depreciation:

$$A = (C - S) \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1} = 5{,}40{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{(1.05)^{40} - 1}$$

$(1.05)^{40} = 7.0400$; $7.0400 - 1 = 6.0400$

$$A = 5{,}40{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{6.0400} = 5{,}40{,}000 \times 0.008278 = ₹4{,}470 \text{ per year}$$

Accumulated fund at year 15:

$$\text{ASF}_{15} = A \cdot \frac{(1.05)^{15} - 1}{0.05} = 4{,}470 \times \frac{2.0789 - 1}{0.05} = 4{,}470 \times 21.5786 = ₹96{,}456$$

$$V_{15} = C - \text{ASF}_{15} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 96{,}456 = ₹5{,}03{,}544$$

> **SFM: V₁₅ = ₹5,03,544**

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | V₁₅ (₹) |
|---|---|
| SLM | 3,97,500 |
| DBM (4%) | 3,25,260 |
| SFM (5%) | 5,03,544 |

> **Answer (b):** As tabulated above.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Numerical Valuation Problems:**

- **Perpetuity YP** = $1/r$ — used for **land**.
- **Limited-period YP** = $\dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ — used for **buildings**.
- **Capitalized Value** = **Net Income × YP** — *never* apply YP to gross rent.
- **Sinking Fund** = $(C - S) \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ — for **replacement reserve**.
- **SLM Depreciation** = $\dfrac{C - S}{n}$ — constant yearly drop.
- **DBM Depreciation** = $b \cdot V_{prev}$ — geometric, front-loaded.
- **SFM Depreciation** = Sinking fund contributions, accumulated at compound interest.
- **Rental Method** sequence: Gross Rent → Outgoings (Repairs + Taxes + Sinking Fund) → Net Income → × YP → Capitalized Value.
- **Comparison Method** requires *normalizing* for age by adding back depreciation.
- **Profit Method** uses *Goodwill* = Super Profit × YP.
- **Free-hold > Lease-hold value** — always; difference = loss to lease-holder.
- **Reversionary problems**: value the *future* perpetuity, then discount to present.
- **Mortgage problems**: Forced Sale Value × (1 − Safety Margin) = Bank's safe limit.
- **Always convert r to decimal** — 10% = 0.10, not 10.
- **Use the year of valuation as "present"** — don't accidentally use 1980 as base year.
- **Composite rate of return** = weighted average by capital fraction.
- **For BELT method in Module 3 land valuation** — front belt has highest depth × rate, decreasing toward rear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:**
> - Always **state the formula first** (1 mark).
> - Then substitute values (2 marks).
> - Then compute (3 marks).
> - Then conclude with a **boxed answer and units** (1 mark).
> - KTU gives **1 mark for free** if all units are written correctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Valuation of Land with Buildings: Rental Method, Direct Comparison of Capital, Valuation Based on Profit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Valuation of Land with Buildings: Rental Method, Direct Comparison of Capital, Valuation Based on Profit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — Syllabus Anchor (PECET862)**
> This module deals with the practical numerical methods by which a *valuer* arrives at the **Capital Value (CV)** of a land-cum-building complex. The three methods covered — **Rental, Direct Comparison of Capital, and Profit-based Valuation** — together cover almost all classes of income-generating real estate in Kerala (rental flats, commercial buildings, hotels, theatres, hospitals, showrooms, etc.).

## 1.1 Rental Method of Valuation

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
The **Rental Method of Valuation**, also called the *Income Capitalization Method* or *Years' Purchase (YP) Method*, is a technique in which the **Capital Value** of a property is estimated by **capitalising** its **Net Annual Rent (NAR)** at a *rate of interest* consistent with a safe, long-term investment, and multiplying the same by an appropriate factor called the **Year's Purchase (YP)**.

Mathematically, the basic governing equation is:

$$ \text{Capital Value} = \text{Net Annual Rent} \times \text{Year's Purchase (YP)} $$

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Fixed-Deposit Mirror"
> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine your father has a Government Security that pays him **₹1,000 every month** (₹12,000 per year) for life, and the safe interest rate is **8 %**. How much is that security worth today? A banker will say: *"I would pay you ₹12,000 ÷ 0.08 = ₹1,50,000."* That ₹1,50,000 is exactly the *Year's Purchase* concept applied to an *annual income*. **A property is no different** — it is an "income-generating asset," and the valuer is merely asking, *"If this rent were an annuity, what principal would fund it?"*

### Why Rental Method? (Utility)
- It is the **only logical method** for *income-producing* properties (rented flats, offices, shops, warehouses).
- It is mandatory for **bank mortgage valuation** under the *SARFAESI / RBI* framework for commercial assets.
- The **Income Tax Act (Sections 22–27)** also relies on "reasonable expected rent" — a derivative of this method.

---

## 1.2 Direct Comparison of Capital Method

### Formal Definition
The **Direct Comparison of Capital Method** (also called the *Sales Comparison Method* or *Market Data Method*) determines the value of a subject property by **comparing it with the recent, known sale prices of similar properties** in the same locality, and making appropriate **adjustments** for differences in *time, location, age, condition, plot shape, frontage, depth, and amenities*.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Used-Car Valuation"
> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** When you want to sell your *2018 Maruti Swift VXi* in Kochi, you don't price it by some formula — you check *OLX / CarWale* to see what *similar 2018 Swift VXis* in Kochi sold for, then you *adjust* for: (a) your car's extra km, (b) accidental history, (c) better tyres, (d) service records. The **adjusted comparable price IS your car's value**. Real estate valuers do the same with land and buildings.

---

## 1.3 Valuation Based on Profit

### Formal Definition
The **Profit Method of Valuation** (also called the *Capitalisation of Profits Method* or *Profits Basis*) is applied to properties whose **value is directly linked to the trading profit** earned by the business conducted on the premises — e.g., **hotels, cinemas, theatres, hospitals, petrol pumps, bowling alleys, and amusement parks**. The capital value is the product of the *Net Annual Profit attributable to the property* and a *Profit Multiplier* (a special form of Year's Purchase for that trade).

$$ \text{Capital Value} = \text{Net Annual Profit (of the trade)} \times \text{Profit Multiplier (PM)} $$

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Going-Concern Idea"
> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** A *business* is a *bundle of rights* — the building, the customers, the staff, the brand, the licences. If you buy a running *Saravana Bhavan* franchise, you are **not** just buying 2,000 sq.ft of land + a kitchen — you are buying the **future stream of profits**. A buyer will pay "X times the annual profit" because he knows the profit will continue. That *X* is the **Profit Multiplier**. The same logic applies to a 3-star hotel, where the property is inseparable from the *trading activity* that produces profit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Highlight:**
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 paper, questions on the three methods are usually framed as:
> - 3-mark short definitions / "Differentiate between…" questions
> - 7-mark numerical problems (Rental & Profit methods)
> - 7-mark descriptive answers (types, suitability, comparison)
> Always present the **formula first**, then the **substitution**, then the **boxed final answer** — KTU examiners reward method-sketching even when arithmetic is slightly off.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rental Method — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Procedural Framework (How a Valuer Operates)

The valuer does **not** directly apply the formula. The following *workflow* is followed:

1. **Step 1 — Establish the Gross Rent (GR).**  
   Take the *reasonable* (not actual) gross rent, as if the premises were let in the open market, *excluding* any special relationship between landlord and tenant.

2. **Step 2 — Deduct Outgoings (O).**  
   The following items are subtracted to get the *Net Annual Rent*:
   - **Repairs & Maintenance** (1 % – 6 % of GR, depending on age)
   - **Property Tax / Municipal Tax** (as per Panchayat / Municipality schedule)
   - **Insurance Premium** (fire, earthquake, structure)
   - **Management & Collection Charges** (5 % – 10 % of GR)
   - **Vacancy & Bad-Debt Allowance** (2 % – 10 % of GR)
   - **Sinking Fund Contribution** (NOT a regular outgo, but added for buildings with limited life — see §2.1.4)

3. **Step 3 — Determine the Net Annual Rent (NAR).**

   $$ \text{NAR} = \text{GR} - \text{Total Outgoings (O)} $$

4. **Step 4 — Select the Rate of Interest (*r*).**  
   This must be the **safe rate of return** offered by Government Securities (G-Secs) or comparable risk-free instruments, **plus a small risk premium** (50–150 basis points) for property-specific risks.

5. **Step 5 — Determine the Remaining Economic Life (*n*)** of the building (in years).

6. **Step 6 — Read / Compute the Year's Purchase (YP).**

7. **Step 7 — Multiply.**  
   $$ \text{CV} = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP} $$

8. **Step 8 — Add Separately Valued Components.**  
   Land is *reversionary* (perpetual) → it uses **YP for perpetuity = 1/r**. The building's NAR portion uses **YP for *n* years** (deferred/term).

---

### 2.1.2 The Year's Purchase (YP) — The Engine of the Rental Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Year's Purchase (YP):**  
> The **Year's Purchase (YP)** is the figure (a pure number) that, when multiplied by the *net annual income* of a property, gives its *present capital value*. It is essentially the **reciprocal of the rate of interest** for perpetuity, and a more complex annuity factor for a *limited* term.

**Mathematical Forms of YP**

| # | Situation | Mathematical Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|-----------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Perpetuity** (Freehold Land) | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Capitalised in perpetuity |
| 2 | **Limited Period — Annuity-Due form** (paid at start of year) | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r\,(1+r)^{n-1}}$ | YP rises for the same *r* as *n* falls |
| 3 | **Limited Period — Ordinary Annuity** (paid at end of year) | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r\,(1+r)^{n}}$ | Slightly lower YP than (2) |
| 4 | **Sinking-Fund (SF) Method** (with SF) | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r + \text{SF rate}}$ | Used when landlord replaces building at end of life |

Where:
- *r* = rate of interest (decimal)
- *n* = remaining useful life (years)
- *SF* = Sinking-Fund rate

### 2.1.3 Worked Numerical — "Why YP Falls as *r* Rises"

| *r* | YP for Perpetuity (1/*r*) | YP for 30 years | YP for 50 years |
|-----|---------------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 6 % | 16.67 | 14.09 | 15.76 |
| 8 % | 12.50 | 11.26 | 12.23 |
| 10 % | 10.00 | 9.43 | 9.91 |
| 12 % | 8.33 | 7.95 | 8.30 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU viva / exam:** Examiners frequently ask — *"If the RBI raises interest rates, will property value fall?"* The answer is **YES**, because YP falls. The rental method is **rate-sensitive**, while the *Sales Comparison Method* is not.

### 2.1.4 Sinking Fund Method — Why and When

If a building has a **finite life**, the landlord must eventually *demolish and rebuild* it. To do this from the rental income, he sets aside a fraction each year (the *sinking fund*). The YP formula then becomes:

$$ \text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r + i} $$

where *r* = interest rate, *i* = sinking-fund instalment rate for *n* years. This **lowers the YP** and is *more conservative* than the ordinary annuity YP.

---

## 2.2 Direct Comparison of Capital — Theoretical Logic

### 2.2.1 Six Steps a Valuer Follows

1. **Identify 3–5 comparable properties** recently *sold* (not quoted) within 1–2 km radius, of similar size, age, use, and road width.
2. **Verify the transactions** are *genuine arm's-length* sales (not gift / family transfer).
3. **Adjust for time** (price-index / construction-cost index).  
   Example: comparable sold in 2021 at ₹X → in 2024 valuation: $X \times \dfrac{\text{Cost Index}_{2024}}{\text{Cost Index}_{2021}}$.
4. **Adjust for plot features** (frontage, depth, shape, corner plots, slope).
5. **Adjust for building features** (age, residual life, finishes, lift, parking, RCC/load-bearing, age of services).
6. **Average the adjusted unit rates** to arrive at the **Capital Value**.

### 2.2.2 Key Adjustment Formula (per-unit-rate method)

$$ \text{Value of Subject Property} = \text{Area} \times \left[ \overline{\text{Unit Rate of Comparables}} \pm \sum_{k} \Delta_k \right] $$

where each $\Delta_k$ is the *rupee adjustment* for the *k*-th feature (e.g., +₹500/sq.ft for additional car park; −₹300/sq.ft for older construction).

### 2.2.3 Suitability & Limitations

| ✅ Suitable For | ❌ Not Suitable For |
|----------------|-------------------|
| Residential flats & independent houses | Unique / landmark properties |
| Standard commercial offices | Hotels, hospitals, theatres |
| Vacant plots in developed layouts | Properties with restricted sale (lease, trust, Wakf) |
| Standard warehouses / industrial sheds | Income-generating properties (use Rental instead) |

---

## 2.3 Valuation Based on Profit — Theoretical Logic

### 2.3.1 Why a *Profit Multiplier* (PM) and not a *YP*?

A *Year's Purchase* assumes the income is **stable and perpetual** (or term-certain). In trading concerns (hotels, cinemas), the **profit can fluctuate wildly** with:
- Seasonality
- Managerial efficiency
- Brand & goodwill
- Competitor entry

Hence the *capitalisation factor* is **empirical** (set by the trade), not a *theoretical annuity* factor. The PM is determined by examining **recent sale prices of similar trading concerns** and dividing by their **annual net profit**.

### 2.3.2 General Procedure

1. **Estimate Gross Receipts / Turnover** of the *trading* activity (rent of rooms, ticket sales, OPD receipts, etc.).
2. **Deduct All Operating Expenses**, including:
   - Staff salaries, consumables, electricity, water
   - Manager's / Operator's remuneration
   - Routine maintenance, licence fees
   - **Operator's Profit (entrepreneurial return)** ← critical
3. **Arrive at Net Trading Profit attributable to the property itself** (i.e., excluding the operator's skill premium).
4. **Capitalise** at a *trade-specific multiplier*:

$$ \text{CV} = \text{Net Annual Profit (property only)} \times \text{PM} $$

### 2.3.3 Indicative Profit Multipliers (Industry Averages, India)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The multipliers below are *indicative*, and the KTU examiner may quote slightly different figures in the question stem — always **use the multiplier given in the question** in your calculation.

| Trade / Property Type | Multiplier Range (Years' Purchase) | Remarks |
|-----------------------|------------------------------------|---------|
| 5-Star Hotel | 4 – 6 | On *gross receipts* after management fee |
| 3-Star Hotel | 3 – 5 | On *net profit* after operator's remuneration |
| Budget / Lodge | 4 – 7 | Higher multiplier = lower-margin business |
| Single-Screen Cinema | 7 – 10 | Historically high in Kerala |
| Multiplex Cinema | 5 – 8 | Lower than single-screen |
| Theatre / Drama Hall | 4 – 6 | On net annual takings |
| Petrol Pump | 4 – 6 | On *gross margin*, not profit |
| Hospital (private) | 3 – 5 | Highly location-sensitive |
| Bowling Alley / Amusement | 4 – 7 | On gross revenue |

---

## 2.4 Master Formula Sheet (KTU 2024 — High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below must be memorised for the ESE.** Substituting wrong units (monthly vs. annual, *r* in % vs. decimal) is the *single most common* KTU valuation mistake.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Net Annual Rent | $\text{NAR} = \text{GR} - \text{O}$ | GR = ₹/yr, O = ₹/yr |
| 2 | YP — Perpetuity | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Freehold land |
| 3 | YP — Ordinary Annuity (n yrs) | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ | Building with finite life |
| 4 | YP — Annuity-Due (n yrs) | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n-1}}$ | Rent paid in advance |
| 5 | YP — Sinking Fund Method | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r + i}$ | Replace-building case |
| 6 | Capital Value (Building only) | $\text{CV} = \text{NAR}_B \times \text{YP}_{n}$ | $\text{NAR}_B$ = building's NAR |
| 7 | Land Value (Reversionary) | $\text{CV}_L = \text{GR} \times \text{YP}_{\infty} = \text{GR}/r$ | Pure ground rent capitalised |
| 8 | Total CV (Land + Building) | $\text{CV}_{\text{Total}} = \text{CV}_B + \text{CV}_L$ | Standard KTU problem |
| 9 | Profit Method CV | $\text{CV} = \text{Net Profit} \times \text{PM}$ | Trade-specific PM |
| 10 | Adjusted Comparable Rate | $\text{Unit Rate} = \overline{\text{Comp Rate}} \pm \sum \Delta_k$ | Sales Comparison |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Year's Purchase (YP) for a Perpetuity

Let a property yield a **net annual income of ₹1** forever, and let the safe rate of interest be *r* (decimal).  
The capital value *P* must be such that, at rate *r*, the interest equals ₹1:

$$ P \times r = 1 $$

Solving for *P*:

$$ P = \dfrac{1}{r} $$

This is the **Year's Purchase for perpetuity** (also denoted as $\text{YP}_\infty$). It is the foundation for *valuing freehold land* using ground rent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of YP for *n* Years (Ordinary Annuity, Paid at End of Year)

Consider a property yielding ₹1 at the **end** of each year for *n* years. The *present value* of this series is the sum of discounted cash flows:

$$ \text{PV} = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{1}} + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{3}} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}} $$

This is a *geometric progression* with first term $a = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$, common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$, and *n* terms. The sum of a GP is:

$$ S_n = a \, \dfrac{1 - q^{n}}{1 - q} $$

Substituting:

$$ \text{PV} = \dfrac{1}{1+r} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+r}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r}} $$

Simplify the denominator:

$$ 1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r} = \dfrac{r}{1+r} $$

Hence:

$$ \text{PV} = \dfrac{1}{1+r} \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{(1+r)^{n}} \cdot \dfrac{1+r}{r} $$

Cancel $(1+r)$:

$$ \text{PV} = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \,(1+r)^{n}} $$

This is the **standard YP** (ordinary annuity form) for *n* years at rate *r*. It is the multiplier used in **Rental Method for a building with finite life**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick (KTU):** "Top-hat minus one, over *r* × top-hat" — i.e. $\dfrac{x^{n}-1}{r\,x^{n}}$ where $x = 1+r$. This phrase has rescued many viva answers!

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the *Net Annual Rent* Adjustment Identity

For a residential / commercial letting, the relationship is:

$$ \text{NAR} = \text{GR} - \left[ T + M + R_{\text{ep}} + I + V + C_{\text{ol}} \right] $$

where:
- *T* = Property / Municipal tax
- *M* = Management & collection charges
- $R_{\text{ep}}$ = Repairs and maintenance
- *I* = Insurance premium
- *V* = Vacancy & bad-debt allowance
- $C_{\text{ol}}$ = Any other outgoings (e.g., lift maintenance for shops in a complex)

**Each component** is calculated as a *% of Gross Rent* (or as an absolute sum from bills). The valuer's judgement — guided by **Schedule III of the Kerala Building Rules** and **RICS Red Book (UK)** standards — determines the %s.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to *Choose* a Method (KTU Application)

| Property Type | Best Method | Secondary | Why? |
|---------------|-------------|-----------|------|
| Owner-occupied house | Direct Comparison | — | No rent data available |
| Rented flat in city | Rental | Comparison | Steady income stream |
| Standalone shop in mall | Rental | Profit (if hotel) | Comparable rent exists |
| Hotel (3-star) | **Profit** | Rental (of land) | Trade-profit drives value |
| Single-screen cinema | **Profit** | Rental | Trade-profit is the source |
| Vacant land (residential) | Comparison | Belting / Abstractive | No rent; plenty of sales |
| Agricultural land | Comparison | Yield (rental) | Limited comparable sales |
| Petrol pump on highway | **Profit** | Rental | Trade is highly regulated |
| Hospital (private) | **Profit** | Rental | Profit basis is industry-standard |
| Heritage bungalow (rented) | Rental | Comparison | Heritage discount applied to YP |

---

## 3.5 Full Numerical Walk-Through — Rental Method (Kerala-Style)

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A two-storey rented building in Thrissur has a **gross monthly rent of ₹45,000**. The following **annual** outgoings are estimated: Property tax ₹30,000; Repairs ₹25,000; Management ₹15,000; Insurance ₹8,000. Allow a **vacancy allowance of 5 % of gross rent**. The remaining life of the building is **35 years** and the prevailing safe rate of interest is **9 %**. Land is separately valued at **₹20,00,000**. Find the total valuation.

**Step 1 — Convert to Annual Gross Rent:**

$$ \text{GR} = 45{,}000 \times 12 = 5{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

**Step 2 — Vacancy Allowance:**

$$ V = 0.05 \times 5{,}40{,}000 = 27{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 3 — Total Outgoings:**

$$ O = 30{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 27{,}000 = 1{,}05{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 4 — Net Annual Rent:**

$$ \text{NAR} = 5{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}05{,}000 = 4{,}35{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 5 — Compute YP for 35 years at 9 %:**

$$ \text{YP} = \dfrac{(1+0.09)^{35}-1}{0.09 \times (1+0.09)^{35}} $$

$$ (1.09)^{35} \approx 20.10696 $$

$$ \text{YP} = \dfrac{20.10696 - 1}{0.09 \times 20.10696} = \dfrac{19.10696}{1.80963} \approx 10.557 $$

**Step 6 — Capital Value of Building:**

$$ \text{CV}_B = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP} = 4{,}35{,}000 \times 10.557 = 45{,}92{,}295 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 7 — Add Land Value:**

$$ \text{CV}_{\text{Total}} = 45{,}92{,}295 + 20{,}00{,}000 = 65{,}92{,}295 \text{ ₹} $$

> **Answer:** Total valuation of the property ≈ **₹65.92 lakh** (rounded).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** A common mistake is to apply the *perpetuity YP* (= 1/0.09 = 11.11) to a 35-year building — this **over-estimates the value** by ~5 %. Always match the YP to the *remaining life*.

---

## 3.6 Full Numerical Walk-Through — Profit Method

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A 3-star hotel in Munnar has an **average annual gross income of ₹1,20,00,000**. Operating expenses (including staff salary, consumables, electricity, marketing, licence fees) are **70 % of gross income**. The operator/manager's profit is treated as a separate item. A suitable **profit multiplier of 5** is prescribed. Find the capital value of the hotel property by the Profit Method.

**Step 1 — Operating Expenses:**

$$ \text{OpEx} = 0.70 \times 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 = 84{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 2 — Net Profit (after OpEx, before operator's profit):**

$$ \text{NP} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 84{,}00{,}000 = 36{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 3 — Apply Profit Multiplier (PM = 5):**

$$ \text{CV} = \text{NP} \times \text{PM} = 36{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

> **Answer:** Capital Value of the hotel ≈ **₹1.80 crore**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Note (KTU):** Note that ₹1.80 crore is *not* the *enterprise value* — it is the **value of the land + building + equipment + licences** that constitute the property. The *goodwill* of the brand (Marriott, Taj, etc.) is *added* separately by a business valuer, not by a real-property valuer.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical — Direct Comparison of Capital (Sales Comparison)

> **Problem (KTU Model):** Three residential plots in Kakkanad (Kochi) sold recently:
> - Plot A — 10 cents, road frontage 12 m, sold at ₹18,00,000
> - Plot B — 8 cents, road frontage 9 m, sold at ₹13,00,000
> - Plot C — 12 cents, road frontage 15 m, sold at ₹22,00,000
> The **subject property** is a 10-cent plot with 12 m frontage in the same layout. Adjustments: ₹-10,000 per metre shortfall in frontage, ₹+15,000 per cent of excess area. Find the value of the subject property.

**Step 1 — Compute Unit Rate (₹/cent) of each comparable:**

- Plot A: $18{,}00{,}000 / 10 = 1{,}80{,}000$ ₹/cent
- Plot B: $13{,}00{,}000 / 8 = 1{,}62{,}500$ ₹/cent
- Plot C: $22{,}00{,}000 / 12 = 1{,}83{,}333$ ₹/cent

**Step 2 — Average Unit Rate:**

$$ \overline{U} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}62{,}500 + 1{,}83{,}333}{3} = 1{,}75{,}278 \text{ ₹/cent (approx.)} $$

**Step 3 — Adjust subject property to base: Subject has 10 cents, 12 m frontage, so it equals Plot A exactly → no extra adjustment needed beyond the average.**

**Step 4 — Final Value:**

$$ \text{CV} = 10 \text{ cents} \times 1{,}75{,}278 \text{ ₹/cent} = 17{,}52{,}780 \text{ ₹} $$

> **Answer:** ≈ **₹17.53 lakh** (subject to ±5 % adjustment band).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart — Which Valuation Method to Use?

The following Mermaid diagram is a **block-level decision architecture** that a KTU student can use during the ESE (model-paper type) to choose the *correct* method before solving a numerical.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Subject Property Identified] --> B{Is property\nincome-generating\ni.e. rented?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is rent\nstable &\nverifiable?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Rental Method\nCV = NAR x YP]
    C -- No --> E{Are enough\nrecent sales\navailable?}
    B -- No --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Use Direct Comparison\nof Capital Method]
    E -- No --> G{Is the property\na trade/hospitality\nasset?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Use Profit Method\nCV = Net Profit x PM]
    G -- No --> I[Use Belting / Abstractive\nor Residual Method\nout of scope here]
    D --> J[Add Land Value\nCV_total = CV_building + CV_land]
    F --> J
    H --> K[Add Goodwill separately\nif a going concern]
    J --> L[Final Valuation Report]
    K --> L
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Rental Method Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DATA]
        A1[Gross Rent\nRs / month or year]

        A4[Remaining Life n years]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[COMPUTATION ENGINE]
        B1[Annualise GR]
        B2[Vacancy Allowance 2-10 percent]
        B3[Sum Outgoings]

        B5[Read YP from table\nor compute 1 plus r to n]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT]
        C1[Capital Value of Building]
        C2[Land Value\nGR x YP perpetuity]
        C3[Total Valuation\nCV_B plus CV_L]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    A3 --> B5
    A4 --> B5
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    A3 --> C2
    B4 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — The Three Methods Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R[RENTAL METHOD]
        R1[Key input: Net Annual Rent]
        R2[Key multiplier: Years Purchase YP]
        R3[Output: Capital Value]
    end
    subgraph D[DIRECT COMPARISON METHOD]
        D1[Key input: Comparable sale prices]
        D2[Key tool: Adjustment table]
        D3[Output: Capital Value]
    end
    subgraph P[PROFIT METHOD]
        P1[Key input: Net Annual Profit]
        P2[Key multiplier: Profit Multiplier PM]
        P3[Output: Capital Value]
    end
    R --- SHARED[Common Output:\nCapital Value in Rs]
    D --- SHARED
    P --- SHARED
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice the *symmetry*: all three methods give a *Capital Value*, but the *multiplier* differs. Rental uses YP, Comparison uses *adjusted unit rate*, and Profit uses PM. This symmetry is a favourite KTU theory question.

---

## 4.4 Workflow Diagram — Direct Comparison of Capital

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify 3 to 5 comparable\nrecent SOLD properties] --> S2[Step 2: Verify each sale is\narm's length, genuine]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute unit rate\nRs per sqft or Rs per cent]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Time-adjust\nusing cost index]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Adjust for plot features\nfrontage, depth, shape, road width]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Adjust for building features\nage, residual life, finish, parking]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Average the adjusted\nunit rates]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Multiply by subject\nproperty's area to get CV]
```

---

## 4.5 Workflow Diagram — Profit Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Step 1: Estimate gross annual\nreceipts from the trade] --> Q2[Step 2: Subtract operating\nexpenses excluding operator's profit]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Subtract operator's\nremuneration and profit]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Arrive at Net Trading\nProfit attributable to property]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: Identify Profit Multiplier\nPM from trade data 3 to 10]
    Q5 --> Q6[Step 6: CV = Net Profit times PM]
    Q6 --> Q7[Step 7: Add goodwill and\nbusiness value if going concern]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define **Year's Purchase (YP)**. How is it determined for (a) a perpetuity, and (b) a limited period of *n* years?  
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Modified Model)]** — 3 Marks

**Model Answer:**

**Year's Purchase (YP)** is the numerical factor which, when multiplied by the *net annual income* (rent or profit) of a property, gives its present **Capital Value**.

- **For a perpetuity** (e.g., freehold land):  
  $$ \text{YP}_\infty = \dfrac{1}{r} $$  
  where *r* is the safe rate of interest (decimal).

- **For a limited period of *n* years** (e.g., a building with finite life):  
  $$ \text{YP}_n = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r\,(1+r)^{n}} $$

The YP is inversely proportional to *r* and *directly* proportional to *n*. Hence, a higher interest rate *lowers* the property's value, and a longer remaining life *raises* it.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between the **Rental Method** and the **Profit Method** of valuation.  
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modified Model)]** — 3 Marks

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rental Method | Profit Method |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Basis** | Net Annual Rent | Net Annual Trading Profit |
| **Suitable for** | Rented residential / commercial properties | Hotels, cinemas, hospitals, petrol pumps |
| **Income Stream** | Stable, contractually fixed | Variable, trade-dependent |
| **Multiplier** | Year's Purchase (theoretical) | Profit Multiplier (empirical) |
| **Operator's profit** | Not relevant (landlord only) | Deducted explicitly |
| **Outgoings deducted** | Repairs, taxes, insurance, management, vacancy | All operating expenses + operator's remuneration |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Numerical / Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — ESE Pattern:** Part B Module questions carry **14 marks**, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each is split into **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. You must attempt **any one** of A or B.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### Q.A.(a) Numerical — Rental Method with Land + Building [7 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Adapted)]**  
> A 3-storey rented building in Kozhikode yields a **gross monthly rent of ₹60,000**. Annual outgoings are:  
> - Property tax: ₹40,000  
> - Repairs: ₹35,000  
> - Management: ₹22,000  
> - Insurance: ₹12,000  
> - Sinking-fund contribution for the building: 4 % of GR  
> - Vacancy allowance: 5 % of GR  
>  
> The **safe rate of interest is 8 %** and the **remaining life of the building is 40 years**. The land is valued separately at **₹25,00,000**. Calculate the **total valuation** by the Rental Method.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gross Annual Rent (GR):**

$$ \text{GR} = 60{,}000 \times 12 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 2 — Vacancy & Sinking-Fund Allowances:**

$$ V = 0.05 \times 7{,}20{,}000 = 36{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

$$ \text{SF} = 0.04 \times 7{,}20{,}000 = 28{,}800 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 3 — Total Outgoings:**

$$ O = 40{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 22{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 28{,}800 = 1{,}73{,}800 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 4 — Net Annual Rent:**

$$ \text{NAR} = 7{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}73{,}800 = 5{,}46{,}200 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 5 — Year’s Purchase for 40 years at 8 % (Sinking-Fund form already applied; use ordinary-annuity YP here):**

$$ \text{YP} = \dfrac{(1.08)^{40}-1}{0.08\,(1.08)^{40}} $$

$$ (1.08)^{40} \approx 21.7245 $$

$$ \text{YP} = \dfrac{21.7245-1}{0.08 \times 21.7245} = \dfrac{20.7245}{1.73796} \approx 11.925 $$

**Step 6 — Capital Value of Building:**

$$ \text{CV}_B = 5{,}46{,}200 \times 11.925 = 65{,}13{,}435 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 7 — Total Valuation (incl. Land):**

$$ \text{CV}_{\text{Total}} = 65{,}13{,}435 + 25{,}00{,}000 = 90{,}13{,}435 \text{ ₹} $$

> **Final Answer:** Total Valuation ≈ **₹90.13 lakh** (rounded to nearest ₹100).

---

#### Q.A.(b) Descriptive — Types & Suitability of Rental Method [7 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Adapted)]**  
> Explain the **different types of Rental Method** of valuation. State the **conditions** under which each is adopted.

**Model Answer:**

The Rental Method has four principal variants:

1. **Direct Capitalisation (Freehold / Perpetual)**  
   - **Formula:** $\text{CV} = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}_\infty = \text{NAR}/r$  
   - **Used when:** The property is freehold land, or the income is perpetual (e.g., ground rent from a long-term lease on land where the tenant owns the superstructure).

2. **Term Capitalisation (Leasehold / Limited Period)**  
   - **Formula:** $\text{CV} = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}_n$  
   - **Used when:** The property has a *finite economic life* (e.g., RCC building with 40-year residual life).

3. **Reversionary / Deferred Capitalisation**  
   - **Formula:** $\text{CV} = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}_n$ where *n* is the *deferred* period (e.g., freehold land under a 99-year lease — the land's value becomes a *reversion* to the lessor after 99 years, so a reversionary factor is applied to the capitalised ground rent).  
   - **Used when:** A present lease has a known *unexpired* term; the property reverts to the owner at lease-end.

4. **Annuity Method (with Sinking Fund)**  
   - **Formula:** $\text{CV} = \text{NAR} \times \dfrac{1}{r + i}$ where *i* = sinking-fund instalment rate.  
   - **Used when:** The landlord must *replace* the building at the end of its life and therefore sets aside an annual SF contribution *from* the rent.

> **Suitability in Kerala Context:** The *Term* and *Reversionary* methods are most common in Kerala — the latter for *Vadakara, Thalassery* and *Kochi* port-area leases that pre-date 1971 (Kerala Land Reforms Ordinance).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks**

#### Q.B.(a) Numerical — Profit Method for a Hotel [7 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Adapted)]**  
> A 4-star hotel in Kumarakom has the following data:  
> - Average annual **gross revenue** from rooms, F&B, and banquets: **₹3,50,00,000**  
> - **Operating expenses** (staff, consumables, electricity, marketing, licence): **62 % of gross revenue**  
> - **Operator's profit** (entrepreneurial return): **8 % of gross revenue**  
> - Recommended **Profit Multiplier (PM)** for 4-star category: **5**  
>  
> Compute the **Capital Value** of the hotel property using the Profit Method.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Operating Expenses:**

$$ \text{OpEx} = 0.62 \times 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}17{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 2 — Operator's Profit:**

$$ \text{OprProfit} = 0.08 \times 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 = 28{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 3 — Net Profit attributable to Property:**

$$ \text{NP} = 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}17{,}00{,}000 - 28{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}05{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

**Step 4 — Apply Profit Multiplier (PM = 5):**

$$ \text{CV} = \text{NP} \times \text{PM} = 1{,}05{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = 5{,}25{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

> **Final Answer:** Capital Value of the hotel property ≈ **₹5.25 crore**.

---

#### Q.B.(b) Descriptive — Conditions & Examples of Profit Method [7 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**  
> Discuss the **conditions under which the Profit Method** of valuation is adopted. Enumerate any **four examples** of properties valued by this method. Compare its **advantages** over the Rental Method.

**Model Answer:**

**Conditions for Adoption of the Profit Method:**

1. The property is used for a *specific trade* (hospitality, entertainment, healthcare) whose value is inseparable from the *business conducted* on it.
2. **Reliable trading accounts** of recent years are available (typically 3 years' audited P&L).
3. The **profit trend is stable** (or shows a *declining* trend that the valuer can normalise).
4. The property is *rarely sold* for an *alternative use* — i.e., it is a *specialised asset*.
5. **Adequate data** on similar trading concerns (their sale prices and profits) exists to derive a *multiplier*.

**Four Examples:**

| # | Property Type | Typical PM |
|---|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | 3-Star / 4-Star Hotel | 4 – 6 |
| 2 | Single-Screen Cinema | 7 – 10 |
| 3 | Private Multi-Speciality Hospital | 3 – 5 |
| 4 | Petrol Pump (highway) | 4 – 6 |

**Advantages of the Profit Method over the Rental Method:**

| Aspect | Advantage |
|--------|-----------|
| **Realism** | Captures the *actual* earning power of a trading asset, which *rent alone* may understate. |
| **Going Concern** | Reflects the value of the *business* (goodwill + licences + repeat customers), not just the bricks. |
| **Industry benchmarking** | Allows comparison across the *trade* (e.g., comparing two 3-star hotels in different cities via PM). |
| **Court acceptance** | Widely accepted by IT, banks, and the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal for *trading concerns*. |

> **Disadvantage:** The PM is *subjective* and can vary ±20 % between valuers; hence the KTU examiner often *prescribes* the PM in the question stem to maintain uniformity.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Easy Marks (Module 3 — Methods of Valuation):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up YP formulas** — Using perpetuity YP (= 1/*r*) for a *finite-life* building inflates the value by 5–15 %. **Always check "remaining life."**
> 2. **Forgetting to annualise** — Rent is given *monthly* in 70 % of KTU numericals. Multiplying YP by the *monthly* rent is a classic 7-mark killer.
> 3. **Not deducting the operator's profit** — In Profit-Method questions, students often capitalise the *gross* profit. The operator's profit is NOT a property-derived income and must be deducted.
> 4. **Direct Comparison — using quoted, not transacted, prices** — Brokers' asking prices are 8–12 % higher than actual sale prices. Examiners explicitly mark down for this.
> 5. **Forgetting to add land value** — In Rental Method, when outgoings are deducted and YP applied to the building, the *land* is still there. Many students present only the building's value and *omit the land addition* (3–4 marks lost).
> 6. **Unit confusion in Direct Comparison** — Mixing ₹/cent and ₹/sq.ft leads to massive errors. Always normalise to a single unit first.
> 7. **Vacancy allowance** — Often missed. KTU examiners love to test this (5 % of GR).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Methods of Valuation (PECET862)**

- 🔑 **Three Methods of Valuation** (Land + Buildings):
  1. **Rental Method** — for *rented* properties → CV = NAR × YP
  2. **Direct Comparison of Capital** — for *owner-occupied* / open-market sale properties → adjusted unit-rate method
  3. **Profit Method** — for *trading-concern* properties (hotels, cinemas, hospitals) → CV = Net Profit × PM

- 🔑 **Year's Purchase (YP)** — the multiplier:
  - Perpetuity: YP = 1/*r*
  - Finite life: YP = [(1+*r*)^*n* − 1] / [*r* (1+*r*)^*n*]
  - Sinking Fund: YP = 1/(*r* + *i*)

- 🔑 **Outgoings (deduct from Gross Rent):** Tax + Repairs + Insurance + Management + Vacancy + (optional) Sinking Fund.

- 🔑 **Land & Building must be valued separately**, then summed, when the building has a *finite life*. Land is *reversionary* (perpetuity YP).

- 🔑 **Profit Multipliers (Indicative):** Hotels 4–6, Cinemas 7–10, Hospitals 3–5, Petrol pumps 4–6, Theatres 4–6.

- 🔑 **Direct Comparison — 6 steps:** Identify comparables → Verify sales → Compute unit rate → Time-adjust → Feature-adjust → Average × Area.

- 🔑 **Always annualise the rent** before any YP multiplication. **Always use the multiplier given in the question** for Profit-Method problems.

- 🔑 **Higher rate of interest → lower YP → lower CV** (interest-rate sensitivity of the Rental Method).

- 🔑 **Sinking Fund** is a *conservative* approach used when the landlord must replace the building at the end of its life.

- 🔑 **For KTU viva:** Be ready to explain *why* a particular method is chosen for a particular property class — the decision matrix in §3.4 is your cheat sheet.

- 🔑 **Examiner's most-loved question:** *"Compare Rental Method and Profit Method"* — memorise the 6-row table in Q2 above (5.1).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Methods of Valuation of Land with Buildings** ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Examples
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Examples

# Numerical Examples – Valuation of Real Properties

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Examples in Valuation** are structured computational exercises that apply the mathematical models of property valuation—Years' Purchase (YP), Sinking Fund, Depreciation, and Capitalized Value—to real estate scenarios. They consolidate theoretical formulas into quantifiable engineering decisions.

### Formal Definition

A **numerical valuation problem** is a deterministic computational exercise wherein the *present worth*, *future worth*, *annual sinking fund contribution*, *depreciated value*, or *capitalized rent* of a real property (land, building, or both) is computed using accepted financial mathematics, as prescribed under the KTU 2024 syllabus framework.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of valuation as **"translating future money into today's terms."** A building that earns ₹10,000 per month *forever* is not worth ₹10,000—it's worth a *lump sum* today that, if invested at an interest rate, would generate that ₹10,000 monthly. The translator is the **Years' Purchase (YP)** factor. Numerical examples simply put numbers into this translator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation Problems:**
> - **Rate of Interest (r)** → Usually **8% to 12% per annum** (unless stated otherwise).
> - **YP (Years' Purchase)** → A multiplier; dimensionless.
> - **Scrap/Salvage Value (S)** → Typically **10% of original cost** if not given.
