> - **Life of Building (n)** → Usually **60 to 100 years** for residential, **40 to 60 years** for commercial.

### Key Terminology Recap

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| C | Original Cost of Building | ₹ |
| S | Salvage / Scrap Value | ₹ |
| n | Useful Life | Years |
| r | Rate of Interest | % p.a. |
| YP | Years' Purchase | Number |
| A | Annual Sinking Fund | ₹/year |
| I | Annual Income / Rent | ₹/year |
| CV | Capitalized Value | ₹ |
| D | Depreciation | ₹ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Logic Chain of Property Valuation

Every numerical problem in Module 3 follows a **5-step logic chain**:

1. **Identify the Property Type** → Land-only, Building-only, or Land + Building.
2. **Choose the Valuation Method** → Rental, Comparison, Profit, or Cost method.
3. **Compute the Gross Income / Rent** → Based on market data.
4. **Deduct Outgoings** → Repairs, taxes, insurance, management (~25–40% of gross).
5. **Apply YP or Depreciation Model** → To obtain capitalized or depreciated value.

### Why YP Exists (The "Why")

> If you invest **₹X** today at interest rate **r**, the annual interest you receive is **X × r**. Conversely, to get an annual income of **₹I** in perpetuity, you need **X = I / r**. This X is the **Capitalized Value**, and **1/r is the YP**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | YP in Perpetuity | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Land (indefinite life) |
| 2 | YP for Limited Period (n years) | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ | Building (finite life) |
| 3 | Capitalized Value | $CV = \text{Net Income} \times YP$ | Core valuation equation |
| 4 | Annual Sinking Fund (A) | $A = S \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Replacement reserve |
| 5 | Straight Line Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | Equal yearly amount |
| 6 | Book Value (SLM) | $V_n = C - nD = C - \dfrac{n(C-S)}{n}$ | At year n |
| 7 | Declining Balance (WDV) | $D_n = C \cdot b(1-b)^{n-1}$ | b = depreciation rate |
| 8 | Sinking Fund Depreciation | $D = (C - S) \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Compound interest version |
| 9 | Total Annual Cost of Building | $\text{TAC} = I_{interest} + I_{sinking} + I_{repairs} + I_{taxes}$ | For rental method |
| 10 | Gross Rent Multiplier | $GRM = \dfrac{\text{Sale Price}}{\text{Annual Rent}}$ | Quick comparison tool |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of RERA-compliant valuations, bank mortgage assessments, property tax disputes, and government acquisition (LA Act) compensation** in India. Every civil engineer in valuation consultancy uses them daily.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. Do not skip any line—KTU valuation expects each step on the answer sheet.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 1: Years' Purchase (YP) in Perpetuity vs. Limited Period**

**Problem Statement:**
A property yields a net annual income of **₹1,20,000**. The interest rate is **10% p.a.** Find the Capitalized Value (CV) assuming:
*(a)* The property has a **perpetual** life (e.g., land).
*(b)* The property has a **limited** life of **50 years** (e.g., a building).

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Perpetual Life**

The formula for YP in perpetuity is:

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{r}$$

Given: $r = 10\% = 0.10$

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10 \text{ years}$$

The Capitalized Value is:

$$CV = \text{Net Income} \times YP$$

$$CV = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 10 = ₹12{,}00{,}000$$

> **Answer (a): CV = ₹12,00,000 (Twelve Lakhs)**

---

**Part (b): Limited Life of 50 Years**

The formula for YP for a limited period is:

$$YP_{n} = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$$

Given: $r = 0.10$, $n = 50$ years.

First, compute $(1+r)^n$:

$$(1.10)^{50} = 117.3909$$

Now substitute:

$$YP_{50} = \frac{117.3909 - 1}{0.10 \times 117.3909} = \frac{116.3909}{11.73909}$$

$$YP_{50} = 9.9148 \text{ years}$$

Therefore:

$$CV = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 9.9148 = ₹11{,}89{,}776$$

> **Answer (b): CV = ₹11,89,776**

**Interpretation:** Notice that for a 50-year building, CV is slightly *less* than for perpetual land (₹12L vs ₹11.90L). This reflects the **finite earning capacity** of a structure that will eventually collapse.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 2: Sinking Fund Calculation**

**Problem Statement:**
A building worth **₹10,00,000** today has a life of **40 years** and a scrap value of **₹1,00,000**. The interest rate is **8% p.a.** Find the **annual sinking fund contribution** required to replace the building at the end of its life.

#### Solution:

The sinking fund formula is:

$$A = S_f \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$

Where $S_f$ = Net amount to be replaced = $C - S = 10{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹9{,}00{,}000$.

Given: $r = 0.08$, $n = 40$.

Compute the denominator:

$$(1.08)^{40} = 21.7245$$

$$(1.08)^{40} - 1 = 20.7245$$

$$r \times [(1.08)^{40} - 1] = 0.08 \times 20.7245 = 1.65796$$

The annual sinking fund is:

$$A = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{20.7245} = ₹43{,}425 \text{ per year}$$

> **Answer: A = ₹43,425 per annum**

**Verification:** A small amount saved each year, compounded at 8%, will accumulate to ₹9,00,000 in 40 years, enough to rebuild the structure.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 3: Depreciation by Three Methods**

**Problem Statement:**
A building costs **₹5,00,000** with a useful life of **25 years** and a scrap value of **₹50,000**. Compute the depreciated value at the **end of 10 years** using:
*(a)* Straight Line Method
*(b)* Declining Balance Method (rate = 5%)
*(c)* Sinking Fund Method (r = 6%)

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Straight Line Method (SLM)**

Annual depreciation is constant:

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{25} = ₹18{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

After 10 years, total depreciation = $10 \times 18{,}000 = ₹1{,}80{,}000$.

$$V_{10} = C - (\text{Total Depreciation}) = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 = ₹3{,}20{,}000$$

> **Answer (a): V₁₀ = ₹3,20,000**

---

**Part (b): Declining Balance Method (DBM)**

The depreciation each year is a fixed *percentage* of the *diminishing* book value. Given rate $b = 5\% = 0.05$.

| Year | Book Value (Start) | Depreciation (5% of BV) | Book Value (End) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 25,000 | 4,75,000 |
| 2 | 4,75,000 | 23,750 | 4,51,250 |
| 3 | 4,51,250 | 22,563 | 4,28,687 |
| 4 | 4,28,687 | 21,434 | 4,07,253 |
| 5 | 4,07,253 | 20,363 | 3,86,890 |
| 6 | 3,86,890 | 19,345 | 3,67,545 |
| 7 | 3,67,545 | 18,377 | 3,49,168 |
| 8 | 3,49,168 | 17,458 | 3,31,710 |
| 9 | 3,31,710 | 16,586 | 3,15,124 |
| 10 | 3,15,124 | 15,756 | **2,99,368** |

> **Answer (b): V₁₀ = ₹2,99,368**

---

**Part (c): Sinking Fund Method (SFM)**

Annual depreciation is the **sinking fund contribution** needed to fund the *depreciation amount* $(C - S)$:

$$A = (C - S) \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{(1.06)^{25} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.06)^{25}$:

$$(1.06)^{25} = 4.2919$$

$$(1.06)^{25} - 1 = 3.2919$$

$$A = 4{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{3.2919} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.018227 = ₹8{,}202 \text{ per year}$$

After 10 years, accumulated sinking fund:

$$S_{10} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{10} - 1}{r} = 8{,}202 \times \frac{(1.06)^{10} - 1}{0.06}$$

$$(1.06)^{10} = 1.7908$$

$$S_{10} = 8{,}202 \times \frac{0.7908}{0.06} = 8{,}202 \times 13.1808 = ₹1{,}08{,}108$$

$$V_{10} = C - S_{10} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}08{,}108 = ₹3{,}91{,}892$$

> **Answer (c): V₁₀ = ₹3,91,892**

**Comparative Insight:**

| Method | V₁₀ (₹) | Comments |
|---|---|---|
| SLM | 3,20,000 | Linear; ignores interest |
| DBM (5%) | 2,99,368 | Aggressive; front-loaded |
| SFM (6%) | 3,91,892 | Conservative; respects time value |

> **Conclusion:** SFM gives the *highest* book value because depreciation is funded by the *growth* of small deposits, not the full scrap-value gap.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 4: Valuation by Rental Method (Total Cost Approach)**

**Problem Statement:**
A two-storied building has:
- Plinth area = **100 m² per floor**
- Cost of construction = **₹2,000/m²**
- Expected life = **60 years**
- Scrap value = **10% of cost**
- Annual repairs = **1% of cost**
- Annual taxes & insurance = **0.5% of cost**
- Sinking fund rate = **5% p.a.**
- Net annual rent = **₹30,000**

Find: *(a)* Capitalized value of the building. *(b)* Total value of the property if land value is ₹5,00,000.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Cost of Building**

$$C = 2 \times 100 \times 2000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Annual Outgoings**

| Item | Calculation | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Repairs | 1% of 4,00,000 | 4,000 |
| Taxes & Insurance | 0.5% of 4,00,000 | 2,000 |
| Sinking Fund (A) | $3{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{(1.05)^{60}-1}$ | 4,800 |
| **Total Outgoings** | | **10,800** |

Compute Sinking Fund precisely:
- $(1.05)^{60} = 18.6792$
- $(1.05)^{60} - 1 = 17.6792$
- $A = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 0.05 / 17.6792 = 18{,}000 / 17.6792 = ₹1{,}018$ per year

Let me recalculate using $S = 10\% \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}000$:

Net amount to be replaced = $C - S = 4{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000 = ₹3{,}60{,}000$

$$A = 3{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{17.6792} = ₹1{,}018$$

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Repairs (1%) | 4,000 |
| Taxes & Insurance (0.5%) | 2,000 |
| Sinking Fund (A) | 1,018 |
| **Total Outgoings** | **7,018** |

**Step 3: Net Income**

$$\text{Net Income} = \text{Gross Rent} - \text{Outgoings} = 30{,}000 - 7{,}018 = ₹22{,}982$$

**Step 4: Years' Purchase (Limited Period, 60 years, 5%)**

$$YP_{60} = \frac{(1.05)^{60} - 1}{0.05 \times (1.05)^{60}} = \frac{17.6792}{0.05 \times 18.6792} = \frac{17.6792}{0.93396}$$

$$YP_{60} = 18.9293 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5: Capitalized Value of Building**

$$CV_{building} = 22{,}982 \times 18.9293 = ₹4{,}34{,}911$$

**Step 6: Total Value**

$$V_{total} = V_{land} + V_{building} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}34{,}911 = ₹9{,}34{,}911$$

> **Answer:** **(a) Building CV = ₹4,34,911** | **(b) Total Property Value = ₹9,34,911**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 5: Valuation by Direct Comparison Method**

**Problem Statement:**
A property is being valued. Three comparable properties in the locality recently sold as follows:

| Property | Plinth Area (m²) | Sale Price (₹) | Age (years) | Life (years) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 150 | 18,00,000 | 10 | 60 |
| B | 200 | 22,00,000 | 20 | 60 |
| C | 180 | 19,50,000 | 5 | 60 |

The subject property has plinth area = **170 m²**, age = **15 years**, life = **60 years**, and life of property = 60 years. Compute the value.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute rate per m² of each comparable**

| Property | Rate (₹/m²) | Depreciation | Effective Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 18,00,000 / 150 = 12,000 | 10/60 = 16.67% | 12,000 / (1 - 0.1667) = 14,400 |
| B | 22,00,000 / 200 = 11,000 | 20/60 = 33.33% | 11,000 / (1 - 0.3333) = 16,500 |
| C | 19,50,000 / 180 = 10,833 | 5/60 = 8.33% | 10,833 / (1 - 0.0833) = 11,818 |

**Step 2: Average effective rate**

$$\text{Avg Rate} = \frac{14{,}400 + 16{,}500 + 11{,}818}{3} = \frac{42{,}718}{3} = ₹14{,}239/\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Gross value of subject property (assuming new)**

$$V_{new} = 14{,}239 \times 170 = ₹24{,}20{,}630$$

**Step 4: Depreciate for 15 years (SLM)**

$$V_{15} = 24{,}20{,}630 \times \left(1 - \frac{15}{60}\right) = 24{,}20{,}630 \times 0.75 = ₹18{,}15{,}473$$

> **Answer: Valued Price = ₹18,15,473**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 6: Valuation by Profit Method (for Hotels, Theatres, Shops)**

**Problem Statement:**
A hotel has:
- Annual gross profit = **₹12,00,000**
- Total expenses (excluding interest) = **₹7,00,000**
- Capital invested = **₹40,00,000**
- Expected rate of return = **12% p.a.**

Find the goodwill and total value of the hotel.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Net Profit (after deducting expected return on capital)**

$$\text{Net Profit (after expected return)} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Expected return on capital} = 12\% \times 40{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}80{,}000$$

$$\text{Actual Profit} - \text{Expected Profit} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = ₹20{,}000$$

**Step 2: Goodwill using Years' Purchase of Super Profit**

YP at 12% (perpetuity) = 1/0.12 = 8.33 years.

$$\text{Goodwill} = 20{,}000 \times 8.33 = ₹1{,}66{,}600$$

**Step 3: Total Value of Hotel**

$$V_{total} = V_{tangible} + \text{Goodwill} = 40{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}66{,}600 = ₹41{,}66{,}600$$

> **Answer: Goodwill = ₹1,66,600; Total Value = ₹41,66,600**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 7: Annuity and Free-Hold vs. Lease-Hold**

**Problem Statement:**
A property is on a **30-year lease**. The net annual rent is **₹80,000** and the interest rate is **9% p.a.** Find:
*(a)* Value of the lease-hold (30-year) interest.
*(b)* Value of the free-hold (perpetual) interest.
*(c)* Loss to lease-holder = Free-hold value − Lease-hold value.

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Lease-hold (30 years) YP**

$$YP_{30} = \frac{(1.09)^{30} - 1}{0.09 \times (1.09)^{30}}$$

$(1.09)^{30} = 13.2677$

$$YP_{30} = \frac{13.2677 - 1}{0.09 \times 13.2677} = \frac{12.2677}{1.1941} = 10.2736 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{lease} = 80{,}000 \times 10.2736 = ₹8{,}21{,}888$$

**Part (b): Free-hold (Perpetual) YP**

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.09} = 11.11 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{free} = 80{,}000 \times 11.11 = ₹8{,}88{,}888$$

**Part (c): Loss to Lease-Holder**

$$\text{Loss} = 8{,}88{,}888 - 8{,}21{,}888 = ₹67{,}000$$

> **Answer:** (a) **₹8,21,888** | (b) **₹8,88,888** | (c) **₹67,000**

**Real-world use:** This is exactly how RERA determines compensation for flat purchasers on a 99-year lease.

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 8: Reversionary Value (Land + Building)**

**Problem Statement:**
A building yields a net income of **₹60,000 p.a.** for the next **25 years**, after which the land alone will yield ₹30,000 p.a. perpetually. Interest rate = **10% p.a.** Find the total present value.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: PV of building income (25 years)**

$$YP_{25} = \frac{(1.10)^{25} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{25}}$$

$(1.10)^{25} = 10.8347$

$$YP_{25} = \frac{9.8347}{1.08347} = 9.0770 \text{ years}$$

$$PV_{building} = 60{,}000 \times 9.0770 = ₹5{,}44{,}620$$

**Step 2: PV of land (reversion at year 25 + perpetuity starting year 26)**

Value of land in perpetuity (at year 25):

$$V_{land@25} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.10} = ₹3{,}00{,}000$$

Discount this to present (year 0) using $(1.10)^{25} = 10.8347$:

$$PV_{land} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10.8347} = ₹27{,}689$$

**Step 3: Total Present Value**

$$V_{total} = 5{,}44{,}620 + 27{,}689 = ₹5{,}72{,}309$$

> **Answer: Total Present Value = ₹5,72,309**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 9: Mortgage Valuation (Bank Loan Perspective)**

**Problem Statement:**
A building is valued at **₹20,00,000** (market value). The bank's policy allows a loan of **70%** of market value, and applies a safety margin of **20%** on the *forced sale* price. If the forced sale price is 80% of the market value, find the maximum loan that can be safely granted.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Forced Sale Value**

$$V_{forced} = 80\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹16{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Apply Safety Margin (deduct 20% from forced sale value)**

$$V_{safe} = 80\% \times 16{,}00{,}000 = ₹12{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 3: Apply 70% Loan-to-Value (LTV)**

$$\text{Max Loan} = 70\% \times 12{,}80{,}000 = ₹8{,}96{,}000$$

> **Answer: Maximum Safe Loan = ₹8,96,000**

---

### **NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 10: Composite Rate of Interest**

**Problem Statement:**
Capital of **₹5,00,000** is invested, **½ in 8% stock, ¼ in 9% stock, and ¼ in 10% stock.** Find the **average rate of return**.

#### Solution:

$$\text{Weighted Avg} = (0.5 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 10)$$

$$= 4.0 + 2.25 + 2.50 = 8.75\%$$

> **Answer: Average Rate = 8.75% p.a.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The Valuation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Real Property] --> B{What is the asset?}
    B --> C[Land only]
    B --> D[Building only]
    B --> E[Land + Building]

    C --> F[Valuation by Direct Comparison]
    C --> G[Valuation by Belting Method]

    D --> H[Rental Method]
    D --> I[Cost Method with Depreciation]
    D --> J[Profit Method if commercial]

    E --> K[Land at Market Rate]
    E --> L[Building at Depreciated Cost]
    E --> M[Sum: Composite Value]

    F --> N[Final Capitalized Value]
    G --> N
    H --> N
    I --> N
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Architecture of a Valuation Report

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identification of Property] --> B[Inspection and Data Collection]
    B --> C[Choice of Method]
    C --> D[Compute Gross Income]
    D --> E[Deduct Outgoings]
    E --> F[Apply YP Factor]
    F --> G[Add Land Value]
    G --> H[Final Valuation Report]
    H --> I[Certified Valuer Sign-off]
```

### Diagram 3: Depreciation Method Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Original Cost C] --> L1{Depreciation Method}
    L1 --> M1[Straight Line: D = CSn]
    L1 --> M2[Declining Balance: b% of WDV]
    L1 --> M3[Sinking Fund: Compound Interest]

    M1 --> Out1[Equal Yearly D]
    M2 --> Out2[High D initially, decreases]
    M3 --> Out3[Smaller D, accumulates via interest]

    Out1 --> Final[Book Value at year n]
    Out2 --> Final
    Out3 --> Final
```

### Diagram 4: Rental Method Computational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plinth Area × Rate = Cost C] --> P2[Salvage S = 10% C]
    P2 --> P3[Life n in years]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Annual Sinking Fund A]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Annual Repairs R]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Annual Taxes T]
    P6 --> P7[Total Outgoings = A plus R plus T]
    P7 --> P8[Net Income = Rent minus Outgoings]
    P8 --> P9[YP for n years at r%]
    P9 --> P10[Capitalized Value = NI × YP]
```

### Diagram 5: Mermaid Diagram - Numerical Example Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Read problem] --> B[Step 2: Identify given values]
    B --> C[Step 3: Select formula]
    C --> D[Step 4: Substitute]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute]
    E --> F[Step 6: Verify with intuition]
    F --> G[Step 7: Final answer with units]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert % to decimal** (e.g., writing r = 10 instead of 0.10). This alone can cost **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Not deducting outgoings** before multiplying by YP — many students apply YP to *gross* rent instead of *net* rent.
> 3. **Mixing up SLM, DBM, SFM** — note that SFM uses *compound* interest formulas; SLM does not.
> 4. **Not specifying units (₹)** in final answers — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Rounding off too early** — keep **4 decimal places** during calculation, round only at the end.

---

### **Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Years' Purchase (YP). What is its value for a perpetual income stream at 8% p.a.?

**Model Answer:**
Years' Purchase (YP) is the multiplier that converts a periodic income into its present capitalized value. It is mathematically defined as the present value of a Re. 1 annuity.

$$YP = \frac{1}{r}$$

For $r = 8\% = 0.08$:

$$YP = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \text{ years}$$

> **Marks: 3**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between Straight Line Method and Sinking Fund Method of depreciation (any three points).

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Straight Line Method (SLM) | Sinking Fund Method (SFM) |
|---|---|---|
| Depreciation | Equal annual amount | Variable; funded by interest growth |
| Interest on investments | Ignored | Considered at compound rate |
| Annual cost to owner | Constant | Variable; decreases with time |
| Suitability | Short-life assets | Long-life buildings |

> **Marks: 3**

---

### **Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A building costs **₹8,00,000** to construct. Its useful life is **50 years** and the scrap value is **₹80,000**. Calculate the **annual sinking fund** and the **total amount accumulated in the sinking fund at the end of 20 years**, assuming the interest rate is **6% p.a.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The same building yields a monthly rent of **₹12,000**. The owner spends **8%** of the annual rent on repairs, **₹4,000 p.a.** on taxes, and the sinking fund from part (a) is set aside annually. Calculate the **Capitalized Value of the building** using the rental method at **6% p.a.** for a **50-year life**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Sinking Fund Calculation (7 marks)**

Given:
- $C = ₹8,00,000$
- $S = ₹80,000$
- $n = 50$ years
- $r = 6\% = 0.06$

Net replacement amount:

$$S_f = C - S = 8{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = ₹7{,}20{,}000$$

Annual sinking fund:

$$A = S_f \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1} = 7{,}20{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{(1.06)^{50} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.06)^{50} = 18.4202$

$$(1.06)^{50} - 1 = 17.4202$$

$$A = 7{,}20{,}000 \times \frac{0.06}{17.4202} = 7{,}20{,}000 \times 0.003444 = ₹2{,}479.7 \text{ per year}$$

**Accumulated Sinking Fund after 20 years:**

$$\text{ASF} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{20} - 1}{r} = 2{,}479.7 \times \frac{(1.06)^{20} - 1}{0.06}$$

$(1.06)^{20} = 3.2071$

$$\text{ASF} = 2{,}479.7 \times \frac{2.2071}{0.06} = 2{,}479.7 \times 36.7856 = ₹91{,}219$$

> **Answer (a):** Annual Sinking Fund = **₹2,480/year**; ASF after 20 years = **₹91,219**

---

**Part (b) — Capitalized Value (7 marks)**

Given:
- Monthly rent = ₹12,000 → Annual gross rent = ₹1,44,000
- Repairs = 8% of 1,44,000 = ₹11,520
- Taxes = ₹4,000
- Sinking Fund (A) = ₹2,480
- $n = 50$, $r = 6\%$

Total outgoings = $11{,}520 + 4{,}000 + 2{,}480 = ₹18{,}000$

Net income = $1{,}44{,}000 - 18{,}000 = ₹1{,}26{,}000$

Years' Purchase for 50 years at 6%:

$$YP_{50} = \frac{(1.06)^{50} - 1}{0.06 \times (1.06)^{50}} = \frac{17.4202}{0.06 \times 18.4202} = \frac{17.4202}{1.10521} = 15.7619 \text{ years}$$

Capitalized Value:

$$CV = \text{Net Income} \times YP = 1{,}26{,}000 \times 15.7619 = ₹19{,}86{,}000$$

> **Answer (b): Capitalized Value = ₹19,86,000**

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A property yields a **net annual income of ₹1,50,000** perpetually. The interest rate is **10% p.a.** Find the Capitalized Value. If the property is on a **20-year lease** instead, find the lease-hold value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A building costs **₹6,00,000** to construct, has a life of **40 years**, and scrap value of **₹60,000**. Compute the book value at the end of **15 years** using:
*(i)* Straight Line Method
*(ii)* Declining Balance Method (rate = 4%)
*(iii)* Sinking Fund Method (r = 5%) **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Perpetual vs. Lease-hold Value (7 marks)**

**Perpetual (Free-hold):**

$$YP_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{free} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 10 = ₹15{,}00{,}000$$

**Lease-hold (20 years):**

$$YP_{20} = \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{20}}$$

$(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$

$$YP_{20} = \frac{5.7275}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.67275} = 8.5136 \text{ years}$$

$$V_{lease} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 8.5136 = ₹12{,}77{,}040$$

**Loss to lease-holder** = $15{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}77{,}040 = ₹2{,}22{,}960$

> **Answer (a):** **Free-hold = ₹15,00,000**; **Lease-hold = ₹12,77,040**; **Loss = ₹2,22,960**

---

**Part (b) — Three Depreciation Methods (7 marks)**

Given: $C = ₹6,00{,}000$, $S = ₹60{,}000$, $n = 40$, age = 15 years.

**(i) Straight Line Method:**

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000}{40} = ₹13{,}500 \text{ per year}$$

$$V_{15} = C - 15D = 6{,}00{,}000 - 15 \times 13{,}500 = 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}02{,}500 = ₹3{,}97{,}500$$

> **SLM: V₁₅ = ₹3,97,500**

**(ii) Declining Balance Method (4%):**

Using the formula $V_n = C \cdot (1-b)^n$:

$$V_{15} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times (0.96)^{15} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5421 = ₹3{,}25{,}260$$

> **DBM: V₁₅ = ₹3,25,260**

**(iii) Sinking Fund Method (5%):**

Annual depreciation:

$$A = (C - S) \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1} = 5{,}40{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{(1.05)^{40} - 1}$$

$(1.05)^{40} = 7.0400$; $7.0400 - 1 = 6.0400$

$$A = 5{,}40{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{6.0400} = 5{,}40{,}000 \times 0.008278 = ₹4{,}470 \text{ per year}$$

Accumulated fund at year 15:

$$\text{ASF}_{15} = A \cdot \frac{(1.05)^{15} - 1}{0.05} = 4{,}470 \times \frac{2.0789 - 1}{0.05} = 4{,}470 \times 21.5786 = ₹96{,}456$$

$$V_{15} = C - \text{ASF}_{15} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 96{,}456 = ₹5{,}03{,}544$$

> **SFM: V₁₅ = ₹5,03,544**

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | V₁₅ (₹) |
|---|---|
| SLM | 3,97,500 |
| DBM (4%) | 3,25,260 |
| SFM (5%) | 5,03,544 |

> **Answer (b):** As tabulated above.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Numerical Valuation Problems:**

- **Perpetuity YP** = $1/r$ — used for **land**.
- **Limited-period YP** = $\dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ — used for **buildings**.
- **Capitalized Value** = **Net Income × YP** — *never* apply YP to gross rent.
- **Sinking Fund** = $(C - S) \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ — for **replacement reserve**.
- **SLM Depreciation** = $\dfrac{C - S}{n}$ — constant yearly drop.
- **DBM Depreciation** = $b \cdot V_{prev}$ — geometric, front-loaded.
- **SFM Depreciation** = Sinking fund contributions, accumulated at compound interest.
- **Rental Method** sequence: Gross Rent → Outgoings (Repairs + Taxes + Sinking Fund) → Net Income → × YP → Capitalized Value.
- **Comparison Method** requires *normalizing* for age by adding back depreciation.
- **Profit Method** uses *Goodwill* = Super Profit × YP.
- **Free-hold > Lease-hold value** — always; difference = loss to lease-holder.
- **Reversionary problems**: value the *future* perpetuity, then discount to present.
- **Mortgage problems**: Forced Sale Value × (1 − Safety Margin) = Bank's safe limit.
- **Always convert r to decimal** — 10% = 0.10, not 10.
- **Use the year of valuation as "present"** — don't accidentally use 1980 as base year.
- **Composite rate of return** = weighted average by capital fraction.
- **For BELT method in Module 3 land valuation** — front belt has highest depth × rate, decreasing toward rear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:**
> - Always **state the formula first** (1 mark).
> - Then substitute values (2 marks).
> - Then compute (3 marks).
> - Then conclude with a **boxed answer and units** (1 mark).
> - KTU gives **1 mark for free** if all units are written correctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valuation of Apartment: FSI, Super Built-up Area, Undivided Share of Land
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Valuation of Apartment: FSI, Super Built-up Area, Undivided Share of Land

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Floor Space Index (FSI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSI (Floor Space Index)** — also called **Floor Area Ratio (FAR)** — is the ratio of the total permissible built-up area on a plot to the **net plot area** of the land.

$$
\text{FSI} = \frac{\text{Total Built-up Area of all Floors}}{\text{Net Plot Area}}
$$

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Pizza Slice" Concept:**  
Imagine your plot of land is a large pizza. The municipal authority tells you how thick a stack of pizza slices you can stack on it (the FSI). A plot in a dense city like Mumbai has a thick stack (FSI ≈ 3.0–5.0), while a suburb may only allow a thin stack (FSI ≈ 1.0–1.5). The "thickness" of the stack represents the total floor area you are allowed to construct on that land.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology:** While **FSI** is the term used in Kerala, Maharashtra, and most of India, the term **FAR (Floor Area Ratio)** is used in NCR (Delhi, Gurgaon, Noida). They mean the **same thing**.

---

### 1.2 Super Built-up Area (SBA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Super Built-up Area (SBA)** is the total saleable area of an apartment, which includes the apartment's enclosed built-up area **plus** a proportionate share of the building's common areas (lobby, staircase, lift, amenities, etc.).

$$
\text{SBA} = \text{Built-up Area} + \text{Proportionate Common Area}
$$

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Office Floor" Concept:**  
Imagine you rent a workstation inside a large co-working office. Your desk area is the *Carpet Area*. The desk plus your partition wall plus the area covered by your chair is the *Built-up Area*. But you are billed for more — because you also share the reception, the pantry, the washroom, and the conference room. That extra, invisible share is the *Super Built-up Area*. Real-estate developers bill apartments based on SBA because it includes all shared infrastructure.

---

### 1.3 Undivided Share of Land (UDS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Undivided Share of Land (UDS)** is the **proportionate, undivided share of the total plot area** that legally belongs to each apartment owner in a multi-storey building. It is computed based on the apartment's area relative to the total built-up area of the building.

$$
\text{UDS} = \frac{\text{Apartment SBA}}{\text{Total SBA of Building}} \times \text{Total Plot Area}
$$

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Cake with Icing" Concept:**  
Suppose a building sits on a 10,000 sq.ft. plot. There are 20 apartments of equal size. Each flat owner legally owns 500 sq.ft. of land — but they cannot point to a specific 500 sq.ft. because the land is shared beneath the entire building. The land is *undivided*. The "icing" of the cake (the superstructure) sits on the "cake" (the land), and every flat owner gets an undivided slice of the cake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Legal Anchor (Kerala):** The **Kerala Apartment Ownership Act, 1983** formally governs the concept of UDS, common areas, and apartment ownership. UDS determines each owner's land rights in case of demolition, redevelopment, or sale of the parent land.

---

### 1.4 Three-Tier Area Hierarchy (At a Glance)

| Term | What It Covers | Typical Share of SBA |
|---|---|---|
| **Carpet Area (CA)** | Area enclosed within walls; usable indoor area | 65 % – 70 % |
| **Built-up Area (BUA)** | CA + thickness of walls + balconies/verandas | 85 % – 90 % |
| **Super Built-up Area (SBA)** | BUA + proportionate common area (lobby, stairs, lift, amenities) | 100 % (Billed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RERA (Real Estate Regulatory Authority) Mandate (2017 onwards):** All builders in India are legally required to quote prices and advertise based on **Carpet Area**, not Super Built-up Area. This has drastically reduced the misuse of SBA-based marketing.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Floor Space Index (FSI) — Mechanics

#### 2.1.1 Operational Definition
FSI is a **land-use control** mechanism. The municipal authority decides the maximum FSI permitted in a given zone (residential, commercial, industrial). The developer cannot exceed the total permissible built-up area.

#### 2.1.2 Logical Steps of FSI Compliance

1. Identify the **net plot area** (deducting any road widening, set-backs, reservations).
2. Refer to the **zonal development plan** to find the permissible FSI for that locality.
3. Compute **maximum permissible total built-up area** = FSI × Plot Area.
4. Distribute the total built-up area across permissible floors (subject to height restrictions, ground coverage, FAR rules).
5. Pay **premium FSI charges** if the developer wishes to construct beyond the standard limit.

#### 2.1.3 Premium FSI & Transferable Development Rights (TDR)
- **Premium FSI:** Developer pays an additional charge to the municipality to exceed the standard FSI.
- **TDR (Transferable Development Rights):** A certificate issued when a piece of land is acquired for public use (e.g., road widening). The landowner can sell this TDR to other developers, who can use it to increase their FSI elsewhere.

---

### 2.2 Super Built-up Area (SBA) — Mechanics

#### 2.2.1 Components of SBA

$$
\text{SBA} = \text{Carpet Area} + \text{External Wall Area} + \text{Balcony/Veranda} + \text{Proportionate Common Areas}
$$

The proportionate common area typically includes:
- Lift and lift-lobby
- Staircase
- Corridor / passage
- Pump room, transformer room, generator room
- Clubhouse, gym, swimming pool (sometimes)
- Parking area (covered)

#### 2.2.2 Loading Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loading Factor** is the multiplier applied to Carpet Area to obtain Super Built-up Area.

$$
\text{Loading Factor} = \frac{\text{SBA}}{\text{CA}}
$$

A typical loading factor in India ranges between **1.25 to 1.50**. A higher loading factor means the buyer pays more for less usable space.

#### 2.2.3 Why Developers Use SBA
Developers use SBA as the billing unit because:
- It includes the cost of common-area construction (lobby, lift, amenities).
- It simplifies pricing of mixed-use apartments.
- It was historically the prevalent norm before the RERA Act (2017).

---

### 2.3 Undivided Share of Land (UDS) — Mechanics

#### 2.3.1 Why UDS Matters
UDS is critical because:
- It is the **only legal proof of land ownership** for the flat buyer.
- It determines each owner's **share in the land cost**, which is taxed differently from construction cost.
- It governs the **proportionate compensation** during redevelopment or acquisition.
- It determines **stake in the building society/owners' association**.

#### 2.3.2 The Apartment Ownership Triad

Every flat owner holds three distinct rights:

1. **Exclusive right** over the apartment unit (the four walls + interior).
2. **Undivided share (UDS)** in the land on which the building stands.
3. **Common right** to use the common areas along with other owners (lobby, stairs, lifts).

#### 2.3.3 UDS Distribution — Logical Steps

1. Determine the **total land area** of the parent plot.
2. Determine the **total SBA** of all apartments in the building.
3. Determine the **SBA of the specific apartment**.
4. Apply the UDS formula.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FSI / FAR | $\text{FSI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Land-use planning |
| 2 | Maximum Buildable Area | $\text{A}_{\text{max}} = \text{FSI} \times \text{Plot Area}$ | sq.ft. or sq.m. | Feasibility analysis |
| 3 | Carpet Area (CA) | $\text{CA} = \text{Built-up Area} - \text{Walls} - \text{Balconies}$ | sq.ft. or sq.m. | RERA billing |
| 4 | Built-up Area (BUA) | $\text{BUA} = \text{CA} + \text{Wall Thickness} + \text{Balcony}$ | sq.ft. or sq.m. | Estimation |
| 5 | Super Built-up Area (SBA) | $\text{SBA} = \text{BUA} + \text{Common Area Share}$ | sq.ft. or sq.m. | Developer billing |
| 6 | Loading Factor | $L = \dfrac{\text{SBA}}{\text{CA}}$ | $1.25 \le L \le 1.50$ | Pricing audit |
| 7 | Undivided Share of Land | $\text{UDS} = \dfrac{\text{SBA}_{\text{apt}}}{\text{SBA}_{\text{building}}} \times \text{Plot Area}$ | sq.ft. or sq.m. | Legal land rights |
| 8 | UDS Fractional | $\text{UDS}_{\text{fraction}} = \dfrac{\text{SBA}_{\text{apt}}}{\text{SBA}_{\text{building}}}$ | Ratio (no unit) | Sale deed record |
| 9 | Land Value per Flat | $\text{LV} = \text{UDS} \times \text{Rate per sq.ft. of Land}$ | ₹ | Cost segregation |
| 10 | Construction Value per Flat | $\text{CV} = \text{SBA} \times \text{Rate per sq.ft. of Construction}$ | ₹ | Cost segregation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Utility:** During valuation, the valuer **segregates** the total apartment value into (a) Land Component (based on UDS) and (b) Construction Component (based on SBA). The land component appreciates; the construction component depreciates. This split is the foundation of depreciation-based valuation under the **Income Approach** and **Cost Approach**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of UDS Formula

**Starting Principle:** Total UDS across all apartments must equal the **total land area** (conservation of land).

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{UDS}_i = \text{Plot Area}
$$

**Step 1:** Assume each apartment's UDS is **proportional to its SBA**:

$$
\text{UDS}_i = k \cdot \text{SBA}_i
$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality.

**Step 2:** Substitute in the conservation equation:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} k \cdot \text{SBA}_i = \text{Plot Area}
$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $k$:

$$
k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{SBA}_i = \text{Plot Area}
$$

**Step 4:** Note that the sum of all SBAs equals the total SBA of the building:

$$
k \cdot \text{SBA}_{\text{building}} = \text{Plot Area}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $k$:

$$
k = \frac{\text{Plot Area}}{\text{SBA}_{\text{building}}}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the original equation:

$$
\boxed{\text{UDS}_i = \frac{\text{SBA}_i}{\text{SBA}_{\text{building}}} \times \text{Plot Area}}
$$

---

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Maximum Buildable Area from FSI

**Given:** FSI limit, plot area.

**Step 1:** The total floor area of all storeys combined = Sum of individual floor areas:

$$
\text{Total Floor Area} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} A_j
$$

**Step 2:** By definition of FSI:

$$
\text{FSI} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} A_j}{\text{Plot Area}}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for total floor area:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{m} A_j = \text{FSI} \times \text{Plot Area}
$$

**Step 4:** The maximum possible sum is bounded by the municipal FSI limit:

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{max, total}} = \text{FSI}_{\text{max}} \times \text{Plot Area}}
$$

---

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of Loading Factor in Terms of Common-Area Percentage

Let:
- $\text{CA}$ = Carpet Area of the apartment
- $C_{\text{apt}}$ = Common-area share added to the apartment

Then:

$$
\text{SBA} = \text{CA} + C_{\text{apt}}
$$

The loading factor:

$$
L = \frac{\text{SBA}}{\text{CA}} = \frac{\text{CA} + C_{\text{apt}}}{\text{CA}} = 1 + \frac{C_{\text{apt}}}{\text{CA}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\frac{C_{\text{apt}}}{\text{CA}} = L - 1}
$$

> **Example:** If $L = 1.30$, then the common area adds **30 %** to the carpet area.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Style)

#### Example 1 — Basic UDS Calculation

> A residential building has 5 floors + ground floor, with **4 flats per floor** and **1 flat on the ground floor**. Each flat has an SBA of **1,200 sq.ft.** The total plot area is **12,000 sq.ft.** Calculate the UDS of one flat.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute total number of flats.

$$
n = (5 \text{ upper floors} \times 4) + 1 = 20 + 1 = 21 \text{ flats}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total SBA of building.

$$
\text{SBA}_{\text{building}} = 21 \times 1{,}200 = 25{,}200 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply UDS formula.

$$
\text{UDS} = \frac{1{,}200}{25{,}200} \times 12{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UDS} = 0.04762 \times 12{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{UDS} = 571.43 \text{ sq.ft.}}
$$

---

#### Example 2 — FSI Feasibility Check

> A developer owns a **2,400 sq.ft.** plot in a zone where permissible FSI is **1.75**. The developer plans to build 4 floors (ground + 3) with **600 sq.ft.** of construction per floor. Is the proposal within the FSI limit? What is the maximum extra buildable area?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute proposed total built-up area.

$$
\text{Total BUA} = 4 \times 600 = 2{,}400 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute proposed FSI.

$$
\text{FSI}_{\text{proposed}} = \frac{2{,}400}{2{,}400} = 1.00
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with permissible.

$$
1.00 < 1.75 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Proposal is within limit. Approved.}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute maximum permissible built-up area.

$$
\text{BUA}_{\text{max}} = 1.75 \times 2{,}400 = 4{,}200 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute extra area possible.

$$
\text{Extra} = 4{,}200 - 2{,}400 = \boxed{1{,}800 \text{ sq.ft. (un-utilised FSI)}}
$$

---

#### Example 3 — Comprehensive Cost Segregation for Valuation

> A 1,200 sq.ft. SBA flat has UDS of **500 sq.ft.** Market rate of land is **₹ 4,500/sq.ft.** and construction rate is **₹ 2,000/sq.ft.** (RCC, including amenities). Compute the value segregation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Land component.

$$
\text{Land Value} = 500 \times 4{,}500 = \text{₹ } 22{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Construction component.

$$
\text{Construction Value} = 1{,}200 \times 2{,}000 = \text{₹ } 24{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3:** Total value.

$$
\text{Total Value} = 22{,}50{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 46{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Step 4:** For depreciation, the **construction component** alone depreciates (using straight-line method over 60 years' life):

$$
\text{Annual Depreciation} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{60} = \text{₹ } 40{,}000/\text{year}
$$

> The land component does **not** depreciate — it only **appreciates**. This is why segregation is critical.

---

#### Example 4 — Loading Factor Audit

> A builder quotes a 1,400 sq.ft. SBA flat at ₹ 3,200/sq.ft. The actual carpet area is 980 sq.ft. Compute the loading factor and the actual price per sq.ft. of carpet area.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Loading factor.

$$
L = \frac{1{,}400}{980} = 1.4286
$$

**Step 2:** Quoted price per SBA sq.ft.

$$
\text{Rate}_{\text{SBA}} = ₹ 3{,}200
$$

**Step 3:** Effective price per carpet sq.ft.

$$
\text{Rate}_{\text{CA}} = ₹ 3{,}200 \times L = 3{,}200 \times 1.4286 = \boxed{₹ 4{,}571.43/\text{sq.ft. of CA}}
$$

> The buyer is effectively paying ₹ 4,571 for every usable sq.ft., not ₹ 3,200 — a **42.86 %** premium on real usable space.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Area-Hierarchy Flow

This diagram shows the **logical progression** from raw plot to billable SBA and the UDS linkage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[PLOT OF LAND] --> B0[Net Plot Area]
    B0 --> C0[FSI Check by Municipality]
    C0 --> D0[Maximum Permissible Built-up Area]
    D0 --> E0[Building Construction]
    E0 --> F0[Carpet Area per Flat]
    F0 --> G0[Add Wall Thickness and Balconies]
    G0 --> H0[Built-up Area per Flat]
    H0 --> I0[Add Proportionate Common Area]
    I0 --> J0[Super Built-up Area per Flat]
    J0 --> K0[Billed Area for Sale]
    J0 --> L0[UDS Computation]
    L0 --> M0[Legal Land Ownership Right]
    A0 --> L0
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — UDS Allocation Logic

This diagram explains the **decision flow** for distributing land share to each apartment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Total Plot Area and Total SBA known] --> S1[Compute total SBA of building]
    S1 --> S2[For each apartment: read SBA of apartment]
    S2 --> S3[Compute ratio of apartment SBA to total SBA]
    S3 --> S4[Multiply ratio by total plot area]
    S4 --> S5[Record UDS in sale deed]
    S5 --> S6[Allocate society share certificate]
    S6 --> S7[End: UDS established for owner]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Valuation Triangulation

This diagram maps how FSI, SBA, and UDS feed into a **comprehensive apartment valuation**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Plot Area] --> A2[FSI Check]
    A2 --> A3[Permissible Buildable Area]
    A3 --> A4[Total SBA of Building]
    A4 --> A5[Per Flat SBA]
    A5 --> A6[Construction Cost Component]
    A5 --> A7[Proportionate UDS]
    A7 --> A8[Land Cost Component]
    A6 --> A9[TOTAL FLAT VALUE]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Apply Depreciation on Construction Only]
    A9 --> A11[Appreciation Applied on Land Only]
    A10 --> A12[Final Valued Price]
    A11 --> A12
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Loading-Factor Economics

This diagram shows how loading factor inflates effective cost.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Quoted Price per SBA] --> R1[Multiply by Loading Factor L]
    R1 --> R2[Effective Price per Carpet Area]
    R3[Total SBA of Flat] --> R4[Quoted Total Price]
    R3 --> R5[Carpet Area of Flat]
    R5 --> R6[Actual Usable Space]
    R4 --> R7[Price per Carpet Area]
    R6 --> R7
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Define Floor Space Index (FSI). How is it different from Floor Area Ratio (FAR)?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **FSI (Floor Space Index)** is the ratio of the total permissible built-up area on a plot to the net plot area. Mathematically,
> $$\text{FSI} = \frac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Net Plot Area}}$$
>
> **FAR (Floor Area Ratio)** is a synonymous term used primarily in Northern India (Delhi NCR). Both FSI and FAR represent the **same physical quantity** and are dimensionless ratios. The term used depends purely on regional convention; the concept is identical. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Explain the term 'Undivided Share of Land' (UDS) with its significance in apartment valuation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Undivided Share of Land (UDS)** is the proportionate, undivided share of the parent plot that legally vests with each apartment owner, computed as:
> $$\text{UDS} = \frac{\text{SBA of apartment}}{\text{Total SBA of building}} \times \text{Total Plot Area}$$
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. It establishes the apartment owner's **legal interest in the land** under the Kerala Apartment Ownership Act, 1983.
> 2. It determines the **land-cost component** of the apartment value, used in cost-segregation for valuation and depreciation calculation.
> 3. It governs **compensation** in case of compulsory acquisition or redevelopment. (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — UDS, FSI & Cost Segregation

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

A residential building is constructed on a plot of **8,000 sq.ft.** in a zone where permissible FSI is **2.0**. The building has **4 floors (G + 3)** with **4 flats per floor**, each having a Carpet Area of **900 sq.ft.** The wall thickness adds 8 % to the carpet area, balconies add 5 %, and the building has a common-area loading factor of **1.30**.

**(a) Compute the following (7 marks):**
1. Total built-up area of the building
2. Super Built-up Area of one flat
3. Undivided Share of Land of one flat
4. Total land value for the building at ₹ 5,000/sq.ft.

**(b) Discuss (7 marks):**
1. Why the land value computed from UDS does not depreciate while the construction value does.
2. What happens to the UDS of a flat in the event of demolition and reconstruction of the building.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Number of flats and total carpet area.**

$$
n = 4 \text{ floors} \times 4 \text{ flats} = 16 \text{ flats}
$$

$$
\text{Total Carpet Area} = 16 \times 900 = 14{,}400 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 2: Built-up area of one flat (Carpet + 8 % walls + 5 % balconies).**

$$
\text{BUA}_{\text{flat}} = 900 \times (1 + 0.08 + 0.05) = 900 \times 1.13 = 1{,}017 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 3: Super Built-up Area of one flat (Loading Factor = 1.30).**

$$
\text{SBA}_{\text{flat}} = 1{,}017 \times 1.30 = 1{,}322.10 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 4: Total SBA of the building.**

$$
\text{SBA}_{\text{building}} = 16 \times 1{,}322.10 = 21{,}153.60 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 5: Total Built-up Area (excluding common area, before loading).**

$$
\text{Total BUA} = 16 \times 1{,}017 = 16{,}272 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 6: UDS of one flat.**

$$
\text{UDS} = \frac{1{,}322.10}{21{,}153.60} \times 8{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UDS} = 0.0625 \times 8{,}000 = \boxed{500 \text{ sq.ft.}}
$$

> *(Alternatively, the UDS can also be computed using CA-weighted ratio: $900/14{,}400 \times 8{,}000 = 500$ sq.ft. — both methods yield the same value when flats are uniform.)*

**Step 7: Total land value.**

$$
\text{Total Land Value} = 8{,}000 \times 5{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

> **Note:** This is the total market value of the land; for one flat, land value = 500 × 5,000 = **₹ 25,00,000**.

#### Part (b) — Discussion

**(b.1) Why land value does not depreciate but construction does:**

Land is a **non-reproducible, non-wasting asset**. It has infinite useful life and is governed by economic, demographic, and locational factors that typically push its value upward (appreciation). Construction, on the other hand, is a **reproducible asset with finite life**. Wear and tear, structural fatigue, weathering, and obsolescence gradually reduce its serviceability. Therefore, the **construction component depreciates** under the straight-line or diminishing-balance method, while the **land component either remains constant or appreciates**. This dichotomy is the foundation of cost-based valuation under the **Cost Approach**.

**(b.2) UDS in case of demolition and reconstruction:**

When a building is demolished (whether due to structural failure, age, or collective redevelopment decision), the **building structure ceases to exist**, but the **UDS of each owner in the land persists**. The land remains the same; only the superstructure is rebuilt. The owners — or their legal heirs/successors — continue to hold the same proportional right in the land. During reconstruction:

- New flats are re-allocated, often using the same UDS ratio.
- If a builder undertakes redevelopment, each UDS-holder is allotted a new flat (or compensation) proportionate to their UDS.
- The **sale deed must be re-executed** to reflect the new building and the same UDS.

> Hence UDS is a **permanent, inheritable right** in the land, independent of the building that stands on it.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

A builder is developing a **5,000 sq.ft.** plot in a residential zone with permissible FSI of **1.80**. The builder plans **ground + 4 floors** (5 floors total), with the following construction scheme:

- Carpet area per flat = **850 sq.ft.**
- 4 flats per floor
- Walls add 10 % of CA; balconies add 6 % of CA
- Common-area loading factor = **1.35**
- Construction cost = **₹ 2,200/sq.ft. of SBA**
- Land cost = **₹ 4,000/sq.ft. of UDS**

**(a) Calculate (7 marks):**
1. Maximum permissible built-up area as per FSI
2. Super Built-up Area of one flat
3. UDS of one flat
4. Total project value (land + construction)

**(b) Explain (7 marks):**
1. The relevance of **RERA 2017** in redefining the role of Super Built-up Area.
2. The difference between **Carpet Area**, **Built-up Area**, and **Super Built-up Area** with neat explanation.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Maximum permissible BUA.**

$$
\text{BUA}_{\text{max}} = \text{FSI} \times \text{Plot} = 1.80 \times 5{,}000 = 9{,}000 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 2: BUA of one flat (CA + 10 % walls + 6 % balcony).**

$$
\text{BUA}_{\text{flat}} = 850 \times 1.16 = 986 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 3: SBA of one flat.**

$$
\text{SBA}_{\text{flat}} = 986 \times 1.35 = 1{,}331.10 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 4: Total flats.**

$$
n = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ flats}
$$

**Step 5: Total SBA of building.**

$$
\text{SBA}_{\text{building}} = 20 \times 1{,}331.10 = 26{,}622 \text{ sq.ft.}
$$

**Step 6: UDS of one flat.**

$$
\text{UDS} = \frac{1{,}331.10}{26{,}622} \times 5{,}000 = 0.0500 \times 5{,}000 = \boxed{250 \text{ sq.ft.}}
$$

> *(Equivalently, since all flats are equal, $\text{UDS} = 5{,}000 / 20 = 250$ sq.ft.)*

**Step 7: Total project value.**

- Land value (one flat) = 250 × 4,000 = ₹ 10,00,000
- Construction value (one flat) = 1,331.10 × 2,200 = ₹ 29,28,420
- Value of one flat = ₹ 39,28,420
- Total project value = 20 × 39,28,420 = **₹ 7,85,68,400**

> **Check on FSI compliance:**
> Total BUA (excl. common) = 20 × 986 = 19,720 sq.ft. Total SBA = 26,622 sq.ft.
> Effective FSI (on SBA basis) = 26,622 / 5,000 = 5.32 — **but** municipal FSI is calculated on the BUA (carpet + walls + balcony), not on SBA. The proposal is **within** the permissible 1.80 FSI if the BUA basis is used.

#### Part (b) — Discussion

**(b.1) Relevance of RERA 2017:**

The **Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2017** is a landmark legislation that overhauled property marketing in India. Its key contributions to apartment area definitions:

1. **Mandatory Carpet-Area-Based Pricing:** All builders must now quote and advertise property prices based on **Carpet Area**, not Super Built-up Area. This protects buyers from the inflated, opaque SBA-based pricing.
2. **Standardized Definition of Carpet Area:** RERA legally defines carpet area as the net usable floor area within walls, excluding external walls, balconies, verandas, and common areas. This eliminates ambiguity.
3. **Disclosure of Common Areas:** Builders must declare all common-area inclusions and any loading factor in the agreement for sale.
4. **Carpet Area vs Plinth Area Distinction:** RERA mandates that "carpet area" be the primary metric, while "plinth area" and "super area" can be supplementary.

> RERA has effectively transformed **SBA from a marketing tool into a regulatory disclosure**.

**(b.2) Three-Tier Area Distinction:**

| Area Type | Definition | Includes | Excludes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carpet Area (CA)** | Area of the floor that can be covered by a carpet; net usable area within inner walls | All internal rooms, kitchen, bathroom, internal walls | Outer walls, balconies, common areas |
| **Built-up Area (BUA)** | CA + thickness of external walls + balconies/verandas | Carpet area, walls, balconies | Common areas, amenities |
| **Super Built-up Area (SBA)** | BUA + proportionate share of common areas | All of BUA, plus lift, lobby, stairs, amenities | — (Billed area) |

> The hierarchy is: **CA ⊂ BUA ⊂ SBA**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing FSI with BUA basis:** Most students compute effective FSI using SBA, which is **wrong** for compliance. Municipal authorities use **Plinth / Built-up Area** (excluding common area). A proposal may be **within FSI on BUA basis** but appear to exceed on SBA basis.
> 2. **Forgetting the sum-of-areas check:** When computing UDS, students often divide by the *carpet area* of the building instead of the **total SBA**. The correct denominator depends on how the builder has registered the apartment — but UDS is most commonly computed using the **SBA-based ratio**.
> 3. **Ignoring RERA:** A 3-mark question on "what is carpet area" is incomplete without mentioning **RERA 2017**. Examiners award full marks only for RERA-aware answers.
> 4. **Not segregating land and construction value:** In valuation problems, students often apply a uniform depreciation to the entire flat value. The correct approach is to **depreciate only the construction component**, leaving the land component untouched.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Checklist (Last-Minute Revision):**

- ✅ **FSI = Total BUA / Plot Area** — municipal control, dimensionless.
- ✅ **FAR = FSI** — same concept, different regional terminology.
- ✅ **Permissible BUA = FSI × Plot Area** — used for feasibility checks.
- ✅ **Carpet Area < Built-up Area < Super Built-up Area** — strict inclusion hierarchy.
- ✅ **Loading Factor = SBA / CA** — typically 1.25 to 1.50; higher means costlier to buyer.
- ✅ **UDS = (SBA of flat / Total SBA of building) × Total Plot Area** — gives the land ownership right.
- ✅ **UDS does not depreciate**; **construction component depreciates** — this is the basis of cost-segregation.
- ✅ **RERA 2017 mandates Carpet-Area-based pricing** — SBA is now a secondary disclosure.
- ✅ **Kerala Apartment Ownership Act, 1983** governs UDS and common-area rights in Kerala.
- ✅ For uniform flats: **UDS = Plot Area / Number of Flats** (shortcut).
- ✅ Total built-up area = Sum across all floors; the **ground coverage** (footprint) is separate from FSI.
- ✅ **Premium FSI** allows developers to exceed standard limit by paying additional municipal charges.
- ✅ **TDR (Transferable Development Rights)** is a tradable FSI certificate used in redevelopment.
- ✅ Common areas include: lift, lobby, stairs, parking (covered), pump room, clubhouse, generator room.
- ✅ In valuation, **land appreciates**, **construction depreciates** — segregate first, then apply.
- ✅ Apartment ownership triad: **Exclusive Flat + UDS in Land + Common-Area Rights** — all three are required.
- ✅ UDS is **permanent and inheritable** — survives demolition, sale, and redevelopment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valuation for Bank: Purposes - Security, Primary and Collateral
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Valuation for Bank: Purposes - Security, Primary and Collateral

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation for Bank Purposes** is the specialized process of determining the **present market value**, **realizable value**, and **forced sale value** of a real property (land, building, or both) by a competent and approved valuer, for the purpose of providing an independent, unbiased, and technically sound opinion of the property's worth to a lending institution (bank or financial institution) against which a loan, mortgage, or financial accommodation is proposed to be sanctioned.

In the context of a bank's lending decision, the property offered by the borrower is broadly classified into two distinct categories of **Security**:

**A) Security (Umbrella Term):**
A comprehensive legal and financial term denoting the **entire bundle of assets** (both movable and immovable) that a borrower pledges, mortgages, hypothecates, or assigns to a bank as a safeguard against the recovery of the loan amount in the event of default.

**B) Primary Security:**
The asset that is **directly created, purchased, or financed** by the bank loan. It is the **principal and obvious asset** offered to the bank that is intrinsically connected with the end-use of the borrowed funds. The bank's first recourse in case of default is the primary security.

**C) Collateral Security:**
The **additional or supplementary asset(s)** offered by the borrower over and above the primary security, to **strengthen the bank's confidence** and to bridge any deficit in the value of the primary security relative to the loan amount. It acts as a **secondary line of defence** for the lender.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "House-Sitting and the Trusty Friend"**

Imagine you (the **borrower**) want to borrow ₹50 Lakhs from a friend (the **bank**) to buy a house. Your friend will not just hand over the money based on a verbal promise. He will ask: *"If you don't return the money, what do I get back?"*

1. **The house you are buying with that money** is the **PRIMARY SECURITY**. It is the *main thing* being bought with the loan, and it is the *first* thing the friend will claim if you default. *"If you don't pay, I take back the house I helped you buy."*

2. Now, suppose the house you wish to buy is old, located in a remote area, and your friend feels that the house alone is **not enough** to recover the full ₹50 Lakhs if you default. He then says: *"I am uncomfortable. Give me something EXTRA — perhaps your ancestral land, your FD receipts, or your gold jewellery."* This **extra item** is the **COLLATERAL SECURITY**. It is the *backup plan* — a safety net to make the friend feel doubly protected.

3. The **entire process** of your friend asking *"How much is this house actually worth in today's market?"* and getting a certified valuer to inspect and report the value is the **VALUATION FOR BANK PURPOSES**. The valuer tells the friend: *"This house is worth ₹60 Lakhs in the open market."* Now your friend feels safe to lend up to ₹48 Lakhs (keeping a **20% margin** of ₹12 Lakhs as his own safety cushion).

So:
- **Valuation** = The professional opinion of the property's worth.
- **Security** = The umbrella term for *everything* offered.
- **Primary** = The main asset (the house being bought).
- **Collateral** = The backup asset (the extra land/FD/gold).

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Banking Metrics to Memorise (Bold-emphasised):**

- **Market Value (MV):** The estimated amount for which a property would exchange on the date of valuation between a willing buyer and a willing seller in an arm's length transaction, after proper marketing. **Unit: ₹ (Rupees) or ₹ per sq.ft.**
- **Realizable Value:** The price a property would fetch if sold with **reasonable efforts within a reasonable time** (typically 85–90% of Market Value).
- **Forced Sale Value (FSV):** The price a property would fetch if sold **urgently under distressed conditions** (typically 70–80% of Market Value).
- **Margin (Haircut):** The percentage of the property value that the bank **does NOT finance**, kept as a cushion. **Standard: 20% to 30%** depending on property type and risk.
- **Loan-to-Value (LTV) Ratio:** The ratio of the loan amount sanctioned to the market value of the security. **Regulated by RBI: 75%–90%** depending on loan amount.
- **Security Coverage Ratio:** The ratio of the market value of all securities to the loan amount outstanding. Banks prefer **Security Coverage Ratio ≥ 1.25** (i.e., security value should be at least 125% of loan).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Logical Framework: Why Banks Need Valuation

Banks are **risk-averse financial intermediaries**. They accept deposits from the public (which carry an implicit guarantee of safety) and lend to borrowers (which carry an inherent credit risk). To balance this, banks follow the cardinal principle:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *"Never lend more than what the security is worth, and always keep a margin."*

A bank's lending decision is governed by three pillars: **Creditworthiness of the Borrower (C), Capacity to Repay (C), and Collateral (C)** — the famous **"3 Cs of Credit"**. Valuation of real property directly supports the **Collateral (C)** pillar.

### The Step-by-Step Logic of Bank Valuation:

**Step 1 — Request for Loan:**
The borrower approaches the bank with a proposal (e.g., purchase a flat for ₹70 Lakhs, requests loan of ₹55 Lakhs).

**Step 2 — Identification of Security:**
The bank identifies what is being offered as security. Is it the flat being purchased? Is there additional land? Are there FDs? The bank classifies these into **Primary** and **Collateral**.

**Step 3 — Independent Valuation:**
The bank appoints an **approved/empaneled valuer** (an independent technical expert) to physically inspect the property, verify documents, and determine the **present market value** of the security.

**Step 4 — Application of Margin & LTV:**
The bank applies its internal **margin policy** (e.g., 25% for residential property). The **maximum loan amount** is then:
**Loan Amount = Market Value × (1 − Margin %)**
So: ₹70 Lakhs × 0.75 = **₹52.5 Lakhs** (which is LESS than the requested ₹55 Lakhs, so the bank caps the loan at ₹52.5 Lakhs).

**Step 5 — Sanction and Mortgage:**
The bank sanctions the loan amount, the property is **mortgaged** (legally transferred as security), and the loan is disbursed. The valuation report becomes a **permanent record** in the bank's loan file.

**Step 6 — Periodic Re-valuation (if required):**
For large loans, NPAs (Non-Performing Assets), or significant market shifts, the bank may order a **re-valuation** to ensure the security still adequately covers the outstanding loan.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Primary Security vs. Collateral Security

The distinction between **Primary** and **Collateral** security is a high-frequency KTU examination topic. The following analytical matrix compares them across multiple dimensions:

| **Parameter** | **Primary Security** | **Collateral Security** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The main asset directly linked to the loan's end-use. | Additional asset(s) pledged to supplement the primary security. |
| **Purpose of Loan** | The loan is used to **acquire, construct, or improve** this asset. | The loan is **NOT used** for acquiring this asset. |
| **Legal Recourse** | The bank's **FIRST recourse** in case of default (SARFAESI Act, 2002). | The bank's **SECOND recourse** — invoked only if primary is insufficient. |
| **Examples** | House for a home loan; commercial building for a business loan; machinery for an equipment loan. | Ancestral land, additional flat, Fixed Deposits, LIC policies, gold, shares, third-party guarantee. |
| **Documentation** | Equitable Mortgage / Registered Mortgage / Hypothecation. | Equitable Mortgage / Pledge / Assignment / Lien. |
| **Valuation Focus** | Highest weightage — full technical valuation. | May need separate valuation or accepted at face value (e.g., FDs). |
| **Risk Weight (Banking)** | Lower risk weight (e.g., 50–75%). | Higher risk weight (e.g., 100–150%). |
| **Insurability** | Must be insured (comprehensively). | May or may not be insured. |
| **KTU Exam Phrasing** | "Main security" or "Principal security" | "Additional security" or "Subsidiary security" |

---

## 2.3 The Three "Faces" of Value in Bank Valuation

A KTU-savvy student must remember that a bank valuer is **not** asked to give only the **Market Value**. Banks specifically ask for **three values** to make prudent lending decisions:

1. **Market Value (MV)** — for loan eligibility calculation.
2. **Realizable Value (RV)** — for assessing the *practical* recovery amount in a reasonable timeframe. **RV ≈ 85% to 90% of MV**.
3. **Forced Sale Value (FSV)** — for the **worst-case scenario** (auction under SARFAESI). **FSV ≈ 70% to 80% of MV**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hierarchy of Values (from optimistic to pessimistic):**
> **Market Value ≥ Realizable Value ≥ Forced Sale Value**
> (MV > RV > FSV)

The bank will normally base its loan eligibility on **Market Value**, but will use the **FSV** to stress-test the security — i.e., *"Even if we are forced to auction the property under distressed conditions, will the FSV still cover the loan?"*

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the most frequently tested in KTU examinations for Module 3. Memorise the relationships, not just the symbols.**

| **#** | **Concept / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Explanation** | **Typical Unit / Value** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loan Amount | $\text{Loan} = \text{MV} \times (1 - \text{Margin \%})$ | Bank finances only a portion of the Market Value. | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | Loan-to-Value Ratio (LTV) | $\text{LTV} = \dfrac{\text{Loan Amount}}{\text{Market Value}} \times 100$ | Percentage of property value funded by the bank. | % (RBI: 75% – 90%) |
| 3 | Margin (Bank's Cushion) | $\text{Margin} = \text{MV} - \text{Loan Amount}$ | The bank's own risk buffer in absolute terms. | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 4 | Margin Percentage | $\text{Margin \%} = 100 - \text{LTV \%}$ | The unfinanced portion of the property value. | % (Typically 10% – 25%) |
| 5 | Security Coverage Ratio (SCR) | $\text{SCR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Market Value of Security}}{\text{Loan Amount}}$ | How many times the security covers the loan. | Ratio (≥ 1.25 preferred) |
| 6 | Realizable Value | $\text{RV} = \text{MV} \times \text{Realisation Factor}$ | Conservative market price after reasonable time. | ₹ (≈ 0.85 × MV to 0.90 × MV) |
| 7 | Forced Sale Value | $\text{FSV} = \text{MV} \times \text{FSV Factor}$ | Distress auction price. | ₹ (≈ 0.70 × MV to 0.80 × MV) |
| 8 | Distress Discount | $\text{Distress Discount} = \text{MV} - \text{FSV}$ | The cost of forced liquidation. | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 9 | Distress Discount % | $\text{Discount \%} = \dfrac{\text{MV} - \text{FSV}}{\text{MV}} \times 100$ | Stress-test cushion. | % (Typically 20% – 30%) |
| 10 | Combined Value (Multiple Securities) | $\text{Total Security} = \text{MV}_{\text{primary}} + \text{MV}_{\text{collateral}}$ | Aggregate value of all pledged assets. | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 11 | Effective LTV (with collateral) | $\text{Effective LTV} = \dfrac{\text{Loan}}{\text{MV}_{\text{primary}} + \text{MV}_{\text{collateral}}} \times 100$ | LTV after accounting for collateral support. | % |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Banking Fields

- **Banking & Finance:** Foundation of mortgage underwriting, SARFAESI recovery, NPA resolution, and Basel-III capital adequacy norms.
- **Civil Engineering:** The valuer must understand structural soundness, age, depreciation, and remaining useful life of buildings — all civil engineering concepts directly applied.
- **Legal Profession:** Valuation reports are submitted as evidence in DRT (Debt Recovery Tribunal), SARFAESI courts, and consumer forums.
- **Insurance:** Banks insist on **comprehensive insurance** of the primary security (the property). The valuation determines the **sum insured**.
- **Public Sector:** Used by NHB (National Housing Bank), HUDCO, LIC Housing Finance, and state housing boards for bulk property finance.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The derivations below are deliberately written in exhaustive, granular steps because KTU board examiners reward **methodology** as much as final answers. Showing the logical progression of each formula from first principles ensures full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Fundamental Loan-Value Equation

**Statement of the Problem (Axiom):**
A bank never finances the *entire* market value of a property. It deliberately keeps a portion (called **margin**) as its own risk capital. The bank's rule is:

> *"Maximum Loan = Market Value of Security minus a pre-defined Margin."*

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation:

**Step 1:** Let the variables be defined as:
- $MV$ = Market Value of the property (₹)
- $L$ = Loan amount sanctioned by the bank (₹)
- $M$ = Margin kept by the bank in absolute terms (₹)
- $m$ = Margin expressed as a percentage (decimal form, e.g., 0.20 for 20%)

**Step 2:** The bank finances *whatever is left* after removing the margin. So, mathematically:
$$L = MV - M$$

**Step 3:** Now, the absolute margin $M$ is itself a *fraction* of the Market Value. By definition:
$$M = m \times MV$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Equation 3 into Equation 2:
$$L = MV - (m \times MV)$$

**Step 5:** Factor out the common term $MV$:
$$L = MV \times (1 - m)$$

**Step 6:** This is the **fundamental loan-amount equation**. The bank multiplies the market value by a **financing factor** $(1 - m)$ which is always less than 1.

**Step 7:** For example, if the bank policy is 25% margin (i.e., $m = 0.25$), the financing factor becomes $(1 - 0.25) = 0.75$. So the bank advances **75% of the market value** — the Loan-to-Value ratio is 75%.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Loan-to-Value (LTV) Ratio from First Principles

**Step 1:** By definition, LTV is the ratio of loan to property value, expressed as a percentage:
$$\text{LTV \%} = \frac{L}{MV} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the expression for $L$ from Equation 5 of §3.1:
$$\text{LTV \%} = \frac{MV \times (1 - m)}{MV} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** The $MV$ terms cancel out (algebraic simplification):
$$\text{LTV \%} = (1 - m) \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Converting the decimal back to a percentage form, the **invariant relationship** is:
$$\text{LTV \%} + \text{Margin \%} = 100\%$$

**Step 5:** Worked example: If LTV is 80%, then Margin = 100% − 80% = 20%. If Margin is 30%, then LTV = 100% − 30% = 70%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Security Coverage Ratio (SCR)

**Step 1:** When there are *multiple* securities (primary + collateral), the total security is the sum of individual market values:
$$\text{Total Security} = MV_{\text{primary}} + MV_{\text{collateral}}$$

**Step 2:** The Security Coverage Ratio is defined as how many times the security "covers" the loan:
$$\text{SCR} = \frac{\text{Total Market Value of Security}}{\text{Loan Amount Outstanding}}$$

**Step 3:** For a bank to feel "safe," SCR should be at least 1.0 (security equals loan). For prudent lending, **SCR ≥ 1.25** (security is 125% of loan).

**Step 4:** Worked numerical example:
- Primary property MV = ₹60,00,000
- Collateral property MV = ₹20,00,000
- Loan sanctioned = ₹50,00,000
- Then: Total Security = ₹60,00,000 + ₹20,00,000 = ₹80,00,000
- SCR = ₹80,00,000 / ₹50,00,000 = **1.60** (Excellent coverage; the bank is well-protected).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem: A KTU-Style Bank Valuation Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the exact type of 14-mark problem that appears in KTU End Semester Examinations. Solve it on your own, then verify against the model solution below.**

**Problem Statement:**
Mr. Ravi approaches a bank for a home loan of ₹80 Lakhs to purchase a residential flat. The bank's empaneled valuer submits the following report:
- Market Value of the flat (Primary Security) = **₹1,00,00,000**
- Market Value of an ancestral plot offered additionally (Collateral Security) = **₹30,00,000**
- Realisable Value of the flat = **₹90,00,000**
- Forced Sale Value of the flat = **₹75,00,000**

The bank's policy is:
- **Margin = 25%** on the Market Value of the flat.
- **LTV cap = 80%** of the total security value.

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the maximum loan amount the bank can sanction under both policies. Which policy is more restrictive? [7 Marks]
(b) Calculate the Security Coverage Ratio and comment on whether the security is adequate. If the flat had to be auctioned under SARFAESI, how much shortfall would the bank face assuming the entire loan is disbursed? [7 Marks]

---

### MODEL SOLUTION (with full step-by-step working):

### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 — Calculate Loan Eligibility under Margin Policy (25% on Flat MV):**

Using the fundamental loan equation from §3.1:
$$L_1 = MV_{\text{flat}} \times (1 - m)$$
$$L_1 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.25)$$
$$L_1 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75$$
$$\boxed{L_1 = ₹75{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Loan Eligibility under LTV Cap Policy (80% of Total Security):**

The LTV cap considers **both** primary and collateral securities:
$$L_2 = (MV_{\text{flat}} + MV_{\text{ancestral plot}}) \times \text{LTV \%}$$
$$L_2 = (1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000) \times 0.80$$
$$L_2 = 1{,}30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80$$
$$\boxed{L_2 = ₹1{,}04{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Restrictive Policy (the bank's golden rule):**

The bank will sanction the **LOWER** of the two amounts to protect itself:
$$L_{\text{sanctioned}} = \min(L_1, L_2) = \min(75{,}00{,}000, 1{,}04{,}00{,}000)$$
$$\boxed{L_{\text{sanctioned}} = ₹75{,}00{,}000}$$

**Conclusion of Part (a):** The **Margin Policy (25%)** is more restrictive. The bank will sanction **₹75 Lakhs**, which is **₹5 Lakhs less** than the borrower's request of ₹80 Lakhs. The borrower must arrange the balance ₹5 Lakhs from his own sources.

---

### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Calculate Security Coverage Ratio (SCR):**

Using the formula from §3.3:
$$\text{SCR} = \frac{MV_{\text{flat}} + MV_{\text{ancestral plot}}}{L_{\text{sanctioned}}}$$
$$\text{SCR} = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000}{75{,}00{,}000}$$
$$\text{SCR} = \frac{1{,}30{,}00{,}000}{75{,}00{,}000}$$
$$\boxed{\text{SCR} = 1.733}$$

**Step 2 — Comment on Adequacy:**

Since the bank's prudent benchmark is **SCR ≥ 1.25**, the achieved SCR of **1.733** means the security is **highly adequate**. The bank is over-protected by approximately 73.3% above the loan amount.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Shortfall under Forced Sale (SARFAESI Auction):**

Under the **Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest (SARFAESI) Act, 2002**, the bank can auction the property under distressed conditions. In the worst case, both the primary and collateral may be sold at their forced sale values.

**Forced Sale Value of flat** = ₹75,00,000 (given in the report)
**Forced Sale Value of ancestral plot** = (Assuming typical 25% distress discount on collateral, since not given) = ₹30,00,000 × 0.75 = ₹22,50,000

**Total Recovery under FSV:**
$$\text{Total Recovery} = \text{FSV}_{\text{flat}} + \text{FSV}_{\text{ancestral plot}}$$
$$\text{Total Recovery} = 75{,}00{,}000 + 22{,}50{,}000 = ₹97{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Shortfall:**

$$\text{Shortfall} = L_{\text{sanctioned}} - \text{Total Recovery}$$
$$\text{Shortfall} = 75{,}00{,}000 - 97{,}50{,}000$$
$$\boxed{\text{Shortfall} = -₹22{,}50{,}000 \text{ (i.e., a SURPLUS, not a shortfall)}}$$

**Final Conclusion of Part (b):** Even under the **worst-case SARFAESI auction** scenario, the bank recovers **₹97.5 Lakhs** against a loan of **₹75 Lakhs** — a surplus of **₹22.5 Lakhs**. The security is **exceedingly adequate** and the borrower is in a very safe position. The bank should have no recovery concerns.
