## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECET862]**
> *Define "Environmental Factors" in the context of real property valuation. List any four major environmental factors that affect the value of a property.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Environmental factors, in real property valuation, are the **physical, locational, climatic, ecological, and socio-physical attributes** of a property's surroundings — both natural and man-made — that influence its utility, desirability, and market value. These factors may be *positive amenities* (e.g., a scenic view) or *negative disamenities* (e.g., industrial pollution), and must be carefully assessed by the valuer.

**Four Major Environmental Factors (1 Mark):**
1. **Air Quality** — presence of smoke, dust, industrial emissions.
2. **Noise Levels** — proximity to highways, airports, railways.
3. **Natural Hazards** — flood, landslide, earthquake, coastal erosion.
4. **Aesthetic Features** — view, green cover, water body proximity.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECET862]**
> *Explain the concept of "Hedonic Pricing Method" in environmental valuation. State its relevance in the Indian real estate context.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
The Hedonic Pricing Method (HPM) is a regression-based valuation technique that decomposes a property's total price into contributions from individual attribute bundles — Location ($L$), Structure ($S$), Environment ($E$), and Neighbourhood ($N$). The log-linear form,

$$\ln(P) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 L + \beta_2 S + \beta_3 E + \beta_4 N + \varepsilon$$

allows the valuer to **isolate the marginal price** of each environmental attribute (e.g., ₹X per dB of noise reduction, ₹Y per metre of sea view).

**Relevance in India (1 Mark):**
In the Indian context — particularly Kerala — HPM is used in:
- Stamp-duty market-value determination,
- Land acquisition compensation disputes (LA Act 2013),
- Smart City project environmental impact valuation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to mention the direction** of the adjustment (positive vs negative) — *lose 2 marks*.
> - Students often **omit the principle** (e.g., Principle of Anticipation, Regression) that justifies why the environmental factor matters — *lose 1 mark*.
> - Students apply **only the additive model** without checking whether factors are correlated (e.g., flood + poor drainage) — *lose 2 marks* on the methodology.
> - In Kerala-specific questions, students miss the **CRZ / Coastal Regulation Zone** angle — *lose 2 marks*.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECET862 Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the various **environmental factors** that affect the valuation of real properties, with specific reference to the Kerala context. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A residential building in Ernakulam has a base value of ₹80,00,000. Apply the percentage adjustment method for the following environmental factors and compute the adjusted market value:
>
> | Factor | Adjustment |
> |--------|-----------|
> | Sea view (yes) | +7% |
> | Heavy traffic noise | -4% |
> | Industrial area 1.5 km away | -6% |
> | Public park 300 m away | +3% |
> | Located in 1-in-100-year flood zone | -10% |
>
> Assume factors are independent. Use **both** additive and multiplicative models and comment on the difference. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — (7 Marks):**

Environmental factors affecting real property valuation in the Kerala context can be classified into six categories:

**1. Physical / Topographical Factors (1.5 Marks)**
- **Topography**: Sloped vs flat terrain. Kerala's undulating Western Ghats, midland laterite, and coastal alluvium create *very different* drainage, foundation cost, and view attributes.
- **Soil type**: Laterite (midland), alluvial (lowland), sandy (coastal) — each has different bearing capacity, affecting foundation cost and thus value.
- **Drainage pattern**: Properties with *natural slope-aided drainage* are valued higher than those in water-logged lowlands (e.g., Kuttanad below MSL).

**2. Climatic / Atmospheric Factors (1.5 Marks)**
- **Air quality**: AQI from urban traffic (Kochi, Kozhikode) and industry (Eloor, Ambalamedu). CPCB data is the standard reference.
- **Humidity & rainfall**: Kerala receives ~3000 mm annual rainfall. Coastal belt has high salinity; high humidity damages finishes and reduces structural life → value discount.
- **Wind direction**: Prevailing SW monsoon can carry industrial smoke into residential zones → discount.

**3. Water & Aquatic Factors (1 Mark)**
- **Proximity to backwaters, rivers, sea**: A *view* is a positive amenity; *saline intrusion* and *flooding* are negative. The 2018 Kerala floods showed 1-in-100-year flood impact.
- **Water-logging & drainage**: A chronic Kuttanad / Kochi problem.

**4. Natural Hazard Factors (1.5 Marks)**
- **Flood plains** — identified by KSNDMC and CWC.
- **Landslide-prone zones** — Western Ghats districts (Wayanad, Idukki, Malappuram).
- **Coastal erosion zones** — CRZ-I and CRZ-III under CRZ Notification 2019.
- **Seismic zones** — Kerala largely falls in Zone III (IS 1893).

**5. Acoustic & Aesthetic Factors (1 Mark)**
- **Noise pollution** — NH66, NH544, airport flight paths, railway corridors.
- **Aesthetic value** — view corridors, heritage precincts (Fort Kochi, Mattancherry), green cover.

**6. Socio-Environmental Factors (0.5 Marks)**
- Neighbourhood character, slum density, civic infrastructure, cultural disamenities (crematoriums, slaughterhouses).

**Synthesis (qualifying sentence):**
Together, these factors can adjust a base value by **±40%** in extreme cases (e.g., a property in a high-landslide, high-flood zone may have a 40–50% discount, while a sea-view plot in a clean-air zone may have a 15–25% premium).

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — (7 Marks):**

**Given:**

$$V_{base} = ₹\ 80{,}00{,}000$$

Adjustments: $p_1 = +0.07$, $p_2 = -0.04$, $p_3 = -0.06$, $p_4 = +0.03$, $p_5 = -0.10$

**Step 1: Additive Model (Equation F1)**

$$\sum p_i = 0.07 - 0.04 - 0.06 + 0.03 - 0.10 = -0.10$$

$$V_{adj}^{add} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + (-0.10))$$

$$V_{adj}^{add} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{V_{adj}^{add} = ₹\ 72{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 2: Multiplicative Model (More Accurate)**

$$V_{adj}^{mult} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times (1.07)(0.96)(0.94)(1.03)(0.90)$$

**Compute step by step:**

$$1.07 \times 0.96 = 1.0272$$

$$1.0272 \times 0.94 = 0.965568$$

$$0.965568 \times 1.03 = 0.994535$$

$$0.994535 \times 0.90 = 0.895082$$

$$V_{adj}^{mult} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.895082$$

$$\boxed{V_{adj}^{mult} \approx ₹\ 71{,}60{,}656}$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Comment**

The two models yield a difference of:

$$\Delta = 72{,}00{,}000 - 71{,}60{,}656 = ₹\ 39{,}344$$

$$\text{Percentage difference} = \dfrac{39{,}344}{72{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 0.55\%$$

**Comment (1 Mark):**
The multiplicative model is **slightly more conservative** because it accounts for the compounding effect of correlated discounts. The difference of ~0.55% is small here because the adjustments are individually < 10%, but it grows substantially when large adjustments (>20%) are involved. **Best practice in KTU valuation reports is to use the multiplicative model and disclose the difference.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECET862 Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **EAT-RAD classification of environmental factors** used in property valuation. Describe each domain with two real-world examples from Kerala. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A 10-cent plot in Thiruvananthapuram city is located 200 m from the international airport runway. The base land value is ₹1,20,00,000. The valuer identifies the following environmental factors:
>
> - Aircraft noise: −12%
> - Vibration from low-flying aircraft: −3%
> - View constraint (no high-rise allowed): −4%
> - Excellent road connectivity (NH66): +6%
>
> Using the **multiplicative adjustment model**, compute the adjusted value. State two justifications for the choice of multiplicative model here. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — (7 Marks):**

**EAT-RAD Framework (3 Marks) — Explanation & Schematic:**

The EAT-RAD framework systematically classifies environmental factors into six domains for thorough valuation audit. (See Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for visual reference.)

| Domain | Full Name | Real-World Example 1 (Kerala) | Real-World Example 2 (Kerala) |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **E** | Ecological | Periyar Tiger Reserve green belt premium (Punarjani value) | Vembanad Lake backwater view (Kumarakom) |
| **A** | Atmospheric | Eloor industrial corridor AQI 200+ → value crash | Kochi city's PM2.5 hot-spots (Kaloor) |
| **T** | Topographical | Wayanad laterite plateau (good drainage) | Kuttanad below-sea-level (poor drainage) |
| **R** | Risk | 2018 Kerala flood zone (Pathanamthitta, Aluva) | 2019 Pettimudy landslide zone (Munnar) |
| **A** | Acoustic | Calicut airport flight path (Kondotty houses) | Shoranur–Mangalore railway line |
| **D** | Demographic | Fort Kochi heritage neighbourhood (positive) | Slum pockets of Ernakulam (negative) |

**Description (4 Marks — 0.5 each pair, ~50 words each):**

1. **Ecological** — refers to biodiversity, vegetation, water-body proximity. Kerala's backwater-front plots (Punnamada, Kumarakom) command a +10–25% premium due to ecological aesthetics.

2. **Atmospheric** — refers to air-quality metrics (AQI, PM2.5, PM10, SO₂). Properties downwind of Eloor industrial zone (Eloor, North Paravur) suffer a 15–25% discount per CPCB AQI data.

3. **Topographical** — refers to slope, elevation, soil-bearing, drainage. Laterite midland plots (Vadakara, Thodupuzha) command higher values than alluvial lowlands (Kuttanad) due to superior drainage.

4. **Risk** — refers to natural and man-made hazards. Properties in KSNDMC's 1-in-100-year flood-line (Aluva, Chalakudy) saw a 20–40% post-2018 discount per Kerala State Disaster Management Authority data.

5. **Acoustic** — refers to noise and vibration. Houses under the Calicut airport flight path (Kondotty, Ramanattukara) show a 10–18% discount; railway-corridor properties (Shoranur) show a 10–15% discount.

6. **Demographic** — refers to neighbourhood character, population density, and social amenities. Heritage zones (Fort Kochi, Jew Town) carry a +15–20% premium; high-density slum-adjacent properties see a -10–25% discount.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $V_{base} = ₹\ 1{,}20{,}00{,}000$; Adjustments: $-0.12, -0.03, -0.04, +0.06$.

**Step 1: Identify the model.**

Because the four factors (noise, vibration, view constraint, connectivity) are **physically correlated** (e.g., aircraft noise and vibration are not independent — they come from the same source), the **multiplicative model is preferred**.

**Step 2: Compute step by step.**

$$V_{adj} = V_{base} \times \prod_{i=1}^{4} (1 + p_i)$$

$$V_{adj} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times (0.88)(0.97)(0.96)(1.06)$$

**Compute progressively:**

$$0.88 \times 0.97 = 0.8536$$

$$0.8536 \times 0.96 = 0.819456$$

$$0.819456 \times 1.06 = 0.868623$$

**Step 3: Final Answer.**

$$V_{adj} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.868623$$

$$\boxed{V_{adj} \approx ₹\ 1{,}04{,}23{,}480}$$

**Step 4: Justification for the Multiplicative Model (2 Marks):**

**Justification 1 — Correlated Factors:** Aircraft noise and low-altitude vibration originate from the **same source** (overflying aircraft) and occur **simultaneously**. They are not independent events, so adding their percentage discounts would understate the cumulative effect. The multiplicative model correctly accounts for this coupling.

**Justification 2 — Compounding Effect on Large Discounts:** The single largest adjustment (-12%) is borderline large. When any single factor exceeds 10%, the cross-product terms $p_i p_j$ in the multiplicative expansion are no longer negligible (e.g., $-0.12 \times -0.03 = +0.0036$, a 0.36% recovery). The multiplicative model preserves these second-order effects faithfully.

**Bonus remark:** A 13% gross discount in a high-demand airport-connected location is moderate. Comparable airport-adjacent land in metro India typically sees 18–30% discounts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU PECET862 Module 4**

- **Definition:** Environmental factors are *physical, locational, climatic, ecological, socio-physical* attributes of a property's surroundings influencing market value.
- **EAT-RAD Classification:** Ecological, Atmospheric, Topographical, Risk, Acoustic, Demographic — *memorize the six domains and one Kerala example each*.
- **Five Environmental Domains (Broader):** Physical, Atmospheric, Acoustic, Aesthetic, Hazardous.
- **Additive Model** (F1) — used when factors are **independent** and adjustments are **small** ($<10\%$).
- **Multiplicative Model** — used when factors are **correlated** or any single adjustment is **large** ($>10\%$).
- **Hedonic Pricing Model (F4):** Regression-based; $\beta_3$ in log-log form is an **elasticity**, not a marginal effect.
- **Flood Discount (F10):** Use $1 / (1 + F \times PPL)$ where $F$ is flood probability and $PPL$ is peak percent loss.
- **Kerala-Specific Hazards:** Floods (2018), Landslides (Pettimudy 2019, Wayanad 2024), Coastal Erosion (CRZ), Industrial pollution (Eloor, Ambalamedu), Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ Notification 2019).
- **Statutory Reference:** Indian Stamp Act 1899, Section 4 — *market value includes all amenities and disadvantages*.
- **Land Acquisition Act 2013:** Solatium of 100% + 12% additional — environmental factors can justify higher compensation.
- **Common Examiner Traps:** Forgetting direction of adjustment, missing CRZ angle in coastal questions, using additive when multiplicative is required, ignoring Principle of Anticipation.
- **Mnemonic to remember adjustment range:** **"A-P-E-N"** — Air (-5 to -25%), Proximity (-3 to -20%), Environment (+5 to +20%), Noise (-3 to -15%).
- **Highest single-environment premium in Kerala:** Sea view property with clean-air environment → up to **+25%** over comparable without view.
- **Highest single-environment discount in Kerala:** Landslide + flood + industrial zone → up to **-50%** (rare, but documented post-2018/2019).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word of Advice (KTU Board Examiner Perspective):**
> In the exam, *always* (a) state the **principle** you are applying, (b) **justify the direction** of adjustment, (c) **state the model** (additive or multiplicative) and *why*, and (d) show **step-by-step calculation**. Examiners award **process marks** generously — a partially-correct numerical answer with a clearly-stated method scores higher than a correct number with no method.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Professional Ethics: Model Code of Conduct as Notified by MCA under the Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules 2017
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Professional Ethics: Model Code of Conduct as Notified by MCA under the Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules 2017

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Model Code of Conduct (MCoC)** for Registered Valuers is a statutory code of professional and ethical behaviour prescribed under **Rule 18** read with **Schedule III** of the **Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017**, as notified by the **Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA), Government of India**, in exercise of powers conferred by **Section 247 of the Companies Act, 2013**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statutory Anchor:** The MCoC is **not a voluntary guideline** — it is a **legally enforceable code** applicable to every individual or firm registered as a valuer with the MCA/IBBI. Breach of any clause constitutes "professional misconduct" and is punishable with suspension, cancellation of registration, monetary penalty, or imprisonment under the parent Act.

A **Registered Valuer (RV)** is defined under **Rule 2(1)(q)** of the 2017 Rules as a person registered with the designated authority (presently the **Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India — IBBI**) under Rule 4, who undertakes valuation of assets in accordance with these Rules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **judge in a courtroom**. A judge cannot:
- Accept gifts from the defendant,
- Discuss the case privately with the plaintiff,
- Deliver a verdict in favour of a friend,
- Argue his own case while wearing the robe.

Similarly, a **Registered Valuer** is a *quasi-judicial* professional whose opinion determines the **worth of an asset** — and on that number depend loans, mergers, takeovers, shareholder payouts, and insolvency distributions. To protect the public interest, the MCA has imposed a **Code of Conduct** that functions like a *Hippocratic Oath* for valuers: it tells them how to *behave*, not just how to *calculate*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Trigger Event:** The Companies Act, 2013 (Section 247) made it **mandatory** that any valuation of assets for purposes such as share buy-back, compromise/arrangement, and insolvency resolution **must** be carried out only by a Registered Valuer. This gave rise to the need for a uniform ethical code — the Model Code of Conduct.

### 1.3 Statutory Framework Snapshot

| Instrument | Year | Issued By | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Companies Act, 2013 | 2013 | Parliament of India | Parent legislation; **Section 247** empowers Central Government to frame valuation rules |
| Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017 | 2017 (notified 18 Oct 2017) | **MCA, Govt. of India** | Prescribes eligibility, registration, duties, ethics, discipline |
| IBBI (Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India) Act, 2016 | 2016 | Parliament | Designated authority for insolvency-related valuers |
| Valuation Standards | 2018 onwards | ICAI RVO / IBBI | Technical valuation methodology |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam Pointers:** Always remember the **three-layer hierarchy**:
> **Companies Act, 2013** (parent) → **Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017** (rules) → **Schedule III — Model Code of Conduct** (ethics).
> Examiners love questions that test whether you know **where** a particular rule lives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Pillars of the Model Code of Conduct

Schedule III codifies **seven guiding principles** that govern the conduct of every Registered Valuer. Each principle carries both a **positive duty** (what the valuer *must* do) and a **negative duty** (what the valuer *must not* do).

#### Pillar 1 — Integrity and Fairness
- The valuer shall act **honestly, in good faith, and with intellectual honesty**.
- The valuer shall **not** be associated with any fraudulent, illegal, or deceptive activity.
- The valuer shall **not** make any misleading, exaggerated, or unverified statement in a valuation report.
- **Why:** The valuation report is a *public document* relied upon by banks, tribunals, and shareholders.

#### Pillar 2 — Objectivity
- The valuer shall conduct every engagement with **objectivity** and free from bias.
- The valuer shall **disclose** every real or potential conflict of interest.
- The valuer shall **not** allow any third party (client, management, lender) to influence professional judgment.
- **How:** Maintain a **Conflict-of-Interest Register** and recuse from assignments where interest exists.

#### Pillar 3 — Professional Competence and Due Care
- The valuer shall maintain **professional knowledge and skill** at the level needed to serve clients well.
- The valuer shall comply with **applicable valuation standards** and applicable laws.
- The valuer shall **not** accept an engagement for which he/she lacks competence.
- **Why:** A poorly trained valuer is as dangerous as a dishonest one — both produce wrong numbers.

#### Pillar 4 — Confidentiality
- The valuer shall **not** disclose confidential information obtained during an engagement.
- The valuer shall **not** use confidential information for personal or third-party benefit.
- **Exception:** Disclosure is permitted when required by law, court, or regulatory authority.

#### Pillar 5 — Professional Behaviour
- The valuer shall **not** engage in any conduct that discredits the profession.
- The valuer shall **not** solicit or accept gifts, hospitality, or inducements that may compromise independence.
- The valuer shall conduct himself/herself with dignity and decorum.

#### Pillar 6 — Independence and Impartiality
- The valuer shall be **independent** of the entity being valued and the parties commissioning the valuation.
- The valuer shall **not** have any direct or indirect financial interest in the asset being valued.
- The valuer shall **not** have any close personal or business relationship that may impair independence.

#### Pillar 7 — Compliance with Laws
- The valuer shall comply with the **Companies Act, 2013**, the **Rules**, applicable valuation standards, and any other law governing the engagement.
- The valuer shall **co-operate** with the regulatory authority (MCA / IBBI) and disciplinary bodies.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important rapid-revision tool** for this Module 4 topic. Memorise the clause–duty mapping.

| Pillar No. | Code Principle (Schedule III) | Core Positive Duty | Core Negative Duty |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Integrity and Fairness** | Act honestly & in good faith | No association with fraud; no misleading statements |
| 2 | **Objectivity** | Disclose conflicts; report truthfully | No bias; no third-party influence |
| 3 | **Professional Competence and Due Care** | Maintain skill; follow standards | No acceptance of work beyond competence |
| 4 | **Confidentiality** | Protect client information | No disclosure; no personal use |
| 5 | **Professional Behaviour** | Maintain decorum | No gifts; no discreditable conduct |
| 6 | **Independence and Impartiality** | Be independent of client & asset | No financial/relational interest |
| 7 | **Compliance with Laws** | Obey Companies Act, Rules, Standards | No violation of any applicable law |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Valuation Practice

The Code of Conduct is **not academic** — it has **engineering and economic consequences** in the real world:

- **Bank Lending:** Banks like SBI, HDFC, and PNB accept valuation reports only from valuers compliant with the MCoC. A non-compliant report is rejected outright.
- **Insolvency Resolution:** Under the **IBC, 2016**, the resolution professional appoints registered valuers whose reports determine **resolution plan viability** — bias here can mean losses of hundreds of crores.
- **Mergers & Acquisitions:** Share-swap ratios in mergers (e.g., HDFC–HDFC Bank merger) are based on registered valuer reports.
- **Disciplinary Action:** Breach leads to **suspension (up to 5 years)**, **permanent cancellation**, and **monetary penalty under Section 247(3)** of the Companies Act.
- **Forensic Value:** Courts (NCLT, NCLAT, Supreme Court) routinely cite Schedule III in cross-examining valuation reports.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Compliance Matrix

### 3.1 How to Apply the Code — A Six-Step Compliance Workflow

Because this is a *legal-regulatory* topic rather than a numerical one, the "derivation" here is the **analytical procedure** by which a Registered Valuer demonstrates compliance with the Code. Every valuation engagement must pass through these six steps:

**Step 1 — Engagement Acceptance Screening**
Before accepting any valuation assignment, the valuer screens the engagement against the following checks:
- Is the valuer **competent** for the asset class? *(Pillar 3)*
- Does any **conflict of interest** exist? *(Pillar 2 & 6)*
- Is the valuer **independent** of the client and the asset? *(Pillar 6)*

If the answer to any check is *No*, the valuer must **decline** the engagement in writing.

**Step 2 — Disclosure of Conflicts**
Even where conflict is *potential*, the valuer must issue a **Conflict-of-Interest Disclosure** to the client and the regulatory authority, as required under **Rule 10** of the 2017 Rules. The disclosure must list:
- Direct financial interests,
- Indirect interests (e.g., family, partners),
- Past relationships (auditor, advisor, employee),
- Any consideration received or expected.

**Step 3 — Engagement Letter Execution**
A formal **Engagement Letter** must be signed, covering:
- Scope of work,
- Valuation date and purpose,
- Fee structure (must not be contingent on value),
- Timeline and deliverables,
- Confidentiality terms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contingent Fee Rule (Rule 11):** A valuer shall **not** accept a fee that is contingent upon the valuation result. This is a direct application of Pillar 2 (Objectivity) and Pillar 6 (Independence).

**Step 4 — Conduct of Valuation**
The valuation must follow:
- **Valuation Standards** issued by the authority (currently IBBI-recognised standards),
- **Due process** — site visit, document verification, market study,
- **Documentation** — every assumption must be supported by evidence.

**Step 5 — Reporting with Integrity**
The valuation report must be:
- **Truthful** — no inflated or deflated numbers (Pillar 1),
- **Complete** — disclose all material facts (Pillar 2),
- **Clear** — no vague or misleading language (Pillar 1),
- **Signed** by the valuer with registration number.

**Step 6 — Post-Engagement Obligations**
- Maintain **records for at least 5 years** (Rule 17),
- Cooperate with **disciplinary enquiries** (Rule 24),
- Submit **annual compliance reports** to the authority.

### 3.2 The Compliance Matrix — Clause-by-Clause

| Step | Statutory Anchor | Pillars Applied | What Examiner Expects |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rule 11, Schedule III Para 3 | Pillar 3, 6 | Cite competence test |
| 2 | Rule 10, Schedule III Para 2 | Pillar 2, 6 | List four categories of conflict |
| 3 | Rule 12, 13; Schedule III Para 4 | Pillar 4, 7 | Highlight contingent-fee prohibition |
| 4 | Rule 8, 9, 18; Schedule III Para 3, 7 | Pillar 3, 7 | Mention standards & due process |
| 5 | Rule 14, 15, 16; Schedule III Para 1, 2 | Pillar 1, 2 | Emphasise truthful reporting |
| 6 | Rule 17, 24; Schedule III Para 5, 7 | Pillar 4, 5, 7 | Mention 5-year record retention |

### 3.3 Disciplinary Mechanism — How a Breach is Prosecuted

The 2017 Rules provide a structured disciplinary process under **Rule 24**:

- **Level 1 — Inquiry by Disciplinary Committee** on receipt of complaint.
- **Level 2 — Show-cause notice** to the valuer with full disclosure of allegations.
- **Level 3 — Personal hearing** and opportunity to present defence.
- **Level 4 — Order** — may include warning, suspension (up to 5 years), or permanent cancellation of registration.
- **Level 5 — Monetary penalty** under **Section 247(3)** of the Companies Act, 2013.
- **Level 6 — Criminal prosecution** in cases of fraud, where the matter is referred to the police/SFIO.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students write only "the valuer may be punished" — this is too vague. You **must** cite the specific rule and section (e.g., *"punishable under Section 247(3) of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Rule 24 of the 2017 Rules"*). Examiners deduct marks for generic answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. What is the Model Code of Conduct for Registered Valuers, and under which statutory instrument is it notified?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — allocation shown):**
The Model Code of Conduct (MCoC) is a statutory code of professional and ethical behaviour prescribed under **Rule 18** read with **Schedule III** of the **Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017** *(1 mark)*, notified by the **Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA), Government of India** *(1 mark)*, in exercise of powers conferred by **Section 247 of the Companies Act, 2013** *(1 mark)*. The Code contains **seven principles**: Integrity, Objectivity, Competence, Confidentiality, Professional Behaviour, Independence, and Compliance with Laws. It is legally binding on every person registered as a valuer and breach constitutes professional misconduct.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. List any three duties of a Registered Valuer under the Model Code of Conduct.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per duty):**
1. **Integrity and Fairness (Pillar 1):** The valuer must act honestly and in good faith, and must not be associated with any fraudulent or misleading statement *(1 mark)*.
2. **Objectivity (Pillar 2):** The valuer must conduct every engagement free from bias and must disclose any conflict of interest, real or potential *(1 mark)*.
3. **Confidentiality (Pillar 4):** The valuer must not disclose confidential information obtained during the engagement, nor use it for personal or third-party benefit *(1 mark)*.

*(Acceptable alternative: any three of the seven pillars, with one-line explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper Pattern]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Model Code of Conduct for Registered Valuers** as notified under the Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017. Discuss all seven principles laid down in Schedule III. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **disciplinary mechanism** prescribed under the Rules for breach of the Code. What penalties can be imposed? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) The Model Code of Conduct — Seven Principles (7 Marks)

The Model Code of Conduct is contained in **Schedule III** of the Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017, read with **Rule 18**, and is notified under **Section 247 of the Companies Act, 2013** *(1 mark for citation)*.

The seven principles are:

1. **Integrity and Fairness (Para 1 of Schedule III):** A registered valuer must act honestly, in good faith, and with intellectual honesty. He must not be associated with any fraudulent or deceptive activity and must not make any misleading, exaggerated, or unverified statement in a valuation report. The valuation number must reflect the valuer's true professional opinion *(1 mark)*.

2. **Objectivity (Para 2):** The valuer must conduct every engagement with objectivity and without bias. Any real, potential, or perceived conflict of interest must be disclosed in writing to the client and the regulatory authority. The valuer must not allow undue influence from any party to compromise his professional judgment *(1 mark)*.

3. **Professional Competence and Due Care (Para 3):** The valuer must maintain professional knowledge and skill at the level required for the engagement. He must not accept work for which he is not competent, and must comply with applicable valuation standards and laws. Due care must be exercised in collection, verification, and analysis of data *(1 mark)*.

4. **Confidentiality (Para 4):** The valuer must protect confidential information obtained during the engagement and must not disclose it to any third party, nor use it for personal benefit. Disclosure is permitted only when required by law, court, or regulatory authority *(1 mark)*.

5. **Professional Behaviour (Para 5):** The valuer must conduct himself with dignity and decorum and must not engage in any conduct that discredits the profession. The valuer must not solicit or accept gifts, hospitality, or inducements that may compromise independence or objectivity *(1 mark)*.

6. **Independence and Impartiality (Para 6):** The valuer must be independent of the entity being valued and the party commissioning the valuation. He must not have any direct or indirect financial interest in the asset, nor any close personal or business relationship that may impair independence *(1 mark)*.

7. **Compliance with Laws (Para 7):** The valuer must comply with the Companies Act, 2013, the 2017 Rules, applicable valuation standards, and any other law governing the engagement. The valuer must cooperate with the regulatory authority and disciplinary bodies *(1 mark)*.

#### (b) Disciplinary Mechanism and Penalties (7 Marks)

Breach of the Model Code of Conduct constitutes **professional misconduct** and triggers disciplinary action under **Rule 24** of the 2017 Rules, read with **Section 247 of the Companies Act, 2013** *(1 mark for citation)*.

**The Disciplinary Process (4 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Complaint or Reference:** Proceedings may be initiated on a complaint by any aggrieved party or on a reference by the regulatory authority *(0.5 mark)*.
- **Step 2 — Preliminary Scrutiny:** The authority examines whether a *prima facie* breach exists *(0.5 mark)*.
- **Step 3 — Reference to Disciplinary Committee:** If a prima facie case is made out, the matter is referred to the Disciplinary Committee constituted under Rule 24 *(0.5 mark)*.
- **Step 4 — Show-Cause Notice and Hearing:** The valuer is given a written notice specifying the allegations and is afforded a reasonable opportunity to present his defence, including a personal hearing *(1 mark)*.
- **Step 5 — Order:** The Committee passes a reasoned order. The valuer has a right of appeal to the appellate authority *(1 mark)*.

**Penalties (2 marks):**
The Disciplinary Committee may impose:
- **Warning or censure** *(0.5 mark)*,
- **Suspension of registration** for a period up to five years *(0.5 mark)*,
- **Permanent cancellation** of registration, striking the name from the Register of Valuers *(0.5 mark)*,
- **Monetary penalty** under **Section 247(3)** of the Companies Act, 2013, which may extend to **one lakh rupees**, and in case of continuing failure, a further fine up to **five thousand rupees per day** *(0.5 mark)*. In cases of fraud, the matter may be referred for **criminal prosecution**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> When answering "disciplinary mechanism" questions, students frequently:
> 1. Write *only* about penalties, omitting the procedural steps (Rule 24 procedure). This loses **3–4 marks**.
> 2. Forget to cite **Section 247(3)** of the Companies Act, 2013 for monetary penalty — examiners specifically check for the parent Act.
> 3. Confuse **Rule 24** of the 2017 Rules with the IBC code — these are different statutes; **mention the correct Rule and Section every time**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper Pattern — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Define a **Registered Valuer** under the Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017. Explain the **eligibility criteria** and **registration procedure**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **duties, obligations, and restrictions** imposed on a Registered Valuer under the 2017 Rules, with specific reference to the Model Code of Conduct. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Definition, Eligibility, and Registration (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **Registered Valuer (RV)** is defined under **Rule 2(1)(q)** of the Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017, as a person registered with the **designated authority** under Rule 4 of the Rules. A registered valuer is authorised to undertake valuation of assets and liabilities for purposes specified under the Companies Act, 2013 *(1 mark)*. The registration is **asset-class specific** — a valuer may be registered for **land and building, plant and machinery, or securities and financial assets** *(1 mark)*.

**Eligibility Criteria (3 marks):**
- **Qualification (Rule 3 & 4):** The applicant must hold a recognised qualification in the relevant asset class, such as a degree in civil/mechanical engineering for plant and machinery, or a degree in civil engineering/architecture for land and building, or a professional qualification for securities *(1 mark)*.
- **Experience (Rule 4):** The applicant must have post-qualification experience of not less than **5 years** in the relevant asset class *(1 mark)*.
- **Membership (Rule 4):** The applicant must be a member of a **Recognised Valuer Organisation (RVO)** registered with the authority. RVOs include professional bodies such as the Institution of Engineers (India), ICAI Registered Valuers Organisation, and IBA *(1 mark)*.

**Registration Procedure (2 marks):**
- The applicant must apply to the **designated authority** in the prescribed form (Form A), along with the requisite fee and supporting documents *(0.5 mark)*.
- The authority scrutinises the application, may call for additional information, and if satisfied, grants a **certificate of registration** with a unique registration number *(0.5 mark)*.
- The registration is valid for a period of **5 years**, renewable thereafter on application. The Register of Valuers is maintained by the authority and is accessible to the public *(0.5 mark)*.
- The authority may **reject** an application if the applicant is not fit and proper, has been convicted of an offence, or has been previously disqualified *(0.5 mark)*.

#### (b) Duties, Obligations, and Restrictions (7 Marks)

**General Duties (3 marks):**
- **Rule 7 — Functions:** The valuer must make an impartial, true, and fair valuation of the assets, prepare a valuation report in accordance with the Rules and applicable standards, and assist the authority in disciplinary proceedings if required *(1 mark)*.
- **Rule 8 — Valuation Standards:** The valuer must conduct valuation in accordance with the **valuation standards** notified by the Central Government or the authority *(1 mark)*.
- **Rule 9 — General Obligations:** The valuer must maintain records, submit reports to the authority when called upon, and uphold the dignity of the profession *(1 mark)*.

**Specific Restrictions (2 marks):**
- **Rule 10 — Conflict of Interest:** The valuer must not take up an assignment in which he has a direct or indirect interest, and must disclose any conflict in writing *(1 mark)*.
- **Rule 11 — Restrictions:** The valuer must **not charge a contingent fee** (a fee that depends on the valuation result), must not act for both sides in a transaction, and must not accept a valuation that does not involve an independent professional judgment *(1 mark)*.

**Disclosures and Records (1 mark):**
- **Rule 14–16:** The valuer must disclose the valuation methodology, key assumptions, and any qualifications in the report *(0.5 mark)*.
- **Rule 17:** The valuer must maintain records of every valuation for a period of **at least 5 years** from the date of the report *(0.5 mark)*.

**Model Code of Conduct Linkage (1 mark):**
All the above duties, obligations, and restrictions flow from and are reinforced by the **seven principles of the Model Code of Conduct (Schedule III)** — particularly Pillar 1 (Integrity), Pillar 2 (Objectivity), Pillar 6 (Independence), and Pillar 7 (Compliance with Laws). The Code is the *ethical backbone* that gives meaning to the procedural rules.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Question B:**
> 1. Writing only the *definition* and skipping *eligibility* — you will lose 3 marks.
> 2. Saying "experience of 3 years" — the correct number is **5 years post-qualification**.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **Recognised Valuer Organisation (RVO)** membership — this is mandatory under Rule 4.
> 4. Confusing "valuation report" duties (Rule 7) with "code of conduct" duties (Schedule III) — they are distinct but interrelated; examiners want to see you link them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember — Last-Minute Revision (read twice before exam):**

- **Statutory Source:** Model Code of Conduct = **Rule 18 + Schedule III** of the **Companies (Registered Valuers and Valuation) Rules, 2017**, notified by **MCA** under **Section 247 of the Companies Act, 2013**.
- **Seven Pillars of Schedule III:** *Integrity, Objectivity, Competence, Confidentiality, Professional Behaviour, Independence, Compliance with Laws* — memorise the order.
- **Authority:** The designated authority for registration and discipline is the **Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (IBBI)** for insolvency-related valuers; the **MCA** retains the parent statute.
- **Eligibility:** Recognised qualification + **5 years post-qualification experience** + **RVO membership**.
- **Registration Validity:** **5 years**, renewable.
- **Asset Classes:** Land & Building; Plant & Machinery; Securities & Financial Assets.
- **Conflict of Interest:** Must be **disclosed in writing** under Rule 10.
- **Contingent Fee:** **Prohibited** under Rule 11 — fee must not depend on valuation result.
- **Record Retention:** **Minimum 5 years** under Rule 17.
- **Disciplinary Forum:** **Rule 24** of the 2017 Rules + **Section 247(3)** of the Companies Act for penalties.
- **Maximum Penalty:** Fine up to **₹1,00,000** + further fine up to **₹5,000 per day** for continuing default + suspension/cancellation.
- **Two Statutory Citations Always:** *Rule + Section* (e.g., *Rule 18 read with Schedule III* and *Section 247 of the Companies Act, 2013*).
- **Three Names to Recall:** MCA (notifier), IBBI (authority), RVO (membership body).
- **One Line Definition:** *The MCoC is a statutory code under Schedule III of the 2017 Rules that prescribes the ethical duties of a Registered Valuer.*

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Professional Ethics: Model Code of Conduct.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethical Considerations under Terms of Engagements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethical Considerations under Terms of Engagements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Terms of Engagement (ToE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Terms of Engagement (ToE) in Real Property Valuation** is a formal, written contractual agreement executed between the **Valuer** (the independent professional offering the opinion of value) and the **Client** (the party commissioning the valuation). It explicitly defines the **scope, purpose, basis of value, assumptions, special assumptions, limiting conditions, reporting obligations, fee structure, timeline, and the rights/duties** of both parties, in accordance with recognised valuation standards such as the **International Valuation Standards (IVS)**, **RICS Red Book**, and the **Institution of Valuers (IOV) / ISVAI** code of conduct.

In simple KTU-board language:

> [!NOTE]
> *"Terms of Engagement is the 'rule-book' that the valuer and the client sign before the valuation begins. It tells everyone what is being valued, why it is being valued, how it will be valued, and what the valuer is **NOT** responsible for."*

---

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Ethical Considerations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethical Considerations under Terms of Engagements** are the **mandatory moral, professional, and legal principles** that a valuer must strictly uphold **before, during, and after** accepting a valuation engagement. They are governed by the **Code of Ethics** issued by the **International Ethics Standards (IES)**, the **IVS 2020 (Effective 31 January 2022)**, and the **Council of the Institution of Valuers**. These considerations ensure that the valuation opinion is **credible, defensible, unbiased, and socially responsible**.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏠 Real-World Analogy: The "Honest Real Estate Doctor"**
> Imagine a property as a **patient** and the valuer as a **doctor**.
> 1. **Before the check-up** → The doctor (valuer) signs a **consent form (ToE)** with the patient (client) clearly stating: *"I will check your heart, not your brain. I will charge ₹X. I will keep your reports private."*
> 2. **During the check-up** → The doctor cannot accept a **bribe from a pharmaceutical company** to prescribe their drug (no conflict of interest). The doctor must report the **true blood pressure**, not what the patient wants to hear (no inflating value for a loan).
> 3. **After the check-up** → The doctor cannot share the patient's HIV status with neighbours (confidentiality). The doctor cannot sign the report without actually examining the patient (no fictitious valuation).
>
> **Ethical Considerations = The "Hippocratic Oath" of the valuer.**
> **Terms of Engagement = The signed "Consent Form" between doctor and patient.**

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic is **High-Yield** in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups this topic under **Module 4** because it forms the **"Legal & Ethical Backbone"** of any valuation report. Examiner's mark-sheets consistently show **2-mark to 5-mark questions** on ethical breaches and **14-mark case-study questions** on conflict of interest / independence. Skipping this chapter = guaranteed marks lost.

### 1.5 Core Constituents of the Topic (Master Map)

| **S. No.** | **Constituent** | **Quick Tag** |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | Definition & Purpose of ToE | Foundation |
| 2 | Components / Clauses of ToE | Structure |
| 3 | The Five Ethical Principles (IES Code) | Core Pillars |
| 4 | Independence & Objectivity | Pillar 1 |
| 5 | Confidentiality | Pillar 2 |
| 6 | Competence & Due Care | Pillar 3 |
| 7 | Conflicts of Interest & Disclosure | Pillar 4 |
| 8 | Professional Behaviour & Integrity | Pillar 5 |
| 9 | Consequences of Ethical Breach | Penalty |
| 10 | Case Studies & KTU Exam Traps | Application |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard "Terms of Engagement" Document

A **ToE** in valuation is **not** a casual email — it is a **legally binding letter / agreement** that must contain the following **ten mandatory clauses** (as mandated by **IVS 101 — Scope of Work**, RICS Red Book, and the **Indian Institution of Valuers**):

1. **Identification of the Valuer** — Name, firm, RICS/IVS/IOV membership number, professional qualifications.
2. **Identification of the Client** — Name and address of the party commissioning the report.
3. **Identification of the Asset** — The real property being valued (land, building, plant, or a going concern).
4. **Purpose of Valuation** — Mortgage, sale, purchase, insurance, taxation (stamp duty, capital gains), compulsory acquisition, financial reporting (Ind AS 16 / IFRS), etc.
5. **Basis of Value** — Market Value, Fair Value, Investment Value, Synergistic Value, Liquidation Value, etc. (as per **IVS 104**).
6. **Valuation Approach & Method** — Sales Comparison, Income Capitalisation, Cost / Depreciated Replacement Cost (DRC), or Residual Method.
7. **Assumptions and Special Assumptions** — What the valuer is *assuming to be true* without verification.
8. **Limiting Conditions** — What the valuer is *NOT* doing (e.g., not a structural survey, not testing soil).
9. **Date of Valuation & Reporting Date** — Stating clearly the **"as on" date** (critical for stamp duty cases).
10. **Fee, Liability, and Dispute Resolution** — Cost of the report, limit of liability, and arbitration clause.

---

### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Ethical Conduct (IES Code of Ethics)

The **International Ethics Standards Board for Accountants (IESBA)** has codified **five fundamental principles** that every professional valuer must obey. These are **universally adopted** by RICS, IVSC, IOV, and ISVAI:

#### 🏛️ **Pillar 1 — Integrity**
The valuer must be **straightforward and honest** in all professional and business relationships. Inflating a property's value to help the client get a larger bank loan = **breach of Integrity**.

#### 🏛️ **Pillar 2 — Objectivity**
The valuer must **not allow bias, conflict of interest, or undue influence** of others to override professional judgement. If the valuer's own firm is buying the property, they **cannot** value it for the seller.

#### 🏛️ **Pillar 3 — Professional Competence and Due Care**
The valuer must maintain **professional knowledge and skill** at the level needed to ensure the client receives competent service. Valuing a heritage haveli without knowing heritage valuation norms = **breach of Competence**.

#### 🏛️ **Pillar 4 — Confidentiality**
The valuer must **respect the confidentiality of information** acquired as a result of professional relationships. Disclosing a builder's secret land bank purchase to a rival = **breach of Confidentiality**.

#### 🏛️ **Pillar 5 — Professional Behaviour**
The valuer must comply with **relevant laws and regulations**, and avoid any conduct that discredits the profession. Accepting a ₹50,000 Diwali gift from a developer whose land is being valued = **breach of Professional Behaviour**.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Ethics + ToE Comparison Table

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important table** for your KTU board exam. Memorise the *keywords* in the right column — examiners award marks for these exact phrases.

| **Ethical Principle** | **Mandatory Action by Valuer** | **Breach Example (KTU Favourite)** | **Standard / Reference** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Integrity** | Be honest in report; no manipulation of comparables | Inflating value by 20% to help client get a higher home loan | IVS 101, IES Code §110 |
| **Objectivity** | Avoid bias; withdraw if conflict exists | Valuing a property for both buyer and seller simultaneously | IVS 101, IES Code §120 |
| **Competence** | Accept only assignments within expertise; CPD | Valuing a 5-star hotel without hospitality-sector knowledge | IVS 101, IES Code §130 |
| **Confidentiality** | Do not disclose client data; secure storage | Sharing bank valuation with builder's competitor | IVS 101, IES Code §140 |
| **Professional Behaviour** | Follow law; no inducements; dignified conduct | Accepting a 2-week foreign trip from a developer client | IVS 101, IES Code §150 |
| **Independence** | No financial interest in the asset; no family ties | Valuer's brother is the seller of the property being valued | RICS Red Book, IVS 105 |
| **Disclosure** | Declare all conflicts; clarify assumptions | Hiding a subsidence issue in the assumptions section | IVS 101, RICS PS 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question uses the words *"The valuer is also a director of the company being valued"*, the answer is **always** — *"This is a breach of Independence / Objectivity under the IES Code, and the valuer MUST either withdraw from the engagement OR obtain a written, informed consent from all stakeholders as per IVS 105."*

---

### 2.4 The "Threats-and-Safeguards" Framework (Conceptual)

The IES Code identifies **five categories of threats** to ethical conduct, and for each threat it prescribes **safeguards**. This is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** format in KTU.

| **Type of Threat** | **Meaning** | **Real-World Example** | **Required Safeguard** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Self-Interest Threat** | Valuer has a financial stake | Valuer owns 10% shares in the buyer's company | Disclose, recuse, or use a second partner to review |
| **Self-Review Threat** | Valuer audits his own prior work | Valuer valued the land last year, now he is auditing the builder | Use a completely independent reviewing valuer |
| **Advocacy Threat** | Valuer promotes a client's position | Valuer writes a report defending the client in a tax dispute | Step out of advocacy role; act only as valuer |
| **Familiarity Threat** | Close relationship with client | Valuer's son is the seller's CA | Withdraw or document informed consent |
| **Intimidation Threat** | Pressure to alter opinion | Builder threatens to blacklist the firm | Document pressure; report to RICS; refuse to alter report |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a "valuation" course, the ethical framework is **directly applied** in:
> 1. **Banking & Mortgage Valuation** — Banks (SBI, HDFC, ICICI) refuse to accept valuations from firms with conflict of interest.
> 2. **RERA Compliance** — Builders must disclose the valuer's independence in project registration.
> 3. **Income Tax & Stamp Duty** — Any ethical breach in valuation can lead to **200% penalty** under the **Income Tax Act, 1961 § 43CA / 50C**.
> 4. **Land Acquisition (LA Act 2013)** — Ethical independence is mandatory for the **District Collector's approved panel of valuers**.
> 5. **NCLT & IBC Cases** — Liquidators must appoint **completely independent** valuers, or the resolution plan is struck down.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & KTU Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this topic is **qualitative + procedural**, the "derivation" here is the **step-by-step ethical decision-making algorithm** a valuer must follow. KTU examiners often give a **case study** and ask: *"Identify the ethical issues, cite the principle breached, and prescribe the remedy."* This section gives you the exact algorithm.

---

### 3.1 The "10-Step Ethical Engagement Algorithm" (KTU 14-Mark Answer Skeleton)

**Step 1 — Receive the Enquiry**
A prospective client approaches the valuer with a property to be valued.

**Step 2 — Conflict-of-Interest Pre-Screening**
The valuer checks: *"Do I (or my firm) have any financial, family, or business relationship with the client, the asset, or any interested third party?"*

$$ \text{Conflict Status} = \begin{cases} \text{None} & \Rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 3} \\ \text{Identified} & \Rightarrow \text{Go to Step 9 (Safeguard)} \end{cases} $$

**Step 3 — Verify Professional Competence**
The valuer checks: *"Do I have the technical knowledge, experience, and resources to value this asset class?"*

$$ \text{Competency Score} = \frac{\text{Relevant Projects Completed} \times \text{Asset-Class Match}}{\text{Complexity Factor}} $$

> If Competency Score $< 1.0$ → Refer to a more experienced colleague or decline the assignment.

**Step 4 — Issue the Draft Terms of Engagement Letter**
The valuer drafts a **ToE** containing all **ten mandatory clauses** listed in Section 2.1.

**Step 5 — Negotiate & Sign the ToE**
Both parties sign. **No field work begins** until the ToE is signed.

**Step 6 — Document the Assumptions**
The valuer clearly states every **assumption** (e.g., *"Title is assumed to be clear and marketable"*) and **special assumption** (e.g., *"Property is assumed to have 10 years' remaining economic life"*).

**Step 7 — Conduct Independent Inspection & Research**
The valuer personally inspects the property, takes photographs, verifies dimensions, studies the title, and collects **at least 3 comparable transactions** within the **preceding 12 months** (per IVS 105).

**Step 8 — Apply the Valuation Methods & Cross-Check**
The valuer applies the **appropriate method** (Sales Comparison / Income / Cost / Residual) and **reconciles** the results.

**Step 9 — Apply Ethical Safeguards (if any conflict was found)**
The valuer applies one or more of the following safeguards:

$$ \text{Safeguard} = \begin{cases} \text{Withdrawal from Engagement} \\ \text{Information Barriers (Chinese Walls)} \\ \text{Informed Consent of All Parties (Written)} \\ \text{Independent Partner Review} \\ \text{Rotating the Engagement Team} \end{cases} $$

**Step 10 — Issue the Valuation Report with Disclosures**
The final report contains the **ToE reference, assumptions, special assumptions, limiting conditions, basis of value, date of valuation, and the valuer's signature with membership number**.

---

### 3.2 The KTU "Breach Identification & Remedy" Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In the 14-mark KTU question, you will be given a **case study** with 4–5 events. For each event, you must:
> 1. **Identify** the principle breached.
> 2. **Cite** the IVS / IES Code reference.
> 3. **Prescribe** the remedy.

Below is the **exhaustive KTU mapping matrix**:

| **KTU Case-Study Event** | **Ethical Principle Breached** | **IVS / IES Code Reference** | **Correct Remedy** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Valuer's spouse owns the adjoining plot whose value is being enhanced | Independence / Familiarity | IVS 105, IES Code §120, R220 | Withdraw from engagement; refer to another firm |
| Valuer accepts a 15% "success fee" linked to a target value | Objectivity / Self-Interest Threat | IVS 101, IES Code §120 | Decline success fee; accept only a fixed professional fee |
| Valuer copies the report of another firm and changes the logo | Integrity | IES Code §110 | Disciplinary action; criminal prosecution for plagiarism |
| Valuer signs the report without physically inspecting the property | Competence & Due Care | IVS 101, IVS 105 | Report is invalid; valuer must redo the inspection or be struck off the roll |
| Builder requests valuer to change the value in a draft report | Integrity / Intimidation | IES Code §110, §150 | Refuse in writing; withdraw; lodge complaint with RICS / IOV |
| Bank official leaks the valuer's report to the builder's rival | Confidentiality (Bank's side) | IES Code §140 | Bank liable; valuer should use watermarked, password-protected PDFs |
| Valuer values a property 5% higher in exchange for a paid "consultancy" with the builder | Self-Interest / Advocacy | IES Code §110, §120, §150 | Revoke membership; fine; criminal prosecution under IPC 420 |
| Valuer certifies a property as "heritage-grade" without any expertise in heritage valuation | Competence | IES Code §130, RICS Red Book | Decline engagement; refer to a heritage-valuation specialist |
| Valuer is asked to value the same property twice — once for buyer, once for seller, same week | Self-Review / Familiarity | IVS 105, IES Code §120 | Refuse the second engagement; inform both parties in writing |
| Valuer's assistant is bribed to insert fake comparable sales | Integrity | IES Code §110, IPC 420 | Firm liable; sack the assistant; report to police; inform client |

---

### 3.3 The "Three-Tier Compliance Test" (KTU Favourite)

Whenever the examiner gives a scenario, mentally run this **3-tier test**:

**Tier 1 — Has the ToE been signed and documented?**
If **No** → *Procedural breach under IVS 101 — no work should have begun.*

**Tier 2 — Are there any threats (Self-Interest, Self-Review, Advocacy, Familiarity, Intimidation)?**
If **Yes** → *Apply safeguards (withdraw / wall / consent / review).*

**Tier 3 — Does the report disclose all assumptions, conflicts, and limiting conditions?**
If **No** → *Disclosure breach under IVS 101 and RICS PS 2 — report is incomplete.*

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pro Tip:** Always end your 14-mark answer with this **3-tier test** applied to the case study. Examiners award **2–3 grace marks** for this structured conclusion.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — KTU-Style Application

> **Case Study (14 marks):**
> *"Mr. Arjun is a partner in a firm 'SquareFeet Valuers LLP'. He has been engaged by M/s GreenBuild Developers to value a 2-acre land parcel in Kochi for bank loan purposes. During the engagement, Mr. Arjun discovers that his wife's cousin is a 30% shareholder in GreenBuild Developers. The bank sanctions 80% of the requested loan based on Mr. Arjun's report. Two months later, the borrower defaults and the bank discovers the value was inflated by 25%."*

**Solution Skeleton (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Identification of Issues (5 marks):**
   - Breach of **Independence** (IVS 105) — family relationship via wife's cousin.
   - Breach of **Objectivity** (IES Code §120) — conflict of interest not disclosed.
   - Breach of **Disclosure** (IVS 101) — failure to declare the relationship to the bank.
   - Possible **Integrity** issue (IES Code §110) — value inflated without justification.

2. **Reference to Standards (4 marks):**
   - IVS 101 — Scope of Work.
   - IVS 105 — Valuation Approaches and Methods.
   - IES Code of Ethics §110, §120, §140.
   - RICS Red Book Global Standard PS 2.

3. **Prescribed Remedies (3 marks):**
   - Mr. Arjun should have **withdrawn** or obtained **written informed consent** from the bank.
   - An **independent reviewing partner** should have signed off the report.
   - The bank should be **informed in writing** of the relationship.

4. **Conclusion & Penalty (2 marks):**
   - The report is **invalid and indefensible**.
   - Mr. Arjun may be **struck off the IOV / RICS roll**.
   - The bank can **sue the firm for damages** and **report to the regulator**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of Terms of Engagement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Enquiry for Valuation] --> B{Conflict of Interest Check}
    B -- No Conflict --> C[Verify Competency and Expertise]
    B -- Conflict Found --> D[Apply Safeguards or Withdraw]
    C -- Competent --> E[Draft Terms of Engagement ToE Letter]
    C -- Not Competent --> F[Refer to Specialist or Decline]
    E --> G[Sign ToE by Valuer and Client]
    G --> H[Document Assumptions and Special Assumptions]
    H --> I[Site Inspection and Data Collection]
    I --> J[Apply Valuation Method Sales Income Cost]
    J --> K[Cross Check and Reconcile Values]
    K --> L[Apply Five Pillars of Ethics]
    L --> M[Issue Final Valuation Report with Disclosures]
    M --> N[Retain Records for Minimum 5 Years]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**Define "Terms of Engagement" in the context of real property valuation. List any four mandatory clauses that it must contain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Terms of Engagement (ToE) is a formal, written contractual agreement between the valuer and the client, executed *before* a valuation assignment begins. It defines the **scope, purpose, basis of value, assumptions, limiting conditions, and fee** for the valuation, in compliance with **IVS 101** and the **RICS Red Book**.
>
> **Four Mandatory Clauses (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Identification of the Asset** — the real property being valued.
> 2. **Purpose of Valuation** — e.g., mortgage, taxation, compulsory acquisition.
> 3. **Basis of Value** — e.g., Market Value, Fair Value, Investment Value.
> 4. **Date of Valuation & Reporting Date** — the "as on" date critical for stamp-duty and financial-reporting cases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**State and briefly explain any THREE fundamental principles of the IES Code of Ethics that a valuer must follow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Integrity (1 mark):** The valuer must be straightforward and honest in all professional relationships. Inflating the value of a property to help a client obtain a larger bank loan is a breach of integrity under **IES Code §110**.
> 2. **Objectivity (1 mark):** The valuer must not allow bias, conflict of interest, or undue influence to override professional judgement, as mandated by **IES Code §120**.
> 3. **Confidentiality (1 mark):** The valuer must not disclose confidential information acquired during the engagement, as mandated by **IES Code §140**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Every Part B question in this module offers an **INTERNAL CHOICE** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Students must answer **ONE** of the two. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]**
*Explain the "Terms of Engagement" in valuation. Discuss the essential components of a well-drafted ToE letter as per IVS 101, and explain why each component is critical for the credibility of the valuation report.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The Terms of Engagement is the foundational contract between the valuer and the client that governs the entire valuation assignment. As per **IVS 101 — Scope of Work**, no field work should begin before the ToE is signed.

**2. Essential Components (5 marks — 0.5 each for 10 components):**
- **Identification of the Valuer** — establishes accountability.
- **Identification of the Client** — defines to whom the report is addressed.
- **Identification of the Asset** — removes ambiguity about *what* is being valued.
- **Purpose of Valuation** — dictates the *basis of value* (e.g., Market Value for sale, Fair Value for IFRS reporting).
- **Basis of Value** — selects the appropriate IVS 104 standard.
- **Valuation Approach & Method** — sales comparison, income, cost, or residual.
- **Assumptions & Special Assumptions** — declares what is assumed to be true.
- **Limiting Conditions** — clarifies what the valuer is *not* doing (e.g., structural survey, soil test).
- **Date of Valuation & Reporting Date** — anchors the value in time.
- **Fee, Liability, and Dispute Resolution Clause** — protects both parties legally.

**3. Why Each Component is Critical (1 mark):**
Each component creates a **defensive shield** for the valuer in case of litigation. For example, a clearly stated *"no structural survey"* clause protects the valuer from being sued if a hidden defect emerges later.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** A well-drafted ToE is the **first line of defence** of any valuation report.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]**
*Discuss the ethical responsibilities of a valuer regarding **Independence, Objectivity, and Confidentiality**. Provide two real-world examples of breach for each principle and the remedy prescribed by the IES Code.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Independence (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* The valuer must have no financial, family, or business interest in the property or the parties involved.
- *Breach Example 1:* Valuer's wife owns 30% of the developer's company. **Remedy:** Withdraw from engagement (IVS 105).
- *Breach Example 2:* Valuer's firm previously sold the property 6 months ago. **Remedy:** Disclose the prior involvement in writing; obtain informed consent from the bank.

**2. Objectivity (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* The valuer's opinion must not be swayed by pressure from the client.
- *Breach Example 1:* Builder requests a "target value" of ₹10 Cr; valuer inflates from ₹7.5 Cr. **Remedy:** Refuse, withdraw, and lodge complaint with RICS / IOV.
- *Breach Example 2:* Valuer accepts a 15% success fee linked to achieving a target value. **Remedy:** Decline success fee structure; accept only a fixed professional fee.

**3. Confidentiality (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* All information gathered during the engagement must remain private.
- *Breach Example 1:* Valuer shares the bank valuation report with the developer's rival. **Remedy:** Disciplinary action; termination; civil suit.
- *Breach Example 2:* Valuer's assistant clicks a photo of the title deed and posts it on LinkedIn. **Remedy:** Dismiss the assistant; inform the client; review data-security protocols.

**4. Concluding Statement (1 mark):**
These three principles form the **trust triangle** between the valuer, the client, and the public. A breach of any one principle erodes the credibility of the entire valuation profession.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]**
*Describe the **five fundamental principles** of the IES Code of Ethics. How do they apply to a real property valuer in India?*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (0.5 mark):**
The **International Ethics Standards Board (IESBA)** has codified **five fundamental principles** that are universally adopted by **RICS, IVSC, IOV, and ISVAI**.

**2. The Five Principles (4 marks — 0.8 each):**
- **Integrity** — Be honest and straightforward. *Application:* Never falsify comparable sales data.
- **Objectivity** — Avoid bias and conflict of interest. *Application:* Disclose family ties with the developer.
- **Professional Competence and Due Care** — Maintain knowledge and skill. *Application:* Undertake 20 hours of CPD annually.
- **Confidentiality** — Protect client information. *Application:* Use password-protected PDFs and encrypted cloud storage.
- **Professional Behaviour** — Comply with laws and avoid discrediting conduct. *Application:* Refuse to value without inspecting.

**3. Indian Context (2 marks):**
- The **Institute of Valuers (IOV)** under the **Valuers' Registration Scheme** mandates these principles.
- The **Income Tax Act, 1961 § 43CA / 50C** penalises inflated valuations at **200% of the differential**.
- **RERA 2016** and **LA Act 2013** require ethical independence of the panel valuer.

**4. Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Adherence to the five principles is not optional — it is the **license to practice** as a Registered Valuer in India.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]**
*What are the **"Threats to Ethical Conduct"** identified by the IES Code? Explain any THREE threats with suitable real-world examples and the safeguards prescribed for each.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (0.5 mark):**
The IES Code identifies **five categories of threats** that can compromise ethical conduct. For every threat, **safeguards** must be applied at the firm, engagement, or national level.

**2. Three Threats with Examples and Safeguards (6 marks — 2 marks each):**

**Threat 1 — Self-Interest Threat**
- *Meaning:* Valuer has a financial stake in the outcome.
- *Example:* Valuer owns equity shares in the buyer's company.
- *Safeguard:* Disclose the holding; recuse from the engagement; use a second partner to review.

**Threat 2 — Advocacy Threat**
- *Meaning:* Valuer's objectivity is compromised by promoting the client's position.
- *Example:* Valuer writes a report defending the client in a tax-evasion case at the ITAT.
- *Safeguard:* Decline the advocacy role; act only as an independent valuer.

**Threat 3 — Intimidation Threat**
- *Meaning:* Valuer is pressured to alter the opinion.
- *Example:* Builder threatens to blacklist the firm if the value is not increased.
- *Safeguard:* Document the threat in writing; inform RICS / IOV; refuse to alter the report.

**3. Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
A diligent valuer must **routinely self-assess** for these threats before accepting and during the execution of every engagement.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Top 7 Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ToE with the Valuation Report.** They are different documents. ToE is signed *before* the work; the report is the *output* of the work.
> 2. **Writing "confidentiality" instead of "confidentiality" with a proper example.** Always give an *example* of breach — abstract answers fetch 0 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to cite the IVS / IES Code section number.** Examiners award **0.5 mark** for the section number (e.g., *IES Code §110*). Skipping it = losing the mark.
> 4. **Listing threats without safeguards.** A 7-mark question on threats MUST include **both** the threat and its safeguard. Only listing the threats = 3 marks lost.
> 5. **Not applying the Three-Tier Compliance Test** at the end of a case study. This is a **guaranteed 2-mark bonus** that 80% of students skip.
> 6. **Writing "valuer should be ethical"** — this is vague. Write **"valuer must withdraw from the engagement as per IVS 105 and report the conflict to the client in writing."** Specificity = marks.
> 7. **Forgetting Indian context.** Always anchor your answer in **Indian statutes** — IVS 101, RICS Red Book, IOV, Income Tax Act 43CA / 50C, RERA 2016, and LA Act 2013.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🔁 Rapid Revision Checklist — Ethical Considerations under ToE**

- **Terms of Engagement (ToE)** = written contract signed *before* valuation; mandates **ten clauses** under **IVS 101**.
- The **five pillars of ethics** (IES Code) = **Integrity, Objectivity, Competence, Confidentiality, Professional Behaviour**.
- The **five threats** = **Self-Interest, Self-Review, Advocacy, Familiarity, Intimidation**.
- **Safeguards** = Withdrawal, Chinese Walls, Informed Written Consent, Independent Partner Review, Rotation of Teams.
- **Independence** is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU Module 4 — family ties, prior involvement, and equity holdings are the three classic breach triggers.
- **Disclosure** is non-negotiable — **every assumption, conflict, and limiting condition** must be in writing.
- **Records** must be retained for a **minimum of 5 years** after the engagement.
- **Indian Legal Anchors** to remember: **IVS 101, IVS 104, IVS 105, RICS Red Book PS 2, IOV Code, Income Tax Act § 43CA / § 50C, RERA 2016, LA Act 2013**.
- **KTU Triggers for a 14-mark case study question:** *"Builder is also the valuer"*, *"Valuer is the seller's relative"*, *"Bank official leaks the report"*, *"Valuer signs without inspection"*, *"Success-fee-based engagement"*.
- **Closing formula for any KTU answer:** *"As per the IES Code of Ethics and IVS 101, the valuer must [action], document [disclosure], and if breach occurs, [remedy]."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET862 - VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Salient Features of Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act 2016

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA)** is an Indian Parliamentary legislation enacted on **26 March 2016** and notified on **1 May 2016** (with full effect from **1 May 2017**). It is the first dedicated central statute aimed at regulating the previously unorganized, opaque, and consumer-exploitative real estate sector in India. The Act mandates the registration of real estate projects, registration of real estate agents, disclosure of project timelines and approvals, protection of home-buyers' financial interests, and establishment of Real Estate Regulatory Authorities (RERAs) and Appellate Tribunals at the State level.

For a KTU valuation perspective, RERA is critical because it directly affects:
- The **market value** of under-construction properties.
- The **risk profile** and **discount rate** applied by valuers.
- The **timing of cash flows** for developers and investors.
- The **legal clarity** required for collateral-based lending by banks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECET862 – Module 4):**
> The student is expected to understand how RERA redefines *project viability analysis*, *risk-adjusted valuation*, and *consumer protection compliance* in real property valuation practice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a scenario before RERA: A builder in Kerala announces a **50-flat apartment project** in 2015, accepts booking amounts from 50 families, but uses **70 % of the collected money** to fund a new project in Bengaluru. The Kerala project gets stuck for **6 years** — no completion, no refund, no accountability. This was the **pre-RERA reality** in India.

**RERA is the "watchman with a notebook" that:**
1. **Registers** every project and every agent (no more unverified builders).
2. **Forces disclosure** of layouts, carpet area, timelines, and approvals publicly.
3. **Locks the buyer's money** in a designated **Escrow Account** — the builder can only withdraw a *proportionate amount* tied to construction progress.
4. **Punishes delays** with quantified penalties and imprisonment.
5. **Provides a fast-track tribunal** for dispute resolution within **60 days**.

In valuation terms: RERA converts an **"unverifiable, high-risk, opaque asset"** into a **"regulated, escrow-backed, transparently documented asset"** — which directly reduces the *risk premium* component in any Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) valuation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** As per RERA Section 4, the **Carpet Area** (usable floor area excluding walls, common areas, balconies, verandahs) is the *sole* basis for sale — not the inflated "super built-up area" used pre-RERA. For a valuer, this is the legally accepted **measurement standard** for valuation.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture of RERA (Why and How)

The Act is built on **Five Operational Pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Mandatory Registration
- **Why?** To prevent fly-by-night operators and bring the sector under formal supervision.
- **How?** Every project with land area **≥ 500 sq.m** OR with **≥ 8 apartments** must be registered with the State RERA. Builders must submit authentic layout plans, approvals, title deeds, and timelines at the time of registration.

### Pillar 2 — Transparency and Disclosure
- **Why?** Information asymmetry was the single biggest cause of buyer exploitation.
- **How?** The promoter must publish on the RERA website: project layout, sanctioned plans, status of land, proforma of agreement, and quarterly progress updates. Section 11 imposes a **disclosure duty** on the promoter.

### Pillar 3 — Financial Discipline (Escrow Account)
- **Why?** To prevent diversion of buyer funds.
- **How?** **70 % of project receivables** must be deposited in a designated **Escrow Account** with a scheduled bank. Withdrawals are permitted only on **certified progress certificates** from a CA, engineer, and architect.

### Pillar 4 — Consumer Protection (Allottee Rights)
- **Why?** To restore equity between a powerful builder and an individual buyer.
- **How?** Section 13–18 grant the allottee rights to: title of property, refund with interest on false promises, claim of defect rectification, and compensation for delay.

### Pillar 5 — Adjudication and Enforcement
- **Why?** A law without enforcement is meaningless.
- **How?** RERA Authority (quasi-judicial), Appellate Tribunal, and Adjudicating Officer ensure penalties ranging from **5 % to 10 %** of project cost, with imprisonment up to **3 years** for repeat offenders.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Provisions of RERA 2016

| **Section** | **Provision** | **Practical Significance for Valuation** |
|-------------|---------------|------------------------------------------|
| Section 2(v) | Defines **"Real Estate Project"** | Defines valuation scope boundary |
| Section 2(w) | Defines **"Carpet Area"** | Standard measurement unit for valuation |
| Section 3 | **Registration of Project** mandatory | Confers marketability to the property |
| Section 4 | Withdrawal of registration possible | Risk of project cancellation enters valuation |
| Section 5 | **Functions of Promoter** | Establishes due diligence baseline |
| Section 7 | **Timely completion obligation** | Penalty for delay = encumbrance on cash flows |
| Section 11 | **Duty of Promoter to disclose** | Reduces information asymmetry in valuation |
| Section 12-13 | **Prohibited activities** | Risk of non-compliance adds risk premium |
| Section 14-18 | **Rights of Allottee** | Buyer protection increases marketability |
